posts.summary: This 2003 workshop report explores how to scale XP and Agile methods beyond small teams, featuring perspectives from Ken Schwaber, Philippe Kruchten, and Martin Fowler. The key tension throughout is whether scaling Agile is even the right question to ask. Practitioners shared techniques like seeding new teams with architecture founders and coordinating sync points between autonomous teams. However, Martin Fowler's keynote challenged the premise entirely, arguing that many teams are unnecessarily large and should scale down rather than scale up their methodology. The conference highlighted that communication-centric practices pose the greatest scaling challenges, while coding mechanics like TDD and pair programming can scale more readily.
key_points: Scaling can be achieved by proving architecture early with a core team, then seeding new teams with original architects as founders
Communication-centric XP practices (standups, iteration planning) are harder to scale than coding practices (TDD, refactoring, pairing)
Ken Schwaber observed that average performers often rise to excellence in supportive, self-organizing team environments
Most literature targets developers who already know how to code - broader adoption requires 'cookbooks for people who don't know how to cook'
When asked if their projects could maintain productivity after removing the weakest half of the team, most attendees raised their hands
money_quotes: Business logic is an oxymoron. There is nothing logical about most aspects of business.
Today we have the cook books for people who know how to cook. What we do not have today are cook books for people who do not know how to cook.
The greatest challenge we see with scaling XP, is the same challenge we see every large project faces - how to effectively communicate and coordinate large numbers of people.
People who have been categorized as average, or even poor, often rise to excellence given a supportive, creative, team oriented environment.

Martin asked the audience to raise their hand if they had ever been on a project that could have kept about the same level of productivity after removing the weakest half of the team. The majority of the room raised their hands.
Instead of first looking at ways to scale XP and Agile, Martin recommended teams first look for ways of scaling down projects that are unnecessarily large.
posts.key_insight: Before asking how to scale Agile methods to large teams, first ask whether your team needs to be that large in the first place.

posts.summary: Fowler explores the trade-offs between putting domain logic in application code versus SQL queries when building enterprise applications. He presents three approaches to a business rule calculation: transaction scripts, domain models, and complex SQL queries. While SQL can be dramatically faster (20x in his example) and more concise for certain operations, in-memory approaches offer better modifiability, testability, and encapsulation. He advocates for keeping domain logic in memory by default, using optimized SQL only for performance hot spots, and notes that views can help avoid duplication when using SQL-based approaches.
key_points: Complex SQL queries can dramatically outperform in-memory approaches (20x faster in his example) by doing selection and aggregation in the database rather than schlepping thousands of rows to the client
Domain models separate data access from business logic, meaning query optimizations don't require changing domain logic code, while transaction scripts require rewriting everything
Views are the SQL equivalent of avoiding duplication - they can encapsulate business logic definitions in one place, but this technique is underutilized in practice
Encapsulation through in-memory layers becomes essential when data comes from multiple sources (not just SQL databases), making SQL-only approaches insufficient
Testability favors in-memory approaches since you can use service stubs to replace database connections, enabling faster test execution
money_quotes: My philosophy is that most of the time you should focus on writing maintainable code. Then use a profiler to identify hot spots and then replace only those hot spots with faster but less clear code.
Performance isn't the only factor in deciding what route to go, but it's often a concluding one. If you have a hot spot that you absolutely need to improve, then other factors come second.

One of the biggest differences between the transaction script and the domain model is the impact of changing the query structure. For the transaction script, it pretty much means altering the entire script.
It seems that people rarely discuss using views like this to avoid duplication. Books I've seen on SQL don't seem to discuss doing this kind of thing.
SQL makes some things easy, but other things more difficult. Some people find SQL easy to work with, others find it horribly cryptic. The teams personal comfort is a big issue here.
If you want portability keep logic out of SQL.
Common practice in SQL seems to not test. Indeed it's not uncommon to find essential views and stored procedures not even held in configuration management tools.
posts.key_insight: Default to keeping domain logic in memory for maintainability and encapsulation, but pragmatically use optimized SQL queries for performance-critical hot spots identified through profiling.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses whether large projects can use agile techniques, acknowledging that agile methods were designed for smaller teams and haven't been fully proven at scale. He argues that rather than focusing on scaling agile up, teams should first try to scale their projects down. Drawing on experience from XP, FDD, and RUP, he notes that most projects could lose half their people without slowing down, as large teams carry significant communication and management overhead. The counterintuitive conclusion is that smaller teams of more capable people are faster and cheaper, even at higher individual cost.
key_points: Agile techniques haven't been fully proven at large scale yet - new methods are naturally tried on smaller projects first
Different agile approaches have different sweet spots: XP works for up to 20, FDD for mid-tens, RUP has been used with 200+
Most projects could lose about half their people without slowing down
Large teams carry significant overhead in communication and management
Scaling agile up should be the last resort, not the first choice
money_quotes: Scaling Agile methods is the last thing you should do.
A better approach is to try to scale down your project.
Most projects could lose about half the people of the project without making things go slower.
Using smaller teams staffed with more able people is usually faster and cheaper, even if everyone is more individually expensive.
For software projects I think the primary measure of large is number of people.
With any technique or technology I wouldn't recommend using it on anything larger than twice the size of something you've used it successfully already.
posts.key_insight: Instead of asking how to scale agile up to large teams, first ask whether you can scale your project down to fit agile's natural strengths.

posts.summary: This brief post addresses a common UML diagramming question: how to represent collections like ArrayList in class diagrams. Fowler explains that collections shouldn't be shown as separate classes; instead, use associations with multiplicity markers. The asterisk (*) implies a collection relationship. For ordered collections like lists, use the {ordered} constraint. Special collection types can be indicated with stereotypes, but this should be rare.
key_points: Collections are represented through associations with multiplicity, not as explicit classes on the diagram
UML strictly uses set semantics for multivalued properties; ordered collections require the {ordered} constraint
Most developers pragmatically use lists for collections despite UML's set-oriented default
Stereotypes like <<doubly linked list>> can indicate special collection types but should be used sparingly
money_quotes: Usually you don't show the collection on the class diagram as a class.
By marking the association with a * multiplicity you imply that a collection is in play.
To be strict with the UML you should always use a set for your collections since multivalued properties have set semantics in UML.
In practice most people use lists for collections (if nothing else it can help testing).
However you should find you only do this very rarely.
posts.key_insight: UML class diagrams represent collections through association multiplicity rather than explicit collection classes, with set semantics as the default and {ordered} constraints for lists.

posts.summary: This post examines how different object-oriented languages implement access modifiers to control which classes can access features of other classes. Fowler traces the evolution from C++'s three-modifier system through Java and C#'s variations, contrasting these with Smalltalk's object-based (rather than class-based) approach to access control. He notes that access control can always be circumvented through reflection or memory manipulation, meaning its true purpose is communication rather than enforcement. The post concludes by introducing the concept of 'published' interfaces as a distinction more important than public vs private.
key_points: C++, Java, and C# all have different interpretations of 'protected' access, creating subtle but important distinctions between languages
Smalltalk's access model is object-based rather than class-based—instances of the same class cannot access each other's private data
Access modifiers can be circumvented through reflection or memory manipulation in virtually all languages
The distinction between 'published' interfaces (exposed to external teams) and merely 'public' ones (exposed within a project) is more significant than public vs private
money_quotes: In C++ friends can touch each others' private parts.
In Smalltalk's world view access is based on objects, so since Martin and Kent are different objects Martin has no business getting at Kent's fields.
The point of access control is not to prevent access, but more to signal that the class prefers to keep some things to itself.
Using access modifiers, like so many things in programming, is primarily about communication.
I believe that the distinction between published and public is more important than that between public and private.
posts.key_insight: Access modifiers are fundamentally about communication between programmers rather than enforcement of access restrictions, and the most important distinction is not public vs private but published vs unpublished.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses whether using the protected access modifier for class data is good OO design. He argues there's no universal answer—it depends on whether you're building for internal team use or external frameworks. For internal hierarchies, protected data is acceptable since you control all the code. For frameworks with external subclasses, protected fields become part of your published interface and should be avoided due to the fragile base class problem. His default is private fields, rarely changed to protected.
key_points: The appropriateness of protected data depends on whether code will be extended by your team or external clients
Internal hierarchies using subclasses for polymorphic behavior can reasonably use protected data
Framework classes intended for external override should treat protected members as published interface
The fragile base class problem means subclasses can break superclasses when given deep access
Publishing fields directly is generally bad, so protected fields in published interfaces should be avoided
money_quotes: I think the reason this topic gets so difficult is because people look at it either from the angle of in-team or cross-team development, and it's this point of view that dominates.
After all it's all my code - I'm using the subclasses to provide polymorphic behavior, not to provide modularity.
If you're intending that clients outside your control will override a certain class, then you should treat your protected features as part of your published interface.
Subclasses can easily break superclasses if you're not careful - this is referred to as the fragile base class problem.
Publishing fields is a bad idea, so I wouldn't do it in that case.
I'll admit my default is to make fields private and I only rarely change it to protected.
posts.key_insight: Whether protected data is appropriate depends entirely on your audience: internal polymorphism justifies it, but external extensibility demands treating protected members as published interface.

posts.summary: Fowler challenges the Standish Group's CHAOS report definition of project 'failure,' which labels projects unsuccessful if they're late, over budget, or missing features. He argues that Windows 95, though notoriously late, was a business success—proving the definition flawed. The real failure, Fowler contends, is in estimation, not execution. True success should be measured by return on investment, though this is notoriously difficult to quantify. Business satisfaction relative to cost, while fuzzy, is the honest measure.
key_points: The CHAOS report's 34% success rate measures schedule/budget adherence, not actual business value
Being late or over budget doesn't equal failure—Windows 95 proves this
What we call 'project failure' is often 'estimation failure'
ROI is the ideal success metric but is nearly impossible to measure accurately
Business success is inherently fuzzy—'otherwise computers would be CEOs'
money_quotes: Rather than saying that a project is failed because it is late, or has cost overruns - I would argue that it's the *estimate* that failed.
So the CHAOS report isn't chronicling software project failure, it's chronicling software estimation failure.
Windows 95 was horribly late yet was extremely successful for Microsoft's business.
This may be an unsatisfactorily fuzzy definition, but many business activities are just as fuzzy. Otherwise computers would be CEOs.
posts.key_insight: Projects labeled as 'failures' for missing estimates may actually be successes—we're measuring the wrong thing when we track schedule adherence instead of business value.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the confusing distinction between aggregation and composition in UML. The confusion stems from pre-UML methods having different semantics for part-whole relationships, which couldn't be reconciled during standardization. Aggregation (white diamond) carries no standard meaning beyond regular association and is essentially a 'modeling placebo.' Composition (black diamond) has actual semantics, specifically that an object can only be part of one composition relationship at a time.
key_points: UML aggregation and composition confusion stems from pre-UML methods having incompatible definitions of part-whole relationships
Aggregation has no standard semantics beyond regular association - it's a 'modeling placebo'
Composition enforces that an object can only be part of one composition relationship at a time
If you encounter aggregation in a diagram, you should ask the author what they mean by it
money_quotes: Few things in the UML cause more consternation than aggregation and composition.
Aggregation has no semantics beyond that of a regular association. It is, as Jim Rumbaugh puts it, a modeling placebo.
People can, and do, use it - but there are no standard meanings for it.
If you see it, you should inquire as to what the author means by it.
I would advise not using it yourself without some form of explanation.
posts.key_insight: UML aggregation is semantically meaningless - a 'modeling placebo' - while composition actually constrains objects to belong to only one whole at a time.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that refactoring is useful for platform building, but its effectiveness depends heavily on the platform's development approach. The key challenge is that platforms typically have Published Interfaces, which create ripple effects when changed and make refactoring more difficult. However, many refactorings don't affect the published interface and can be done freely within internal boundaries. Fowler contrasts two platform-building styles: FoundationPlatform (where APIs must be fixed early, limiting refactoring) and HarvestedPlatform (where refactoring is extremely useful). He advocates for the harvested approach, noting he's seen many failures with the foundation approach.
key_points: Published interfaces are the main constraint on refactoring platforms, as changes create severe ripple effects for users
Many refactorings don't touch published interfaces and can be done freely within internal boundaries
Languages typically don't provide clean ways to mark published interfaces, requiring extra work to establish boundaries
FoundationPlatform approaches require early API stabilization, limiting refactoring's usefulness
HarvestedPlatform is a better approach where refactoring becomes extremely valuable
money_quotes: Published interfaces are usually a pain for refactoring because any refactoring that changes a published interface is made much more difficult.
Sadly languages usually don't allow you to mark published interfaces very cleanly, so you'll usually have to do some extra work to set up a properly published section to your interface.
I've seen a lot of failures in building platforms that way.
I think HarvestedPlatform is a much better way to go, and refactoring is *extremely* useful when you are building a HarvestedPlatform.
posts.key_insight: The viability of refactoring in platform development depends on your platform-building philosophy—harvesting platforms from working code enables refactoring, while building foundation-first constrains it.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that while Java is a reasonable first choice for teaching object-orientation due to its ubiquity and job market value, learners benefit from exposure to dynamically typed alternatives. He recommends Ruby over Python for its purer OO model and blocks feature, which teaches code structuring concepts difficult to grasp otherwise. He dismisses Smalltalk despite personal affection because scripting languages have practical everyday utility. Most importantly, he insists that OO must be learned by building things, not just studying principles or UML diagrams.
key_points: Java's dominance in OO teaching is justified by practical factors (jobs, tooling, syntax familiarity) but shouldn't be the only language taught
Dynamic typing provides valuable contrast to static typing, and every developer needs a scripting language for automation
Ruby's blocks feature teaches powerful code structuring concepts that serve as an entry point to functional programming
Practical utility matters: even Smalltalk lovers don't use it professionally anymore, making scripting languages better teaching tools
Software design understanding comes from doing, not reading—learners must build things and try different approaches
money_quotes: Java/C#/C++ show a certain style of OO programming, and if you're going to introduce OO to people I think it's good to show an alternative.
Ruby's more pure in its OO (useful if that's what you're learning) and for me feels significantly cleaner.
Blocks are a powerful programming tool that teaches a number of ideas about code structuring that's hard to grasp otherwise - and provides an entry point for functional languages.
The advantage of a scripting language is that it's something that a professional programmer will use regularly, while even a Smalltalk-lover like me hasn't cranked up an image in years.
For me software design is much like mathematics. While you can get a shallow appreciation by reading or listening you only really understand it by doing.
To really understand OO you really have to build something - and be encouraged to try it different ways.
posts.key_insight: True understanding of object-orientation comes not from studying principles but from building things in multiple languages that show different OO styles.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces his 'bliki' concept—a hybrid between blogs and wikis that combines the best of both formats. He finds blogs too ephemeral, with content that quickly becomes irrelevant, while wikis tend toward rambling disorganization. The bliki allows short-form posts like a blog but builds a cross-linked, lasting body of work like a wiki. He intends to use it for forming ideas too short for full articles and for providing lasting answers to common questions. The term was coined by Ward Cunningham, and Fowler built his implementation in Ruby during a flight.
key_points: Blogs are too ephemeral—writing that disappears quickly isn't worth the effort of creating it
Wikis enable quick content creation but tend toward disorganized rambling
The bliki hybrid captures blog freshness (RSS, recent changes) with wiki durability (cross-linked, lasting content)
Parentage metaphors apply: a hybrid inherits some characteristics from each parent, not all
The implementation was surprisingly simple—a few hundred lines of Ruby
money_quotes: Blog sounds like something I should pay a physician to remove.
I find writing too hard to want to spend it on things that disappear.
Like a blog, it allows me to post short thoughts when I have them. Like a wiki it will build up a body of cross-linked pieces that I hope will still be interesting in a year's time.
I think this reading misunderstands the nature of parenthood. After all, I'm a cross between my mother and father, but I don't inherit all my mother's characteristics.
In my case I rolled my own in few hundred lines of Ruby on a flight from Boston to Bangalore.
posts.key_insight: The best writing format balances immediacy with durability—capturing thoughts quickly while building something that remains valuable over time.

posts.summary: The post examines the 'UML as Blueprint' approach, which attempts to apply traditional engineering practices to software by having designers create detailed specifications that programmers then implement. Fowler explains that this mode of UML usage focuses on completeness, with forward engineering producing designs detailed enough that coding becomes a straightforward, low-thought activity. In reverse engineering, blueprints convey detailed code information graphically. This approach requires sophisticated CASE tools for round-trip engineering between diagrams and code, with some 'tripless' tools using source code directly as the repository.
key_points: UML as Blueprint attempts to separate design from coding, mimicking how blueprints work in physical engineering disciplines
This approach assumes rare, expensive designers should focus on detailed specifications while cheaper coders handle implementation
Forward engineering blueprints should be complete enough that programming 'requires little thought'
Round-trip tools support both forward and reverse engineering through a shared repository
Tripless tools use source code as the repository with diagrams as a graphical viewport
money_quotes: This would allow rare and expensive software designers to concentrate on the blueprints while many cheaper coders concentrate on construction.
That design should be sufficiently complete that all design decisions are laid out and the programming should follow as a pretty straightforward activity that requires little thought.
The term CASE has become a dirty word and vendors try to avoid it now.
I like to think of these as tripless tools.
posts.key_insight: The blueprint approach to UML assumes software design can be separated from construction like physical engineering, but the history of CASE tools becoming 'a dirty word' hints at the limitations of this analogy.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the proposition that UML could serve as a programming language, where detailed diagrams compile directly into executable code. He expresses skepticism about whether graphical programming is inherently more productive, noting his experience with failed graphical environments that were slower than writing code. He observes that even genuinely productive languages struggle for adoption due to factors beyond pure productivity. He warns that UML/MDA proponents often mistakenly believe they're achieving platform independence when they're actually committing to a different platform.
key_points: Graphical programming doesn't succeed merely by virtue of being graphical - usability and speed matter more
Drawing diagrams (like flowcharts) is often slower than writing equivalent code
Programming language adoption depends on many factors beyond raw productivity
UML and MDA constitute a platform commitment, not platform independence
money_quotes: I don't believe that graphical programming will succeed just because it's graphical.
Compare coding an algorithm to drawing a flow chart for it.
Most people I know don't program for a living in the language they consider to be the most productive.
Languages need a lot of things to come together for them to succeed.
Using the UML and MDA is a platform, and you are just as much committed to it as any other platform.
posts.key_insight: The appeal of higher-level abstraction doesn't guarantee adoption or productivity gains—graphical programming must prove itself faster and more practical than writing code, not just more visual.

posts.summary: A Foundation Platform is built before the applications that will use it, based on upfront analysis of anticipated needs. While this approach seems logical—creating a stable API before application development begins—Fowler argues it consistently fails in practice. The core problem is that it's nearly impossible to accurately predict platform requirements without real application experience. This leads to platforms with unnecessary capabilities that don't actually match what applications need. Fowler recommends the alternative Harvested Platform approach instead.
key_points: Foundation Platforms are built by analyzing anticipated needs before any applications exist
The approach requires a stable API before application work begins to avoid knock-on effects from platform changes
Despite sounding reasonable, this approach consistently fails in practice
The fundamental problem is the difficulty of understanding real platform needs without actual usage
Platforms built this way end up bloated with unnecessary features while missing what's actually needed
money_quotes: While this sounds reasonable in theory, I've always seen this work badly in practice.
The problem is that it's very hard to understand the real needs of the platform.
Often its capabilities don't really match what that the applications really need.
posts.key_insight: You cannot accurately predict platform requirements through upfront analysis—real understanding only comes from building actual applications first.

posts.summary: Fowler advocates building platforms through an emergent, bottom-up process rather than upfront design. The approach starts by building a well-designed application without attempting to make it generic. When building subsequent applications with overlapping needs, duplication is factored out into a shared 'proto-platform' that stabilizes over multiple iterations. This harvesting approach, while seemingly less efficient than building a foundation platform first, proves more effective in practice.
key_points: Start by building a well-factored application, not a platform
Identify duplication only when building the second application with similar needs
Factor shared code into a common area that becomes the proto-platform
Keep everything in a single codebase for the first few applications before separating
This emergent approach works better than upfront platform design despite appearing less efficient
money_quotes: To build a platform by harvesting, you start by not trying to build a platform, but by building an application.
While you build the application you don't try to develop generic code, but you do work hard to build a well-factored and well designed application.
As you find duplication you factor out into a common area, this common area is the proto-platform.
While this sounds harder and less efficient than FoundationPlatform it seems to work better in practice.
posts.key_insight: Platforms should emerge from the concrete needs of multiple applications rather than be designed speculatively upfront.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler responds to Robert Cringely's criticism of refactoring by agreeing with several specific complaints while defending the practice overall. Fowler acknowledges that refactoring stable code, having separate teams refactor others' code, and misusing the term for any code change are all legitimate problems. However, he strongly disagrees with Cringely's conclusion that 80% of refactoring wastes time and should be stopped by managers. Fowler argues that refactoring's value lies in making code easier to change, which increases productivity and prevents the 'death spiral' of patching poorly designed code.
key_points: Refactoring should only be applied to code you expect to change in the future, not stable code
Teams should refactor only their own code, not wander into other teams' codebases
Refactoring is a specific disciplined process using small semantics-preserving transformations, not a synonym for any restructuring
The value of refactoring is that improved design makes code easier to change, increasing productivity
Without refactoring, developers waste time working around poor designs with patches that make things worse
money_quotes: The whole point of refactoring is that it improves the design of existing code. The value of well designed code is that it is easier to change.
There's no point refactoring code that's stable.
The idea of one team wandering around refactoring other teams' code without telling anyone is certainly not something that I would recommend.
Refactoring is a very particular process that uses a series of small semantics-preserving transformations to change a code base.
Time after time I've seen people waste time working their way around poorly designed code with patches that only make it worse.
Refactoring is a way to get out of that particular death spiral - which is why I consider it to be such a valuable technique.
posts.key_insight: While misapplications of refactoring deserve criticism, the practice itself remains valuable because improving design makes code easier to change and prevents the death spiral of accumulating patches on poor designs.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler addresses the common confusion around UML use case diagram relationships, specifically 'include' and 'extend'. He dismisses 'extend' as not worth learning, arguing it won't meaningfully impact projects. He endorses 'include' for extracting duplicated or logically distinct steps, but warns against overuse. His broader point is that use case diagrams themselves are nearly worthless—the real value lies in the textual descriptions they represent.
key_points: The 'extend' relationship in UML use cases should be ignored entirely as it provides no meaningful project value
The 'include' relationship is useful for extracting duplicated steps or logically coherent chunks between use cases
Use case diagrams are 'very close to useless'—the actual value is in the textual content describing the use cases
Alistair Cockburn's book is the definitive resource on use cases, superior to any brief advice
money_quotes: You won't find an explanation here, rather my advice is to ignore extend.
Just pretend it doesn't exist.
Using extend properly isn't going to make any significant difference to your project - you almost certainly have much more important issues to worry about.
Use case diagrams are very close to useless.
The real value of use cases lies in the content - the text that describes them.
The diagram makes a visual table of contents, but nothing more.
posts.key_insight: Don't get caught up in the theoretical nuances of UML use case relationships—the diagrams are just visual tables of contents, and the real value lies entirely in the textual descriptions.

posts.summary: The OMG has formally adopted UML 2, marking the most significant overhaul to the Unified Modeling Language since its inception. The update brings substantial changes including new sequence diagram notation, renamed diagram types, redesigned activity diagrams, and composite structure notation. Fowler has been tracking the standard's development closely and updating UML Distilled accordingly, with a new edition expected in late summer 2003.
key_points: UML 2 represents the biggest revision to UML since the standard was first established
Sequence diagrams gain Interaction Frame notation for better expressiveness
Activity diagrams are decoupled from state diagrams, gaining independent capabilities
Composite structure notation enables hierarchical class descriptions
Collaboration diagrams are renamed to communication diagrams
money_quotes: In practice this means that UML 2 is agreed on.
It represents the biggest overhaul to the UML since the UML was originally agreed on.
Activity diagrams are no longer a special case of state diagrams - as a result they get a host of new things.
posts.key_insight: UML 2 fundamentally restructures key diagram types, particularly freeing activity diagrams from their previous dependence on state machine semantics.

posts.summary: Fowler presents two practical approaches for substituting service stubs during testing while keeping real implementations for production. The first approach, from Jeremy Stell-Smith, uses an Abstract Factory with a stack-based implementation that simplifies test setup and teardown. The second approach, from Kraig Parkinson, uses a prototype pattern where individual services can be replaced directly. Both patterns solve the common problem of isolating code under test from external dependencies like persistence layers.
key_points: Abstract Factory pattern with a stack of implementations makes managing test stubs cleaner and easier to set up/tear down
Prototype pattern offers a lighter-weight alternative where individual facades can be swapped for testing
Both approaches use static getInstance() methods as the seam for substituting test doubles
The prototype approach with inline anonymous classes resembles Mock Objects' expectation-setting pattern
Cleanup code can go either in finally blocks within tests or in tearDown methods
money_quotes: A common problem with test-enhanced designs is how to create Service Stubs in test mode while letting the real thing be there for production (and for some tests).
As well as the Abstract Factory capability the test factory also has the nice ability to have a stack of implementations - this makes setup of factories easier.
You can think of this as a lightweight way of doing Mock Objects.
posts.key_insight: Strategic use of factories and prototypes as seams in your design enables clean substitution of service stubs for testing without polluting production code.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on an unusual conference pairing where enterprise architecture and security were presented together, noting that attendees found the combination strange. He argues this reaction reveals a fundamental problem: security is wrongly treated as a separate, siloed concern rather than something that should pervade entire development teams. The post emphasizes that security cannot be bolted on through encapsulated classes but must be integrated into how teams think and work. Fowler endorses David LeBlanc's approach of prioritizing developer education over security review gates.
key_points: The fact that architecture and security are seen as 'diverse topics' reveals a problematic siloed mentality in the industry
Security cannot be added to applications through encapsulated classes - it requires team-wide thinking
Many eyeballs don't lead to secure code; you need many educated eyeballs
Developer education is more effective than relying on security review checkpoints
Internet-facing and corporate intranet applications especially require security thinking to pervade the team
money_quotes: Security is seen as some separate topic area which sits in its silo.
Security isn't something you can just add to an application by putting in a few encapsulated classes here and there.
Security thinking should pervade a whole team.
Many eyeballs don't lead to secure code - you need many educated eyeballs.
Educating developers is a key part of the picture, with less emphasis on review steps with security groups.
posts.key_insight: Security must be treated as a cross-cutting concern that pervades team thinking, not a separate specialty that can be bolted on or delegated to review gates.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses whether developers should create specific typed collection classes (like EmployeeList) rather than using standard library collections. While typed collections offer type-safety benefits by ensuring only correct types are added and avoiding downcasts on retrieval, Fowler argues against them. He notes that encapsulated collections already protect type safety on updates, and the boilerplate code required for typed collections isn't worth the marginal benefit of avoiding downcasts on access.
key_points: Typed collections ensure only appropriate types are added and provide properly typed elements on retrieval
The type-safety argument is weakened when collections are properly encapsulated
The boilerplate code required for typed collections outweighs the benefits
Code generation can reduce the pain but still isn't worth the complexity
This advice assumes a pre-generics world (Java/C# before parameterized types)
money_quotes: If you're using a collection you should be making it an EncapsulatedCollection, so that protects the type safety on updates.
Handling the downcasting on access is a nice idea, but involves a lot of boilerplate code.
On the whole, however, it isn't worth the trouble.
You can reduce the pain of this with code generation - but my take is that it's more trouble than it's worth.
posts.key_insight: Encapsulation provides sufficient type safety for collections, making dedicated typed collection classes an unnecessary overhead in most cases.

posts.summary: Fowler briefly introduces AgileDox, a tool developed by colleague Chris Stevenson that automatically generates documentation from JUnit test cases. He acknowledges the concept sounds ridiculous at first glance but notes this is characteristic of 'Wardish' ideas - referring to Ward Cunningham's tendency toward surprisingly simple yet effective solutions.
key_points: AgileDox automatically generates documentation from JUnit test cases
The idea seems absurd on the surface but follows a pattern of deceptively simple innovations
The tool exemplifies 'Wardish' thinking - ideas that sound ridiculous but prove valuable
money_quotes: Sounds ridiculous, but then that's what Wardish ideas are like.
posts.key_insight: The best developer tools often emerge from ideas that initially seem absurd but embrace radical simplicity.

posts.summary: Fowler critiques the IEEE's Software Engineering Book of Knowledge (SWEBOK), which aims to define the profession's core knowledge and potentially serve as the basis for licensing exams. He argues that SWEBOK is dangerously biased toward plan-driven, waterfall methodologies and largely excludes agile approaches. The document treats design as separate from programming, emphasizes measurement-based control despite productivity being unmeasurable, and requires comprehensive requirements specifications upfront. Fowler agrees with Cem Kaner that the software industry is not mature enough to codify a definitive body of knowledge, as multiple competing schools of thought still exist.
key_points: SWEBOK could become the legal standard for malpractice lawsuits, making deviation from its practices legally risky even if better alternatives exist
The document reflects waterfall thinking: separate design phases, detailed upfront specifications, and measurement-based process control
SWEBOK ignores the role of people and human interaction in software process, focusing instead on Scientific Management principles
The Gang of Four design patterns book was notably relegated to secondary reading rather than recommended references
The software engineering field lacks sufficient consensus to produce a legitimate body of knowledge for licensing purposes
money_quotes: If the SWEBOK is the basis for the licensing exam, the practices in the SWEBOK will be treated as the basis for malpractice lawsuits.
People who adopt what might be much better practices, but practices that conflict with the SWEBOK, will risk harsh criticism in court.
The process section relied heavily on the notions of measurement based control, which is seriously flawed since I've observed that you CannotMeasureProductivity.
It's clearly strongly based on Scientific Management principles, and as such utterly ignores the role of people and human interaction issues in process.
Our industry is not sufficiently mature to produce a body of knowledge of this kind yet.
Business people don't consider government as a synonym for efficiency.
posts.key_insight: Codifying software engineering practices into a licensable body of knowledge is premature and dangerous when the field still lacks consensus, as it would legally enshrine one school of thought while penalizing potentially superior alternative approaches.

posts.summary: Fowler responds to a reader who complained about 'unnecessary history' in UML Distilled by defending the importance of historical context in understanding software and business practices. He argues that problematic situations exist for reasons rooted in the past, using UML's aggregation and composition concepts as an example of something that only makes sense through historical context. The post extends this principle beyond software to business practices and political life, advocating for historical investigation when encountering puzzling systems or rules.
key_points: Understanding why something is problematic today requires knowing how it got that way
UML's aggregation/composition distinction only makes sense through the history of debates that shaped it
Historical digging often provides enlightenment when trying to understand confusing software or business rules
The past pervades our work, business practices, and political life more than we realize
money_quotes: When you look at a problematic situation in the here and now, it got that way for a reason.
Often you cannot make sense of the now without understanding how it got there.
More than once I've felt lost trying to understand why a piece of software works the way it does and found that some historical digging led to a little enlightenment.
So when you come across a puzzling piece of software or business rules, consider looking into the history of it got that way.
The past has a way of giving understanding to the present.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the history behind puzzling software or business rules often provides the context necessary to make sense of seemingly irrational present-day decisions.

posts.summary: Enterprise-wide conceptual modeling groups typically pursue a single comprehensive model to enable integration, but this approach is fundamentally flawed because large models become either too complex or too abstract for people to understand. The shift from shared database integration to messaging-based integration removes the need for a single canonical model. Instead, organizations can use multiple overlapping canonical models that only cover what needs to be communicated between applications, built incrementally through harvesting rather than upfront planning. This approach simplifies both the technical and political challenges of enterprise integration.
key_points: Single enterprise models fail because largeness and abstractness both create comprehension difficulties - this is an essential, not accidental problem
Messaging-based integration enables multiple canonical models that can overlap without sharing the same structure, requiring only translation between overlapping parts
Canonical models can be harvested incrementally as applications communicate, replacing n×n translation paths with n paths to a canonical hub
Models only need to cover what's communicated between applications, not everything that can be represented
Data modelers resist this approach despite having valuable skills to offer, because they're attached to single enterprise-wide models as the 'proper' foundation
money_quotes: A single conceptual model is a tricky beast to work with.
Many times I've run into the complaint that while a model is really good - hardly anyone understands it.
Any large enterprise needs a model that is either very large, or abstract, or both. And largeness and abstractness both imply comprehension difficulties.
While it's not the best approach in theory, it better recognizes the practical problems of integration - especially the political problems.
Overlaps between models need not share the same structure, although there should be a translation between the parts of models that overlap.
The result breaks down the modeling problem, and I believe simplifies it both technically and politically.
posts.key_insight: Multiple overlapping canonical models, built incrementally and covering only what needs to be communicated, are more practical than a single comprehensive enterprise model because they avoid the inherent comprehension problems of large or abstract unified models.

posts.summary: This is a personal appreciation of the BBC television series 'The Singing Detective' from the 1980s, written by Dennis Potter. Fowler considers it the finest piece of television ever made, praising its dense multi-layered plot, exceptional dialogue, memorable acting performances, and innovative use of musical numbers. He particularly admires how Potter brings together four overlapping storylines in the final episode with surprising plot twists. The post serves as a recommendation for viewers who enjoy intellectually challenging, complex narratives.
key_points: The show's plot weaves four storylines across present, memory, and fantasy that all serve a psychological journey toward recovery
Potter's writing excels at both macro-level plot construction and micro-level scene details that suggest entire relationships in brief moments
Michael Gambon's performance is remarkable for making a dislikeable character sympathetic while physically constrained to moving only his eyes and mouth for two hours
Potter's approach to musical numbers is uniquely original - reveling in incongruity rather than avoiding it, using songs for both humor and dark commentary
The show rewards repeated viewings as layers of meaning and the craft of the final episode's closure become more apparent
money_quotes: Many people, including me, consider it the finest piece of television they've ever seen.
I often describe this show to Americans as one that appeals to people who found Twin Peaks too straightforward.
It's only after three or four viewings that I've really appreciated how well he does it.

Michael Gambon as Philip - who has to make his character both dislikeable and yet gain our compassion. If that challenge is not enough, he has to do this for the first two hours by only moving his eyes and mouth.
The schoolteacher who, if my memory of Jon Amiel's commentary serves, is the schoolteacher who begins where our nightmares end. She succeeds in being both captivating and frightening.
Potter's approach is to revel in the incongruity.
This is a program that is a world beyond anything that usually graces television screens.
posts.key_insight: Great television can achieve artistic complexity rivaling any medium when gifted writers, actors, and directors commit to challenging their audience rather than simplifying for accessibility.

posts.summary: Fowler responds to criticism that patterns books offer nothing new to experienced developers by arguing this misses the point entirely. Patterns are meant to capture existing field knowledge, not present original ideas. The real value of patterns books lies in their role as communication tools, helping experienced developers transfer knowledge to less experienced team members. Since few teams consist entirely of seasoned developers, patterns provide a shared vocabulary for mentorship and skill transfer.
key_points: Patterns deliberately capture existing knowledge rather than presenting original ideas
The criticism that patterns books teach nothing new to experts actually validates their purpose
Patterns serve as communication tools between experienced and less experienced developers
Knowledge transfer is one of the most important responsibilities of experienced team leaders
money_quotes: Not just is this true, but it's the whole point of patterns.
Patterns are there to capture knowledge from the field, not to present original ideas.
One of the most important things an experienced leader can do is pass on her skills.
Few teams consist of entirely of seasoned developers.
posts.key_insight: Patterns exist not to teach experts new concepts, but to give them a shared vocabulary for transferring their hard-won knowledge to others.

posts.summary: The post argues that the standard practice of mapping SQL varchar types directly to programming language strings is potentially flawed because it ignores the fixed-length constraint of database columns. Fowler proposes creating a fixed-length string type that knows its maximum length and can validate input accordingly. This type could throw exceptions, truncate, or execute custom behavior when given oversized input. The approach also enables better UI design since widgets can query the string's maximum length and constrain input accordingly. The post uses this specific example to illustrate broader principles about respecting data store constraints, creating simple value types, and avoiding validation logic duplication.
key_points: SQL varchars have fixed lengths while application strings typically don't, creating update risks
A fixed-length string type can carry its maximum length as instance data and validate assignments
UI widgets can interrogate these types for their size, enabling better input constraints
Insulating the data store is valuable but you must still respect its constraints
Developers are often reluctant to create simple value types that could make code easier
money_quotes: Simple, obvious, but perhaps it's wrong.
Insulating the data store is good, but you still have to live by the constraints it imposes on you.
People are often reluctant to create simple value types when they can often make things much easier.
Putting simple validations in the UI results in a nicer interface. The trick is to find ways of doing that without duplicating the validation logic.
Usually such information gets lost.
posts.key_insight: Domain types should encode not just values but also the constraints imposed by their persistence layer, preventing errors and enabling smarter UI design.

posts.summary: This post explains why UML uses the term 'multiplicity' instead of 'cardinality' when describing relationships between entities. While data modelers commonly use 'cardinality' to describe how many entities can be linked (like one-to-many), the UML designers chose 'multiplicity' because it's more technically accurate according to dictionary definitions. Cardinality properly refers to the actual number of elements in a set, whereas multiplicity describes the allowable range of values that cardinality can take. The distinction matters because UML aims for precise terminology in its formal specification.
key_points: Data modeling traditionally uses 'cardinality' to describe relationship constraints (one-to-many, etc.)
UML deliberately chose 'multiplicity' over the more common 'cardinality' term
The dictionary definition of cardinality refers to actual count, not allowable ranges
Multiplicity is formally defined as a lower and upper bound specification
money_quotes: UML avoids the term cardinality preferring to use multiplicity.
The reason for the change is that the dictionary definition of cardinality is 'the number of elements in a particular set or other grouping'.
According to this the data modeling usage is actually wrong.
Rumbaugh defines multiplicity as 'A specification of the range of allowable cardinality values - the size - that a set may assume'.
posts.key_insight: The data modeling community's use of 'cardinality' is technically incorrect—cardinality means actual count, while multiplicity correctly describes the allowable range of counts in a relationship.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that the common metaphor of 'software architect' fundamentally misunderstands the actual role of building architects. Building architects primarily handle client interaction and focus on layout and appearance, but are not responsible for structural integrity—that's the structural engineer's job. The metaphor suggests software architects are chief designers, but building architects are actually one professional among several who must collaborate effectively. Fowler warns that putting any single role in charge of the customer relationship requires effective cross-discipline communication, which often fails in practice.
key_points: Building architects focus on client interaction and aesthetics, not structural integrity—that responsibility belongs to structural engineers
The closest software equivalent to a building architect is actually a user-interface designer, not a technical architect
Architects who control the client relationship often resist changes from other disciplines even when those changes would significantly reduce costs
User-interface designers often fail to account for deeper technological concerns like concurrency and locking strategies
Building architects, despite winning the battle to control building design, are typically the lowest paid skilled workers on the job
money_quotes: A software architect is seen as a chief designer, someone who pulls together everything on the project. But this is not what a building architect does.
An architect is not responsible for the structural integrity of the building. That's the role of the structural engineer.
Given the focus on client interaction I think the closest equivalent in software development is a user-interface designer.
One of the problems I've seen with the world of otherwise excellent user-interface people is that they don't take into account the costs of different user-interface schemes.
Dave Rice likes to point out that an application's concurrency and locking strategies cannot be separated from the user-interface experience.
Software Architects: be careful what you wish for!
posts.key_insight: The building architect metaphor for software architects is doubly misleading: it both overstates the technical authority of building architects and understates the need for cross-discipline collaboration in any complex design work.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that direct developer-customer interaction in agile methods serves a purpose beyond just improving requirements communication: it increases developer enjoyment and motivation. While agile practices like daily collaboration and on-site customers are typically justified for reducing misunderstandings, Fowler contends that connecting developers to the people who benefit from their work is intrinsically motivating. When developers see their software valued and used, they produce better work. Relying on intermediaries or documents breaks this crucial motivational link.
key_points: Agile methods emphasize frequent, direct interaction between developers and customers to improve communication
The overlooked benefit of developer-customer interaction is increased developer enjoyment and motivation
Motivated teams translate directly to higher productivity and better business value
Developers find deep satisfaction in seeing their work used and valued by real customers
Intermediaries and documents break the motivational connection between creators and users
money_quotes: But I think these arguments, while valid, have missed another vital reason for direct developer-customer interaction - enjoyment.
A team that's enjoying their work is a team that's motivated to do good work.
In raw business terms, this translates to much higher productivity and better value for your development budget.
Every developer I know enjoys seeing his work used and valued.
There's nothing more satisfying than having a customer tell you how your software makes her job more enjoyable, or have a business tie software to revenue.
If all of you see of the customers is intermediaries, or worse still documents, then that motivational link is broken.
posts.key_insight: Direct developer-customer interaction isn't just about clearer requirements—it's about connecting creators to the human impact of their work, which is a powerful and often overlooked source of motivation.

posts.summary: Fowler distinguishes between use cases and user stories, two requirements organization techniques often confused as synonymous. While many in the XP community view stories as simplified use cases, Fowler argues they serve fundamentally different purposes: use cases create narratives about how users achieve goals through system interaction, while stories break requirements into estimable chunks for planning iterations. The granularity differs because stories must fit within short iterations, meaning one use case might spawn multiple stories. Teams can choose to use both, either alone, or hybrid approaches depending on their needs.
key_points: Use cases and stories organize requirements for different purposes: narrative understanding vs. planning and estimation
Stories are typically more fine-grained because they must be completable within a one-to-two week iteration
A single use case may correspond to one story, multiple stories, or stories may not map to use cases at all
Teams don't necessarily need both - they can use cases early then break into stories, go direct to stories, or annotate use cases for scheduling
money_quotes: Use cases organize requirements to form a narrative of how users relate to and use a system.
XP stories break requirements into chunks for planning purposes.
Because these uses of requirements are different, heuristics for good use cases and stories will differ.
A story may not even show up in a use case narrative, such as adding a new asset depreciation method to a pop up list.
As in many things, in theory you do but in practice you don't.
posts.key_insight: Use cases and user stories serve different purposes - narrative understanding versus iteration planning - so their appropriate use depends on what a team needs to accomplish, not on which is 'better.'

posts.summary: Fowler explores the design challenge of modeling currency in software systems. While currency typically works well as a simple value object using just the currency code, he describes a system that required mutable 'pip values' for UI nudge buttons. Since value objects should be immutable, the solution involved splitting currency into two classes: a value object for immutable data like the currency code, and a reference object for mutable data like pip values. This 'bi-semantic' pattern is rare, and most systems can use simple value objects for currency.
key_points: Currency typically works well as a simple value object containing just the currency code
Value objects should be immutable, which becomes problematic when some currency attributes need to change
The solution was splitting into two classes: a value object for immutable data and a reference object for mutable data
The value object delegated certain methods (like pip value) to the reference object held in a lookup table
This bi-semantic pattern is unusual and rarely needed in practice
money_quotes: For many systems currency works well as a value, the key part you need is the internationally recognized currency code.
This violates a very useful rule of value objects - that they are immutable.
The value object was passed around most of the time, but some methods in the value object, such as pip value, delegated to the reference object.
This bi-semantic behavior is unusual - it's the only time I've seen it.
posts.key_insight: When a concept needs both immutable identity and mutable attributes, split it into a value object and a reference object that work together.

posts.summary: Fowler traces the origins of the term 'refactoring' by interviewing key figures in the software community. The term derives from the mathematical concept of factoring expressions into simpler components. Bill Opdyke, who wrote the first thesis on refactoring, recalls coining related terminology during a walk with Ralph Johnson, riffing on 'Software Factory' to propose 'Software Refactory.' The concept emerged independently in both Smalltalk and Forth communities, with the earliest known print usage appearing in Leo Brodie's 1984 book Thinking Forth.
key_points: The term draws from mathematical factoring, where expressions are broken into logical components for easier manipulation
Most early practitioners couldn't recall where the term originated, suggesting organic community adoption
Bill Opdyke and Ralph Johnson coined 'Refactory' as a play on 'Software Factory,' emphasizing that software is design, not manufacturing
The concept developed independently in both Smalltalk and Forth programming communities
The first known printed use of 'refactoring' appears in Thinking Forth (1984)
money_quotes: You can take an expressions such as x^2 + 5x + 6 and factor it into (x+2)(x+3). By factoring it you can make a number of mathematical operations much easier.
When I asked around the creators of refactoring, the common answer was that they had no idea. The term had been around for a while and they don't know where it came from.
They surmised that since software development was more like design than like manufacturing, it would be better to call it a Software Refactory.
We're pretty sure that this usage didn't pass from the Forth community to the Smalltalk community, but developed independently.
posts.key_insight: The term 'refactoring' emerged organically from the insight that software development resembles design rather than manufacturing, with the concept independently arising in multiple programming communities.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on the evolution of reuse in object-oriented programming, from individual classes to frameworks, and introduces the concept of 'seedwork' as an alternative. While frameworks provide part-built applications you extend in controlled ways, seedworks offer minimal functionality you copy and modify freely. Though this copy-paste approach contradicts conventional wisdom about reuse, Fowler argues seedworks may be more practical because good frameworks are extremely hard to create. He concludes that while reuse within an application is vital, reuse across applications faces fundamental social and technical barriers that make imperfect alternatives worth considering.
key_points: The promise of OO reuse evolved from classes to frameworks, with mixed results especially on the business side
Seedworks offer minimal functionality you copy and modify freely, trading upgradeability for simplicity and ownership
Good frameworks are very hard to create; seedworks are 'not as useful but easier to create and use'
Shared library versioning remains an unsolved problem (DLL-hell, dependency conflicts)
Application boundaries are primarily social constructions, making cross-application reuse fundamentally harder than within-application reuse
money_quotes: A framework is supposed to be a part-baked application that you extend in controlled ways to provide what you need. A seedwork is some minimal functionality that you modify however you like to get what you need.
Seedworks are not as useful as a good framework, but are easier to create and use. The point is not whether they are ideal, but just whether they are useful.

I've found that reuse (or avoiding duplication) within an application is vital. But reuse across applications is much tougher, primarily because an ApplicationBoundary is primarily a social construction.
This is the kind of copy and paste reuse that many people, including me, deride. Maybe I shouldn't be so scornful.
We still haven't really figured out how to deal with shared libraries that upgrade on different schedules.
posts.key_insight: The pragmatic value of reuse depends on context—perfect reuse across application boundaries faces social barriers that make simpler, 'imperfect' approaches like seedworks worth considering.

posts.summary: Fowler critiques the Model Driven Architecture (MDA) claim of 'platform independence' as fundamentally misleading. He points out that Java already provides platform independence from hardware and operating systems, so MDA's claim to be platform-independent while generating Java code is contradictory. MDA merely redefines 'platform' to mean the programming environment, but OMG standards (UML, MOF, XMI) are themselves just another platform. The argument is further weakened by the fact that MDA's Platform Independent Model lacks basic capabilities like I/O libraries, making it less useful than the platforms it claims to abstract away.
key_points: MDA's definition of 'platform' (programming environment) differs from the conventional meaning (hardware/OS), creating a misleading claim
Java already provides hardware and OS platform independence, making MDA's 'platform independence' redundant
OMG standards that comprise MDA's PIM are themselves a platform, so no additional independence is gained
The PIM platform is actually less capable than Java or .NET since it lacks basic functionality like I/O libraries
MDA may have other benefits, but platform independence is not a valid selling point
money_quotes: Hang on a moment, isn't the whole point of Java to be platform independent? So why would I want some platform independent technology that generates another platform independent technology?
When MDA talks about platform independence, it's treating your programming environment as the platform. But this is complete hogwash.
All you are doing is swapping one (hardware/OS) platform independent programming environment for another. You aren't getting any more independence.
How exactly do you do that in the standard OMG PIM platform? Well you can't, because there are no I/O libraries defined in the OMG PIM standards.
The platform independent argument has no foundation.
posts.key_insight: MDA's claim of platform independence is semantically hollow because it merely substitutes one platform abstraction for another while providing less capability than the technologies it generates.

posts.summary: Fowler clarifies the distinction between dependency and association in UML modeling. Association represents a persistent link like a field in a class—the relationship is always there. Dependency is broader and vaguer: it exists when changes to one element may cause changes to another, encompassing things like parameter types and temporary variables. Since dependencies are ubiquitous, diagrams should only show the important ones. Associations inherently imply dependency, so drawing both would be redundant. UML 2 resolved meta-model problems from UML 1 that had caused confusion with transient links and stereotypes like 'parameter'.
key_points: Association represents persistent relationships like fields—you can always ask an order for its customer
Dependency is a vague, general relationship where changes to one element may affect another
Be selective about which dependencies to show on diagrams—there are far too many to display all of them
Associations already imply dependency, so showing both would be redundant
UML 2 fixed meta-model issues from UML 1 that had caused confusion around transient relationships
money_quotes: A dependency exists between two elements if changes to the definition of one element (the supplier) may cause changes to the other (the client).
You don't want to show every dependency on a UML diagram - there are far too many.
You need to be very selective and show only those that are important to whatever it is you are communicating.
Associations also imply dependency, if there is an association between two classes, there is also a dependency.
The difference between a permanent slot and a relationship that's only present within the context of a method is rather important.
posts.key_insight: Association represents permanent structural relationships while dependency captures any potential for change impact—and since associations already imply dependency, good UML diagrams show only the dependencies that matter for communication.

posts.summary: Fowler explains his decision to abandon XSLT in favor of Ruby for XML-to-HTML transformations on his website. After years of proficiency with XSLT, he found that Ruby's clean syntax, proper object-oriented features, and excellent XML library made transformation tasks significantly easier. He demonstrates his approach using Ruby's reflection capabilities to mimic XSLT's template-matching behavior while gaining the full power of a general-purpose programming language. The post questions whether XSLT's specialized approach is worth its syntactic verbosity and functional limitations.
key_points: XML's verbosity makes it a poor syntax for programming languages - 'you can't see the program' through the noise
XSLT's painful subroutine calling actively discourages good practices and encourages code duplication
Ruby's reflection enables XSLT-like template matching while providing a clean, flexible programming environment
XSLT handles simple transformations well but becomes baroque or impossible for complex tasks
General-purpose scripting languages may be better suited for XML transformation than specialized tools like XSLT
money_quotes: XML makes a lousy syntax for a programming language. There's way too much noise in there and as a result you can't see the program.
XSLT makes calling subroutines so painful that you are seriously discouraged from using them, which encourages duplicate code.
XSLT handles simple tasks well, but is baroque when it comes to more complicated things.
There's still much I like about the power of XSLT, but I hate the syntax and the walls you keep running into.
The bigger question is whether you're better off with scripting language for this kind of task than XSLT.
posts.key_insight: A general-purpose scripting language with good XML libraries often beats a specialized transformation language because clean syntax and proper abstraction mechanisms matter more than domain-specific features.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes his journey from frustration with HTML and proprietary formats to adopting XML with XSLT transformations for all his writing. He started with a refactoring catalog around 2000, then expanded to his entire website and eventually wrote his P of EAA book entirely in XML. The approach lets him write with logical markup while generating both HTML and print output from a single source. He details his toolchain including text editors, XSLT stylesheets, Ant build scripts, and CVS for version control, emphasizing how plain text formats enable powerful automation. Fowler concludes that XML is excellent for technical writers comfortable with scripting, though non-programmers should wait for better XML-aware word processors.
key_points: Separating content from presentation through logical markup enables single-source publishing to both web and print formats
Plain text formats like XML unlock powerful tooling: version control, scripting, automated builds, and editor flexibility
XSLT is painful to write but powerful once mastered, working best when matching patterns rather than driving output
Automatic code import from tagged source files eliminates the nightmare of keeping sample code synchronized with documentation
CVS version control is essential for both collaboration and personal writing safety, treating text like code
money_quotes: I've always been a fan of logical styles when writing. As soon as I start doing anything non-trivial in a word processor I like to set up logical styles that match the way the content of my writing is structured.
The DRY principle is a good thing!
One of the strengths of XML as a format is that you can use whatever text editor you like to type things in with.
XSLT is a pain of a language - a wonderful reason why you shouldn't use anything XML based as a programming language.
I really like the separation that XML/XSLT gives me. I find it much easier to write with a simple set of logical tags.
Most authors have nightmares on sample code, almost always something goes wrong and they have sample code that isn't consistent or doesn't compile.
Just as programmers should always work with version control, so should authors!
posts.key_insight: Treating writing like code—with plain text formats, version control, build scripts, and automated transformations—gives technical authors the same power and reliability that programmers enjoy with source code.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler highlights the often-overlooked 15 principles of Extreme Programming, which sit between XP's well-known values and practices. Kent Beck presented these principles at JAOO, reminding attendees they were 'cunningly hidden' in the White Book's chapter on Basic Principles. The principles serve as a bridge between abstract values and concrete practices, helping teams choose between alternatives and guide local adaptation. Fowler argues these principles are essential for understanding XP's essence and for making appropriate process modifications that stay true to XP's spirit.
key_points: XP has 15 principles divided into 5 fundamental (Rapid Feedback, Assume Simplicity, Incremental Change, Embracing Change, Quality Work) and 10 further principles
Principles bridge the gap between vague values and potentially dogmatic practices
The principles weren't widely discussed because they were primarily prepared for the White Book rather than refined through community wiki discussions
Local adaptation—altering the process to fit conditions—is one of XP's biggest challenges, and the principles provide guidelines for which adaptations work
Principles help distinguish adaptations that align with XP from those that 'go against the XP grain'
money_quotes: âYesâ, he replied, âcunningly hidden in a chapter called 'Basic Principles'â
Principles were a step between the universality (and vagueness) of values and the concreteness (and dogmatism) of practices
A value may be vague. One person's simple is another person's complex. A principle is more concrete. Either you have rapid feedback or you don't
One of the biggest issues with XP, and indeed with any agile method, is how to do the essential local adaptation where you alter the process to fit the local conditions
They are part of the essence of XP that every skilled XPer both knows, and finds difficult to communicate
posts.key_insight: XP's 15 principles serve as the crucial middle layer that helps teams adapt practices to local conditions while staying true to the methodology's core values.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the question of how to represent local variables on UML class diagrams, concluding that you simply shouldn't do it. He notes that in his experience, showing local variables on class diagrams rarely communicates anything useful. If absolutely necessary, he recommends using a dependency with a keyword rather than a stereotyped association. The distinction between method-scoped relationships and class-lifetime relationships is important enough that associations should be reserved for the latter.
key_points: Local variables don't belong on UML class diagrams - there's rarely a need to show them
If forced to show local variable relationships, use a dependency with a keyword, not a stereotyped association
UML 1.3 used stereotyped associations due to meta-model limitations, but UML 2 fixed this
Method-scoped relationships are fundamentally different from class-lifetime relationships
Associations should be reserved for relationships that persist for the life of a class
money_quotes: The simple answer to this is that you don't.
Where I've seen it done, it hasn't communicated much that's useful.
My recommendation is to use a dependency with a «keyword» and take a long cold shower afterward.

I think there is an important difference between relationships that are only there within the span of a method invocation, and relationships that hold for the whole life of a class - and the latter relationships are far more important.
posts.key_insight: Class diagrams should focus on persistent structural relationships, not transient method-scoped ones, because the lifetime of a relationship determines its architectural significance.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses criticism that his book lacks enterprise architecture content by distinguishing between enterprise application architecture (designing individual applications) and enterprise architecture (organizing multiple applications). He explains why enterprise architecture initiatives typically fail: they're driven by IT management but must work with applications controlled by business sponsors who prioritize their own delivery timelines. The political reality is that application teams with business backing will always win against central architecture groups. Success requires submitting to these political forces by focusing on incremental business value, minimizing application team costs, and favoring loosely-coupled integration approaches like messaging.
key_points: Enterprise architecture initiatives follow a predictable boom-bust cycle: grand launch, declining influence, quiet death, then repeat
Central architecture groups fail because they lack the political power of application teams backed by business sponsors
The business sponsor holds the 'ace card' - they'll back application teams who resist architecture mandates that delay delivery
Success requires focusing on integration techniques rather than overarching plans, with minimal impact on application teams
Good application architecture (especially presentation-domain separation) makes enterprise integration much easier
money_quotes: Two or three years later, not much has been done and the enterprise architecture group isn't getting their phone calls returned.
The problem for central architecture groups is that they are driven by IT management, but the applications they are looking to organize are driven by business needs.
Since the application is directly connected to providing business value, and the central architectural team isn't, the application team wins.
These initiatives should be less about building an overarching plan for applications, and more about coming up with techniques to integrate applications in whatever way they are put together.
Application boundaries are primarily social constructs and they aren't likely to conform to anyone's forward plans.
I think you also need to focus on approaches that minimize coupling between applications, even if such approaches are less efficient than a more tightly coupled approach might be.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise architecture initiatives fail not due to technical shortcomings but because they lack the political power of application teams who have business sponsors backing their priorities.
