posts.summary: Martin Fowler advocates for strict separation between presentation (UI) and domain logic as a fundamental design principle. He outlines five key benefits: improved understandability, code reuse across multiple presentations, better testability, easier API creation, and recognition that these areas require different skills. He notes this principle is frequently violated due to ignorance and frameworks that make intermixing too easy. Fowler emphasizes this is a logical separation, not a physical client/server one, and extends the concept to include web services and APIs as forms of presentation.
key_points: Separation makes both presentation and domain logic easier to understand independently
The same domain code can support multiple presentations without duplication, including programmatic APIs and services
UI code is hard to test, so pushing logic into the domain layer improves testability
Many frameworks inadvertently encourage mixing domain logic into presentation code
This is a logical separation principle, not a physical deployment concern
money_quotes: One of the most useful design principles that I've found and followed is that of keeping a good separation between the presentation aspects of a program (the user interface) and the rest of the functionality.
User interfaces are hard to test, separation keeps more logic in more testable places.
Don't make the mistake that this is a client/server physical separation. Even if all your code is running on the same machine, it's well worth making this logical separation.
Remember that such things as web services are also presentations, even though they are used by computer users rather than human users.

I often see this principle violated. I think this is partly due to lack of knowledge, and partly due to the fact that many frameworks make it much too easy to intermix domain logic into the presentation.
posts.key_insight: Presentation-domain separation is a logical design principle that improves testability, reusability, and comprehension—and web services are presentations too.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler discusses the notable absence of refactoring tools for C and C++ despite their existence for Smalltalk, Java, and C#. He explains that the complexity of C++, particularly the pre-processor, has hindered tool development. Research at UIUC led by Ralph Johnson and Alejandra Garrido is tackling C refactoring first, as it shares C++'s challenges while avoiding some complexity. The core technical problem is that accurate refactoring requires operating on abstract syntax trees, but macros and conditional compilation create distance between source text and the AST. The C Refactory project has made progress, including successfully parsing the Linux kernel source code.
key_points: C++ lacks refactoring tools despite being the language of the first refactoring thesis by Bill Opdyke
The C pre-processor, particularly conditional compilation and macros, poses the biggest challenge for building refactoring tools
Accurate refactoring requires operating on abstract syntax trees, but macros create a gap between program text and the AST
The C Refactory project builds a 'macro-aware AST' that holds variants of code that would be compiled under different conditions
The research tool has been tested against the Linux kernel source code
money_quotes: One language conspicuous by its absence is C++, despite appeals.
Such difficulties don't stop the determined for long.
The essential problem is that accurate refactoring operates on the abstract syntax tree (AST) of the program, but macros distance the program text from the AST.
As a result a C refactoring tool needs to build a macro-aware AST which holds within it the variants of the AST that would be compiled.
It's hairy work but Alejandra has had some success, including reading in source to the Linux kernel as part of the testing for the research tool.
posts.key_insight: Building refactoring tools for C requires solving the fundamental problem that macros and conditional compilation create distance between source code text and the abstract syntax tree that refactoring must operate on.

posts.summary: Fowler identifies three distinct modes of using UML: as sketch, as blueprint, and as programming language. He argues that disagreements about what UML should contain stem from these different fundamental perspectives on its purpose. UML 2's changes toward greater precision serve blueprint and programming language users but burden sketchers with unnecessary complexity. Fowler predicts the UML will diverge into a formal standard and informal conventional usage, with the informal variants potentially fragmenting further as they drift from the standard.
key_points: People disagree about UML because they have fundamentally different views of its purpose - sketch, blueprint, or programming language
Your perception of UML is shaped by which mode you were first introduced to
UML 2's precision-focused changes serve formal users but make it harder for sketchers
The informal 'sketch' usage of UML is already diverging from the standard in non-UML books
This divergence risks fragmenting informal UML usage as it drifts further from the standard
money_quotes: People differ about what should be in the UML because there are differing fundamental views about what the UML should be.
When someone else's view of the UML seems rather different to yours, it may be because they use a different mode to you.
Your perception of the UML may be affected by the mode in which it was introduced to you.
These changes increase the bulk of the UML - and thus make it harder for those who prefer UmlAsSketch.
The UML may well diverge into similar but different things: the standard UML and a conventional, informal usage for the sketchers.
posts.key_insight: Tools and notations mean different things to different users based on how they use them, and adding precision for one group's needs can burden others.

posts.summary: Fowler observes that UML 2's development was driven by MDA and programming language use cases, leaving sketchers—the majority of practitioners—dissatisfied. Surprisingly, even the MDA community itself shows disdain for UML, preferring simplified subsets. This creates an identity crisis for UML: sketchers find the standards increasingly irrelevant, while MDA tools fragment into proprietary dialects. Fowler questions whether UML can serve as a meaningful interchange mechanism when no community truly embraces the full standard.
key_points: UML 2 was designed for MDA and formal modeling, ignoring the needs of sketchers who use UML informally
Sketchers bear some responsibility for being ignored since they don't participate in UML committees
Even MDA proponents don't embrace full UML, preferring simplified or extended subsets
The fragmentation into tool-specific UML dialects threatens interoperability and portability
MOF may replace UML as the interchange mechanism, but practical portability remains uncertain
money_quotes: Most people I talk to are interested in UmlAsSketch and this group isn't very impressed with UML 2.
Sketchers were pretty much ignored. This was largely their own fault as sketchers aren't interested enough in the UML to take an active role in the UML committees.
Disdain for UML is pretty rampant amongst the UmlAsProgrammingLanguage community too.
The pro-MDA speakers based their assumptions on the fact that they would be using a simplified subset of UML, and emphasized that you should not judge MDA on the UML.
I hear more mutterings from sketchers about the growing irrelevance of UML standards.
Will users of MDA tools get portability in practice, or will each tool turn into its own proprietary language?
posts.key_insight: When a standard tries to serve formal tooling needs while ignoring informal practitioners, it risks becoming a language that nobody actually wants to use as designed.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the common misconception that iterative development is a new or untried approach. He notes that most clients he encounters are unfamiliar with iterative development or view it skeptically as unproven. In reality, iterative development has existed for a long time under various names. He points readers to a Craig Larman and Vic Basili article in IEEE Software that documents this history and demonstrates a long track record of successful iterative projects.
key_points: Most clients either haven't heard of iterative development or consider it new and untested
Iterative development has actually been around for a long time under various names
There is documented history of successful projects using iterative approaches
The Larman and Basili IEEE Software article provides evidence of this long history
money_quotes: Most of the clients I run into have not heard of iterative development, or consider it to be a new and relatively untried phenomenon.
In contrast iterative development has been around for a long time under various names.
posts.key_insight: Iterative development is not a new or unproven methodology but rather has a long documented history of success that most practitioners simply aren't aware of.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes the Anemic Domain Model anti-pattern, where domain objects contain data but no behavior, with all logic pushed into service classes. He argues this approach fundamentally violates object-oriented principles by separating data from process. The pattern incurs the costs of domain modeling (like O/R mapping complexity) without delivering the benefits of organizing complex logic through OO techniques. Fowler clarifies that while service layers are appropriate, they should coordinate thin orchestration tasks while the domain layer contains the actual business logic. He suspects the pattern's popularity stems from developers unfamiliar with proper domain models and technologies like J2EE Entity Beans that encouraged the separation.
key_points: Anemic domain models look like real domain models with proper nouns and relationships, but lack behavior—they're just bags of getters and setters with logic moved to services
The pattern violates core OO principles of combining data and process, essentially becoming procedural programming disguised as objects
You pay the full cost of domain modeling (O/R mapping complexity) while getting none of the benefits for organizing complex logic
Service layers are valid but should be thin coordination layers, not repositories for all business logic
Technologies like J2EE Entity Beans and data-centric backgrounds contribute to this anti-pattern's prevalence
money_quotes: The fundamental horror of this anti-pattern is that it's so contrary to the basic idea of object-oriented design; which is to combine data and process together.
What's worse, many people think that anemic objects are real objects, and thus completely miss the point of what object-oriented design is all about.
In essence the problem with anemic domain models is that they incur all of the costs of a domain model, without yielding any of the benefits.
The anemic domain model is really just a procedural style design, exactly the kind of thing that object bigots like me (and Eric) have been fighting since our early days in Smalltalk.
In general, the more behavior you find in the services, the more likely you are to be robbing yourself of the benefits of a domain model.
If all your logic is in services, you've robbed yourself blind.
Now, the more common mistake is to give up too easily on fitting the behavior into an appropriate object, gradually slipping toward procedural programming.
posts.key_insight: Domain objects without behavior are a costly illusion—you get all the complexity of domain modeling with none of its power to organize business logic.

posts.summary: When building services for a service-oriented architecture, developers should also create a service stub that clients can use for testing. This stub should provide canned responses, simulate error conditions, and run on client machines. The stub must accurately mimic the real service's behavior. Providing such a stub makes it easier for clients to integrate with your service, increasing the likelihood of adoption.
key_points: Build a service stub alongside every service you create
Stubs should provide canned responses to fixed requests and simulate error conditions
Stubs must be runnable on the client's machine
Ensuring stub behavior matches real service behavior is essential
Making integration easier increases service adoption
money_quotes: When you build your service, also build a service stub that your clients can use to test against.
By providing a stub for your clients, you make it much easier for your clients to use your service; which of course means that your service is more likely to be used.
posts.key_insight: Providing a testing stub alongside your service is not just a convenience for clients—it's a strategic decision that increases your service's adoption by lowering the integration barrier.

posts.summary: A project timeline created during retrospectives helps teams visualize events and their impact on a project. Tim Mackinnon demonstrated an effective sticky note method at XPday where team members place color-coded stickies on a wall timeline: green for good events, orange for problematic ones, and yellow for neutral. A second pass adds emotional context with pink stickies for happy feelings and blue for frustration. The completed timeline then drives discussion about lessons learned and future improvements.
key_points: Timelines should show both events and their effects on the project
Color-coding separates event types (good/problematic/neutral) from emotional responses (happy/frustrated)
The two-pass approach captures both objective events and subjective team feelings
Timelines serve as discussion triggers for mining lessons learned
Mismatches between event types and feelings (like good events with frustrated feelings) reveal hidden insights
money_quotes: A timeline should show the various events that occurred during the project, and how they affected the project.
It's particularly interesting to look at the timelines for such things as good events with lots of blue feelings - a surprisingly common case.
posts.key_insight: Effective retrospective timelines capture not just what happened, but how the team felt about it—and the disconnects between the two often reveal the most valuable insights.

posts.summary: Fowler questions whether general-purpose programming languages are the right tool for writing acceptance tests. He notes that commercial UI testing tools use proprietary languages, while practitioners like Brett Pettichord advocate for scripting languages like Ruby or Python. However, Fowler suggests that since tests operate by example with specific scenarios rather than handling arbitrary values, they may require a fundamentally different kind of language. He points to FIT as a potentially groundbreaking approach to this problem.
key_points: Commercial UI testing tools use proprietary languages, which some practitioners question
Tests operate by example, describing specific scenarios rather than handling any possible value
Programming languages designed for general computation may not be ideal for test specification
FIT may represent a significant innovation in test language design
money_quotes: The point about tests is that they operate by example.
They don't try to cover how to handle any value, instead they describe specific scenarios and responses.
I wonder if this implies a different kind of programming language is required.
Perhaps this is the truly startling innovation in FIT.
posts.key_insight: Tests operate fundamentally differently from production code—by example rather than abstraction—which may require a different kind of language altogether.

posts.summary: This brief post explains the origin of the term 'POJO' (Plain Old Java Object), which Fowler coined with Rebecca Parsons and Josh MacKenzie in 2000. The term emerged during preparation for a conference talk advocating for encoding business logic in regular Java objects instead of Entity Beans. They observed that developers seemed reluctant to use simple objects because they lacked a prestigious-sounding name, so they invented one. The acronym subsequently gained widespread adoption in the Java community.
key_points: POJO stands for Plain Old Java Object
The term was coined in September 2000 by Fowler, Rebecca Parsons, and Josh MacKenzie
It emerged from advocacy for using regular objects over Entity Beans for business logic
The authors theorized that simple objects needed a 'fancy name' to gain acceptance
The term successfully caught on in the industry
money_quotes: We wondered why people were so against using regular objects in their systems and concluded that it was because simple objects lacked a fancy name.
So we gave them one, and it's caught on very nicely.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the adoption of good technical practices is hindered not by their merit but by their lack of impressive-sounding terminology.

posts.summary: Fowler explores the idea of carrying your computing environment on portable media rather than lugging a laptop everywhere. Inspired by Knoppix, a bootable Linux distribution, he envisions burning your OS, applications, and configuration to a CD while keeping data on a USB drive. This would let you walk up to any available computer and have your full working environment. While acknowledging laptops are still needed for planes and other computerless situations, he suggests combining this approach with ultra-portable devices like PDAs for a more flexible computing setup.
key_points: The real portability problem isn't data (networks and USB handle that) but applications and their configurations
Knoppix enables carrying a complete customized OS and application environment on a CD or large USB drive
Setting up a new computer is mostly about 'training it' with your applications and preferences
Ultra-portable devices like PDAs could handle light tasks while bootable media handles serious work on available hardware
A functional Knoppix customization fits in around 500MB, within range of period storage media
money_quotes: The problem isn't your data, networks can schlep that around, and USB memory devices are getting big enough to easily hold a working set.
Any time you get a new computer there's always time to spent training it, meaning installing all the apps you need and setting up the configuration.
In many cases the advantage of the laptop over a random desktop is the presence of my application environment.
Burn the OS, all the apps you need, and your static configuration stuff onto a CD.
Then when you get to your hotel, walk up to the in-room PC, insert your CD and your USB fob for your data, and you have all you need.
posts.key_insight: The true value of a personal computer isn't the hardware but the configured application environment, which could theoretically be made portable on removable media.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explores how to represent derived information in UML diagrams, using the example of a rectangle's area (derived from height and width). He contrasts showing derived values as operations versus using UML's derived property notation (prefixed with '/'). Fowler argues that from an encapsulation perspective, users of a class shouldn't know or care which properties are stored versus calculated. He suggests marking properties as derived indicates a constraint relationship, not implementation details. Ultimately, he emphasizes team consistency matters more than which convention you choose, noting UML is less rigorous than it claims to be.
key_points: UML provides a derived property notation (prefix with '/') for values calculated from other attributes
Some teams only use attributes for fields while others use derived markers based on naming conventions
Encapsulation principle suggests users shouldn't know whether a property is stored or calculated
Derived markers indicate constraints between values, not implementation specifics
Team consistency in notation matters more than which approach you choose
money_quotes: One of the key properties of an object is that they are encapsulated.
If I'm the user of the rectangle class I actually should not know or care that the circumference is calculated.
The implementor might store the height and circumference and calculate the width - I shouldn't be able to tell.
It matters less which approach you take than it does that you follow a consistent style within your team.
It's one of the many cases where the UML is less rigorous than it would like to think it is.
posts.key_insight: Encapsulation means clients shouldn't know whether information is stored or derived, so UML's derived notation should indicate logical constraints rather than implementation choices.

posts.summary: Fowler celebrates the DVD release of Sergio Leone's 'Once Upon a Time in the West,' arguing it's a masterpiece of slow, atmospheric filmmaking. He describes how the film's deliberate pacing, minimal dialogue, and visual storytelling create a hypnotic quality that rewards patient viewers. The opening ten-minute scene of gunmen waiting at a train station exemplifies the film's approach—drama through stillness rather than action. Fowler compares it favorably to Leone's other works, including 'The Good, the Bad and the Ugly,' noting how this film stretches a simple western plot into something operatic through its measured revelations and Morricone's pre-composed score.
key_points: The film's quality is evident in its opening: ten minutes of minimal dialogue and action that establishes its atmospheric, slow-paced approach
Leone tells stories visually through characteristic ultra close-ups rather than exposition, requiring viewers to read emotions in underplayed expressions
Morricone's score was composed before filming, creating an unusually tight fusion of music and visuals
The simple, predictable western plot is elevated by how Leone 'drips the story out drop by drop'
Despite its deliberate pace, the film maintains suspense and isn't difficult to follow
money_quotes: In that scene lies the slow pace of the whole film... But also in that scene you see the atmosphere and beauty of that film, which makes it one of my all time favorites.
The real dialog isn't spoken, it's seen in Leone's characteristic ultra close-ups.
People don't explain what they are thinking, you have to see it through their expressions, which are not exaggerated as in traditional drama, but underplayed.
I like a comment I've heard about this film—an opera where the arias aren't sung, they're stared.
The music and visuals are fused together with a memorable score that may not be Wagnerian in sound, but is certainly Wagnerian in its narrative intent.
It has a slow, hypnotic quality and I think there's a lot really going on.
posts.key_insight: Great storytelling can emerge from restraint and visual understatement, where what remains unspoken and slowly revealed creates deeper engagement than conventional dramatic techniques.

posts.summary: Fowler explores why object-oriented programming and iterative/incremental development have been historically linked, despite no inherent technical dependency between them. He argues that OO's fine-grained modularity through classes and polymorphism makes code changes easier, which is essential for IID's approach of growing design alongside code. The Smalltalk community's influence was disproportionately large, bringing both OO concepts and IID-friendly tooling. Social factors also played a role, as OO leaders championed iteration from the start, creating a community where both were assumed defaults.
key_points: OO's fine-grained modularity through encapsulation and polymorphism makes code easier to change, which is essential for iterative development
Smalltalk contributed far more than just OO—its IDE, immediate compilation, and environment features all supported iteration
The Unix and Lisp communities demonstrate that modularity and iteration can exist independently of OO
Social factors matter: OO leaders championed iteration, which shaped the entire community's assumptions
The link between OO and IID is one of affinity, not necessity
money_quotes: The whole point of IID is that you don't have a comprehensive design at the beginning, you grow the design and the code together.
Even though the number of Smalltalkers are (and were) small, they were disproportionately loud.
The leaders of the OO world pushed IID right from the beginning, which is why it's no surprise that the leaders of the agile movement almost entirely are OO people.
When the leadership embraces both movements so fervently that has an effect on the whole community.
Objects provide a very fine grained modularity, where the program is divided into small substitutable modules with separation of interface and implementation.
posts.key_insight: Objects and iteration share a strong affinity because OO's modularity enables the evolutionary design that IID requires, and because the same community leaders championed both approaches from the start.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler addresses the widespread misuse of the term 'refactoring' in the software industry. While pleased that the concept has gained popularity, he's concerned that people use it incorrectly to describe any restructuring activity. He clarifies that refactoring is a specific technique involving small, behavior-preserving transformations where the system is never broken for more than a few minutes. General restructuring is valid but distinct from refactoring, and maintaining this precision in terminology matters for clear communication.
key_points: Refactoring has become a commonly misused term in the software industry, often applied to any restructuring activity
True refactoring uses small behavior-preserving transformations and never leaves the system broken for extended periods
Restructuring is the general activity; refactoring is a specific technique within that broader category
Refactoring requires well-defined behavior to preserve, making it inapplicable to documents or other non-executable artifacts
Precision in terminology enables clearer communication about different approaches to changing code
money_quotes: If somebody talks about a system being broken for a couple of days while they are refactoring, you can be pretty sure they are not refactoring.
Refactoring is a very specific technique, founded on using small behavior-preserving transformations.
If you are doing refactoring your system should not be broken for more than a few minutes at a time.
I don't see how you do it on something that doesn't have a well defined behavior.
There may be other good techniques for restructuring, but they are different.
posts.key_insight: Refactoring is not a synonym for restructuring—it specifically means making small, behavior-preserving changes that never break the system for more than minutes at a time.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that XML is overused and should be applied judiciously based on context. He categorizes XML's suitability into three tiers: excellent for textual markup like writing books, tolerable for describing hierarchic data despite its verbosity, and terrible as a programming language. He points to XSLT as the prime example of XML's failure as a programming construct, noting that the noise and complexity become intolerable when control logic enters the picture.
key_points: XML is overused beyond its appropriate applications
XML excels at textual markup - Fowler uses it for writing books and blog entries
For hierarchic data, XML is 'good enough' and benefits from tool standardization despite verbosity
XML fails completely as a programming language due to excessive syntactic noise
XSLT exemplifies XML's unsuitability for programming - even subroutine calls become painful
money_quotes: XML has been around for a while now, and it's used a lot - indeed a lot more than it should be.
Like most tools XML is good for some things and not for others.
Having a standard for tools to cluster around outweighs the negatives.
Once you start putting structures like control logic the noise of XML becomes intolerable.
No language can be good that makes a subroutine call so painful.
posts.key_insight: XML should be matched to its strengths - markup and data description - rather than stretched into programming constructs where its verbosity becomes a crippling liability.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses a fundamental misunderstanding about agile methods: they are people-oriented rather than process-oriented. He emphasizes that agile practitioners view process as a second-order effect on project success, with individuals and interactions taking precedence. The post argues that evaluating agile processes by success/failure metrics misses the point entirely. Instead, the proper measure is whether teams find the process helpful. Fowler concludes with a crucial distinction: in the agile view, teams succeed or fail—not processes.
key_points: Process is a second-order effect on project success in agile thinking
Process-oriented people fundamentally misunderstand agile when they evaluate it through traditional metrics
Agile processes aren't designed to make teams succeed; they're designed to help teams succeed
The correct evaluation criteria is whether teams find a process helpful, not success/failure counts
Success and failure belong to teams, not to processes
money_quotes: One of the most difficult things for many people to understand about agile methods is the people orientation of agile.
Those who are interested in agile processes all agree that process is a second-order effect on project success.
Individuals and Interactions are more valuable than Process and Tools.
Agile processes aren't about making a team succeed.
Evaluating an agile process in terms of numbers of successes or failures is the wrong measure.
The true assessment is whether teams find a process helpful to their success.
In the agile view it's teams that succeed or fail, not processes.
posts.key_insight: Agile processes should be evaluated not by whether they produce successful projects, but by whether teams find them genuinely helpful—because ultimately it's teams that succeed or fail, not the processes they follow.

posts.summary: Two similarly-sized enterprise projects (100 KLOC each) have drastically different build times: one takes an hour, the other 2-3 minutes. The key differentiator is database access during testing. The slow project has about 50% of tests hitting the database directly, while the fast project uses an object-relational mapping layer (Neo) that isolates the application from the database. This architectural decision means only the ORM layer tests require database access, with most tests running entirely in-memory.
key_points: Build time differences of 20-30x can exist between similar-sized projects
Database-accessing tests are the primary cause of slow build times
An ORM layer that properly isolates business logic enables fast in-memory testing
Architectural decisions about data access have direct impact on development velocity
money_quotes: One can do a full build and test in an hour, the other takes 2-3 minutes.
Both projects have a big test suite and it's the tests that take the most of the long build time, in particular the tests that access the database.
Only the tests for neo hit the database, most tests are all in-memory and thus much faster.
posts.key_insight: Proper architectural isolation of database access through an ORM layer can reduce build times by an order of magnitude by enabling in-memory testing for the majority of your test suite.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on the limitations of build tools like Ant and Make, arguing that while they work well for simple builds with straightforward tasks and dependencies, they struggle when builds require conditional logic and complex programming constructs. He notes that using XML for Ant seemed reasonable initially since it describes hierarchical data well, but he now believes a full programming language is more appropriate. He points to Rake, a Ruby-based build tool, as a promising alternative because it allows builds to be written as actual programs while still supporting declarative task and dependency specifications.
key_points: Simple builds work well with declarative task/dependency models, but complex builds need real programming language constructs
XML was a reasonable early choice for Ant but proved to be a poor fit for build scripts that require conditional logic
Build files for larger systems often end up being generated by other programs, indicating the limitations of purely declarative approaches
Rake represents a better approach by embedding build declarations within a full programming language
Having access to loops, subroutines, and list operations directly in build files proves valuable even for smaller tasks
money_quotes: Simple builds are easy to express as a series of tasks and dependencies. For such builds the facilities of ant/make work well. But more complex builds require conditional logic, and that requires more general programming language constructs - and that's where ant/make fall down.
XML is a tolerable way of describing hierarchical data, and a hierarchy seemed to fit a build scripts demands. We've both concluded since that a programming language is more the way to go.
The interesting thing about the rakefiles is that they are regular ruby programs with a few conventions and support to allow you to declare tasks and dependencies.
Since it is a full blown programming language I would expect it to work well for larger builds.
posts.key_insight: Build tools should be embedded in real programming languages rather than constrained to declarative XML or custom syntax, because complex builds inevitably require conditional logic and general programming constructs.

posts.summary: This foundational article introduces Dependency Injection as a pattern for assembling software components, contrasting it with the Service Locator pattern. Fowler explains how both patterns solve the problem of connecting components to their dependencies without hardcoding implementations, enabling pluggable architectures. He details three forms of Dependency Injection (constructor, setter, and interface injection) with examples from PicoContainer and Spring. The article concludes that while both patterns achieve decoupling, Dependency Injection is preferable for reusable components, while Service Locator may be simpler for application-specific code. The core principle is separating configuration from use.
key_points: Dependency Injection and Service Locator both solve the plugin problem of connecting components without hardcoding implementations
Constructor injection is preferred by default, but setter injection is useful when constructors become unwieldy
Service Locator creates a dependency on the locator itself, while Dependency Injection has no such dependency from component to injector
The choice between patterns matters most for reusable components; for application code, Service Locator's straightforward behavior gives it a slight edge
Configuration should always be separable from use, whether through programmatic APIs or configuration files
money_quotes: Inversion of control is a common feature of frameworks, but it's something that comes at a price. It tends to be hard to understand and leads to problems when you are trying to debug.
The question is: 'what aspect of control are they inverting?'
Saying that these lightweight containers are special because they use inversion of control is like saying my car is special because it has wheels.
Constructors with parameters give you a clear statement of what it means to create a valid object in an obvious place.
If you are building classes to be used in multiple applications then Dependency Injection is a better choice.
The choice between Service Locator and Dependency Injection is less important than the principle of separating service configuration from the use of services within an application.
I often think that people are over-eager to define configuration files. Often a programming language makes a straightforward and powerful configuration mechanism.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental design principle is separating the configuration of services from their use—how you achieve this separation (via Dependency Injection or Service Locator) is secondary to ensuring the separation exists.

posts.summary: The post explains command-oriented interfaces as an alternative to traditional method-oriented interfaces, where each operation becomes its own command class that can be executed. While more complex than simple method calls, this pattern enables powerful capabilities like decoration for cross-cutting concerns, queuing, network transmission, and caching. Fowler notes that complaints about this pattern usually stem from bloated Transaction Scripts rather than the command pattern itself. He emphasizes that commands are primarily an interface choice—the actual logic can live elsewhere, keeping command classes minimal.
key_points: Command-oriented interfaces replace direct method calls with instantiated command objects that encapsulate operations
Key benefits include easy decoration for transactions/logging, command queuing, network serialization, and result caching
The pattern works best for significant interfaces like service layers and major subsystem boundaries, not routine method calls
Complaints about duplicated logic in commands usually reflect Transaction Script problems, not inherent issues with the command pattern
Commands should be thin interface wrappers—their run method can simply delegate to other classes that contain the actual logic
money_quotes: Essentially you have one command class for each method that you would have in the method-oriented interface.
Command oriented interfaces have a number of benefits. One of the primary ones is the ability to easily add common behavior to commands by decorating the command executor.
Commands can be queued for later execution and (if the commands and their data are serializable) be passed across a network.

This problem is a general issue with Transaction Scripts. It may that command-oriented structure tends to exaggerate this, if only because many people feel that a class needs a page or two of code to be worthwhile.
The choice about using commands is primarily about the interface to clients rather than about the actual implementation of the command logic.
It's perfectly reasonable to have command classes whose run method is just a single line calling out to another method.
posts.key_insight: Command-oriented interfaces are an interface design choice that enables decoration and queuing, but the command classes themselves should remain thin wrappers—keeping logic elsewhere avoids the bloat that gives this pattern a bad reputation.

posts.summary: Fowler highlights an emerging trend of Extreme Programming projects achieving remarkably low defect rates—less than one production bug per month. He cites several examples: a food sorting machine company that dropped from 100 open bugs to one every couple of months, a Chrysler portal team that recorded exactly one bug in all of 2002 while releasing biweekly, and various Thoughtworks projects showing similar results. While emphasizing this is only achieved by a disciplined minority of XP teams, he concludes these rates prove such quality is achievable in software development.
key_points: XP projects are demonstrating defect rates below one production bug per month, a remarkable achievement
Teams achieving these rates tend to be highly disciplined with experienced XP leadership
Low defect rates don't require sacrificing delivery speed—one team released every week or two while maintaining quality
This is not universal to XP adoption, but serious XP adoption commonly yields significant defect reduction
Other processes might achieve similar results, but XP is proving to be an effective tool for getting there
money_quotes: One small but growing trend that particularly interests me is the small but growing number of XP projects that have very low defect rates, by which I mean less than one production bug per month.
Although they started off at the shocking defect rate of one a month, during 2002 they recorded exactly one bug against their system.
During this time they were releasing new version of their software every week or two.
One of these found pre-install government certification reduced from weeks to days.
It does seem to be a common result that teams that do a serious adoption of XP see significantly lower bug rates.
The teams seem to be pretty disciplined and led by people who have a year or two of XP under their belt.
But what I can conclude is that these kinds of bug rates are achievable in software development.
posts.key_insight: Disciplined XP teams are proving that near-zero defect rates are achievable in production software, challenging assumptions about inevitable bugginess in software development.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that Model Driven Architecture (MDA) is unlikely to succeed, comparing it unfavorably to the failed CASE tools of the 1980s. While CASE tools promised to revolutionize software development through graphical modeling, they failed because they couldn't provide a coherent programming environment more effective than alternatives. MDA faces the same fundamental problem: UML, despite efforts to make it computationally complete, doesn't offer a more effective development environment than writing code in modern languages. Fowler notes that even if UML became an effective programming environment, it would still need to achieve mainstream adoption, which isn't guaranteed. He dismisses secondary arguments about standards, platform independence, and the superiority of pictures over text.
key_points: MDA repeats the same promises that CASE tools made in the 1980s, which ultimately failed to deliver
UML works well for sketching design ideas but lacks the formality and cohesion needed to be a complete programming solution
Drawing sequence diagrams or activity diagrams is not as effective as writing code in a modern language
Pictures are not inherently better than text for programming - flow charts versus pseudocode demonstrates this
Even excellent programming environments don't guarantee mainstream adoption, as Smalltalk's history shows
money_quotes: Some people think that Model Driven Architecture (MDA) will be biggest shift in software development since the move from assembler to the first high level languages. Others think that it's nothing more than Night of the Living Case Tools.
I can't see that drawing sequence diagrams or activity diagrams is as good, let alone better, than writing code in a modern language.
As an ex-Smalltalker I know only too well that even the best languages don't always make it into the mainstream.
Much of the boosting of UML seems to be based on the statement that pictures are better than text. In cases this is true, but I see no evidence for that in general.
I would really like to see software development raised by a level of abstraction. But I don't see that the UML provides this abstraction jump - and without it the MDA will not succeed.
Generating pattern implementations is missing at least half the point of patterns.
posts.key_insight: For a new development paradigm to succeed, it must provide a more effective programming environment than existing alternatives - theoretical completeness and industry standards are insufficient without practical superiority.

posts.summary: Fowler initially appreciated C#'s property syntax as a cleaner alternative to Java's getter/setter conventions, noting that properties and fields share the same access syntax. This suggested developers could start with simple public fields and refactor to properties later when needed. However, he discovered that fields and properties have different reflection representations, meaning tools that use reflection break when switching between them. This forces developers to write accessor functions from the start, negating the promised benefit.
key_points: C#'s property syntax is more natural than Java's getX/setX conventions
Properties and fields share identical access syntax in program text, suggesting easy refactoring
Reflection treats fields and properties differently, breaking the promised interchangeability
This reflection mismatch forces defensive coding with accessor functions from the start
money_quotes: The getX and setX conventions of C++/Java always seems rather silly to me, it's much more natural to write obj.X = other.X.
I don't worry about this violating encapsulation, because should I wish to do something more fancy I can just replace it with a property later.
Fields and properties do have the same access in terms of program text, but they are represented differently by reflection.
So now I have to write stupid accessor functions for accessible data values. Sigh. (Or use Ruby.)
posts.key_insight: Language features that appear equivalent in source code but differ at the reflection level create hidden refactoring barriers that undermine their intended benefits.

posts.summary: Fowler challenges the assumption that the relational data model has definitively won the database wars. He observes that while SQL databases dominate persistent storage, in-memory data structures overwhelmingly use the network model, leading developers to constantly transform between the two. He argues that XML's rise has brought hierarchical data models back into relevance, and that growing memory capacities enabling in-memory databases may fundamentally shift our assumptions. Different data models suit different kinds of data, and developers should understand multiple approaches rather than defaulting to relational for everything.
key_points: The relational model dominates databases, but the network model dominates in-memory data structures, creating constant translation overhead
Most developers find the network model easier to work with than the relational model, which partly explains the prevalence of Anemic Domain Models
XML technologies like XPath and XQuery have standardized ways to query hierarchical data, reviving interest in the hierarchic data model
Growing memory sizes and falling prices are enabling in-memory databases with fundamentally different performance characteristics
Different kinds of data work better with different models - relational excels at tabular data but fails for documents like literature
money_quotes: Most developers find the network model easier to deal with than the relational model.
The relational model is perfect for tabular data, but suck really badly if you want to store The Tempest.
People often go to great efforts to turn a relational model on disk to a network model in memory.
While SQL works well for databases, we don't have the equivalent for in-memory processing.
So maybe its time again to dust off those assumptions about which data models make sense.
As memory sizes grow as fast as prices drop, we are increasingly reaching the point where most databases can be kept entirely in memory.
posts.key_insight: The supposed victory of the relational model is an illusion confined to persistent storage; in practice, developers constantly convert to network models in memory, suggesting we should embrace data model pluralism rather than relational monoculture.

posts.summary: Fowler describes the 'directing attitude' as one of two fundamental approaches to software development management. This attitude assumes most developers lack sufficient skill and therefore need constraints to prevent them from causing harm. It manifests in designs and tools that limit what developers can do, keeping them away from complex areas. Fowler notes that proponents of the opposing 'enabling attitude' argue this approach is futile because determined individuals will find ways around any restrictions.
key_points: The directing attitude assumes developer incompetence as the baseline
It manifests through restrictive designs and tools that limit developer autonomy
The goal is preventing harm rather than enabling excellence
Critics argue foolproofing is impossible because 'fools are ingenious'
money_quotes: The directing attitude says that since most developers aren't that good (it's rumored that almost 50% are below average) we need to direct the way they do things.
This direction is to prevent them from causing harm to the system they are working on.
You can't foolproof anything, because fools are ingenious and will figure out how to abuse any system.
posts.key_insight: The directing attitude prioritizes constraint over enablement, assuming developer limitation rather than potential.

posts.summary: Fowler defines the 'enabling attitude' as one of two fundamental approaches to software development tooling and design. This attitude treats developers as responsible professionals who deserve freedom and flexibility in their tools. Rather than restricting what's possible, enabling designs make good practices easy while trusting users to avoid misuse. Critics with a 'directing attitude' argue this approach only works for elite developers and fails the majority of practitioners.
key_points: The enabling attitude assumes developers are responsible professionals who deserve freedom
Tools following this philosophy make good usage easy but don't actively prevent misuse
Users who misuse enabling tools 'deserve all they get' - personal responsibility is paramount
Critics argue this is elitist and only practical for the upper quartile of developers
money_quotes: The enabling attitude takes the view that developers are responsible professionals and so should be given the freedom to do whatever they need to do.
Designs that follow this attitude should make things easy to use well but should assume that developers know what they are doing.
These tools can be misused, but take the attitude that users should know better, and if they don't they deserve all they get.
People with a directing attitude criticize this elitist attitude by saying it can only be used by the upper quartile of software developers.
posts.key_insight: Tool and language design choices often reflect a fundamental belief about whether developers should be trusted with freedom or protected from themselves.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that code examples are essential when writing about design patterns, even abstract ones, because they provide precision and help clarify ideas. He deliberately chooses small, focused examples over realistic interconnected ones, reasoning that readers can more easily understand one concept at a time. He acknowledges this tradeoff means readers don't see how concepts fit together, but believes the focused approach better serves learning. He intentionally keeps supporting code simple, even using bad practices in scaffolding code, to avoid distracting from the core pattern being taught. This is why he doesn't provide downloadable code—the examples contain shortcuts that shouldn't be used in production, and the point is understanding the ideas, not copying the code.
key_points: Focused examples showing one concept are preferable to realistic examples requiring understanding of multiple concepts simultaneously
Supporting code in examples should be deliberately simplified, even at the cost of showing bad practices, to keep attention on the core pattern
Code examples exist to express ideas, not to be copied into real applications
Downloadable code is inappropriate for focused examples because the necessary scaffolding shortcuts would be harmful if used in production
Writing code examples often helps the author clarify their own thinking about an idea
money_quotes: Several times I'm not quite sure about an idea but a code example helps to clarify it for me.
The problem with realistic examples that show multiple concepts is that they are hard to understand because you have understand all the concepts to get the example.
One of the tricks of the focused examples is to keep attention on the point of the example.

I show them as hard coded transfers because I think that makes it easier to understand what's going on, and also allows you to understand Foreign Key Mapping without having to understand Metadata Mapping.
Such things as generating ids for inserts from a static field - which you must *never* do in a real system.
The point of the code examples is the ideas that the code expresses not the code itself.
posts.key_insight: Code examples in technical writing should sacrifice realism for clarity, focusing on one concept at a time even if the supporting code uses shortcuts you'd never use in production.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the common claim that agile methods are only suitable for elite developers, acknowledging that this question is genuinely unanswerable because agile has primarily been adopted by early adopters who tend to be more skilled. He argues that while agile does require some high-ability people on a team, so do plan-driven methods—the difference is how those leaders work with the team. Drawing on Thoughtworks' experience with mixed-ability client teams, he suggests that agile's collaborative nature may actually work well across skill levels, though he admits this is speculation until more average teams try it.
key_points: New techniques are naturally tried first by higher-ability developers, making it impossible to know if they work for average teams until those teams actually try them
Both agile and plan-driven methods require some high-ability people—the difference is whether you get more leverage from plans or from collaboration
The 'individuals and interactions over processes and tools' principle means agilists expect high-ability teams to outperform regardless of methodology
Thoughtworks' mixed-ability co-sourced projects suggest agile's collaborative nature transfers well across skill levels
money_quotes: When a new technique or tool appears, it's usually tried out first by higher ability developers. This is quite a natural response.
This is an unanswerable question, because until it's tried by more average teams, you can't tell how it will work for them.
This PeopleOriented assumption means that agilists will always expect a high-ability cohesive team to do better than a team of lower ability, whichever one follows more agile approaches.
Both approaches require *some* high-ability people, although the way those leaders work with the team is different.
The open question is whether you get more leverage of the high-ability people from plans or from collaboration.
posts.key_insight: The question of whether agile works for average developers is fundamentally unanswerable until average teams actually try it—early adoption bias makes all current evidence unreliable.

posts.summary: This is a personal travel journal from Martin Fowler's three-week vacation to Japan in 2004, not a technical article. He shares highlights including temple gardens in Kyoto, ryokan experiences, and Japanese hospitality. He notes frustrations with the language barrier and lack of hiking information. The post concludes with practical travel tips about trains, luggage, accommodations, and restaurant recommendations.
key_points: Japanese gardens integrate buildings and nature in ways Western gardens don't, with moss treated as beautiful rather than as a weed
Japanese hospitality and service exceeded anywhere else the author has traveled
The language barrier was more difficult in Japan than in Europe or Thailand, particularly with written text
Kyoto works well as a single base for a multi-week trip due to its temples, gardens, and day-trip options
Tourist information offices in Japan were extremely helpful and often had English-speaking staff
money_quotes: What I liked so much about this was the way that garden and building flowed into each other, with strips of garden in between the various buildings.
Even when our common language was confined to little more than 'thank you', we saw time and time again how people would naturally go out of their way to be helpful.
The level of service was better than anywhere else we've been.
American and English gardens tend to treat moss as a weed that disfigures lawns - but we find a moss carpet much more attractive than a lawn.
I've traveled often to places without knowing the language, but I found Japan quite hard to deal with.
posts.key_insight: This is a personal travel post with no technical content—it documents a vacation to Japan with practical tips for travelers rather than insights about software development.

posts.summary: Fowler examines whether agile methods should have certification programs, noting that most capable developers dismiss existing certifications as uncorrelated with actual competence. He argues that while certification schemes are typically weak (relying on multiple-choice memorization) and often become profit-driven industries, the deeper problem for agile is philosophical: agile processes expect self-adaptation to local conditions, making standardized testing inherently difficult. He envisions that meaningful agile certification would require weeks of observation across project stages, which would be expensive. Despite his skepticism, he wishes the industry could develop meaningful certification that rewards competent practitioners.
key_points: Current certification programs rely on easily-marked multiple choice questions that test memorization rather than competence
Certification often becomes a self-serving industry focused on profit margins rather than validating skill
Agile's core principle of self-adaptation makes standardized certification fundamentally harder than for plan-driven processes
Meaningful agile certification would require weeks of observation watching practitioners handle real project situations
A particular school like XP might develop something useful, but it would look very different from current certification programs
money_quotes: Most capable developers I know have very little regard for them
Certification has little correlation with competence
Certification becomes an industry in itself - which encourages a proliferation of tests and updates which increasingly are there to help the profit margins of certification and testing companies
In an agile world, however, every process follows self-adaptation - that is the team is expected to alter the process to suit their local conditions
We considered that it would involve several weeks of observation, watching people deal with the various stages of a software project, seeing them use various skills, including tuning the process
It would help separate the more able people and allow more competent people to be better rewarded for their skill
posts.key_insight: Agile certification is inherently difficult because agile's core principle of self-adaptation contradicts the standardization that traditional certification programs require.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the concern that his influence as a 'guru' causes people to blindly follow his advice. He dismisses this as alarmist, noting that even paid consultants rarely see their specific advice followed. He reframes appeals to authority not as mindless following, but as useful shorthand in debates—referencing someone's arguments rather than reciting them in full. The piece defends this practice while acknowledging it can be seen as lazy argumentation.
key_points: No software thought leader has enough influence to make people blindly follow advice without thinking
Even clients who pay for specific consulting advice rarely follow it
Appealing to authority often means 'consider X's arguments' rather than 'obey X'
Shorthand references to authorities serve a practical purpose in debates, especially for less confident speakers
Dismissing an authority ('I don't care what X says') is equally a form of shorthand argumentation
money_quotes: There is no way I (or any other software loud-mouth) has that much influence.
I don't think I've ever run into anyone who just does exactly what I say without thinking about it.
Even clients who've paid a lot of money for a consultant's specific advice rarely follow it.
'Mr X said do this' may mean: Have you considered all the arguments made by X?
The shorthand is useful, particularly for those who find it difficult to express themselves confidently in boisterous nerd debates.
Arguments such as 'I don't care what X says' are every bit as much a shorthand.
posts.key_insight: Appealing to authority in technical debates is often a practical shorthand for referencing someone's reasoning, not evidence of blind obedience.

posts.summary: The Decorated Command pattern applies the classic decorator pattern to command objects, allowing behavior like transactions and security checks to be layered onto domain commands dynamically. Each decorator wraps a command and adds functionality before or after executing the wrapped command. This pattern is commonly used with Command-Oriented Interfaces and is related to aspect-oriented programming concepts. While it provides the benefits of AOP-style advice, it's limited to advising only the Execute method, unlike full AOP tools like AspectJ that can advise any method or field access.
key_points: Decorated Command is simply the decorator pattern applied to command objects with execute methods
Decorators like TransactionalDecorator and SecurityDecorator can be composed to layer multiple behaviors
The pattern enables dynamic addition of cross-cutting concerns without modifying domain logic
This approach is related to AOP and interceptors but is more limited since only the Execute method can be advised
Full AOP tools like AspectJ offer more flexibility by allowing advice on any method or field access
money_quotes: This is a very common pattern and also very simple, it's really just the decorator pattern applied to commands.
Indeed this ability to add behavior dynamically is one of the big benefits of a CommandOrientedInterface.
This is aspectish, but there's more to aspect oriented programming than this.
In aspect terms, the decorators provide advice to the domain command's Execute method.
However in order to do this, you have to organize everything around the commands, since only the Execute method can be advised.
posts.key_insight: The Decorated Command pattern offers a simple, composable way to add cross-cutting concerns to command objects, providing aspect-oriented benefits within the constraints of a command-oriented architecture.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reflects on the inherent limitations of the general advice he and other software commentators provide. He identifies three key limitations: the gap between general principles and specific circumstances, the narrow experience base of any individual writer, and questions about writers' motives. Despite these limitations, he argues general advice remains valuable as a starting point for decision-making by raising issues readers might not otherwise consider. His preferred outcome is readers who understand the underlying principles but reach their own conclusions, rather than those who blindly follow his recommendations.
key_points: General advice always requires readers to apply it to their specific context—'it depends' is a valid answer when you explain what it depends on
Every writer's experience in software is inherently limited, and the industry changes too quickly for assured certainty
The underlying drivers and principles behind a decision matter far more than the conclusion itself
General advice's value lies in raising issues and providing starting points, not in delivering final answers
Writers cannot prove their motives are pure, making trust an unavoidable element of consuming advice
money_quotes: There's no way I can know all the particular issues and constraints in your project, so you have to take my half-baked comments and finish them off yourself by deciding how they apply to your circumstances.
'It depends' is a good answer if you then say what it depends on.
Everyone's experience in software development is very limited. There's only so much you can do in one career, and its certainly not enough to speak with assured certainty.
Trying to stay unswayed by filthy lucre is subtle and requires concentration.
General advice raises issues that you have to consider in your particular case, reducing the chances that something will be left out because you didn't think of it.

I prefer it if a reader understands the principles that led me to a conclusion, but acted differently to my conclusion, over a reader that followed my conclusion without understanding those principles.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the principles behind advice matters more than following the advice itself—a reader who grasps the reasoning but chooses differently is preferable to one who follows blindly.

posts.summary: Fowler describes his preferred conference panel format, modeled after the British TV show Question Time. He argues that traditional panels waste time on position statements when the real value is in discussion and interaction. His format eliminates opening statements, collects written questions on index cards allowing the moderator to curate them, and focuses on deep discussion of fewer topics rather than superficial coverage of many. The moderator's role is purely facilitation, not contribution, and the format encourages dialogue between audience and panelists.
key_points: Traditional panels spend too much time on position statements that are less interesting than interactive discussion
Written questions on index cards allow the moderator to curate and prioritize for quality and flow
Deep discussion of fewer questions is preferable to superficial coverage of many topics
The moderator should focus solely on facilitation, not offering opinions
Seeding questions in advance and ending with a humorous question improves panel dynamics
money_quotes: It's the interaction on the panels that's the point.
I'd rather a good discussion on fewer questions than try to deal with everything.
For this format the moderator should not give opinions on the questions, I concentrate only on making the panel flow - which is quite enough to do.
This way I can prioritize the questions to get the most interesting issues discussed with a reasonable flow.
posts.key_insight: The value of a panel lies in curated, substantive discussion rather than comprehensive coverage, and good facilitation means prioritizing depth over breadth.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that many software development debates are fundamentally rooted in two opposing attitudes: directing (restricting options to prevent misuse) versus enabling (providing power and trusting developers). He illustrates this through debates on exceptions, static vs dynamic typing, and agile vs plan-driven processes. The distinction isn't binary—people shift between attitudes depending on context—but represents a deep personality-driven tendency that shapes how developers think about tools, languages, and processes. Fowler identifies himself firmly in the enabling camp.
key_points: Software debates often reduce to directing (prevent mistakes through restriction) vs enabling (empower skilled use) attitudes
The same feature can be viewed through either lens—exceptions restrict flow control (directing) or simplify error handling (enabling)
Agile processes are inherently enabling/people-oriented while plan-driven methods are directing
These aren't fixed positions; people shift between them contextually, but underlying personality tendencies persist
Restrictions aren't inherently directing—garbage collection enables focus by removing unwanted concerns
money_quotes: Many debates in software development are underpinned by whether the speaker has a DirectingAttitude or an EnablingAttitude.

Some arguments in favor of static typing talk about how they prevent people from making certain kinds of mistake (directing) while dynamic typers point out how static typing restricts some useful idioms (enabling).
Agile processes are PeopleOriented (enabling), while plan-driven methods seek to ensure that even a poor team can do an acceptable job (directing).
Often people are directing in some cases and enabling in others. But I think there is a deep strain running through here, often a personality issue.
I look at memory management as an enabling technology - it takes away something I don't want to worry about, so I can concentrate better on those things I do care about.
posts.key_insight: Recognizing whether someone's position stems from a directing attitude (restricting to prevent misuse) or enabling attitude (empowering skilled practitioners) reveals the deeper philosophical divide underlying most software debates.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that application boundaries are fundamentally social rather than technical constructs. He responds to Service Oriented Architecture proponents who claim applications are becoming obsolete by pointing out that the difficulty in defining application boundaries actually proves their persistence. Applications exist as social agreements between developers, business users, and budget holders, not as objectively definable technical entities. The boundaries we draw are shaped more by human relationships and politics than by functional or technical considerations.
key_points: Application boundaries are notoriously difficult to define precisely, as evidenced by debates like whether browsers are part of operating systems
SOA proponents predict applications will disappear in favor of assembled services, but Fowler disagrees
Applications are defined by three social perspectives: developer perception of code unity, business perception of functional unity, and budget allocation as a single initiative
Boundary decisions are driven primarily by human inter-relationships and politics rather than technical factors
Acknowledging the social nature of applications is necessary for clearer thinking about software architecture
money_quotes: One of the undecided problems of software development is deciding what the boundaries of a piece of software is.
Applications are social constructions.
We can draw application boundaries in hundred arbitrarily different ways.
It's our nature to group things together and organize groups of people around these groups.
These boundaries are drawn primarily by human inter-relationships and politics rather than technical and functional considerations.
To think about this more clearly I think we have to recognize this uncomfortable fact.
posts.key_insight: Applications are social constructions defined by human perception and organizational politics, not objective technical boundaries.

posts.summary: Fowler distinguishes between two database styles: Application Database (encapsulated within a single application) and Integration Database (shared across applications). He observes that the database community traditionally assumes integration databases, but this creates brittle interfaces with large surface areas that are difficult to change. The rise of Service Oriented Architecture represents a shift toward autonomous applications with their own databases communicating through services or messaging. Fowler advocates for this newer approach, particularly messaging-based integration, as a better default than shared database integration.
key_points: Integration databases create interfaces with large surface areas and limited separation between interface and implementation
The traditional database community assumption of integration databases leads to brittle, hard-to-change application links
SOA represents a shift toward autonomous applications with encapsulated databases communicating via services or messaging
Messaging-based integration is preferable to shared database integration
Mismatched assumptions about database styles between discussion participants causes confusion
money_quotes: Integration databases end up with interfaces that have a large surface area and limited abilities to separate interface from implementation.
The resulting links between applications and databases end up being brittle and thus difficult to change.
In this view of the world the integration database is no longer the default assumption.
Beware of the difference between the two database styles and take it into account when you are discussing issues of database management.
posts.key_insight: The choice between application databases and integration databases fundamentally shapes system architecture, and the industry is rightly moving away from shared database integration toward messaging-based communication between autonomous applications.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the common concern about agile projects producing less documentation for team handovers. He argues that traditional processes often produce unhelpful documentation that becomes outdated under deadline pressure, while agile favors smaller amounts of higher-quality documentation. The recommended approach is face-to-face knowledge transfer by having support team members work alongside the development team before handover. Having operations staff participate during development also improves relationships and produces better, demand-driven documentation. Automated tests serve as an important additional form of documentation.
key_points: Mandatory documentation from plan-driven processes is often unhelpful and quickly outdated under deadline pressure
Face-to-face knowledge transfer through team overlap is more effective than documentation-heavy handovers
Bringing operations staff onto the development team improves both documentation quality and deployment relationships
Documentation written on-demand for a real audience produces better results than process-mandated documentation
Automated tests constitute an important form of living documentation
money_quotes: I've noticed that processes that produce mandatory documentation often produce stuff that isn't very helpful, and under the pressure of deadlines tends not to be kept up to date.
In many ways the agile approach is a preference for a smaller amount of higher quality documentation.
Communications between development and operations are often strained; frequently the needs of operations are ignored by developers.
They found the documentation very helpful because it was written on demand rather than according to a process.
Quality triumphed over the usual quantity.
XP's huge body of automated tests which - as XPers never tire of pointing out - is an important form of documentation in its own right.
posts.key_insight: Effective knowledge transfer in agile comes from face-to-face collaboration and demand-driven documentation rather than comprehensive but stale process-mandated artifacts.

posts.summary: Fowler identifies a dysfunctional architectural pattern where organizations mandate the use of expensive infrastructure software regardless of project fit. The pattern stems from the sunk cost fallacy: having already paid for something, leadership insists on using it to avoid feeling the investment was wasted. This results in projects forced to adopt unsuitable tools, creating extra effort and suboptimal outcomes. The brief post satirically names this anti-pattern to make it recognizable and discussable.
key_points: Organizations often mandate use of expensive software purchases regardless of project suitability
The sunk cost fallacy drives architectural decisions when it shouldn't
Forcing unsuitable infrastructure onto projects creates unnecessary extra effort
Past spending on tools should not dictate future technical choices
money_quotes: I find this to be a sadly common architectural style.
Your company buys some very expensive piece of infrastructure software.
You are then told you must use it on a project even if it's not suitable for the project and causes you extra effort.
After paying all that money for it you don't want it to go to waste do you?
posts.key_insight: Past investment in a technology should never justify forcing its use on projects where it doesn't fit—that's the sunk cost fallacy masquerading as architecture.

posts.summary: Fowler describes UML sketching as an informal, selective approach to using UML diagrams for communication rather than complete specification. Sketches can be used for forward engineering (roughing out ideas before coding) or reverse engineering (explaining existing code). The key is selectivity—showing only interesting or important aspects rather than comprehensive documentation. Sessions are brief and collaborative, often done on whiteboards, with the goal of communicating ideas to teammates rather than producing formal specifications.
key_points: UML sketches prioritize selective communication over comprehensive documentation
Forward sketching helps teams discuss important design issues before coding, often in brief sessions
Reverse engineering sketches explain interesting parts of existing systems without showing every class
The medium should support quick, collaborative work—whiteboards for discussions, lightweight tools for documents
Strict UML rule adherence matters less than clarity of communication
money_quotes: The essence of sketching is selectivity.
You don't talk about all the code you are going to work on, just important issues that you want to run past your colleagues first.
Comprehensiveness is the enemy of comprehensibility.
Their emphasis is on selective communication rather than complete specification.
Since sketching is pretty informal and dynamic you need to do them quickly and collaboratively.
posts.key_insight: Effective UML usage focuses on selective communication of important ideas rather than comprehensive documentation—comprehensiveness is the enemy of comprehensibility.

posts.summary: Fowler discusses his preferences for UML diagramming tools, explaining why he uses Visio with custom templates rather than heavy CASE tools since he's interested in UML as a sketching medium. He expresses desire for cross-platform, open-source alternatives and, most significantly, advocates for text-based UML specification. He argues that textual representations would make layout easier, enable version control with diff/cvs, and provide better collaboration. He explores UmlGraph as a promising direction and sketches out his own ideas for more compact textual syntax for both sequence and class diagrams.
key_points: CASE tools are overkill when UML is used for sketching rather than as a formal repository
Text-based diagram specification has major advantages: easier layout control, version control compatibility, and collaboration
Cross-platform and open-source tools would enable better collaboration than proprietary solutions like Visio
The diagram should be a visualization of the text specification, not the primary editing mechanism
UmlGraph's approach of using pic macros for sequence diagrams points in a promising direction
money_quotes: I draw a lot of UML diagrams, but I don't use CASE tools. The reason is that I'm interested in UmlAsSketch, not in all the repository stuff.
The biggest thing I would like however is to specify my UML diagrams as text.

Text has some advantages. Much of diagram layout is tedious to fiddle with in diagrammatic form, and would be much easier to do textually - using the diagrams as a visualization rather than an editing mechanism.
Also text formats allow you to easily track changes over time with cvs and diff.
The important thing is that I'm glad to see someone going in this direction - and would like to see more.
