posts.key_insight: UML diagrams are most useful when treated as lightweight sketches rather than formal specifications, and text-based diagram syntax offers significant advantages over graphical editors for version control, collaboration, and layout control.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes a technique for rapidly gathering consensus estimates from development teams during XP-style planning. The method involves developers simultaneously revealing finger-based estimates after hearing a user story, allowing the team to quickly identify agreement or disagreement. When estimates diverge significantly, the team discusses further; when they align, they move on. This approach maximizes time spent on genuinely uncertain stories while keeping the entire team engaged in the estimation process.
key_points: Developers show estimates simultaneously (like Rock Paper Scissors) to prevent anchoring bias and reveal true disagreement
Clarifying questions should focus on scope from the customer's perspective, not technical implementation details
Teams need a convention for signaling problematic stories (too large, untestable, etc.)
The technique concentrates discussion time on genuinely uncertain stories rather than straightforward ones
The fun, game-like nature helps maintain team engagement during what could be tedious meetings
money_quotes: Throwing the estimates lets you quickly tell when developers have same similar views on an estimate (so you can note it and move on) or if there is disagreement (when you need to talk about the UserStory in more detail.
Developers ask clarifying questions about the story. They shouldn't discuss technical issues about how to implement, just ask about scope from the customer's point of view.

Notice that it's important to have a convention to say that a story has a problem and can't be estimated - most often because it's too big, but also if it can't be tested or there's some other problem.

Teams using this report that they are able to estimate stories at a very rapid rate, avoiding much discussion on stories that are straightforward to estimate and concentrating time on those that are more problematic.
This keeps everyone engaged in the estimating process. It also helps that it's fun.
posts.key_insight: Simultaneous estimation reveals genuine disagreement instantly, letting teams focus discussion only where it matters.

posts.summary: Asset capture is a strategy for gradually migrating from a legacy system to a new one by identifying and moving subsets of key assets (like employees, trades, or leases) rather than doing a big-bang replacement. The approach requires bi-directional migration capabilities so assets can move back to the old system when needed, reducing risk and handling edge cases. Success depends on event interception to capture relevant events for migrated assets while routing others appropriately. Fowler notes that most rewrites he's worked on used this approach, but teams often didn't capture assets at fine enough granularity due to lacking bi-directional migration.
key_points: Applications can be viewed as managing key assets (employees, trades, leases), and migration can proceed by capturing subsets of these assets
Bi-directional migration is essential—the ability to move assets back to the old system reduces risk and handles cases where assets change in ways the new system can't yet handle
Start with either simple assets (quick wins) or those with needs the old system handles poorly
Event interception is needed to route events to the correct system based on which assets have been captured
Finer-grained asset capturing enables more frequent releases, but requires bi-directional migration to be practical
money_quotes: To gradually cut over to a new system, you can begin by identifying a subset of assets that you'll start with the new system.
To make this work smoothly you'll need a mechanism to migrate assets from the old application to the strangler and back again.

Reverse migration sounds unnecessary, yet it's very effective both to reduce the risk and to handle cases where an asset may dynamically change in such a way that the new application can no longer handle it.
My only regret with it is that we didn't use finer grained capturing to allow more frequent releases.
Often the blocker was not having bi-directional migration. Once that's in place frequent releases are much easier.
posts.key_insight: Bi-directional migration between old and new systems is the key enabler for safe, incremental legacy modernization—without it, teams end up with coarser-grained, riskier releases.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces the Strangler Fig Application pattern as an alternative to risky big-bang system rewrites. Inspired by strangler figs in Australian rainforests that gradually envelop and replace their host trees, the pattern advocates building new systems incrementally around legacy systems rather than attempting dangerous cut-over migrations. The approach reduces risk through steady value delivery and frequent releases. Fowler later revised the name from 'Strangler Application' to 'Strangler Fig Application' to maintain the botanical metaphor and avoid violent connotations that emerged as the pattern gained popularity.
key_points: Big-bang system rewrites are deceptively complex and carry enormous risk, despite seeming straightforward
The strangler fig pattern incrementally builds new functionality around legacy systems, letting the old system be gradually replaced
Shorter release cycles with strangler fig approaches avoid unnecessary features that cut-over rewrites often generate
New applications should be designed to be easily strangled in the future, since all software becomes legacy eventually
The pattern name was updated to 'Strangler Fig' to reinforce the botanical metaphor and reduce violent connotations
money_quotes: You would think such a thing as easy - just make the new one do what the old one did. Yet they are always much more complex than they seem, and overflowing with risk.
An alternative route is to gradually create a new system around the edges of the old, letting it grow slowly over several years until the old system is strangled.
The most important reason to consider a strangler fig application over a cut-over rewrite is reduced risk.
Let's face it, all we are doing is writing tomorrow's legacy software today.
By making it easy to add a strangler fig in the future, you are enabling the graceful fading away of today's work.
Since you can use shorter release cycles with a strangler fig you can avoid a lot of the unnecessary features that cut over rewrites often generate.
posts.key_insight: Legacy system replacement is safer and often more valuable when done incrementally around the edges rather than as a risky big-bang rewrite.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler documents his experience migrating various systems to Debian Linux, including a basement server and desktop machine. He finds Debian's package management (apt-get) excellent when packages are available, but struggles significantly with the installation process, particularly X Windows configuration. While server installation was manageable despite requiring a kernel recompile, the desktop experience involved multiple failed attempts and system breakages during upgrades. He ultimately resorts to copying X configuration from a RedHat installation to fix display issues, highlighting the stark contrast between Linux and Windows desktop setup experiences.
key_points: Debian's apt-get package management makes software installation trivially easy when packages are properly supported
The official Debian installer's X Windows configuration is notoriously difficult, asking questions users cannot reasonably answer
Knoppix-based installers help bypass some pain but still result in systems that break during upgrades
Server installations are less problematic than desktop because they avoid X configuration entirely
The gap between Windows GUI setup simplicity and Linux X configuration remains stark and frustrating
money_quotes: Of course the install process for Debian is famously awful, but the good thing about a server is that you don't have to deal with X, which does a great deal to remove the pain of the process.
The bottom line is that if the application is properly supported under Debian's package management system (apt-get), everything is so easy it feels like cheating.
Even then it's no worse than any other unix, it's just that it feels so much worse because it isn't done with apt-get.
The contrast between setting up Windows on a new desktop and getting X to go can't get more stark.
So the good news is that it's working, but the bad news is that this is a horribly baroque way to sort out a GUI.
posts.key_insight: Debian's excellent package management creates such high expectations that the painful X Windows configuration process feels even more disappointing by contrast.

posts.summary: An Application Database is controlled and accessed by a single application, in contrast to an Integration Database shared across multiple systems. This single-application ownership enables a more concrete, less complex schema tailored to specific needs. Data sharing happens through services or read-only Reporting Databases rather than direct database access. The key benefit is evolvability—since all access is encapsulated by one application, the schema can be safely refactored even in production.
key_points: Application databases serve one application exclusively, leading to simpler, more focused schemas than integration databases
Data sharing with other applications should happen through services or read-only reporting databases, not direct database access
Single-application ownership enables evolutionary database design and production refactoring that would be dangerous with shared databases
The database should be owned by the application team, with an embedded database professional working closely with developers
The concept aligns with Service Oriented Architecture's notion of autonomous applications
money_quotes: Since only a single application accesses the database, the database can be defined specifically to make that one application's needs easy to satisfy.
One the great advantages of an application database is that it is easier to change since all its use is encapsulated by a single application.
Evolutionary database design and database refactoring can be used to make significant changes to an application database's design even after the database is put into production.
An application database schema is usually best designed and controlled by the application team themselves.
posts.key_insight: Constraining database access to a single application enables schema evolution and refactoring that would be impossible with shared integration databases.

posts.summary: Fowler recounts an apocryphal story about an outsourced project where a consultancy made a show of comprehensive JUnit testing, only for the tests to contain no actual assertions. The team achieved 100% code coverage while validating nothing meaningful. The post serves as a cautionary tale about gaming testing metrics and the importance of understanding what code coverage actually measures. Fowler notes that even assertion-free tests aren't entirely useless since they can catch runtime exceptions, but the practice represents a fundamental misunderstanding or deliberate subversion of testing's purpose.
key_points: Tests without assertions can achieve 100% code coverage while proving nothing about correctness
Impressive testing metrics can be gamed, making them unreliable quality indicators on their own
Code coverage data requires careful interpretation - high coverage doesn't guarantee meaningful tests
Even assertion-free tests have minimal value by catching runtime exceptions like null pointers
money_quotes: They proudly showed the client all the tests and the green bar.
However there weren't any assertions in the JUnit tests.
You can do this and have 100% code coverage - which is one reason why you have to be careful on interpreting code coverage data.
Although assertion-free testing is mostly a joke, it isn't entirely useless.
posts.key_insight: Code coverage metrics without examination of assertion quality can create an illusion of thorough testing while validating nothing.

posts.summary: Fowler explains why standard get/set accessors are insufficient for collection fields in object-oriented design. Direct access to collection data structures allows clients to modify data without the owning object's knowledge, violating encapsulation principles. Instead of exposing raw collections, classes should provide specific add and remove methods for modification, and return read-only views, non-updating iterators, or copies for reading. This approach ensures the owning object maintains control over its data and can intervene in all modifications.
key_points: Standard get/set accessors work for single values but break encapsulation for collections
Modification should happen through specific add/remove methods (e.g., addEmployee, removeEmployee) rather than direct collection access
For reading, return read-only views, non-updating iterators, or copies—never the actual underlying collection
The overhead of copying collections is negligible since it only copies object references
money_quotes: The whole point of encapsulation is that an object controls access to its data.
You don't want to give clients direct access to the collection data structure itself - for if you do it allows clients to alter the supplier's data without the supplier being able to intervene.
You don't want to return the actual collection used to store the objects - otherwise people can add and remove the items without using the encapsulating add and remove methods.
posts.key_insight: Encapsulating collections requires different patterns than simple fields—use specific add/remove methods for modification and return read-only views or copies for access to maintain object control over its data.

posts.summary: Fowler declares the Gang of Four's Design Patterns book the best ever written on object-oriented design, possibly any style of design. He notes its enormous influence on the software industry, visible in Java and .NET libraries filled with GOF patterns. However, he cautions that it's not an easy read and recommends readers first become comfortable with basic OO design principles. Despite the learning investment required, he argues the book amply rewards the effort. He also reassures non-C++ programmers that the code examples remain accessible to anyone familiar with C-based languages.
key_points: The Gang of Four book is considered the definitive work on object-oriented design
Its patterns have been deeply embedded into major platforms like Java and .NET
The book requires significant effort and prior OO knowledge to fully appreciate
The investment in understanding the material pays substantial dividends
C++ code examples are accessible to programmers familiar with any C-based language
money_quotes: In my view the Gang of Four is the best book ever written on object-oriented design - possibly of any style of design.
This book has been enormously influential on the software industry - just look at the Java and .NET libraries which are crawling with GOF patterns.
Unlike most books, however, it amply pays that investment.
posts.key_insight: The Gang of Four's Design Patterns book, while demanding significant effort to master, remains the most influential and rewarding text on object-oriented design ever written.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains that hierarchic data models organize data as tree structures, noting they were common in early databases but fell out of favor as relational models dominated databases and network models dominated in-memory programming. The hierarchy's simplicity becomes a limitation with complex data. However, Fowler observes that XML's rise has brought hierarchic models back into relevance, offering standardized manipulation facilities comparable to SQL for relational data.
key_points: Hierarchic models organize data as trees but were displaced by relational (databases) and network (in-memory) models
Hierarchies break down when data complexity increases
XML has revived hierarchic data models by providing standard manipulation tools
The simplicity of hierarchies remains their enduring appeal despite limitations
money_quotes: A hierarchy, while a simple organizational tool, breaks down as you get more complex data.
The simplicity of a hierarchy is hard to deny, and hierarchic models keep showing value.
The big return of hierarchic data models these days is with the rise of XML.
posts.key_insight: Despite being displaced by more flexible data models, hierarchic structures persist because their simplicity provides real value, as demonstrated by XML's success.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler announces that JetBrains has launched their Early Access Program for a C# development tool. He humorously notes that they didn't follow his naming suggestion and called it ReSharper instead. Initial feedback from his colleagues at Thoughtworks has been positive, though they're eager for additional features.
key_points: JetBrains launched an Early Access Program for their C# refactoring tool
The tool was named ReSharper, ignoring Fowler's naming input
Early adopters among Fowler's colleagues are enthusiastic but want more features
money_quotes: Sadly they ignored my naming advice and instead called it ReSharper
Early noise from my colleagues has been enthusiastic if still wanting more
posts.key_insight: JetBrains' entry into C# tooling with ReSharper signals growing demand for refactoring tools beyond the Java ecosystem.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces Knoppix, a Linux distribution that runs entirely from CD without touching the hard drive. He highlights its key advantages: no installation required, compressed filesystem allowing extensive software, good hardware detection, and serving as an easy path to Debian installation. The post notes the ecosystem of Knoppix variations and custom CD images, including Morphix's modular approach to creating specialized distributions.
key_points: Knoppix runs entirely from CD, requiring no hard drive installation - just boot and go
Compressed filesystem packs a full KDE-based system with office tools, browser, and graphics software onto a single CD
Good hardware detection eliminates the traditional Linux pain point of hunting for drivers
Provides an easier installation path to Debian, solving its notoriously painful setup process
A community ecosystem of Knoppix variations allows customized distributions for different purposes
money_quotes: It runs entirely from the CD, without touch the hard drive.
A compressed file system fits a lot of software on the CD.
Knoppix is based on Debian, so it solves a long running problem with Debian - the painful installation process.
There is also a cottage industry of people doing variations on the Knoppix theme, creating different CD images for different purposes.
posts.key_insight: Live CD distributions like Knoppix represented a breakthrough in Linux accessibility by eliminating installation barriers and hardware configuration pain points.

posts.summary: Fowler describes his transition from using a single laptop to working across three different computers (Mac, Windows, Ubuntu). He explains how he keeps files synchronized using Subversion for version control and IMAP for email. While this setup mostly works well, he notes pain points around address book synchronization, calendar data, and news aggregator read states. He advocates for text-based file formats that work across platforms and version control systems.
key_points: Subversion enables seamless file synchronization across machines with the bonus of full version control
IMAP solved the multi-machine email problem that POP couldn't handle
Binary files like address books resist synchronization and merging
Text-based formats (XML, plain text) are inherently more portable and version-control friendly
Multi-platform setups let you use the best tools from each operating system
money_quotes: Any time I switch machines I commit my working directory and do an update on my new machine. Everything keeps nicely synced, and I get full version control too.
Keeping to text files as much as possible is useful.
Part of the advantage of the multiple computer setup is that I can use any application that's particular to one system.
posts.key_insight: Version control systems designed for code synchronization work equally well for managing any text-based working files across multiple machines.

posts.summary: Fowler defines the network data model as a structure where records are connected via pointer links for navigation-based querying. He notes that while network model databases lost to relational databases, the network model remains dominant for in-memory data structures in mainstream programming languages. He draws a connection to object models, which also use pointer-linked structures, but distinguishes true objects by their combination of data and behavior. Anemic domain models, lacking significant behavior, are essentially network data models in disguise.
key_points: Network data models use record types with pointer links for navigation-based querying
Despite relational databases winning, network models remain the primary model for in-memory data in most languages
Object models resemble network models structurally but differ by incorporating behavior with data
Anemic domain models are effectively network data models because they lack meaningful behavior
money_quotes: Network model data bases fell out of favor a while ago to the RelationalDataModel, but this data model is by no means dead.
Indeed it's the primary model for in-memory data.
You can think of object models as a form of network data model, since they also have data structures linked by pointers.
The vital difference is that objects incorporate data and behavior, as a result I think they feel much different in practice.
Many object models (AnemicDomainModel) are really only network data models since they don't have any significant behavior.
posts.key_insight: The network data model never disappeared—it simply moved from databases into the in-memory object graphs of everyday programming, though true objects distinguish themselves by combining behavior with data.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the on-site customer practice from Extreme Programming, which requires a business representative to be physically present with the development team. The customer answers questions, interacts with developers, and is considered a full team member whose contribution is as vital as the developers'. This person comes from the business side, not development, and is responsible for making decisions about business value and describing requirements. While they don't abandon their regular job, physical presence is mandatory, and the role is often filled by a team rather than an individual.
key_points: The on-site customer must be physically present with developers, not remote or occasionally available
Customers are full team members whose success contribution equals that of developers
The customer role belongs to business beneficiaries, not development staff
Customers make business value decisions and describe requirements to the team
The customer role is commonly filled by a team rather than a single person
money_quotes: Indeed they are part of the development team, and recognize that the success of the team depends as much on them as it does on the developers.
The XP customer is from the business side of the organization not the development side.
They don't have to give up their regular job to do this, but they must be physically present.
The customer is someone who will be a beneficiary of the system.
posts.key_insight: True customer involvement in XP means physical presence and equal responsibility for team success, not occasional consultation from the business side.

posts.summary: This post explains the relational data model, contrasting it with the network data model. Fowler describes how relational databases use tables rather than explicit pointers, with links made through joining tables on common values. He notes that while SQL has made the relational model dominant, purists consider SQL a weak implementation. The key distinction from network models is that all relations are treated as the same kind of thing, enabling powerful composability.
key_points: Relational databases link data through common values rather than explicit pointers, though surrogate keys create de facto pointers
SQL's standardization drove relational model dominance, but relational purists view SQL as a weak implementation
The crucial difference from network models is that all relations are the same type of thing, not different record types
Relational expressions operate on relations and produce relations, enabling composability that network models lack
money_quotes: In contrast to NetworkDataModel, there are no explicit pointers between tables, links are made by join tables on common values
It's worth pointing out that many relational fans consider SQL to be be a weak form of the relational model
Expressions in SQL operate on relations and produce relations - which gives the relational model a quality of composability that network models typically don't have
posts.key_insight: The relational model's power comes not from eliminating pointers but from treating all relations uniformly, enabling composable expressions that network models cannot match.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains his criteria and process for accepting books into his signature series at Addison-Wesley/Pearson. The series targets technical leads and architects in enterprise software, focusing on technical rather than management content. He emphasizes quality over quantity, requiring a substantial first draft before consideration rather than just a proposal. The acceptance decision involves review by all existing series authors, not just Fowler himself, creating a peer review system that maintains high standards.
key_points: The series targets 'experience accelerators' for technical leads and architects in enterprise software
Books are only considered at substantial first draft stage, not proposal stage, to ensure quality
All existing series authors review and vote on candidate books, not just Fowler
The focus is deliberately technical rather than management/process oriented
Fowler would rather reject a worthy book than accept a sub-par one
money_quotes: The books I'm looking for are experience accelerators for people who are, or are learning to be, technical leads/architects in enterprise software projects.
I'd rather reject a worthy book than accept one that's sub-par.
Because of my commitment to quality, I'm not going to consider books at the proposal stage.
In many ways I have them as a panel to prevent me from being overly generous to a book I like.
In my view it's in the interests of every author in the series to keep the series quality high.
posts.key_insight: Maintaining a high-quality book series requires evaluating completed work rather than proposals, and distributed judgment among peers rather than a single gatekeeper.

posts.summary: Use cases are a requirements elicitation technique popularized by Ivar Jacobson in the late 80s and early 90s. The post directs readers to Alistair Cockburn's book as the definitive resource on the topic. Fowler argues that while use cases appear in UML as diagrams, the real value lies in the textual descriptions rather than the visual representations. He warns against over-investing time in use case documentation before beginning development.
key_points: Alistair Cockburn's book is the authoritative resource on use cases and should be read before using the technique
Use case diagrams in UML are of little value compared to the textual descriptions they represent
Use cases should be kept short and readable rather than elaborate
Teams should not spend weeks or months on use case documents before beginning development
money_quotes: Use case diagrams are of little value - the key value of use cases lies in the text which is not standardized in UML.
When you do use cases put your energy into the text.
Use cases are at their best when they are short and readable.
You should not be spending weeks, let alone months, generating use case documents before you begin development.
posts.key_insight: The value of use cases lies not in elaborate UML diagrams but in concise, readable text that can be created quickly before development begins.

posts.summary: Fowler coins the term 'Wardish' to describe techniques that appear too simple to be effective but prove surprisingly powerful in practice. He attributes this pattern to Ward Cunningham, inventor of CRC cards, wikis, and Fit testing framework. The post celebrates the counterintuitive value of radical simplicity in software tools and methods.
key_points: Wardish ideas appear 'ludicrously simple' at first glance but reveal unexpected power through use
Three canonical examples: CRC cards for design discussion, wikis for collaborative editing, and Fit for executable test specifications
Ward Cunningham is identified as the master practitioner of this approach to tool and technique design
money_quotes: a technique, tool, or design idea that is clearly too ludicrously simple to be any good, but when you start using it has a power that belies its simplicity
Discussing a design by moving index cards around
A web site where anyone can edit any page
posts.key_insight: The most powerful software techniques often look too simple to work, which is precisely why they get overlooked.

posts.summary: This comprehensive essay traces the evolution of software development methodologies from chaotic 'code and fix' approaches through plan-driven 'monumental' methodologies to agile methods. Fowler argues that agile methodologies are fundamentally different from traditional approaches in two key ways: they are adaptive rather than predictive (embracing change rather than resisting it), and people-oriented rather than process-oriented (recognizing that talented developers cannot be treated as interchangeable resources). The essay explains why software development cannot follow the engineering metaphor of separating design from construction, since in software, construction (compilation) is essentially free and all effort is design work. Fowler surveys various agile approaches including XP, Scrum, Crystal, and Lean Development, while emphasizing that the core principles unite them despite surface differences.
key_points: Software development fundamentally differs from civil engineering because construction is essentially free (compilation) while design constitutes nearly all the work, making the traditional engineering metaphor inappropriate

Agile methods embrace two core principles: adaptivity (welcoming change rather than resisting it through detailed upfront planning) and people-orientation (treating developers as skilled professionals rather than replaceable resources)
Requirements instability is the norm in business software, not an aberration to be prevented through better requirements engineering
Iterative development with frequent working software releases provides the honest feedback mechanism needed to control unpredictable processes
Measurement-based management leads to dysfunction in creative knowledge work; delegatory management where practitioners decide how to do their work is more effective for software development
money_quotes: Creative processes are not easily planned, and so predictability may well be an impossible target.
In software: construction is so cheap as to be free. In software all the effort is design, and thus requires creative and talented people.
A late change in requirements is a competitive advantage.
The people involved are not replaceable parts.

If you expect all your developers to be plug-compatible programming units, you don't try to treat them as individuals. This lowers morale (and productivity). The good people look for a better place to be, and you end up with what you desire: plug-compatible programming units.
There is nothing more frustrating to a developer than seeing their hard work go to waste.
One of the big dangers is to pretend that you can follow a predictable process when you can't.
Documents can hide all sorts of flaws. Untested code can hide plenty of flaws. But when people actually sit in front of a system and work with it, then flaws become truly apparent.
posts.key_insight: Software development is fundamentally a design activity where construction costs are negligible, which means it requires adaptive, people-centered processes rather than the predictive, plan-driven approaches borrowed from traditional engineering disciplines.

posts.summary: Fowler documents a technical issue with his Belkin KVM switch causing erratic mouse behavior when switching to Linux machines. He discovers that changing the mouse protocol configuration in XF86Config from 'IMPS/2' to 'auto' resolves the worst of the problem. The post provides specific configuration file changes that fixed the issue, borrowed from a Morphix install. While the solution isn't perfect—requiring some mouse movement to reactivate after switching—it's a workable fix. He concludes with frustration about X configuration complexity compared to Windows.
key_points: Belkin KVM switch causes mouse to move randomly and click when switching to Linux
Workaround exists by switching virtual consoles (ctrl-alt-f5 then alt-f7) to reset mouse state
Changing mouse protocol from 'IMPS/2' to 'auto' in XF86Config resolves the main issue
The fix requires wiggling the mouse after switching but eliminates the erratic behavior
X Window System configuration remains a significant pain point for Linux desktop users
money_quotes: The worst problem shows itself when I switch into Linux and mouse goes haywire, moving around at random and clicking buttons.
I won't try to figure out what the important change is, I'm pretty ignorant of X and would like to be more so.
One day I hope someone or something will make configuring X as easy as it is to get windows to work.
At the moment this is one of the most painful parts of using Linux as a desktop machine.
posts.key_insight: Even experienced developers sometimes solve configuration problems through trial and error rather than deep understanding, and that's an acceptable approach when it works.

posts.summary: This is a deprecated stub page that redirects readers to a newer article called 'OutcomeOriented'. Fowler explicitly states he no longer considers this page a useful source of information. The original content about functional staff organization has been superseded by his updated thinking on outcome-oriented approaches.
key_points: The concept of 'Functional Staff Organization' has been deprecated in Fowler's thinking
Fowler directs readers to 'OutcomeOriented' as the replacement material
This reflects Fowler's practice of maintaining and updating his bliki over time
money_quotes: I no longer consider it a useful source of information.
posts.key_insight: Fowler's thinking on organizational structures has evolved from functional staff organization toward outcome-oriented approaches.

posts.summary: Fowler defends Thoughtworks' practice of openly sharing intellectual property, including expertise and tools like CruiseControl. He argues this isn't altruism but enlightened self-interest: sharing ideas generates publicity, attracts clients, builds reputation, and accelerates personal learning. The approach also benefits companies by keeping talented employees motivated and engaged. Rather than hoarding competitive advantages, giving away knowledge creates more value than it costs.
key_points: Sharing intellectual property generates publicity and attracts clients who want help applying general ideas to specific situations
Writing and publishing code helps solidify understanding—many write precisely because they don't fully understand something yet
Reputation and respect are valuable rewards independent of financial gain
Open source development functions as ongoing research, advancing both personal knowledge and the broader field
Companies benefit from open IP through cumulative reputation and by keeping knowledge-sharing employees motivated
money_quotes: Open Intellectual Property is a principle that can be enormously rewarding.
The best ideas in the world can't bring in piles of cash if nobody knows about them.
Offering general ideas is valuable, but there are LimitationsOfGeneralAdvice, so people are likely to hire you to apply the general ideas to specific situations.
We are programmers, and programs are our principal means of expression.
I often write articles because I don't properly understand something and writing helps me fix it in my mind.
I think Open Source development has become an important source of research.
A company who denies the latter rewards may de-motivate or lose the most critical assets it has.
posts.key_insight: Freely sharing intellectual property isn't charity—it's a strategy that generates publicity, attracts work, builds reputation, accelerates learning, and keeps talented people engaged.

posts.summary: This is a deprecated stub page that redirects readers to newer content. Martin Fowler indicates he no longer considers this page on functional staff organization to be useful. He directs readers to his BusinessCapabilityCentric page for better coverage of the topic. The original content has been entirely replaced with this deprecation notice.
key_points: The author actively maintains and updates his content, deprecating outdated material
Functional staff organization concepts have evolved into business capability centric thinking
Fowler prioritizes directing readers to his best current thinking rather than preserving historical posts
money_quotes: This page is now deprecated as I no longer consider it a useful source of information.
posts.key_insight: Good technical writers maintain their content over time, deprecating outdated ideas and redirecting readers to evolved thinking.

posts.summary: This is a deprecated stub page that redirects readers to a different article. Martin Fowler has determined the original content is no longer useful and directs visitors to his ActivityOriented page for better coverage of the topic of technical staff organization.
key_points: The original content on technical staff organization has been deprecated
Fowler recommends the ActivityOriented page as a replacement resource
money_quotes: This page is now deprecated as I no longer consider it a useful source of information.
posts.key_insight: Fowler maintains his content by deprecating outdated pages and redirecting to better resources rather than leaving stale information.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces the concept of 'Courtesy Implementation' - adding methods to a class that don't naturally belong to it but simplify the overall system design. Using the composite pattern with boxes and elephants, he demonstrates how having an Elephant class implement num_elephants (returning 1) eliminates ugly type-checking conditionals in the traversal code. He argues this practice, while initially feeling semantically wrong, parallels mathematical conventions like x^0=1 that exist because they make everything else work better.
key_points: Type-checking conditionals (like kind_of?) are a code smell that suggest polymorphism might be needed
Leaf classes in composite patterns can implement methods as a 'courtesy' to simplify tree traversal logic
Mathematical definitions like x^0=1 serve as analogies - they feel unintuitive but make the broader system work
Models should be designed to suit how we want to perceive and work with the world, not strict real-world accuracy
Courtesy implementations trade semantic purity for practical simplicity
money_quotes: The kind_of? test in num_elephants is a smell, since we should be wary of any conditional that tests the type of an object.
I think of it as getting the leaf class to provide a simple implementation as a courtesy to its role as a node in the hierarchy.
Intuitively I don't think it makes sense to say that any number multiplied by itself 0 times is 1 - why not zero? But the definition makes all the mathematics work out nicely.
Whenever we build a model we are designing a model to suit how we want to perceive the world.
Courtesy Implementations are worthwhile if they simplify our model.
posts.key_insight: Adding methods that don't semantically belong to a class can be justified when they eliminate conditionals and simplify the overall design - pragmatic simplicity sometimes trumps semantic purity.

posts.summary: Fowler shares a practical testing tip from Rich Garzaniti for catching resource leaks in applications that use resource pools like database connections. The common problem is developers forgetting to release resources back to the pool. By setting the pool size to 1 during testing, any unreleased resource will immediately cause the next test to fail, making the leak easy to locate.
key_points: Resource pools (like database connection pools) are prone to leaks when developers forget to release resources
Setting pool size to 1 in tests makes resource leaks immediately visible
This technique narrows down the culprit quickly since the failure occurs in the test immediately following the leak
money_quotes: Teams often get into trouble because people forget to release the resource back to the pool when they are done with it.
To help spot these in testing, make sure you set the resource pool size to 1 in your tests.
That way if some code doesn't release its resource the next test that uses that resource will fail, and you don't have too far to look for the culprit.
posts.key_insight: Setting resource pool size to 1 in tests transforms subtle resource leaks into immediate, easily debuggable test failures.

posts.summary: Fowler draws a parallel between craftsmanship in carpentry and software development, using his wife Cindy's appreciation for fine woodworking details as a lens. He discovers a small but thoughtful detail in Apple's iTunes: the software correctly sorts 'Piano Sonata No.8' before 'Piano Sonata No.10' using natural number ordering rather than alphabetical sorting. This seemingly trivial feature required deliberate effort from a developer who cared about the user experience. Fowler expresses appreciation for this invisible craftsmanship.
key_points: Good craftsmanship often manifests in details that most people never notice
Natural sorting (8 before 10) rather than alphabetical sorting (10 before 8) requires deliberate implementation effort
Software quality includes small touches that 'don't look like much, but are actually quite tricky to do right'
Appreciation for craft extends across disciplines from carpentry to software
money_quotes: She particularly appreciates things that don't look like much, but are actually quite tricky to do right.
Someone, somewhere cared enough to make that happen - I appreciate it.
posts.key_insight: True craftsmanship in software reveals itself in the small, invisible details that someone cared enough to get right, even when most users will never notice.

posts.summary: Fowler explains JUnit's design decision to create a new test object instance for each test method, ensuring test isolation. This prevents tests from affecting each other through shared state, making test order irrelevant and debugging easier. While critics argue setUp/tearDown methods make fresh instances unnecessary, Fowler defends the approach as making isolation automatic rather than requiring programmer discipline. He acknowledges the pattern is unintuitive but considers the benefits worth the implementation complexity, while respectfully noting that reasonable people like the NUnit and TestNG creators chose differently.
key_points: JUnit creates a new test case instance for each test method, automatically reinitializing fields to ensure test isolation
Test isolation prevents cascading failures, allows tests to run in any order, and simplifies debugging when tests fail
Object creation overhead is rarely a valid concern in modern Java - fears about object allocation are based on outdated mental models
For expensive resources like database connections, JUnit's TestSetup decorator allows controlled state sharing across test suites
The original design used inner classes per test but switched to pluggable selector pattern for easier test writing
money_quotes: An important part of framework design is to make it easy to the right thing (isolation) and hard (but not impossible) to do things that cause problems.
There's a lot of fear about creating lots of objects, but most of the time it isn't justified - it's based on an outdated mental model of how object allocation and collection work.
If you use TestSetup you are breaking isolation and broken isolation tends to lead to awkward to find bugs. Don't use it unless you really, really need it.
I don't think those that made that decision have a single-digit IQ, have some complex financial killing in mind, or are embarking on some strange behavior with their lower torsos.
They called the design trade-off differently - and I think life is better when we can respectfully disagree over the fluid nature of software design.
You never have a situation where you're trying to figure out why one test failed and the cause is due to the way another test is written.
posts.key_insight: Good framework design makes the right thing (test isolation) automatic rather than requiring programmer discipline, even if the implementation mechanism is less intuitive.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler provides the canonical definitions of refactoring as both a noun and a verb. As a noun, refactoring refers to a change that improves internal structure while preserving external behavior. As a verb, it describes the process of applying multiple such changes systematically. These definitions emphasize that refactoring must not alter observable behavior.
key_points: Refactoring as a noun is a specific change to internal structure that improves understanding and modifiability
Refactoring as a verb is the systematic application of multiple refactorings
The key constraint is preserving observable behavior throughout
The goals are making code easier to understand and cheaper to modify
money_quotes: Refactoring (noun): a change made to the internal structure of software to make it easier to understand and cheaper to modify without changing its observable behavior.
Refactoring (verb): to restructure software by applying a series of refactorings without changing its observable behavior.
posts.key_insight: Refactoring is defined by its constraint: improving internal structure while preserving observable behavior, making code easier to understand and cheaper to modify.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses whether reordering declarations (like methods and fields) in code constitutes refactoring. He acknowledges the counterargument that since declaration order doesn't affect program execution, it shouldn't count as refactoring. However, he argues that since his definition of refactoring includes making code easier to understand and cheaper to modify, and reordering can improve understandability by grouping related features or explaining how a class works, it qualifies as refactoring. He dismisses execution impact as a red herring, noting that renaming methods also doesn't alter execution yet is clearly refactoring.
key_points: Declaration order in modern languages doesn't affect program execution, leading some to argue reordering isn't refactoring
Refactoring's definition includes making code easier to understand and cheaper to modify, not just changing behavior
Grouping related features together and ordering to explain class functionality improves understandability
The 'doesn't alter execution' argument is a red herring since renaming also doesn't change execution but is clearly refactoring
money_quotes: The order in which you declare features in modern programming languages doesn't alter the program at all.
In my DefinitionOfRefactoring, I used the phrase 'to make it easier to understand and cheaper to modify'.
It can make sense to put related features in a class together.
You can also order the features in such a way to help explain how the class works.
The fact that it doesn't alter the internal execution is a red-herring.
Changing the name of a method doesn't alter the execution, yet renaming is a very important refactoring to improve the understandability of a program.
posts.key_insight: Refactoring is defined by improving understandability and modifiability, not by whether it changes program execution.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses debates about what constitutes a true refactoring, acknowledging that while such definitional discussions risk becoming pedantic, understanding boundaries has practical value. He revisits his intentionally informal definition from his book, highlighting two key phrases open to interpretation: 'observable behavior' and 'easier to understand and cheaper to modify.' He argues that refactoring's essence lies in small, behavior-preserving changes made specifically to improve comprehension and modifiability. Changes made with different purposes, even if structurally similar, don't qualify as refactoring in his view.
key_points: The definition of refactoring is intentionally informal but still precise enough to avoid misuse
'Observable behavior' and 'easier to understand' are deliberately open to interpretation in the definition
Purpose matters: identical changes made for different reasons may not qualify as refactoring
Refactoring is fundamentally about sequences of small behavior-preserving changes
Fowler wants to prevent 'RefactoringMalapropism' - the misuse of the term
money_quotes: As with these discussions, there's always a danger of debating how many angels fit on a pin, but thinking about the boundaries does have some useful purpose.
The same changes made with a different purpose aren't refactoring as I see it.
The essence of refactoring is that of the sequence of small behavior-preserving changes.
Despite the fact that refactoring isn't something that can be formally defined, it's still a pretty precise term.
posts.key_insight: Refactoring is defined not just by what changes you make, but by your purpose in making them—improving understandability and modifiability.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses whether performance optimization should be classified as refactoring, concluding they are distinct activities despite sharing similar code transformations. The key distinction lies in purpose: refactoring aims to improve code clarity while optimization targets execution speed. He argues that success metrics differ fundamentally—refactoring is judged by subjective assessment of readability, while optimization requires profiler measurements. The same code transformation, like introducing a variable, can serve either purpose depending on the developer's intent and evaluation criteria.
key_points: Refactoring and optimization are separate activities defined by their purpose, not their techniques
The same code transformation can be either refactoring or optimization depending on intent
Refactoring success is measured by subjective clarity assessment; optimization requires profiler verification
Performance-critical optimizations should be logged for retesting when the environment changes
money_quotes: I see optimization and refactoring as two separate things, even though they often use the same transformations.
You do refactoring to make the code easier to understand, you do optimization to make it go faster.
When you are refactoring you are thinking about making the code clearer.
You should be using a profiler, before and after the change to ensure that your optimization really did help performance.
So although the two are similar, and share many transformations, I see them as different because their purpose is different.
posts.key_insight: Activities are defined by their purpose rather than their mechanics—the same code change becomes refactoring or optimization based on whether you're optimizing for human understanding or machine performance.

posts.summary: Przemyslaw Pokrywka raised a thought-provoking question about whether Introduce Null Object counts as refactoring when it inadvertently fixes an unknown bug. Fowler argues that refactoring preserves 'observable behavior,' and if a bug wasn't known or cared about, that behavior wasn't truly observable. He concludes that fixing an unknown bug during refactoring doesn't disqualify the change as a refactoring. The post also highlights an important distinction between manual refactoring, which allows judgment calls, and tool-driven refactoring, which must be more conservative. Even automated tools can't guarantee preserved behavior in edge cases like reflection-based method calls.
key_points: Introduce Null Object can alter behavior by replacing null pointer exceptions with default behavior, making it a tricky refactoring
The key criterion for refactoring is preserving 'observable behavior' - what the program essentially does from a user perspective
If a bug wasn't known or wasn't behavior worth preserving, fixing it incidentally still qualifies as refactoring
Manual refactoring allows human judgment calls that automated tools cannot safely make
Even tool-driven refactoring can break code in edge cases like reflection-based method invocation
money_quotes: If you fix a bug you don't know about is this still a refactoring?
I'd argue yes, since you didn't know or care (enough) about the buggy behavior then I'd say that behavior wasn't observable.
Even if you knew about the bug, I'd still argue that it's okay to call this a refactoring if the bug wasn't behavior you cared about preserving.
With manual refactoring you can make judgement calls like this, tools often have to be much more careful.
Even a rename method can be break on refactoring if that method is called by reflection with a name that's read from a file.
posts.key_insight: Observable behavior in refactoring is defined not by what technically changes, but by what users and developers actually know about and care to preserve.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reviews his Magellan Meridian Gold GPS device, received as a Christmas gift. While he finds it an interesting toy rather than a necessity, it has proven most useful for pre-planned bicycle trips around New England. The device excels at showing current location but struggles with on-the-fly route planning due to its small screen. A recent DirectRoute software update adds automatic routing capabilities, but with significant limitations including inability to set multi-point routes and questionable re-routing logic.
key_points: GPS devices are better at showing where you are than telling you where to go without pre-programmed routes
The small screen size makes on-device route planning impractical compared to traditional fold-out maps
Pre-planning routes on a computer and downloading them to the device works well for cycling trips
The DirectRoute software adds useful auto-routing but lacks multi-point routes and road avoidance features
GPS accuracy is generally excellent, with only occasional bizarre location errors
money_quotes: In general the GPS has been good for finding where you are, but not good at telling you where to go - unless you download a route beforehand.
Nothing beats the resolution and screen size of a fold out map.
About once a year I've run into strangeness - such as when biking through Duxbury I looked down at the unit and it told me I was in Estonia.
Massachusetts doesn't believe in road signs.
When we asked for the re-route it instructed us to get off 128 at an exit we'd just passed, then get back on 128 in the same direction.
posts.key_insight: Early GPS devices were useful supplements to traditional navigation methods but lacked the flexibility and intelligence to replace them entirely.

posts.summary: Fowler argues against standardizing story points across multiple teams, calling it at best limited in value and at worst dangerous. The XP estimation system relies on velocity and yesterday's weather for calibration, making the actual units irrelevant—only relative comparison matters. Attempting to normalize story points across teams requires accounting for vastly different factors like team capability, task nature, and estimation tendencies. The real danger is that standardized metrics inevitably get used to compare team performance, causing teams to game their numbers and breaking the feedback loop that makes the system work.
key_points: Story points are merely anchors for the feedback loop of yesterday's weather, not absolute measurements
Normalizing story points across teams requires standardizing team capability, task nature, and estimation tendencies—an extremely difficult undertaking
Standardized metrics will inevitably be used to compare team performance, even when people promise otherwise
Teams will distort measurements to appear more productive, breaking the planning feedback loop
Moving between teams doesn't actually create estimation difficulties because familiarity with the codebase naturally calibrates understanding
money_quotes: I think trying to come up with this at best of limited value, and at worst dangerous.
When you make estimates, the actual units you estimate aren't important - what's important is you estimate by rough comparative value and use Yesterday's Weather for calibration.
Even if everyone swears till they are blue in the face that they won't use it for cross team measurement, there will always be the suspicion that this will happen eventually.
This will cause teams to distort their measurements so that it seems that they get more story points done.
While it would be incredibly valuable to have a way to measure productivity I think the nature of software is such that we Cannot Measure Productivity.
You can't estimate on a new team until you get reasonably familiar with the problem and the current code base.
posts.key_insight: Story points only work as local calibration tools within a team's feedback loop—standardizing them across teams destroys their utility and creates perverse incentives to game the metrics.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces lambdas (also called closures, anonymous functions, or blocks) to programmers coming from C/C++/Java backgrounds who lack exposure to this concept. He explains that lambdas are blocks of code that can be passed as arguments to functions, using Ruby examples to demonstrate their power. The key distinguishing features are closures (ability to reference variables from the defining scope) and concise syntax that encourages frequent use. Fowler argues that while function pointers and anonymous inner classes provide similar capabilities, their verbose syntax prevented adoption of lambda-style patterns in Java. He notes that once programmers experience lambdas, they become indispensable despite seeming minor at first.
key_points: Lambdas are blocks of code that can be passed as arguments and create closures by binding to variables from their defining environment
The crucial difference from function pointers or anonymous inner classes is both the closure capability and the minimal syntax required
Concise lambda syntax is what makes them practical to use frequently - verbose alternatives like Java's anonymous inner classes were abandoned because the resulting code was 'too messy and ugly'
Common practical uses include collection pipelines (select, filter operations) and 'execute around' patterns for resource management like file handling
Lambdas seem minor when first encountered but quickly become something programmers miss when working in languages without them
money_quotes: Essentially a lambda is a block of code that can be passed as an argument to a function call.
Lambdas allow you to do even more interesting stuff.
The first crucial point about lambdas they usually create closures - a block of code plus the bindings to the environment they came from.

Languages that support lambdas allow you to define them with very little syntax. While this might not seem an important point, I believe it's crucial - it's the key to make it natural to use them frequently.
But the resulting code was just too messy and ugly so we gave up.
They are one of those things that seem minor when you first come across them, but you quickly grow to like them.
posts.key_insight: The practical value of lambdas comes not just from their closure capabilities, but critically from concise syntax that makes them natural to use frequently - without that, the pattern gets abandoned despite its power.

posts.summary: This post documents the process of installing Java 1.5 on Debian Sid, which unlike most Debian packages requires manual steps to convert Sun's installer into a Debian package. Fowler walks through using java-package to 'debianize' the Sun JDK binary, handling dependency issues, and configuring alternatives for the Java VM. He notes workarounds for scripts that don't recognize Java 1.5 file names and includes an updated procedure from a reader. The post serves as practical documentation of a frustrating installation process that should be simpler.
key_points: Java isn't in Debian's basic package system, requiring manual conversion of Sun's installer into a .deb package
The java-package tool is the recommended way to debianize Java installations, replacing the obsolete j2se-package
Dependency ordering matters: sun-j2sdk1.5debian must be installed after the main package despite the error message
update-alternatives is needed to switch the default Java VM after installation
money_quotes: Installing most things on Debian is sinfully easy: apt-get install package-name. Sadly Java is an exception since it's not in the basic debian system.
This is what I did. It worked for me, but it may not be the best way or work for you.
However don't panic - if you then install sun-j2sdk1.5debian with apt-get everything installs properly.
posts.key_insight: Even in well-designed package management systems, proprietary software licensing can force users through convoluted manual installation processes.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the common problem of static variables and methods that prevent polymorphic substitution, particularly problematic for testing with service stubs. He presents a refactoring approach that transforms static code into a singleton pattern, enabling test substitution through a loadInstance method. The technique involves converting static data to instance data, moving initialization to a constructor, and delegating static methods to instance methods. While this provides a useful first step, Fowler notes additional work may be needed to handle static initializers that create unwanted dependencies.
key_points: Static variables prevent polymorphism, making it impossible to substitute implementations for testing
The 'Replace Statics With Singleton' refactoring converts static data to instance data while maintaining the same public interface
Adding a loadInstance method enables test substitution by allowing stub subclasses to replace the real implementation
Static initializers that create service instances cause dependency problems even when the service isn't used
Singletons and registries should always be designed with easy substitution and replaceable initialization in mind
money_quotes: One of the big problems with statics (in most languages) is you can't use polymorphism to substitute one implementation with another.
This bits us a lot because we are great fans of testing - and to test well it's important to be able to replace services with a Service Stub.
For testing we might not want to run it against a database at all - a simple stub would just return canned data.
As long as the stub subclasses the AddressBook, we can now test against a stub instead of the real thing.
If you use a singleton (or other form of Registry) make sure that they can be easily substituted and that their initialization can also be easily replaced.
posts.key_insight: Static variables and methods should be refactored into substitutable singletons to enable proper testing through service stubs.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler addresses the misuse of Data Transfer Objects in local contexts, arguing that DTOs are specifically designed for expensive remote calls and should not be used within a single process. He acknowledges that while DTOs serve an important purpose for coarse-grained remote interfaces, using them locally creates unnecessary complexity and painful data mapping overhead. The post clarifies two valid exceptions: presentation-layer mismatches with domain models, and communication between isolated threads via message queues. Fowler warns against speculative distribution boundaries and advocates starting with locally invocable services that can add remotability later if needed.
key_points: DTOs exist specifically to optimize expensive remote calls through coarse-grained interfaces, not for local use
Using DTOs locally is actively harmful: the API becomes harder to use and requires costly data mapping comparable to ORM pain
Designing for speculative future distribution violates Fowler's FirstLaw and adds unnecessary complexity
Valid local DTO-like patterns exist for presentation layer mismatches and inter-thread communication via message queues
Start with domain objects in service layers and add remote facades only when distribution is actually needed
money_quotes: Patterns always have two parts: the how and the when. Not just do you need to know how to implement them, you also have to know when to use them and when to leave them alone.

Not just do you not need them in a local context, they are actually harmful both because a coarse-grained API is more difficult to use and because you have to do all the work moving data from your domain or data source layer into the DTOs.
Don't underestimate the cost of [using DTOs].... It's significant, and it's painful - perhaps second only to the cost and pain of object-relational mapping.
Adding remote boundaries adds complexity.
Start with a locally invocable Service Layer whose method signatures deal in domain objects. Add remotability when you need it (if ever).
posts.key_insight: Patterns require understanding not just how to implement them but when to use them, and DTOs used outside their intended remote-call context create harmful complexity without benefit.

posts.summary: Fowler challenges the common belief that fixed-price contracts are incompatible with agile development. He argues the real impossibility is fixed scope, not fixed price. By fixing price and time while allowing scope to flex, teams can collaborate to deliver the best possible features within constraints. This approach acknowledges uncertainty while still providing contractual predictability, and actually surfaces problems earlier than traditional fixed-scope projects.
key_points: Fixed price contracts are possible in agile; fixed scope contracts are not
Traditional 'fixed price' actually means fixing price, time, AND scope simultaneously, which requires impossible requirements stability
Agile fixed-price works by fixing budget and deadline while collaborating on optimal feature set
Agile projects expect to deliver better products than initially envisioned through learning
When projects go poorly, agile surfaces problems earlier because it doesn't discourage change
money_quotes: Many people believe that you can't do a fixed price contract in an agile project... what it really means is that you can't come up with a fixed scope contract.
Usually when people say fixed price, they mean fixing price, time, and scope. This requires detailed, stable, and accurate requirements.
We have $x to spend and we need a release on 1 Dec. We'll collaborate together to come up with the best set of features to go live with on that date.
The essential difference is that we don't expect things to go according to plan. Instead we expect to deliver a better product than we can currently envision.
Predictive plans tend to discourage change, and thus make it easier to not realize when things are going off course.
posts.key_insight: The perceived conflict between agile and fixed-price contracts dissolves when you realize 'fixed price' should mean fixed budget and timeline with flexible scope, not the impossible trinity of fixing all three.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that fixed scope contracts create a false sense of security for clients. While companies believe fixed scope protects them financially, the real costs extend far beyond the contract price to include opportunity costs and internal resources. Contracting companies exploit this model by deliberately underbidding with plans to profit from change requests. Fowler advocates for agile approaches where scope can flex, noting that Thoughtworks avoids fixed scope contracts for these reasons.
key_points: Fixed scope contracts create a 'mirage' of financial protection that doesn't hold up in practice
The true cost of failed software includes opportunity cost from missing expected business value, not just money paid to contractors
Contracting companies deliberately low-bid fixed scope projects planning to profit on change requests
Some firms actually incentivize sales teams based on how many change requests projects generate
Fixed price contracts can work in an agile manner, but fixing scope is unwise
money_quotes: Many companies like the idea of writing a contract that fixes scope and price because they think it lowers their risk.
You get software written because it has a business value to you - one that's greater than the cost. Otherwise why do it?
If the software isn't satisfactory the financial damage isn't just limited to what you paid for it.
A fixed scope contract only is fixed if the contractor really understands the requirements. But such knowledge is so rare that you can win by banking on its absence.
Such contracting companies deliberately low-bid the fixed price, with the explicit plan of making a profit on change requests.
Some firms actually incentivize their sales and account managers based on how many change requests a project receives.
posts.key_insight: Fixed scope contracts shift risk onto customers rather than away from them, because contractors exploit requirement ambiguity while clients bear both direct costs and opportunity costs when projects fail.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes 'Scope Limbering' - a strategy for transitioning clients from fixed-scope contracts to agile-friendly arrangements. Thoughtworks takes on fixed-scope projects but builds in a large buffer (doubling estimates), then openly tracks how requirement changes consume that buffer without charging the client. When the buffer depletes, the collaborative relationship built over months makes it easy to negotiate additional funding. By the project's end, clients understand that fixed scope is a mirage and agree to flexible arrangements for future work. This approach prioritizes collaboration over confrontation, benefiting both the project and the broader industry's reputation.
key_points: Fixed-scope contracts are fundamentally at odds with agile development because requirements inevitably change
Building a substantial buffer into estimates and absorbing changes transparently builds trust with clients
The real problem with fixed-scope isn't poor estimation but the difficulty of capturing requirements upfront
Low-balling initial bids and profiting on scope changes damages client relationships and the industry's reputation
Collaboration over contract negotiation transforms adversarial dynamics into productive partnerships
money_quotes: A fixed scope contract is totally at odds with agile development
Despite the detailed and heavily reviewed requirements, the requirements changes still came thick and fast
We sought to put the relationship between our companies on a collaborative note rather than a confrontational note

The biggest problem with the fixed scope contract is it immediately pits the client and contractor on opposite sides where they are fighting each other about whether something is a change and who should pay for the change
A tripling of actuals over initial estimates isn't unusual in our industry
It's mainly because it's so hard to get a decent set of requirements
Many delivery companies take advantage of that by low-balling the initial bid and making profit on scope changes
posts.key_insight: Fixed-scope contracts create adversarial relationships, but by absorbing early changes transparently and building trust, you can guide clients to embrace agile's flexibility.

posts.summary: Fowler states his First Law of Distributed Object Design with characteristic brevity: don't distribute your objects. This pithy rule, drawn from his Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture book, warns against the common architectural mistake of splitting objects across network boundaries unnecessarily. The entire post is essentially a memorable one-liner meant to caution architects against over-engineering distributed systems.
key_points: Distribution of objects across network boundaries should be avoided when possible
This principle is foundational enough to be called a 'First Law'
The full reasoning is elaborated in Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture
money_quotes: My First Law of Distributed Object Design: Don't distribute your objects.
posts.key_insight: The complexity and performance costs of distributing objects across network boundaries usually outweigh the benefits, so keep objects local unless you have compelling reasons to distribute them.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on his experience at OOPSLA 2004, covering a range of sessions from a workshop reappraising the Gang of Four design patterns book to Alan Kay's Turing lecture on rediscovering the joy of discovery in programming. He critiques Microsoft Research's product-pitch keynote while praising Kay's vision of empowering users as active participants rather than passive consumers. The conference featured ongoing debates about software development metaphors, with Fowler expressing skepticism about the manufacturing metaphor despite acknowledging good ideas in the 'Software Factories' book. Notable discussions included offshore development fears and hints from Anders Hejlsberg about C#'s future direction toward addressing object-relational impedance mismatch and type inference.
key_points: A workshop voted to remove Factory Method, Bridge, Flyweight, and Interpreter from the GoF patterns, with Singleton receiving a split decision despite its unpopularity
Alan Kay's Turing lecture argued that modern programming education has lost the joy of discovery that characterized the creative explosion of the 1960s
The postmodern programming perspective suggests we should accept 'messy but useful systems' rather than demanding modernist coherence and unifying structures
Fowler is increasingly skeptical of software development metaphors, stating 'the only good metaphor of software development is a dead one'
Anders Hejlsberg indicated C# development is moving toward addressing object-relational impedance mismatch and type inference for combining static and dynamic typing benefits
money_quotes: Most of the others were voted off because people felt they were sufficiently uncommon and that other patterns would probably take their place, sadly we didn't have time to consider new members.

In the Microsoft Research world, we're observers, consumers, secondary participants in an experience someone else has constructed for us. In Alan Kay's vision, we're actors in a world that's actively out there asking us to change it.
In the sixties students were taught that nobody understood what you could and couldn't do with computers, which led to an explosion of creative ideas.
Now programming is more about learning rules about what we've done rather than encouraging people to push boundaries - and as a pattern author I'm more guilty of this than most.
These days I seem to be forming the view that the only good metaphor of software development is a dead one.
A modernist viewpoint hates the messiness of these systems, but a PoMo considers them to be necessary feature of our world.
What was clear is that there is still a palpable fear of what offshore development might lead to in the USA - but I still think much of this is based on the faulty manufacturing metaphor.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's progress is undermined when we treat developers as consumers of established patterns rather than empowering them to push boundaries and discover new possibilities.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces Sergey Dmitriev's Meta Programming System (MPS), an exploratory tool from JetBrains that exemplifies what Dmitriev calls Language Oriented Programming. Fowler reports being impressed after a hands-on demonstration of the system. He situates MPS within a broader emerging movement focused on building software through integrated sets of domain-specific languages. The post briefly notes other players in this space including Intentional Programming and Microsoft's Whitehorse work.
key_points: JetBrains' Sergey Dmitriev has developed the Meta Programming System as an example of Language Oriented Programming
The approach involves building software through closely integrated sets of domain-specific languages
Other notable players in this space include Intentional Programming and Microsoft's Whitehorse work
Fowler signals this as an important area to watch in coming years
money_quotes: I'm very interested in this growing movement, one that looks to build software through closely integrated sets of DomainSpecificLanguages.
It's an area to keep your eye on over the next few years.
posts.key_insight: Language Oriented Programming, which builds software through integrated domain-specific languages, represents an emerging movement worth watching closely.

posts.summary: This is a personal travel post about a dive vacation to Dominica, not a technical article. Fowler and his companion chose Dominica as a new destination alongside their beloved Saba. While Dominica offered good diving along walls, pleasant hiking including a volcanic boiling lake, and the undeveloped character they sought, it didn't capture their hearts the way Saba has. The post concludes that familiarity and Saba's unique peaceful quality make it feel like home.
key_points: Dominica was chosen for being undeveloped without casinos and golf courses
Diving featured walls with abundant sea urchins, requiring good buoyancy control
The boiling lake hike offered a compelling mix of jungle and volcanic terrain
Despite enjoying Dominica, the authors found it couldn't match Saba's special appeal
Familiarity and Saba's small, peaceful character create a sense of home
money_quotes: We picked Dominica because it's a less developed island which avoid the casinos and gold courses that we hate so much.
Every other dive operation reminds how much I like the special qualities of Saba Deep.
We were glad we visited Dominica, but we were at our happiest when we on Saba - it almost felt like home.
posts.key_insight: This is a personal travel reflection rather than a technical post, comparing a new diving destination against a beloved familiar one.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces the concept of 'semantic diff' - a version control capability that would understand the purpose behind code changes rather than just their textual effects.
