Current diff tools can show what changed between versions but cannot recognize that changes resulted from intentional refactoring operations. This limitation makes both code review and merging more difficult than necessary. A semantic diff would identify that an Extract Method refactoring occurred, rather than just showing scattered text modifications.
key_points: Current diff algorithms only understand the effect of changes, not their intent or purpose
Refactoring operations create changes that are semantically unified but appear scattered in traditional diffs
Understanding the purpose of changes would improve both diff readability and merge operations
Tool-assisted refactoring creates an opportunity for capturing semantic intent that current version control misses
money_quotes: A semantic diff would understand the purpose of the change, rather than just the effect.
With current tools they see the change in the program text, but they don't know that I did a refactoring.
Good diff (and merge) algorithms are around for text and binary files. The trouble with these diffs is that they are rather dumb.
posts.key_insight: Version control tools that understood the semantic intent behind changes—like recognizing a refactoring operation—would make code review and merging significantly more effective than treating code as mere text.

posts.summary: Fowler expresses skepticism about using metaphors from other professions to reason about software development, particularly the engineering metaphor which has encouraged harmful separation of design from construction. He acknowledges that metaphors like lean manufacturing can be useful when they help formulate questions about our practices. However, he warns that metaphors become dangerous when used to justify answers directly. The key distinction is using analogies to prompt inquiry versus using them as logical proof for decisions.
key_points: The engineering metaphor has damaged software development by encouraging separation of design from construction
Metaphors are useful for formulating questions but dangerous when used to justify answers
Lean manufacturing's inventory elimination concept helpfully prompts us to question up-front documentation practices
Rationale for decisions must come from thinking about software development itself, not purely from analogical reasoning
Comparing to other activities provides valuable alternative perspectives on our practices
money_quotes: Comparing to another activity is useful if it helps you formulate questions, it's dangerous when you use it to justify answers.
I believe the engineering metaphor has done our profession damage - in that it has encouraged the notion of separating design from construction.
The rationale for doing so must come from thinking about the software development process, not from purely reasoning by analogy.
posts.key_insight: Metaphors from other professions should prompt questions about software development practices, not serve as justifications for answers.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the common objection that certain specialties (security, UI design, databases) cannot be done incrementally and must be planned upfront. He argues that while he may lack expertise in specific fields, history has repeatedly proven such claims wrong. The key challenge is that incremental design requires 'enabling practices' to prevent chaos—testing, continuous integration, and refactoring for software; data migration for databases. Without these practices, incremental approaches will fail, but the benefits of incrementalism—especially risk management and handling volatile requirements—make it worth experimenting to find the right enabling practices for each domain.
key_points: Claims that 'you can't do X incrementally' have repeatedly been proven wrong across domains like application design and database design
Incremental design without enabling practices leads to messy, entropic designs—you need mechanisms that make designs converge toward order
Each specialty requires discovering its own enabling practices, which may differ from software's testing/CI/refactoring trio
Phased upfront design fails frequently and handles volatile requirements poorly
Risk management is the primary argument for incrementalism—it reduces vulnerability to late-stage surprises and schedule slippage
money_quotes: I've seen enough cases where people have said 'you can't use incremental design for x' only to find you can.
To make incremental design work you need something that makes the design converge into order.
Until you find a good set of enabling practices, incremental design is thin ice.
Phased up-front design approaches fail far too frequently—furthermore they do a poor job in the chance of volatile requirements.
Without trying out your designs, you are too vulnerable to things not working out the way you think they would.
posts.key_insight: Incremental design can likely work in any domain, but only if you discover the right enabling practices that prevent entropy and make the design converge toward order.

posts.summary: Fowler reports on an informal voting exercise at an enterprise software workshop where participants proposed and voted on layering principles. Using dot voting with positive and negative votes, the group evaluated common design maxims. Strong consensus emerged around separation of concerns, loose coupling, and keeping UI logic out of business layers. The exercise revealed that some commonly heard principles (like separating teams by layer) were actually widely disliked, while some rarely-practiced principles (like abstracting technological services) had strong support. Fowler acknowledges the informal nature but suggests the results could provoke useful discussion.
key_points: Classic principles like separation of concerns, loose coupling/high cohesion, and no business logic in UI layers received near-unanimous support
The principle of separating development teams by layer was heavily voted down (1/22), despite being commonly practiced
'Distribute at layer boundaries' was strongly rejected, reinforcing that layers are logical, not physical deployment units
Some well-supported principles like abstracting technological services from business logic are rarely implemented in practice
The group showed mixed feelings about strict adjacent-layer-only interaction and service layer wrapping
money_quotes: Layers are a logical artifact that does not imply distribution between layers.
A layer should be wary of exposing lower layers to upper layers.
Business layer only uses abstractions of technological services.
Every layer should have a secret.
Separate development teams by layer.
Rethrow exceptions at layer boundaries.
posts.key_insight: The gap between principles developers espouse and those they actually practice reveals interesting disconnects—some widely heard advice is actually unpopular, while some strongly supported principles go unimplemented.

posts.summary: Fowler shares an insider look at Thoughtworks in early 2005, a company he joined because he found it special and unusual. The firm had grown 50% to $75 million in revenue and nearly 600 employees, though growth exceeded his comfort level. He highlights their rigorous hiring standards targeting the top 0.5-1% of developers, preferring false negatives over bad hires. The post emphasizes their commitment to agile principles, integrated testing practices, and the goal of becoming a fully employee-owned company. Fowler acknowledges ongoing challenges with project selection and the need to ensure work remains fulfilling beyond just profitable.
key_points: Thoughtworks maintains extremely selective hiring (top 0.5-1% of developers), accepting false negatives over hiring mistakes
The company integrates testing throughout development at multiple levels (unit, acceptance) and across roles (programmers, analysts)
Growth of 50% annually, while successful, exceeds Fowler's comfort level
A key organizational goal is becoming fully employee-owned and controlled
Project selection remains a challenge - profitable work isn't always fulfilling work
money_quotes: The reason I gave up my successful life as an independent consultant was because I felt Thoughtworks was a special and unusual company.
I'm sure the recruiting system produces too many false negatives (people we turn down who we should have hired), but I'd still rather take that than the alternative (hiring people we shouldn't).
We tend to hire people who really enjoy delivering applications that matter - and still too many projects throw up all sorts of frustrations that get in the way.
Part of the point of Thoughtworks is to do enjoyable and fulfilling work - money isn't the primary reason most of us are here.
There is no such thing as Thoughtworks's corporate opinion, what we have is the opinion of our employees.
It's a simple slogan, but not easy to sort out the mechanics for a company of our size and international scope.
posts.key_insight: A technology consultancy's long-term success depends not just on hiring elite talent but on selecting projects that keep that talent engaged and fulfilled.

posts.summary: Dot voting is a simple, efficient technique for gathering group input on rankings or selections during meetings and workshops. The method involves posting options on a wall, giving each participant a fixed number of votes to place as dots next to their preferred items, and tallying results. Participants can vote in any order and concentrate multiple votes on single items to show strong preferences. Fowler has used this technique for various purposes including prioritizing discussion topics and even choosing terminology for the Agile Manifesto.
key_points: Dot voting enables quick, parallel voting without requiring people to take turns
Allowing multiple votes on one item lets participants express intensity of preference
The technique is versatile - useful for prioritization, selection, and ranking decisions
Physical visibility (wall-mounted lists) creates transparency in the voting process
money_quotes: From time to time during a meeting or workshop, it's good to get votes on a number things in order to rank or choose a subset.
Don't make people take turns or anything - just say something like 'you get five votes each'.
I also let people put more than one vote on one item if they want to show a strong preference.
posts.key_insight: Effective group decision-making can be achieved through simple, low-ceremony techniques that enable parallel participation and allow expression of preference intensity.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler examines the ball and socket notation introduced in UML 2 for showing required and provided interfaces. He traces its origins from Microsoft's 'lollipop' notation and explains how the intuitive 'mated' ball-and-socket diagram was actually ruled invalid by the UML committee, restricted only to Composite Structure Diagrams. Fowler compares alternative notations including UML 1's dependency arrows and Rumbaugh's style, finding each suited to different complexity levels. Despite the technical invalidity, he expresses preference for the mated notation's clarity and his willingness to break strict UML rules when it communicates intent more effectively.
key_points: The socket notation in UML 2 shows interfaces a class requires, complementing the lollipop notation for interfaces a class implements
The UML committee ruled that mated ball-and-socket notation is invalid outside of Composite Structure Diagrams, despite its intuitive appeal
UML 1's dependency notation and Rumbaugh's explicit interface style are valid alternatives, each with different strengths
Rumbaugh's style excels when showing multiple classes implementing a single required interface
Fowler advocates pragmatism over strict UML compliance when notation clarity is at stake
money_quotes: Since I'm a reasonably hip dude I can get it from iTunes - but since I'm aware that there are other possibilities I use an interface so that I can easily substitute another implementation.
With the ball and socket so close to each other, it only seems natural that they should mate.
The UML committee has decided this was an error and you shouldn't be able to use the ball and socket notation in this way.
For an example like this, I think I prefer the UML 1 style, since there's less notation to convey the same meaning.

Despite the technical invalidity of the mated ball and socket notation, I rather like it as a way to show how interfaces and implementations connect, so am again inclined to break strict UML rules to use it.
posts.key_insight: Good notation should prioritize clear communication of intent over strict adherence to specification rules, even in standardized modeling languages like UML.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on the value of rotation practices at Thoughtworks, both geographic (spending 6-18 months in different countries) and project-based. Geographic rotation widens perspectives and prevents the company from fragmenting into national fiefs, while project rotation brings fresh ideas to teams and spreads knowledge across the organization. He acknowledges the challenges, particularly for people with families and clients who resist losing known team members. The key insight is that knowledge management and reuse are fundamentally human issues, and rotation addresses this by facilitating organic knowledge transfer rather than relying on technology or process.
key_points: Geographic rotation (6-18 months abroad) significantly widens employees' perspectives and prevents organizational fragmentation into national silos
Project rotation consistently reinvigorates teams by bringing fresh ideas and provides variety for rotating individuals
Clients often resist rotation because they fear losing proven talent for unproven newcomers, a reasonable concern given typical consulting practices
Knowledge management and reuse are primarily human issues, not matters of technology or process
Local optimization often impedes global performance, so employees should think about company-wide success rather than office-level success
money_quotes: Living a good length of time in a different country does a huge amount to widen people's perspective of the world.
We are very much of the lean belief that local optimization often gets in the way of global performance.
Time and time again I've listened to project teams talk about how bringing in new people with fresh ideas has invigorated the team.
My mantra was 'pick the people not the company'.
It's long been my view that knowledge management and reuse are primarily human issues - not a matter to technology or process.
Rotation is a practice which we've seen to promote knowledge transfer and reuse, and I think its efficacy is due to the fact that it addresses the human element.
posts.key_insight: Knowledge management and skill propagation are fundamentally human challenges best addressed through rotation practices that move people between projects and geographies, rather than through technology or formal processes.

posts.summary: Microsoft has launched PatternShare, a community resource that aggregates pattern summaries from various influential pattern authors. The platform aims to facilitate discussion and exploration of how different software patterns interconnect. Ward Cunningham, a foundational figure in the patterns community, led much of the development effort. The resource includes contributions from major pattern authors including Fowler, the Gang of Four, POSA authors, Hohpe/Woolf, Evans, and Microsoft.
key_points: PatternShare is a Microsoft-backed community platform for software patterns
The platform aggregates patterns from multiple authoritative sources to show interconnections
Ward Cunningham, with his deep pattern heritage, led the initiative
Contributors include the most influential names in software patterns literature
money_quotes: The idea is to bring together pattern summaries from many pattern authors and provide a platform for discussion and further exploration of the interconnections between them.
Much of the work was led by Ward Cunningham, whose pattern lineage is second to none.
posts.key_insight: Aggregating patterns from multiple authoritative sources into a shared platform enables better understanding of how different patterns interconnect and relate to each other.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler identifies a failure mode in evolutionary design he calls 'abundant mutation,' where teams create multiple competing solutions to the same problem without consolidating around the best approach. He observed this in two projects: a large team where sub-teams independently created different frameworks, and a smaller team with high rotation where newcomers repeatedly abandoned and restarted solutions. While both projects maintained good overall design health due to automated testing, the duplication created unnecessary complexity. Fowler concludes that evolutionary design requires active technical leadership to prune inferior approaches, comparing it to selective breeding rather than pure natural selection.
key_points: Evolutionary design can fail when competing solutions proliferate without being killed off or consolidated
Large teams risk abundant mutation through parallel sub-teams solving common problems differently
High team rotation causes mutation as newcomers abandon partially-complete solutions for new approaches
Despite the mutation problem, automated testing kept both projects healthier than organizational norms
The solution is active design leadership that encourages good traits and discourages poor ones
money_quotes: Mutation is a good and necessary thing, but like many good things you can get too much of it.
Not just was the duplication of effort wasteful, it also made the software more complex than it needed to be.
You might think of this as a case where the environment hadn't killed off the weaker mutations.
Ideally when a competing design appears it either dies quickly or kills off the existing design.
Rather than an evolutionary metaphor, you might think of it more like a breeder encouraging good traits and discouraging poor ones.
Evolutionary design requires attention, skill, and leadership. It's just a different sort of leadership than many commonly think.
posts.key_insight: Evolutionary design without active technical leadership to prune competing solutions leads to wasteful duplication and unnecessary complexity.

posts.summary: Fowler examines whether XP's emphasis on evolutionary design means abandoning traditional planned design. He argues that XP's enabling practices—testing, continuous integration, and refactoring—flatten the change curve enough to make evolutionary design viable, something impossible under traditional 'code and fix' approaches. The key insight is that design hasn't died but transformed: instead of big upfront design, XP enables a balance where refactoring and planning coexist. Fowler acknowledges this requires discipline, technical skill, and someone with 'the will to design' to prevent entropy.
key_points: Evolutionary design only works when enabled by XP practices like testing, continuous integration, and refactoring—without these, it devolves into code and fix chaos
YAGNI (You Aren't Going to Need It) is only valid advice when you have the enabling practices to change things cheaply later
The role of architect shifts from making all decisions to coaching developers and ensuring design quality is maintained
Simplicity is surprisingly hard to define—eliminating duplication is clear, but 'revealing intention' depends on the reader's experience level
Design reversibility is crucial: good designers think about how to defer decisions or make them easy to change later
money_quotes: Design is there to enable you to keep changing the software easily in the long term.
The fundamental assumption underlying XP is that it is possible to flatten the change curve enough to make evolutionary design work.
In XP 'clever code' is a term of abuse.
It's easier to refactor over-design than it is to refactor no design.
The best UML diagrams are not artifacts.
In XP technical leadership is shown by teaching the programmers and helping them make decisions.
If he doesn't trust his programmers why does he hire them?
In order to work, evolutionary design needs a force that drives it to converge. This force can only come from people.
posts.key_insight: Evolutionary design isn't the absence of design—it's design enabled by practices that make change cheap, requiring even more skill and discipline than planned design.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes a dangerous pattern where failover mechanisms, designed to improve reliability, can actually cascade failures across an entire cluster. When a request triggers a bug that crashes a server, the failover system dutifully moves that same toxic request to another server, which also crashes. This can create a loop where servers repeatedly crash and reboot while passing the deadly request between them. The solution is to limit failover attempts for any single request and consider using heterogeneous infrastructure to prevent the same bug from affecting all servers.
key_points: Failover mechanisms can amplify failures when a request itself is the cause of the crash
A single bad request can systematically take down every server in a cluster through the failover chain
Systems should track and limit how many times a request has been failed over
Using diverse infrastructure (different app servers, operating systems, hardware) reduces the risk of a single bug affecting all nodes
money_quotes: So when the failover kicks in, the deadly requests gets moved to another server which it can then bring down in turn.
Get the timing just right and by the time the first server has rebooted, it will be ready to receive that request again.
It's good to failover, but you don't want your farm to do it too often.
If you're running a Java application, consider using a mix of different app servers, operating systems, and hardware.
posts.key_insight: Reliability mechanisms like failover can become vectors for cascading failure when they blindly retry the very operations that caused the original crash.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler briefly notes the continued absence of a comprehensive book on UML activity diagrams, a gap he had previously identified in his book UML Distilled. He recommends Conrad Bock's articles on UML 2.0 as a valuable resource that covers activity diagrams in greater depth. Fowler adds credibility to this recommendation by noting that Bock was one of the leaders of the activity diagram work in UML 2.
key_points: There remains no good comprehensive book specifically teaching UML activity diagrams
Conrad Bock's UML 2.0 articles provide deeper coverage of activity diagrams than UML Distilled
Conrad Bock has authoritative expertise as a leader of the UML 2 activity diagram specification work
money_quotes: I bemoaned the fact that there isn't a good book on teaching UML's activity diagrams. There still isn't.
posts.key_insight: For in-depth understanding of UML activity diagrams, practitioners should turn to Conrad Bock's articles rather than waiting for a comprehensive book that may never materialize.

posts.summary: This is a brief informational post explaining the feed options available for Martin Fowler's website. He offers an updates feed for site changes and a bliki feed that combines blog posts with site updates. The post clarifies that despite using 'RSS Feed' terminology, the feeds are actually Atom format. He also mentions the ThoughtBlogs aggregated feed and notes that older RSS 0.91 feeds are deprecated.
key_points: Two main feeds available: updates-only and bliki (which includes both blog posts and updates)
Feeds use Atom format despite common RSS terminology
ThoughtBlogs feed syndicates content from multiple ThoughtWorks bloggers including Fowler
Legacy RSS 0.91 feeds are deprecated but still functional
money_quotes: Although I've used the term RSS Feed here, they are actually Atom feeds.
This is the one to pick if you want to know about site updates but don't care to read my blog.
I will remove them if I ever have to put any effort to keep them running.
posts.key_insight: A practical housekeeping post explaining subscription options, notable mainly for its pragmatic approach to legacy format deprecation.

posts.summary: Fowler surveys the landscape of pattern catalogs available for enterprise software development, organizing them by their focus areas. He distinguishes between Enterprise Application Architecture (building single applications) and Enterprise Architecture (a broader discipline), noting this confusion is surprisingly common. The catalogs he lists cover enterprise application architecture, enterprise integration, and domain logic, with authors from different backgrounds contributing platform-specific and platform-independent perspectives. Fowler emphasizes that while there's no formal organization connecting these pattern authors, they maintain strong informal connections and frequently review each other's work. He positions asynchronous messaging as one of the most effective integration approaches and highlights domain logic as an often-forgotten yet critical aspect of enterprise applications.
key_points: Pattern catalogs for enterprise software can be organized around three main aspects: enterprise application architecture, enterprise integration, and domain logic

Enterprise Application Architecture and Enterprise Architecture are frequently confused but are quite different disciplines - the former concerns building single applications while the latter is broader
Asynchronous messaging is increasingly viewed as one of the most effective ways to integrate disparate enterprise applications
Domain logic (business rules, validations, calculations) is one of the most important yet often forgotten aspects of enterprise applications
The pattern authors maintain informal but strong connections, frequently reviewing each other's work despite no formal organization
money_quotes: I've increasingly come to the view that integration through asynchronous messaging is one of the most effective ways to integrate disparate enterprise applications.
It's surprisingly common that people confuse the term Enterprise Architecture with Enterprise Application Architecture. That second A-word is all important.
One of the most important, yet often forgot, aspects of enterprise applications is the domain logic.
Building an object-oriented Domain Model is a popular approach to organizing domain logic. It works particularly well with complex domains. It's downside is that it is difficult to do well.

We've often wondered if we should set up some more organized group, but haven't really summoned up enough energy around it to actually make anything happen. Just writing our own work is quite hard enough!
posts.key_insight: Enterprise software patterns naturally organize around three distinct concerns—application architecture, integration, and domain logic—each requiring different thinking and approaches.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on his evolving views on static versus dynamic typing, having moved from preferring static typing to appreciating dynamic languages like Smalltalk and Ruby. He argues that with self-testing code, static typing provides little additional bug-catching value since tests find far more issues. While acknowledging static typing's benefits for IDE tooling and code comprehension, he notes that dynamic languages offer an ineffable 'flow' that makes programming more enjoyable and productive, even with inferior tooling.
key_points: Self-testing code catches most bugs that static types would find, making static typing's bug-prevention benefit largely redundant
Static typing enables superior IDE features like auto-completion and refactoring support
Dynamic typing's advantages are hard to articulate but create a better programming 'flow'
Naming conventions and excellent tooling (as in Smalltalk) can mitigate dynamic typing's readability challenges
Programmer enjoyment has business value because motivation significantly impacts productivity
money_quotes: This is one of those emotive topics where people seem driven to debate rather than listen.
In the presence of SelfTestingCode, most bugs that static types would have were found just as easily by the tests.
Since the tests found much more than type errors, you needed them in either a static or dynamically typed language, so having the static typing gave you little gain.
One of the most frustrating things about the static/dynamic typing debate is that it's very hard to put into words the advantages of working in a dynamically typed language.
Somehow things just seem to flow better when you're programming in that environment.
Working with languages such as Java and C# I always feel the need to skip over text in order to understand what's going on.
There's business value in fun - after all motivation is a major factor in programmer productivity.
posts.key_insight: When you have comprehensive tests, static typing's bug-prevention value diminishes significantly, leaving the debate to hinge on tooling support versus the intangible but real productivity benefits of dynamic language 'flow'.

posts.summary: This brief dictionary-style entry introduces the term 'detestable' as a portmanteau meaning software that cannot be tested. Fowler attributes the coinage to an unnamed member of the Sydney XP group. The post uses wordplay to highlight that untestable software is, by implication, worthy of contempt. It's a characteristically concise Fowler 'bliki' entry that packages a significant software quality principle into a memorable term.
key_points: Untestable software deserves its own pejorative term
The pun equates 'not testable' with 'deserving hatred'
Good software design inherently supports testability
money_quotes: Detestable (adjective): software that isn't testable.
posts.key_insight: Software that cannot be tested is software that deserves contempt—testability is not optional, it's a fundamental quality attribute.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that open source development functions as a research community where developers explore ideas through code rather than academic papers. He observes that the most interesting innovations in enterprise Java have emerged from open source projects. At Thoughtworks, this approach aligns with their philosophy of open intellectual property, where building things serves as a form of publishing. Fowler hopes to eventually give employees dedicated work time for this R&D activity, rather than relegating it to spare time.
key_points: Open source projects serve as a research mechanism where developers explore ideas through programming rather than traditional academic publication
The enterprise Java ecosystem's most significant innovations (Hibernate, JUnit, Spring, etc.) have emerged from open source rather than commercial development
Projects that fade away before reaching version 1.0 are not failures but natural outcomes of an R&D process where exploration matters more than completion rates
Success in R&D should be measured by the quality and impact of successful products, not by the proportion of ideas that become products
money_quotes: That's a notion that sounds strange if you believe that design and programming are separated, but makes a lot of sense if you accept that they are tied together.
We don't have the people who are interested in writing lots of papers for academic conferences - we tend to have people who want to build things. So the open source world is their way of publishing.
A successful R&D isn't measured by what proportion of their ideas turn into products, but rather by how many great products they generate and how great they are.
Someone who starts three projects and turns them all into mediocre products isn't as good as someone who starts a dozen projects and turns only one of them into a killer app.
posts.key_insight: Open source development is fundamentally a research activity where programming serves as the medium for exploring and publishing ideas, and abandoned projects are natural byproducts of healthy R&D rather than failures.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that the most important factor in software development success is hiring high-ability people, as top developers are far more productive than average ones—enough to justify significantly higher salaries. Beyond just ability, he emphasizes that collaborativeness matters equally: pleasant, cooperative people create better working environments. He reflects on his early career decision to avoid working with 'able jerks' and notes that most large companies struggle to create environments where talented people thrive because it requires doing things that don't make sense to traditional managers and accountants.
key_points: A few highly capable developers will produce software more cheaply than many mediocre ones, even at higher individual salaries
Building teams of top performers is hard but achievable—the common belief that 'you can't have a team of A players' is defeatist, not factual
Creating environments where talented people thrive requires unconventional decisions that traditional management often resists
Collaborativeness is as important as ability—pleasant colleagues matter as much as capable ones
The author's personal rule: never work with unpleasant people, regardless of their capability
money_quotes: The critical element in a software development effort are the people you have doing the work.
A few high ability (and expensive) people will be much more productive than many low ability (cheap) developers.
Many just give up saying 'you can't have a team of A players' as if there's some law of nature that prevents it.
You have to create an environment where high ability geeks are comfortable. This involves doing many things that don't make sense to many accountants and managers.
It's not just about ability - it's also about collaborativeness.
I decided then that I wouldn't work with unpleasant people, however capable they might be. I wouldn't fight them, just avoid them.
Whatever the advantages it would bring to hang around with able jerks, it just wasn't worth the hassle.
posts.key_insight: Building great software requires both hiring exceptional talent and cultivating a collaborative culture—ability without pleasantness isn't worth the cost.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that agile methods elevate code to be the primary documentation of a software system, though not the only documentation. He refutes the misconception that this means code should be the sole documentation. The rationale is that code is the only artifact sufficiently detailed and precise to serve this role. However, this principle demands that programmers actively invest effort in making code clear and readable. Fowler attributes poor code readability partly to programmers not treating code seriously as documentation, and advocates for code review, learning programming style, and adapting personal preferences to team needs.
key_points: Code being primary documentation doesn't mean it's the only documentation - supplementary docs are still needed
Code is uniquely qualified as primary documentation because it's the only sufficiently detailed and precise artifact
Most codebases are poor documentation not because code is inherently unclear, but because programmers don't treat clarity as a priority
Code review and feedback from others is essential for achieving clarity, similar to how authors need editors
Professional programmers should adapt their personal coding style to match team conventions for collective readability
money_quotes: Saying that code is documentation isn't saying that a particular code base is good documentation.
Like any documentation, code can be clear or it can be gibberish.
I think part of the reason that code is often so hard to read is because people aren't taking it seriously as documentation.
If there's no will to make code clear, then there's little chance it will spring into clarity all by itself.
There's nothing more important to clear code than getting feedback from others about what is or isn't easy to understand.
A professional programmer is prepared to bend her personal style to reflect the needs of the team.
You can program in your own style on your personal projects, but anything you do in a team should follow the needs of that team.
posts.key_insight: Code's role as primary documentation is not an excuse for poor readability but rather a mandate that programmers must actively invest in making code clear, treating it with the same editorial rigor as any published writing.

posts.summary: Fowler advises against directly accessing system routines for current date/time in code. Instead, developers should create a wrapper that allows the current date/time to be overridden with specific values. This abstraction layer is essential for simplifying testing by making time-dependent code deterministic and controllable.
key_points: Direct system calls for date/time should be avoided in application code
A wrapper abstraction allows overriding the current time with specific values
This pattern is primarily motivated by testing concerns
Deterministic time control enables reliable test scenarios
money_quotes: If you need to get the current date or time in your code, don't access the system routines for that data directly.
Put some form of wrapper around it that allows you to override it by setting the 'current date/time' to a particular value.
This is important to simplify testing.
posts.key_insight: Wrapping system time access enables testable code by allowing controlled, deterministic time values during testing.

posts.summary: Fowler recounts debugging an intermittent test failure where tests passed in Ant but failed in IntelliJ, and passed after restarting the IDE. The root cause was shared static data between tests—specifically, a ClockWrapper that wasn't being reinitialized in all tests. Depending on test execution order, some tests would fail because they inherited stale state from previous tests. The key lesson is that test isolation is critical, and intermittent failures almost always stem from shared, improperly initialized data between tests.
key_points: Intermittent test failures that vary between runs or test suites almost always indicate shared data that isn't properly reinitialized
Even with JUnit's strong isolation via new instance per test, static data can still cause coupling between tests
The instinct to blame tools (IDE, compiler) is usually wrong—the bug is in your code
Test isolation requires creating fresh data for each test, though this trades off against test speed
When debugging intermittent failures, trace any shared or static data and verify it's fully initialized in each test
money_quotes: The bug's always in your code, not the compiler.

If tests sometimes pass and sometimes fail on a run without any code changes, or tests pass when run in some suites but fail when run in others; nine times out of eight the reason is that there is some shared data between tests that isn't being properly reinitialized.
The result is an intermittent failure - always the worst because you can't reliably reproduce it.
Do everything you can to keep your test data isolated.
Try to create fresh data every time (although there is a trade-off here with getting fast test runs).
The more you practice good test isolation, the less chance you have of running into this kind of problem.
posts.key_insight: Intermittent test failures are almost always caused by shared state between tests that isn't properly initialized, making test isolation the fundamental discipline for reliable test suites.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on how IntelliJ IDEA fundamentally changed his expectations for development tools when he first encountered it in late 2000. While initially drawn to its refactoring support, he was more impressed by its use of an in-memory parse tree to provide intelligent code completion and suggestions. The tool was so compelling that it unified Thoughtworks developers who had previously fought over IDE preferences. Fowler considers IntelliJ the first major leap in IDE capability since Smalltalk, marking an inflection point that defined a new era of development tools.
key_points: IntelliJ's key innovation was maintaining a parse tree in memory during editing, enabling intelligent assistance beyond simple text manipulation
The tool converted static typing from a compiler punishment into a helpful editing aid through type-aware completion
IntelliJ achieved something remarkable: unifying opinionated developers at Thoughtworks who previously required 'tear-gas to control the riots' over IDE choices
Eclipse is acknowledged as good but framed as a 'post-IntelliJ IDE' - a compliment that positions IntelliJ as the defining standard
Fowler had long championed Smalltalk's IDE superiority; IntelliJ was the first tool to make him stop that comparison
money_quotes: Suddenly static typing isn't just something the compiler spanks you with - now the editor can give you type correct method completion.
If anyone suggested a standard IDE for Thoughtworks projects we needed tear-gas to control the riots.
Within six months nearly everyone was using IntelliJ. Voluntarily and eagerly.
For me IntelliJ was first leap forwards in IDEs since Smalltalk.
I complement Eclipse by calling it a post-IntelliJ IDE.
Whatever happens in the future, I'll always see IntelliJ as the inflection point of IDEs, a milestone tool.
posts.key_insight: IntelliJ represented a paradigm shift in development tools by using real-time code understanding to transform static typing from a constraint into an intelligent assistant.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the common complaint that agile methods lack rigorous definition, making it impossible to verify if teams are truly practicing agile or to create standardized curricula. He argues this lack of rigor is not a flaw but an essential feature of agile philosophy. Because software development contexts vary enormously and are fundamentally human-oriented, agile methods expect teams to adapt and regularly change their processes. This makes formal conformance testing impossible by design, which Fowler acknowledges is frustrating but unavoidable—like getting wet while swimming.
key_points: The lack of rigorous definition in agile is intentional, not a deficiency—it reflects agile's core philosophy
Software development's varied contexts and human-centric nature make prescriptive processes ineffective
Agile expects teams to decide and continuously evolve their own processes, making conformance testing contradictory to agile principles
Determining whether a team is 'really doing agile' requires experienced practitioners and still invites disagreement
Our profession's comfort with programming computers leads us to mistakenly try programming human systems the same way
money_quotes: This lack of rigorousness is part of the defining nature of agile methods, part of its core philosophy.
It's not effective to try and bind software development to a rigorous process, because that's ignoring the essential nature of the primary (human) components that will execute that process.
It's probably because our profession is to work with computers is what leads us to want to program human systems the same way that we program computers.
Agile methods fundamentally expect teams to decide what process to follow and furthermore expect teams to actively and regularly change their process.
Any attempt to define a rigorous process that can be tested for conformance runs contrary to this philosophy.
There is a sense of 'I know it when I see it', but it requires an experienced practitioner to have that sense.
It's an unfortunate consequence of the activity itself, just as getting wet is an unavoidable consequence of swimming.
posts.key_insight: Agile's lack of rigorous definition is not a bug to be fixed but an essential feature that acknowledges the irreducibly human and variable nature of software development.

posts.summary: This article explores different approaches to making Domain Specific Languages executable through code generation. Fowler walks through progressively more sophisticated techniques: a simple single-pass parser that directly configures a framework, a two-pass approach that builds an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) as an intermediate representation, and finally template-based generation using Velocity. The key insight is that separating parsing from generation via an AST enables multiple input formats (custom syntax, XML) to share the same generator, and that templates allow generating optimized compile-time code rather than runtime configuration.
key_points: A single-pass parser that directly configures the target framework is quick and simple for basic cases
Separating parsing from generation via an Abstract Syntax Tree allows multiple concrete syntaxes (custom text, XML) to share the same generator
Templates enable generating compile-time code with direct method calls rather than runtime reflection-based configuration
Parser generator tools become worthwhile for anything more complex than trivial languages
The AST takes a central role in language workbenches, serving as the bridge between multiple human-readable representations and code generation
money_quotes: The real advantage in the AST lies when we want to read or write multiple formats.

In a language workbench, the abstract representation takes a much more central role than it does in conventional programming - together with the idea that you can have multiple human readable forms for the same abstract representation.
You don't have to get much more complex than this example to make these tools worthwhile.

When I'm doing templating I usually like to first write a hard coded class for a single case, get that class working and debugged, and then (as gradually as I can) replace the hard coded elements with templated elements.
Indeed the lisp style of language oriented programming is more like this, you write a generator that generates lisp code that's executed at run time. This is where lisp's macro capabilities shine.
posts.key_insight: The Abstract Syntax Tree serves as a crucial decoupling layer that enables multiple input syntaxes and output generators to be combined independently, which is fundamental to how language workbenches operate.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the relationship between language workbenches and Model Driven Architecture (MDA), finding that MDA standards are largely unsuitable for language workbench development. He identifies three incompatible camps within MDA: UML PIM, MOF, and Executable UML, with only the first two relevant to language-oriented programming. While MOF shows some promise as an interchange mechanism for DSL schemas, both MDA camps fail to address the critical components of editors and generators. Fowler concludes that MDA standards were designed for different purposes and that premature standardization could harm the still-immature language workbench field.
key_points: MDA encompasses three 'mutually disdainful camps' with incompatible visions: UML PIM, MOF, and Executable UML, creating confusion about what MDA actually means
UML is too complex and semantically incoherent to serve as a foundation for language workbenches - it's 'like building a speedboat out of a truck because they both have a steering wheel'
MOF is a better fit than UML for DSL schemas but still lacks support for editors and generators, two of the three essential language workbench components
Microsoft, Intentional Software, and MPS all ignore MDA standards not out of malice but because these standards don't fit their needs
Model Driven Development practitioners often underestimate the importance of generators, treating code generation as a 'trivial implementation issue' when it actually defines DSL semantics
money_quotes: Steve Cook described the variation as falling into three mutually disdainful camps
UML proponents proud to say that UML has no semantics
It's rather like saying we should build a speedboat out of a truck because they both have a steering wheel
In my view you define standards once you've figured out common elements of working technologies. Language workbenches are too immature to be ready for standards yet
The tendency to play down generators is a major reason why so many programmers don't take modelers seriously
I think the UML is too complex and is too semantically incoherent to act as a serious base for future work
First you need to figure out how to do something, then you should figure out what and how to standardize
posts.key_insight: Standards should follow working technology, not precede it - MDA's premature standardization makes it unsuitable for language workbenches, which need to mature before standardization can be meaningful.

posts.summary: This article demonstrates how to build a Domain Specific Language using JetBrains' Meta Programming System (MPS), a language workbench tool. Fowler walks through creating an 'Agreement DSL' that handles how a utility company reacts to customer events based on service plans. The article covers three core aspects: defining the schema (data model), building projectional editors, and creating code generators. Unlike traditional text-based DSLs, MPS uses projectional editing where users manipulate an abstract representation rather than raw text, enabling rich editor support and symbolic integration between languages. Fowler emphasizes that while the example resembles traditional textual DSLs, the real potential of language workbenches lies in creating entirely new kinds of DSLs we haven't yet imagined.
key_points: Language workbenches use projectional editing - manipulating abstract representations that look like text but aren't, enabling intelligent editor support and context-aware assistance
DSLs can be composed from separate languages (like embedding Excel-style formulas) while maintaining seamless symbolic integration
A language workbench requires defining three interconnected elements: schema (structure), editor (projection), and generator (code output)
Unlike traditional structured editors, modern language workbenches must handle invalid/incomplete input gracefully, allowing programmers to work naturally
The real promise of language workbenches isn't recreating textual DSLs but enabling entirely new kinds of domain languages we can't yet envision
money_quotes: The first thing to note about these fragments is that they are very domain oriented and readable in terms of the domain. Although the COBOL inference hangs over me, I'd venture to say they are readable to a non-programmer domain expert.
It's much harder to see the domain logic in the Java, because Java's grammar does get in the way. This is particularly so for a non-programmer.

You may have noticed that the DSL examples used screen shots rather than text - and that's because although the DSLs look like text they aren't really text. Instead they are projections of the underlying abstract representation.

One of the past problems with these kind of intelligent, or structured, editors is that they couldn't deal with incorrect input. Each bit of input needs to be correct before you move on. Such a requirement is a big usability problem.
This kind of robust behavior in the face of wanton invalidity is an important feature of language workbenches.
Despite the modeling crowd's obsession with saying 'a picture is worth a thousand words', textual representations are still very useful.
I think the really interesting DSLs will actually be quite different. But part of the nature of this work is that we can't really see what they'll look like yet.
posts.key_insight: Language workbenches enable creating domain-specific languages where the visible notation is a projection of an underlying abstract representation, allowing for intelligent editing, language composition, and the potential for entirely new forms of programming that we haven't yet conceived.

posts.summary: This post serves as a companion reading list to Fowler's article on Language Workbenches, collecting resources on various tools and approaches in this emerging field. It covers Intentional Software's work, JetBrains' Meta Programming System, Microsoft's Software Factories initiative, and MDA-based approaches. The post also highlights community contributions including Java ports of examples and Lisp implementations. Fowler signals his ongoing interest in tracking developments in Language Oriented Programming.
key_points: Intentional Software was emerging from stealth mode with promising work on language workbenches
JetBrains' MPS represented a strong vision for Language Oriented Programming with active community support
Microsoft's Software Factories initiative generated significant discussion, including debates about MDA usage
Multiple approaches existed including OMG standards-based tools like Xactium
Community contributions were expanding the practical examples across different languages
money_quotes: For the last few years Intentional Software has been hiding under some deep rocks. They are beginning to peep out now.
Tracing through the Software Factory blogs will also give you some insights as to the debates over the use of MDA, since much of that discussion has been around the Microsoft work.
I intend to keep an eye on this field and expect to post further material on martinfowler.com.
posts.key_insight: Language workbenches were an emerging field in 2005 with multiple competing approaches from Intentional Software, JetBrains, and Microsoft, each representing different visions for Language Oriented Programming.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces and defines 'Language Workbenches' as a new class of tools that enable Language Oriented Programming—building software around multiple Domain Specific Languages (DSLs). He traces the history of DSLs from Unix little languages through Lisp macros to XML configuration files, distinguishing between external DSLs (separate languages requiring translation) and internal DSLs (embedded in host languages). The key innovation of language workbenches is that they store programs as abstract semantic models rather than text files, with editors projecting views of these models. This eliminates the 'symbolic barrier' between DSLs and enables sophisticated IDE features. Fowler concludes that while language workbenches are immature, they could transform software development by enabling domain experts to program directly, though the 'COBOL inference'—that technologies promising to eliminate programmers rarely do—counsels caution.
key_points: Language workbenches fundamentally change programming by making the abstract semantic model the source of truth, with text/visual editors as mere projections
External DSLs offer maximum flexibility but lack symbolic integration with host languages; internal DSLs integrate well but are constrained by host language syntax
The distinction between abstract syntax (structure) and concrete syntax (representation) is crucial—the same DSL can have XML, custom text, or graphical representations
Spreadsheets represent the most successful lay programming environment, suggesting DSLs should emphasize immediate feedback, tool-language integration, and selective information display
Vendor lock-in and version control for non-textual sources remain significant risks for language workbench adoption
money_quotes: Language oriented programming is about describing a system through multiple DSLs.
The key difference here is that the 'source' is no longer the editable textual files. The key source that you manipulate is the abstract representation itself.
The biggest danger in using the term 'language' is that it can lead people to miss the point of what can really be done with language workbenches.
On most projects I visit the biggest issue is the communication between the developers and the business. If that's working well, then you can make progress even with second rate technology.
I'm choosing to look at the C# and Ruby code through language oriented programming glasses. But it's a point of view with a long tradition - Lisp programmers often think of creating DSLs inside Lisp.

So when we think of the DSLs in a language workbench, we should be thinking less of the kinds of languages I've shown here. Instead we should be thinking of things like the next generation of spreadsheets.
If language workbenches live up to their hopes, in ten years time we'll look back and laugh at what we now think DSLs should look like.
posts.key_insight: Language workbenches promise to make Domain Specific Languages practical by eliminating the symbolic barrier between languages and enabling sophisticated tooling, but their true potential lies not in traditional textual or graphical languages but in fundamentally new programming paradigms like enhanced spreadsheets.

posts.summary: This is a brief definitional entry that establishes 'Hollywood Principle' as an alternative name for Inversion of Control. The post serves as a cross-reference in Fowler's bliki (blog-wiki hybrid), directing readers to the fuller explanation of the concept. The Hollywood Principle name comes from the phrase 'Don't call us, we'll call you' - reflecting how frameworks call your code rather than your code calling the framework.
key_points: Hollywood Principle and Inversion of Control are synonymous terms
The entry serves as a navigational aid in Fowler's interconnected bliki format
The name alludes to Hollywood casting calls: 'Don't call us, we'll call you'
money_quotes: A synonym for InversionOfControl.
posts.key_insight: The Hollywood Principle ('Don't call us, we'll call you') is simply another name for the Inversion of Control pattern.

posts.summary: Inversion of Control (IoC) is a fundamental principle distinguishing frameworks from libraries: instead of your code calling library functions, the framework calls your code at designated extension points. Fowler illustrates this with a command-line program (where your code controls the flow) versus a GUI application (where the windowing system controls when your methods are called). This pattern appears across many contexts—event bindings, interface implementations like EJB lifecycle methods, and template methods in testing frameworks like JUnit. Fowler notes confusion has arisen because 'IoC containers' have become associated specifically with dependency injection, despite IoC being a much broader and older concept.
key_points: A library is a set of functions you call; a framework calls your code—this inversion of control is the defining difference
IoC manifests through various mechanisms: closures/lambdas, event subscriptions, interface implementations, and template methods
The Hollywood Principle ('Don't call us, we'll call you') is a colorful synonym for IoC dating to 1983
Modern IoC containers have caused confusion by associating the term specifically with dependency injection, when IoC is a much broader principle
EJB uses inversion of control extensively through lifecycle callbacks, making the framing of IoC containers as 'competitors to EJB' somewhat ironic
money_quotes: The control is inverted - it calls me rather me calling the framework.
Inversion of Control is a key part of what makes a framework different to a library.
A library is essentially a set of functions that you can call. A framework embodies some abstract design, with more behavior built in.
The framework's code then calls your code at these points.
You don't get to control when these methods are called, just what they do. The container calls us, we don't call it.

There is some confusion these days over the meaning of inversion of control due to the rise of IoC containers; some people confuse the general principle here with the specific styles of inversion of control that these containers use.
posts.key_insight: Inversion of Control is not a specific technique like dependency injection, but the fundamental principle that distinguishes frameworks from libraries: the framework controls the flow and calls your code, rather than you calling it.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that 'Service Oriented Architecture' has become meaninglessly ambiguous, with the term encompassing so many contradictory definitions that it's impossible to answer questions about it. He catalogs the wildly different interpretations: web services exposure, application-less architectures with UI aggregators, standardized communication backbones, and asynchronous document messaging. While acknowledging that Object Orientation also suffered from definitional debates, he contends SOA's ambiguity is far worse. His conclusion is that the term is beyond saving and the concrete ideas within it need independent names and descriptions.
key_points: SOA means radically different things to different people - from web services to messaging to organizational backbones
The contradictions are extreme: people claim SOA both separates and combines data and process, is both synchronous and asynchronous
While Object Orientation had definitional debates, SOA's ambiguity is far more severe
SOA has become a 'semantics-free concept' like 'components' and 'architecture'
The valuable concrete ideas hiding under SOA need to be named and described independently
money_quotes: It's a question that's pretty much impossible to answer because SOA means so many different things to different people.
In it's worse form this is 'CORBA with angle brackets'.

I've heard people say the nice thing about SOA is that it separates data from process, that it combines data and process, that it uses web standards, that it's independent of web standards, that it's asynchronous, that it's synchronous, that the synchronicity doesn't matter...
I played it for laughs by asking if anyone else understood what on earth SOA was.
There's far less Object Ambiguity than there is Service Oriented Ambiguity.
I think SOA has turned into a semantics-free concept that can join 'components' and 'architecture'. It's beyond saving.
posts.key_insight: When a technical term accumulates so many contradictory meanings that it can simultaneously mean opposite things, it becomes useless and the concrete ideas within it need their own independent terminology.

posts.summary: Fowler discusses the challenge of maintaining employee-driven decision making as ThoughtWorks grows. While the company aims to be employee-controlled rather than shareholder-driven, most delivery staff are too busy with client work to engage in operational decisions. This creates an 'us vs them' divide between operational management and delivery people. The company has experimented with councils and management rotation but hasn't found a scalable solution yet.
key_points: Technical delivery people generally aren't interested in operational management issues - not from apathy but because their work already fills their mental bandwidth
The gap between ops and delivery creates mutual frustration: delivery complains about decisions affecting their lives, ops complains about business realities being ignored
Personal networking helps bridge gaps in small organizations but doesn't scale, even for exceptional networkers
The company is experimenting with councils and management rotation to prevent ops from becoming a traditional distant executive class
Well-rounded teams matter more than well-rounded individuals - people should work to their strengths rather than be forced into weak areas
money_quotes: How do we avoid this operational management group turning into a traditional executive group who are distant from the day to day delivery issues?
I've always felt we should get people to do what they do best and aim for well-rounded teams rather than getting people to work on their weak areas to try and round out individuals.
Being a social network hub can't be mandatory for operational management, even if it could scale.
Operational managers aren't 'higher' than delivery people - just different.
So by now I hope you grasp the problem (I doubt it's an unfamiliar one) and are gasping for the solution. So am I.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental tension in scaling employee-driven companies is that the people most affected by operational decisions are often least interested in making them, creating an inevitable divide that no amount of good intentions can easily bridge.

posts.summary: Call Super is an anti-pattern in OO frameworks where subclasses must remember to call the superclass method—a sign of poor API design. The solution is the Template Method pattern, where the superclass controls the flow and provides hook methods for subclasses to override. Fowler explores complications with multi-level frameworks (using JUnit as an example), where introducing new hook method names can confuse developers familiar with established conventions. He suggests overriding the calling method instead, going 'up a level' in the template hierarchy. The piece reflects Fowler's 'enabling attitude' toward inheritance, preferring guidance over restrictions like sealing methods.
key_points: Requiring developers to remember to call super is a sign of bad API design—the framework should handle this automatically
Template Method pattern with hook methods eliminates the call super requirement and gives superclass writers more control
Hook methods can be empty, provide defaults, or be abstract depending on expected subclass behavior
Multi-level frameworks create complications where new hook names confuse developers familiar with original conventions
Going 'up a level' to override the calling method can preserve familiar APIs while adding framework behavior
money_quotes: Whenever you have to remember to do something every time, that's a sign of a bad API.
The subclass writer now doesn't have to worry his ugly head about calls to super.
Inheritance is a very intimate relationship. Either the whole thing works or it doesn't.
I can't predict the subclass's needs, so I'd rather say 'go ahead, but on your own head be it', rather than 'no'.
This is always an option worth considering - if a template method doesn't work where you are, consider going up a level.
As usual the power of inheritance comes with responsibility, you have to keep an eye on what changes occur in the super-class.
posts.key_insight: When frameworks require developers to remember ritual calls to super, the solution is redesigning with Template Method—but multi-level frameworks may need to override at a higher level to preserve familiar APIs.

posts.summary: This brief post defines annotations as meta-data attached to program elements that can embellish code with extra behavior. Fowler explains that Java calls these 'annotations' while C# calls them 'attributes,' with C# having them from its first release and Java adding them in version 1.5. He uses C#'s [Obsolete] attribute as a practical example, noting it can mark deprecated elements with custom messages and error/warning levels. Fowler states his preference for the term 'annotation' as the general term since 'attribute' is overloaded with other meanings. He also notes that some languages like Ruby implement annotations through unconventional syntax like class methods.
key_points: Annotations are meta-data on program elements (classes, methods, fields) that embellish code with extra behavior
Java uses 'annotation' terminology while C# uses 'attribute' - C# had them first, Java added them in 1.5
Language platforms provide built-in annotations but allow custom ones
The term 'annotation' is preferred over 'attribute' because 'attribute' has too many other meanings
Some languages implement annotations through non-obvious syntax patterns
money_quotes: An annotation on a program element (commonly a class, method, or field) is a piece of meta-data added to that program element which can be used to embellish that element with extra code.
When writing about programming, I prefer to use 'annotation' as the general term. Although .NET was first, the word 'attribute' is just too widely used for different things.
Languages may provide annotations in ways that don't reflect the syntax of the language.
posts.key_insight: Annotations provide a standardized way to attach meta-data to code elements, though their implementation varies significantly across languages.

posts.summary: Open Space is a self-organizing conference methodology where attendees, rather than organizers, determine the sessions and schedule. The approach uses a simple space/time grid where anyone can propose and convene sessions, with scheduling handled organically through convener negotiation. Fowler describes its application across various scales from small workshops of 20 people to events with hundreds of attendees. The methodology's power comes from increased participation and energy compared to pre-planned conferences, with the 'Law of Two Feet' encouraging movement between sessions when someone isn't learning or contributing.
key_points: Open Space eliminates pre-planned agendas in favor of attendee-driven session proposals and scheduling on a visible grid
The approach scales effectively from small groups of 12-20 people to large conferences of 100-200 attendees
The 'Law of Two Feet' normalizes movement between sessions, breaking the social convention that leaving is rude
Scheduling conflicts are resolved through direct negotiation between conveners and interested attendees
The method reduces organizer workload while producing more participative and energetic events
money_quotes: The unusual (and powerful) thing about Open Space is that you don't pre-plan a list of activities and speakers.
Instead you provide a basic skeleton of time and space, and the attendees figure out what actually happens.
The result is a more participative and energetic event.
A good way to seed a topic is to raise a question that needs to be addressed if you're going to be satisfied with the conference.
The final result is never perfect, but never worse than more planned approaches.
If anyone finds themselves in a place where they are neither learning nor contributing they should move to somewhere more productive.
Many people find this unnatural, there's a belief that it's rude to move, but once you get used to open space then this movement becomes comfortable.
posts.key_insight: Self-organizing conferences produce better outcomes than pre-planned ones because they harness attendee energy and expertise rather than trying to predict it in advance.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler briefly highlights the Photran project at UIUC, which aims to bring refactoring capabilities to Fortran. He directs readers to Brian Foote's entertaining write-up about the project. The post is essentially a quick link recommendation with Fowler's characteristic wit about Foote's writing habits.
key_points: UIUC is developing refactoring tools for Fortran through the Photran project
Brian Foote has written about the project in his distinctive, humorous style
Fowler notes that getting Foote to write is exceptionally difficult despite his talent
money_quotes: Getting him to write anything is usually like trying to pull teeth from a live sabre-tooth tiger while wearing a necklace of freshly killed lamb chops.
posts.key_insight: Even legacy languages like Fortran can benefit from modern refactoring tooling.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on his long-standing appreciation for Ruby, noting both its technical merits (clean syntax, well-designed frameworks) and the quality of its community. While the recent Rails-driven explosion of interest has brought Ruby mainstream attention, Fowler highlights an overlooked advantage: the Ruby community's reputation for being pleasant and welcoming to newcomers. Drawing on his belief that people matter most in software development, he argues that a collaborative, talented community gives Ruby a distinct competitive advantage worth taking seriously.
key_points: Ruby's appeal extends beyond language features to include well-designed frameworks and clean syntax
The Ruby on Rails framework has driven mainstream adoption and received strong endorsements from trusted practitioners
The Ruby community has a reputation for being friendlier and more welcoming to newcomers than other programming communities
Community quality—talented, collaborative, pleasant people—creates competitive advantage for a technology platform
money_quotes: I like the ruby language for its rich yet uncluttered syntax and the well designed frameworks that come with it.
The Ruby community was generally more pleasant and more friendly to newbies.
Time and time again I've found a nice mix of talent, pragmatism, and pleasantness in the Ruby community.
I'm someone who has always believed that in software development PeopleMatterMost.
A community that has a good blend of talented, collaborative people has a distinct competitive edge.
A good reason to take the Ruby phenomenon seriously is the quality of the community that's behind it.
posts.key_insight: A programming language's community culture—its blend of talent, pragmatism, and welcoming atmosphere—can be as important a factor in its success as its technical merits.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains that Thoughtworks is fundamentally not just a software company but founder Roy Singham's experiment in proving conventional business wisdom wrong. The company was built to challenge assumptions that high-ability intellectuals can't collaborate, that nice guys finish last, and that large companies need rigid management structures. Thoughtworks operates with a flat structure, transparency, and trust in employees to make decisions with information and principles rather than procedures. Fowler argues that their social model, not business model, defines the company—and that organizations with the right social model can adapt to changing business conditions. He acknowledges they don't always live up to their ideals but sees this experiment as directly aligned with his belief that people matter most in software development.
key_points: Thoughtworks was founded as an experiment to disprove conventional wisdom about what's possible in corporate organization
The company operates on principles of hiring only high-ability people, flat management, transparency, and trusting employees with decision-making power
Their social model is primary; business model is secondary and more adaptable
Roy Singham's thinking was strongly influenced by Dee Hock, the creator of Visa
Fowler left a comfortable consulting career specifically to participate in this organizational experiment
money_quotes: Thoughtworks is really Roy's Social Experiment.
You can't have a company that entirely consists of high ability people, you need a mix of less able people that the high ability people leverage.
Rather than have procedures, we try to give people on the ground information and rough principles and let them make decisions themselves.
Our belief is that an organization with the right social model can jump business models.
Business models don't last as long now since everything is changing so much faster.
Can you really make a company that can be enjoyable to work in and still make money?
Can you win and still be nice?
posts.key_insight: An organization defined by a strong social model—trust, transparency, and high-caliber people—can outlast and adapt beyond any single business model.

posts.summary: Thoughtworks has opened an office in Xi'an, China, marking the realization of a long-held ambition for several people in the company. Unlike the India office which focuses on offshore development, the China office aims to sell into the growing Chinese domestic economy. The choice of Xi'an over Beijing or Shanghai was driven by its large student population, growing economy, and welcoming software park eager to learn about agile development. Fowler sees this as a long-term investment in a market where software isn't yet fully valued, but where opportunities for custom strategic software development exist.
key_points: Thoughtworks China will focus on selling into the domestic Chinese economy rather than offshore development like India
Software is undervalued in China, seen as something that comes with hardware rather than a strategic asset
China's strength lies in its diverse onshore economy rather than competing with India for offshore work
There's opportunity for early technology adoption in China before Western markets are ready
Internet censorship ('the great firewall') presents barriers to information flow that will hurt development
money_quotes: Software isn't valued much yet. The attitude is that software is something that comes with hardware - rather like it was in the west a couple of decades ago.
I see China's big strength as the fact that it's a more diverse economy which means that the Chinese software industry can mostly work onshore.
Competing with India for off-shoring ends up playing on a field where India is naturally strong.
One of our hopes is that we'll be able to work with technologies in China before people in the west are ready to work with them.
With information such a driving force these days, these kinds of barriers are only going to hurt.
It will take time to figure out where the opportunities are, and if we aren't there we won't be able to find them.
posts.key_insight: Expanding into China represents a long-term strategic bet on a market where software's value isn't yet recognized, but where domestic economic growth and appetite for technological experimentation create unique opportunities distinct from the offshore development model.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that anecdotal evidence, while inferior to rigorous measurement, is essential for advancing software development practices because we fundamentally cannot measure productivity objectively. He defends experience-based learning and storytelling as the primary mechanism by which the profession improves. Fowler criticizes untested theoretical ideas while advocating for sharing real project experiences, even from single cases. The post positions practitioner narratives as valuable 'raw material' that others can build upon, creating collective learning through accumulated stories rather than controlled experiments.
key_points: Software productivity cannot be objectively measured, making 'hard data' comparisons between techniques essentially impossible
Anecdotal evidence from real project experience is valuable and shouldn't be dismissed despite its limitations
Ideas should be tried at least once before being shared - untested theoretical proposals lack credibility
Single project experiences are worth reporting because they provide raw material for others to test and build upon
Professional learning happens through accumulated stories of success and failure, not just rigorous studies
money_quotes: Indeed my whole career is about spreading ideas based on the analysis of anecdotal evidence.
Despite its inferiority to objectively measured phenomena, its unwise to dismiss it. After all, how else can we learn?
The crucial flaw in each of them was that the authors had not tried their idea out - even once.
Just reporting on one particular thing you discovered on a project is useful because it provides raw material for others.
This approach - some people report their story, others copy it with or without success - is the backbone of how a profession can learn.
There is no scientifically rigorous evidence that parachutes prevent harm to people jumping out of airplanes.
posts.key_insight: In a field where productivity cannot be measured, shared practitioner experiences become the essential mechanism for professional learning, even without scientific rigor.
