posts.summary: The post argues that effective planning requires honestly confronting capacity limits rather than cramming features into unrealistic timelines. Using Kent Beck's XP approach as the model, Fowler explains how dividing work into iterations, estimating features, and tracking velocity creates natural constraints. He emphasizes that agile methods are not anti-planning but rather enforce disciplined adaptive planning where adding features always requires removing others. The informal communication from co-locating stakeholders with developers helps build realistic expectations about what can be built.
key_points: Software development suffers from poor understanding of what can actually be accomplished in finite time, leading to overloaded projects
XP's planning mechanism works by tracking velocity and refusing to commit to more features than fit in an iteration
Agile planning is not anti-planning but controlled adaptive planning where changes happen frequently within constraints
Adding features without asking 'what do I take out?' indicates the planning process has broken down
Co-location of stakeholders with developers builds realistic intuition about development capacity
money_quotes: You can't put ten pounds of shit into a five pound bag
One of the big problems with software development is that people have little sense of what can actually be done in finite time.
Too often we see lots of functionality rammed into a bag with no understanding about whether it will fit.
This is the essence of the adaptive planning of agile methods - the plan changes frequently but in a controlled manner.
If you want to add a feature you always have to ask 'what do I take out to make room?'
If you see features added to an agile project without that thinking, without space being made for them; you can safely conclude that the planning is being done badly.
posts.key_insight: Effective planning isn't about predicting the future perfectly but about creating constraints that force honest tradeoffs when scope changes.

posts.summary: This brief conference report covers the author's single day at JAOO 2005, which he considers his favorite conference despite being unable to articulate exactly why. The highlights included meeting Erik Meijer to discuss LINQ's approach to integrated querying across data stores, and catching up with the Poppendiecks about the growing interest in lean manufacturing principles applied to software development. The report also notes a provocative panel discussion questioning the sustainability of corporate-funded open source development.
key_points: LINQ represents a promising approach to declarative querying across SQL databases and XML documents with a novel take on type checking
Lean manufacturing principles are gaining traction as a framework for understanding agile software development
Some observers believe corporate-funded open source development may be unsustainable and could face significant collapse
The best conferences retain a small-conference feel while staying fresh and broad
money_quotes: Somehow it manages to retain the feel of a small conference while staying fresh and broad.
I liked the fact that Eric seems to strike a rare balance between dynamic and static languages, looking for an approach that takes a different route to type checking.
I've long like the Poppendieck's approach of using lean manufacturing as MetaphoricQuestioning to describe agile methods - despite my usual suspicion of such analogies.
I'll stay silent on this question, I prefer to avoid trying to predict the future.
I learned a new problem that Microsofties face: how to deal with your child who asks you why you work for the Evil Empire.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable aspect of technical conferences is often the informal connections and conversations with practitioners working on emerging ideas.

posts.summary: This post reflects on Martin Fowler's experience at OOPSLA 2005, covering various sessions and developments in software engineering. He highlights the Subtext project for its innovative approach to non-text-based programming, discusses emerging concerns about concurrency with multicore processors, and notes advances in refactoring tools. Fowler muses on OOPSLA's identity crisis now that object-orientation has become mainstream, questioning whether the conference still has purpose. He concludes that while OOPSLA may seem directionless, its value lies in its community of practitioners who continue exploring new frontiers beyond OO advocacy.
key_points: Subtext and Language Workbenches represent a shift away from text-based source code toward abstract representations projected for editing
Concurrency is becoming a critical concern with multicore processors, requiring language-based approaches that ordinary developers can handle
Refactoring tools are evolving to use type inference and can automate code migration between library versions
Teaching programming through debugging and refactoring existing code may be more effective than writing from scratch
OOPSLA has transitioned from an advocacy conference to a community gathering, having won the battle for object-orientation's dominance
money_quotes: Java is Diet Cobol
Just because you think you can prove that the opposition can't score a goal is no reason to not play a good goalkeeper
We're all OOers now
Dynamic types are stronger than static types, as they don't flee the field at runtime
Static types give me the same feeling of safety as the announcement that my seat cushion can be used as a floatation device
Eclipse has a 'guess the button' user interface
only 12 times faster than C means you haven't started optimizing
posts.key_insight: Long-running technical communities derive their value not from their original mission but from the ongoing conversations and new explorations of the practitioners they've brought together.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces the concept of post-modern programming as articulated by James Noble and Robert Biddle. The modernist view holds that admirable software systems should be composed of uniform components in simple, consistent ways, exemplified by languages like Smalltalk and Lisp. In contrast, the post-modern view embraces heterogeneous systems where different kinds of software are glued together in various ways, as seen in Perl and Unix. Fowler suggests this 'big bucket of glue' approach isn't necessarily inferior, challenging the aesthetic preference for architectural purity.
key_points: Modernist programming values uniform components composed in simple, consistent ways (e.g., Smalltalk, Lisp)
Post-modern programming accepts diverse components glued together heterogeneously (e.g., Perl, Unix)
The 'big bucket of glue' style of software integration isn't inherently bad
The original academic paper is deliberately written in a post-modern style, lacking a grand narrative
money_quotes: Software development has long had a modernist viewpoint that admirable software systems are composed of uniform components, composed in a uniform and simple way.
A post-modern view is that software is all sorts of different very different stuff glued together in all sorts of different ways.
This style of software (big bucket of glue) isn't a bad thing.
Just beware that it's written in a post-modern style which means it lacks a grand narrative.
posts.key_insight: Heterogeneous, pragmatically-assembled software systems deserve recognition as a legitimate architectural approach rather than being dismissed as inferior to elegant, uniform designs.

posts.summary: Fowler shares a resource for people curious about Smalltalk who want to explore the language. His previously recommended introductory Smalltalk book went out of print, but he discovered it's now available for free download along with other Smalltalk materials. The resources are hosted by Stéphane Ducasse, co-author of a book on reengineering patterns that Fowler recommends.
key_points: Fowler's favorite introductory Smalltalk book is now available as a free download
Stéphane Ducasse hosts a collection of free Smalltalk learning materials
Ducasse co-authored an excellent book on reengineering patterns
money_quotes: From time to time I run into people who want to get a smalltalk and give it a spin to see what the fuss is about.
posts.key_insight: Classic Smalltalk learning resources that were once out of print are now freely available online for those curious about the language.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explores the use of in-memory databases for testing enterprise applications, where database access often creates slow test suites. He presents two main approaches: using SQL in-memory databases like HSQLDB or SQLite, or abstracting database access behind a Repository pattern with simple in-memory data structures. The post also looks beyond testing to production uses, particularly event-sourced architectures where in-memory databases serve as caches of applied change logs. Fowler notes that while Prevayler pioneered this approach, its tight coupling and lack of migration tools caused problems, though the underlying concept remains promising.
key_points: In-memory databases can provide order-of-magnitude speed improvements for test suites that hit databases frequently
Two approaches exist: SQL in-memory databases (HSQLDB, SQLite) or Repository pattern with hash tables and in-memory data structures
Some developers prefer the Repository approach because SQL in-memory databases can mask the architectural smell of missing abstraction layers
Event log architectures can use in-memory databases as caches, enabling high performance for read-heavy workloads
Caching database results keyed by SQL query strings is a useful hybrid approach for testing
money_quotes: When you're developing an enterprise application, tests that hit the database can be a huge time drain when running your test suites.
Since most ThoughtWorkers get the shakes if they haven't had a green bar recently, this makes a big difference to us.
Running SQL in-memory may removes much of the pain of slow access but acts as a deodorant to cover the smell of a missing repository.
If you use an approach that keeps an event log of all changes to your application state, you can treat the in-memory database as a cache of the result of applying the log.
Memory sizes are now enough that many application databases can be loaded into memory.
I think the approach of persistent change logs as systems of record is a fertile ground to explore in the future.
posts.key_insight: In-memory databases dramatically accelerate test suites, but the choice between SQL-based solutions and Repository abstractions reveals deeper architectural decisions about how database access should be organized in your codebase.

posts.summary: This is a brief memorial tribute to John Vlissides, one of the Gang of Four authors who co-wrote the seminal Design Patterns book. Fowler shares his personal grief at learning of Vlissides' death from cancer. He reflects on their professional relationship and the enjoyment he found in Vlissides' company at conferences and during a visit to IBM Research. The post directs readers to a wiki memorial where the patterns community can share stories of Vlissides' influence and support.
key_points: John Vlissides, co-author of the Gang of Four Design Patterns book, passed away after battling cancer
Fowler considered Vlissides someone he most looked forward to seeing at conferences
Vlissides was a dedicated and skilled writer who worked at IBM Research
The patterns community created a wiki memorial for people to share how Vlissides helped or inspired them
money_quotes: John is best known as one of the 'Gang of Four' who produced probably the best book written on software design.
John was one the people I most looked forward to meeting when I attended conferences, I always enjoyed his company and found him very comfortable to be with.
He was a very dedicated and skilled writer, I can only feel regret that I wasn't able to spend more time working with him.
It's very sad to think that I won't bump into him again.
posts.key_insight: The patterns community lost one of its founding contributors in John Vlissides, whose work on the Gang of Four book remains one of the most influential texts in software design.

posts.summary: Command Query Separation (CQS), coined by Bertrand Meyer, divides object methods into two categories: queries that return results without side effects, and commands (or modifiers) that change state without returning values. The principle's value lies in enabling developers to use queries freely and confidently while being more careful with state-changing methods. While Meyer advocates strict adherence, Fowler acknowledges practical exceptions like stack pop operations. The post notes that language-level support for CQS would be valuable but is rare, with C++'s const modifier being one of the few examples.
key_points: Methods should be divided into queries (return results, no side effects) and commands/modifiers (change state, no return value)
The return type serves as a useful convention for distinguishing between queries and commands
Queries can be safely reordered and introduced anywhere, while modifiers require more careful handling
Pragmatic exceptions exist—stack pop is a useful idiom that violates CQS by both returning a value and modifying state
Language-level support for CQS is rare; C++'s const modifier is one of the few examples, and it only applies to observable state
money_quotes: The really valuable idea in this principle is that it's extremely handy if you can clearly separate methods that change state from those that don't.
You can use queries in many situations with much more confidence, introducing them anywhere, changing their order. You have to be more careful with modifiers.
Meyer correctly says that you can avoid having this method, but it is a useful idiom. So I prefer to follow this principle when I can, but I'm prepared to break it to get my pop.
The first edition is the one that had the influence, the second edition is good but you'll need several months in a gym before you can lift it.
The rule about not changing state really only applies to the ObservableState of the system.
posts.key_insight: Separating methods that change state from those that don't makes code easier to reason about, but pragmatic exceptions are acceptable when they provide clear utility.

posts.summary: Lazy initialization is a technique that delays the calculation of a field's value until it's first accessed, useful when computation is expensive and the value isn't always needed. Fowler emphasizes this is purely an optimization technique and should only be applied when there's a real performance problem to solve. He warns about a significant debugging pitfall: inspecting a lazily-initialized field during debugging can trigger state changes that don't occur during normal execution, potentially causing bugs to appear or disappear based on observation.
key_points: Lazy initialization defers expensive calculations until the value is actually needed
This technique improves responsiveness when clients don't always need the initialized value
It should only be used when there's a genuine performance problem - not as a default pattern
Debugging lazy-initialized fields can cause state changes that mask or create bugs
The observable state may remain consistent while actual internal state changes unexpectedly
money_quotes: As with any optimization you shouldn't use this technique unless you have a real performance problem to solve.
Lazy initialization can cause debugging woes because if you try look at the field during debugging you'll cause a state change in the system that doesn't happen under normal use.
This can lead to situations where bugs seem to disappear when you add a print statement - always a good recipe for major ulcers on Friday afternoons.
posts.key_insight: Lazy initialization is an optimization technique that should be applied only when needed, as it introduces subtle debugging complications where observation itself can change program behavior.

posts.summary: Fowler contrasts Minimal Interface design with Humane Interface design in API development. A minimal interface provides only the smallest reasonable set of methods needed for clients to accomplish their goals, excluding convenience methods unless they clear a high bar. The approach prioritizes learnability, keeps classes focused on doing one thing well, and provides a clear stopping point for what to include. For published libraries especially, starting small is safer since removing methods later is nearly impossible.
key_points: A minimal interface includes only methods that cannot be replicated using existing interface methods
Large interfaces are harder to learn and may discourage use of the class entirely
Focusing on essentials helps class designers avoid the common problem of making classes do too much
The minimalist guideline ('can the client already do this?') is simpler and clearer than the humane guideline ('is this useful?')
For published libraries, it's better to start too small and add than start too big and be unable to remove
money_quotes: Interfaces take time to learn. A class with a huge interface is not likely to be used well and may well be off-putting in the first place.
A common problem with class design is to make classes do too much. Focusing on the essentials helps keep cruft out of the class, allowing it to focus on doing one job and doing it well.
If you follow a humane approach, how do you know where to stop?
The minimalist one is simple - if the client can do it with the existing methods, then they don't need an extra one.

When you're working on a library, once you publish it it's very hard to take anything out. As a result it's better to start with something too small and add things, than to have something too big when you can't remove things.
posts.key_insight: A minimal interface trades convenience for learnability and provides a clear, objective rule for what belongs in an API: if clients can already accomplish it with existing methods, don't add another one.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that validation should not be thought of as a context-free property of objects, but rather as something bound to specific actions or contexts. He critiques the common pattern of implementing generic 'isValid' methods, suggesting instead that validation should be tied to specific operations like 'isValidForCheckIn'. The post also challenges the assumption that all validation failures should prevent database saves, referencing Alan Cooper's principle that users should be able to save incomplete information. Fowler concludes that developers tend to be overly restrictive about when objects can be saved.
key_points: Validation should be context-dependent, tied to specific actions rather than abstract object state
Methods like 'isValidForCheckIn' are more useful than generic 'isValid' methods
Saving to a database is itself an action that requires its own validation context
Not all validation failures should prevent an object from being persisted
Developers tend to over-restrict saving, preventing users from storing incomplete work
money_quotes: I think it's much more useful to think of validation as something that's bound to a context - typically an action that you want to do.
Is this order valid to be filled, is this customer valid to check in to the hotel. So rather than have methods like isValid have methods like isValidForCheckIn.
One of the consequences of this is that saving an object to a database is itself an action.
The various validity checks that make this up should be interrogated with the question 'should failing this test prevent saving?'
Alan Cooper advocated that we shouldn't let our ideas of valid states prevent a user from entering (and saving) incomplete information.
While I'm not convinced that this should be an absolute rule, I do think people tend to prevent saving more than they ought.
posts.key_insight: Validation is not an intrinsic property of an object but a contextual question tied to specific actions, and the decision to prevent saving should be deliberately interrogated rather than assumed.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler critiques the practice of creating an interface for every class (like ICustomer/Customer or Customer/CustomerImpl), which mirrors the C/C++ header file pattern. While this approach offers complete substitutability, Fowler argues it creates unnecessary maintenance overhead when multiple implementations don't actually exist. He distinguishes between application code, where Extract Interface refactoring can be applied on demand, and published libraries, where interfaces are more valuable but should be designed around client needs rather than mirroring implementation classes.
key_points: Creating interface-implementation pairs for every class adds synchronization overhead without benefit when only one implementation exists
The pattern obscures cases where multiple implementations genuinely exist by making them indistinguishable from single-implementation classes
Application code can use Extract Interface refactoring on demand, making preemptive interfaces unnecessary
Published libraries benefit more from interfaces, but these should be designed for client needs, not as mirrors of implementation classes
money_quotes: Using interfaces when you aren't going to have multiple implementations is extra effort to keep everything in sync.
Furthermore it hides the cases where you actually do provide multiple implementations.
In an application if you ever need an interface where you don't have one you can just do Extract Interface and you're done.
Interfaces should be designed around your clients' needs, often these don't match the implementation.
Just mimicking the implementation classes in your PublishedInterfaces is rarely the best move.
posts.key_insight: Interfaces should emerge from actual need for abstraction rather than being created preemptively, and when they are needed, they should reflect client requirements rather than implementation structure.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains Eric Evans' classification system for domain objects from Domain Driven Design, which divides objects into Entities (objects with persistent identity), Value Objects (objects defined by their attributes), and Services (standalone operations). He provides practical guidance on distinguishing between these categories, noting that while the definitions are somewhat intuitive, clear implementation patterns exist. Fowler emphasizes that value objects should be immutable and override equality methods, while entities typically don't. He acknowledges the terminology is problematic due to overloaded meanings in other contexts like databases and SOA.
key_points: Entities have distinct identity through time; Value Objects are defined entirely by their attributes; Services are standalone operations typically accessing external resources
Value objects should always be immutable to avoid aliasing bugs - you replace them rather than modify them
The distinction between entities and values is reflected in code: values override equality/hash methods, entities typically don't
Service objects are usually singular within a processing context but implementation mechanism should be hidden from other domain objects
The terminology is heavily overloaded and conflicts with database entities, SOA services, and service layers
money_quotes: The trouble is that the trio are hard to define precisely, they are of the 'I know them when I see them' category.
Entities are usually big things like Customer, Ship, Rental Agreement. Values are usually little things like Date, Money, Database Query.
One important rule to follow is that value objects should be immutable (otherwise you get into all sorts of trouble with aliasing bugs).
To change a value (such as my height) you don't change the height object, you replace it with a new one.
You should ensure that your implementation mechanism is hidden from other domain objects so you can easily change it.
So be wary of assuming people are using these words like this - they are heavily overloaded. Sadly there's not much alternative.
posts.key_insight: Domain objects fall into three categories - Entities, Value Objects, and Services - but recognizing which is which requires intuition and experience rather than rigid definitions, and the terminology is unfortunately overloaded with conflicting meanings elsewhere.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler discusses his practical approach to clothing as a frequent traveler, prioritizing function over fashion. He outlines key criteria for travel clothing: minimal care requirements, easy packing, flexibility across situations, and abundant pockets. The post reviews several clothing suppliers including TravelSmith as his primary source, ScottEVest for pocket-heavy jackets, Rohan for UK shopping, and Brooks Brothers for dress shirts. This is essentially a personal gear recommendation post rather than a technical article.
key_points: Function trumps fashion for traveling geeks - clothes should require minimal care, pack easily, work in multiple contexts, and have plenty of pockets
TravelSmith serves as his primary supplier with excellent customer service and practical travel-focused gear
Different suppliers fill different niches: ScottEVest for pockets, Rohan for UK quality (despite high prices), Brooks Brothers for professional non-iron shirts
The ideal travel shirt can be washed in a hotel sink and be presentable by morning
money_quotes: My philosophy is there isn't much point worrying about the packaging when there's nothing attractive about the contents.
I can never have too many pockets to put things in.
Although they do make you look worryingly australian, they are really handy and practical in sun or rain.
Whenever they make something I really like, they promptly discontinue it.
posts.key_insight: For frequent travelers, clothing should be evaluated purely on functional criteria like care requirements, packability, and versatility rather than style.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on the unexpected debate sparked by his earlier HumaneInterface post, noting most discussion devolved into Ruby vs Java comparisons rather than addressing deeper design principles. He explores why Ruby's Array includes stack and queue operations (push, pop, shift, unshift), tracing this approach back to Smalltalk's culture of using OrderedCollection for multiple purposes. The key insight is that duck typing in dynamic languages enables classes to fulfill multiple roles, while static languages achieve similar flexibility through distinct interfaces on shared implementations. Fowler concludes that judging one language's class design through another language's values may be misguided, as each language's design affords different patterns.
key_points: Ruby's Array includes stack/queue methods because dynamic languages embrace duck typing, letting one class serve multiple roles
Smalltalk culture historically used OrderedCollection instead of dedicated Stack classes, influencing Ruby's design
Static languages like Java achieve similar flexibility through multiple interfaces on shared implementations (e.g., LinkedList implements both List and Queue)
Language design affords different patterns: Java affords small interfaces with static helpers, Ruby affords larger classes with mixed-in methods
Class design should be evaluated within its language's idioms, not judged by another language's values
money_quotes: The Smalltalk feeling is that we can get what we want with our OrderedCollection, so why build another class?
Why is there no Stack in Smalltalk? Well, 'just because'. It is part of the culture to simulate stacks using OrderedCollection.
Static languages like to talk to objects through strict type interfaces, dynamic languages have classes that can fit multiple roles - Duck Typing.
Java's design affords small interfaces, and utility functions provided as static methods on helper classes. Ruby's design affords larger classes with mixed-in utility methods.
Perhaps one of the conclusions from this is that we should be wary of judging the features of a class in one language using the values of another language.
Like with many of these arguments I think the most interesting thing is to try to understand both points of view.
posts.key_insight: A class's interface design should be evaluated within its language's idioms and affordances, not judged through the lens of another language's values.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces the concept of 'Humane Interface' as a design philosophy prevalent in the Ruby community, contrasting it with the minimalist approach common in Java. The humane interface prioritizes making common use cases easy by providing convenience methods like 'last' and 'flatten', rather than forcing users to compose basic operations. While this results in larger APIs (Ruby's Array has 78 methods vs Java List's 25), it reduces code duplication across clients and improves readability. Fowler argues that humane interfaces do more work upfront so clients don't have to, following the principle that good software makes life easy for users. He personally leans toward the humane approach, though acknowledging it's harder to design well.
key_points: Humane interfaces prioritize common use cases over minimal method counts, resulting in larger but more convenient APIs
Ruby's Array provides methods like 'last', 'first', and 'flatten' that could be built from primitives but save clients from duplicating common code
Method naming follows usage frequency - common methods get short names (parse vs strptime), and Ruby even aliases methods (length/size) so users don't have to remember which one to use
The minimalist approach risks multiple clients duplicating code for common cases, forcing readers to learn indirect idioms
Good API design should think of users first and make their common tasks easy for both reading and writing
money_quotes: The essence of the humane interface is to find out what people want to do and design the interface so that it's really easy to do the common case.
Much of an object's strength lies in its behavior, not its data.
If you only try to provide the minimum, you end up with multiple clients duplicating code for common cases.
Even for simple cases like last, readers have to learn an idiom. Why should they have to see something indirect, when a simple method reads directly?
Good software thinks of the users first and makes life easy for them.
Humane interfaces do more work so that clients don't have to.
The rubyist view being that it's easier for the library to have both than to ask the users of a library to remember which one it is.
posts.key_insight: API design should optimize for how users actually work, providing convenience methods for common cases rather than forcing them to compose primitives, because the cost of a larger interface is outweighed by reduced duplication and improved readability across all clients.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his favorite music discoveries of 2005, crediting music writer Michael Stone for leading him to world jazz and international artists. He explores planetary jazz from Turkey, Sweden, and Brazil, Argentine tango, and eclectic folk music. The post serves as a personal music recommendation list organized by genre and geography. Fowler notes that despite embracing digital music, his music spending hasn't decreased. He admits finding it harder to write about music than software, but hopes readers might discover shared tastes.
key_points: World jazz offers refreshing alternatives to repetitive US jazz formats, with unique instrumentation like oud, kanun, and cimbalom
Small specialized retailers like cdroots fill an important niche for international music unavailable on mainstream platforms
The Maria Schneider Orchestra album represents a new distribution model - Internet-only sales that still won Grammy recognition
The best music often defies easy categorization, blending traditional roots with modern production and influences
Digital music consumption hasn't reduced music spending - if anything, the iPod's capacity demands more purchases
money_quotes: The RIAA may say the world is ending, but despite my embrace of digital music with my Slimp3 I don't see my music budget getting any smaller.
My wallet hates you almost as much as my ears love you.
For those who are tired of yet another subset of sax, trumpet, piano, bass and drums I'd suggest casting your eye to Okay Temiz.
It's the only time so far I've been drawn to music via slashdot.
I find it a lot harder to write about music than software - words don't quite do the job.
posts.key_insight: Seeking out international and unconventional music beyond mainstream offerings leads to richer, more satisfying discoveries than staying within familiar genres.

posts.summary: This is a brief redirect notice indicating that the Slimp3 device has been rebranded as the Squeezebox. The original post apparently discussed the author's experience with digital music and the Slimp3 device, but the content has been replaced with a simple pointer to the new product name.
key_points: The Slimp3 hardware device was renamed to Squeezebox
This represents an early example of networked audio streaming technology
money_quotes: These are now called a Squeezebox.
posts.key_insight: The Slimp3 digital music device evolved into the Squeezebox product line.

posts.summary: An Event Poster is a read-only reporting application that displays real-time state information to users, built on event sourcing principles. The application maintains all state in memory, receiving updates only through event streams rather than direct database writes. Because state can be reconstructed by replaying events from an initial snapshot, there's no need to persist application state to disk. This architecture yields dramatic performance improvements and eliminates object-relational mapping complexity. Updates flow through the source system and return via the event stream, ensuring consistency across multiple poster instances.
key_points: Event posters combine real-time reporting displays with event sourcing, where all state changes come through queued event objects
Eliminating database persistence removes disk I/O bottlenecks—one BBC news feed went from seconds per request to hundreds per second
Recovery is simple: reload initial state and replay all events from the queue
Clustering is trivial since all instances receive the same event broadcast and stay synchronized
Updates don't modify local state directly; they're sent to the source system and return through the event stream
money_quotes: There's no need for a database to save the in-memory state of the display.
Should the application fail, it just reloads the initial state and replays all the events in the queue.
All the complexities of mapping between in-memory and database are removed, which allows people to build a good domain model geared towards the display needs and never worry about persisting it.
These days main memory can hold a hell of a lot, it wasn't that long ago when gigabyte databases were considered pretty large.
It's trivially easy to cluster this kind of system. All you have to do is ensure events get broadcast to all the copies.
It doesn't update its own data directly, instead it waits for the appropriate event to come through the stream from the source application.
posts.key_insight: By embracing event sourcing and in-memory state for read-heavy applications, you can eliminate database complexity entirely and gain massive performance improvements, with the event stream serving as both the update mechanism and the recovery log.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explores the concept of implicit interface implementation - the ability to implement a class's public interface without inheriting its implementation. While Java and C# require explicit interface declarations for substitution, this limitation creates friction when testing or replacing library classes. Dynamic languages handle this naturally through duck typing, and COM's strict separation of interfaces from classes also solved this problem. The main practical use case appears to be testing, where developers need to substitute implementations without the constraints of inheritance.
key_points: Every class has an implicit interface (its public members) that languages like Java and C# don't allow you to implement independently
Implicit interface implementation would enable interface-inheritance without implementation-inheritance
The InterfaceImplementationPair pattern exists mainly to work around this limitation, but many developers consider it suboptimal
Dynamic languages sidestep this entirely - you just implement the methods you need and use the object
The practical value seems limited mostly to testing scenarios where you need to substitute implementations
money_quotes: One thing that neither Java nor C# allow you to do is to implement an implicit interface - that is you cannot write class ValuedCustomer implements Customer
There are lots of times when you want to stub out stuff, but it's difficult or impossible unless you have an interface.
It also leads to defining pure interface types when the only reason to do it is to support substitution for testing.
If you want to implement another class's interface all you need to do is implement the same methods and just use the object where you need it.
It's much harder to insert TestDoubles if you can't use a reimplementation - and often subclassing won't do the trick if the superclass wants a real connection to the database.
posts.key_insight: Static languages force artificial interface declarations primarily to enable testing substitution, a problem that dynamic languages and COM's interface-first design solved more elegantly.

posts.summary: Data Clump is a code smell where the same few data items are constantly passed around together instead of being grouped into an object. The refactoring solution is to identify these recurring value groups (like start/end pairs that should be ranges) and turn them into proper objects. Beyond cleaning up parameter lists, this refactoring opens opportunities to move related behavior into the new objects, making the codebase more object-oriented and maintainable.
key_points: Data clumps are groups of values that consistently appear together throughout code, signaling they should be a single object
Primitive values are often the culprits because developers don't think to wrap them in objects
Converting data clumps to objects immediately shrinks parameter lists
The real value comes from discovering behavior that can be moved into the new objects
money_quotes: Whenever two or three values are gathered together - turn them into a $%#$%^ object.
Often data clumps are primitive values that nobody thinks to turn into an object.
The interesting stuff happens as you begin to look for behavior to move into the new objects.
posts.key_insight: Recognizing and refactoring data clumps not only simplifies parameter lists but reveals opportunities to create richer objects with meaningful behavior.

posts.summary: Fowler explains the concept of observable state in object-oriented programming, distinguishing between methods that change state and those that don't. He argues that what matters is not whether internal state changes, but whether those changes are detectable from outside the object through its public query methods. Using a Ruby range class with lazy initialization as an example, he demonstrates how a cache variable changes real state without changing observable state. The key principle is that calling any method from outside should return the same result regardless of internal caching state.
key_points: Separating methods into state-changing and non-state-changing (queries) enables safer composition since queries can be called in any order
Observable state is what can be detected through an object's query methods, not the totality of internal state
Caches and lazy initialization change real state but should never change observable state
The test is whether any external method call returns the same result regardless of internal state changes
money_quotes: Non-state changing methods (which I call queries), can be used in any context without worrying about how they sequence with other methods.
The key here is not so much that they don't change any state, but they don't change observable state.
Putting a value in the lengthCache clearly changes the real state of the object. It doesn't change the observable state because you can't tell that the object's state has changed from the outside.
Caches are an obvious case of state whose changes should never be observable.
posts.key_insight: The distinction between real state and observable state enables powerful patterns like caching and lazy initialization while preserving the safety guarantees of query methods.

posts.summary: Fowler proposes a way to merge Design by Contract (DbC) and Test Driven Development (TDD) approaches, inspired by a conversation with Daniel Jackson. In DbC, class invariants define properties that must always be true and are automatically checked before and after method calls. The idea is to bring this discipline into TDD by defining an explicit invariant-checking method in production classes that gets called during test setup and verification phases. Fowler presents a simple example with a Bowler class where non-negative values for overs, runs, and wickets form the invariant. He admits he hasn't tried this technique himself but offers it as an interesting thought experiment.
key_points: Design by Contract uses class invariants that are automatically checked before and after method execution
TDD could incorporate DbC principles by adding explicit passesInvariant() methods to production classes
Invariant checks in tests should occur after setup and after the exercise phase
This hybrid approach brings formal contract verification into the more pragmatic TDD workflow
The technique remains theoretical—Fowler presents it as an untested idea worth exploring
money_quotes: There's been a long-running, if low-key, argument between the advocates of Design by Contract (DbC) and Test Driven Development (TDD).
A object must always satisfy its invariant (unless it's in the middle of doing anything.)
Applying this idea to TDD means that you define a common method to test the invariant in your production classes and test it in your test code.
I haven't tried this myself, nor am I aware of anyone else who does. But I thought I'd mention it as an interesting thought.
posts.key_insight: TDD could adopt the rigor of Design by Contract by explicitly testing class invariants at setup and verification phases, bridging two historically separate testing philosophies.

posts.summary: XUnit refers to a family of testing frameworks that became foundational to modern software development practices. The frameworks originated with Kent Beck's simple Smalltalk testing tools, which he would rebuild with each team as a 'seedwork' they could customize. JUnit, created by Kent Beck and Erich Gamma during a 1997 flight to OOPSLA, popularized the concept and became essential to the growth of Extreme Programming and Test Driven Development. The framework's simplicity encouraged adoption and spawned ports to nearly every programming language, with varying degrees of adaptation to each language's idioms.
key_points: Kent Beck originally created testing frameworks in Smalltalk as customizable seedworks, rebuilt fresh with each team
JUnit was pair-programmed test-first by Kent Beck and Erich Gamma on a flight to OOPSLA 1997
The red/green bar progress indicator originated from C3's practice of coloring the whole window based on test status
JUnit's simplicity was key to its success, encouraging learning, adoption, and extension by others
Ports to other languages vary from literal translations to sophisticated adaptations like NUnit 2.0's use of C# attributes
money_quotes: Kent wants people to control their own environment, so he liked to have each team build the framework themselves, that way they would feel happy to change it to suit their particular circumstances.
The first version of JUnit was built there, pair programmed, and done test first (a pleasing form of meta-circular geekery).
By being small and simple it encouraged people to learn and use it.
I've seen a huge change of attitude towards testing in the last decade, and I think JUnit played a big role in that.
If you're wondering who to blame for the fact that assertion messages are the first argument instead of following the java convention of putting optional arguments at the end....
posts.key_insight: JUnit's success came not from sophistication but from being small and simple enough that developers felt empowered to learn, use, and extend it.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reflects on the uncomfortable reality of being a 'minor geek celebrity' and explains why he often appears distant or unfriendly at conferences. As a self-described introvert, he finds meeting new people draining and dislikes the assumptions that come with fame—particularly that his writing success implies design genius. He explains his policy of declining photos, autographs, and most speaking invitations, offering this as both explanation and apology to those who may have felt slighted by his behavior.
key_points: Introversion makes meeting new people genuinely exhausting, not just uncomfortable
Writing fame doesn't equate to technical superiority—Fowler acknowledges many peers are better designers
Idolization is harder to handle than hostility because it creates expectations he can't meet
He's established personal boundaries (no photos, limited talks) to protect his wellbeing
The post serves as a preemptive apology for behavior that may seem rude but stems from discomfort, not arrogance
money_quotes: I've always been somewhat of an introvert - that is someone who finds meeting new people a drain on energy.
My fame comes primarily from my skill at writing not my design skills.
It's easy to brush off people who are nasty to me, even if their comments hurt. But it's harder to brush off someone who really wants to listen to me, who respects everything I say.
I find I can't be comfortable with someone who expects more than I know I can give.
I decided a while ago that I wouldn't pose for photos with people, or sign autographs.
I find it very difficult to be friendly with people I don't know - I hope you accept my sincere apologies.
posts.key_insight: Fame creates uncomfortable expectations that exceed what any individual can deliver, and setting boundaries—even ones that appear rude—is a legitimate form of self-preservation.

posts.summary: Code smells are quick-to-spot surface indicators that often point to deeper problems in software systems, a term coined by Kent Beck during the writing of Fowler's Refactoring book. Smells are valuable precisely because they're easy to detect—like a long method that makes your 'nose twitch.' However, smells don't always indicate actual problems; some long methods are perfectly fine. The real value is that smells prompt investigation, leading developers to ask better questions and discover meaningful refactoring opportunities. They're particularly useful for teaching less experienced developers to recognize potential issues.
key_points: Code smells are quick to spot ('sniffable') surface indicators, not necessarily problems themselves
Smells require deeper investigation—they often indicate problems but don't always
The best smells are easy to detect and frequently lead to discovering interesting underlying problems
Data classes (all data, no behavior) exemplify good smells that prompt behavioral refactoring
Smells are effective teaching tools—inexperienced developers can spot them even without knowing how to fix them
money_quotes: A code smell is a surface indication that usually corresponds to a deeper problem in the system.
A smell is by definition something that's quick to spot - or sniffable as I've recently put it.
Just looking at the code and my nose twitches if I see more than a dozen lines of java.
Smells don't always indicate a problem. Some long methods are just fine.
Smells aren't inherently bad on their own - they are often an indicator of a problem rather than the problem themselves.
Often simple questions and initial refactorings can be the vital step in turning anemic objects into something that really has class.
posts.key_insight: Code smells are valuable not because they're problems, but because they're easy-to-spot signals that prompt the right questions and deeper investigation.

posts.summary: Constructor initialization is an approach where all necessary collaborators are passed to an object during creation, ensuring the object is immediately in a well-formed, usable state. Fowler argues this is preferable to setter initialization because it makes dependencies explicit, distinguishes immutable from mutable attributes, and provides a compact one-liner syntax. He addresses common concerns about constructor explosion and long parameter lists, noting these are rarely problems in practice and can signal code smells worth addressing. Constructor initialization should be the default choice, with setter initialization reserved for occasional edge cases.
key_points: Constructor initialization ensures objects are created in a well-formed state ready for immediate use
Declaring collaborators in constructors makes dependencies explicit and easier to understand
The approach naturally distinguishes immutable attributes (no setter) from updatable ones
Concerns about too many constructors are usually unfounded - mandatory collaborators are typically few
Long parameter lists in constructors are a code smell that often reveals data clumps needing their own objects
money_quotes: By doing this you are always sure you have an object in a reasonably well-formed state that's ready to be used.
Declaring all the necessary collaborators in the constructor makes it clear which collaborators are needed, making it easier to see how to get the class going.
This approach makes it easy to see the difference between immutable and updatable attributes as immutable attributes don't have a setting method.
Constructor Initialization is my first choice.
A large list of construction parameters, like any large parameter list, is a CodeSmell.
Usually when I see these I find that many of the parameters are DataClumps and should be replaced by their own object.
posts.key_insight: Constructor initialization should be the default approach because it guarantees objects are created in a valid, usable state while making dependencies explicit and immutability obvious.

posts.summary: Fowler describes setter initialization as an alternative to constructor initialization, where you create an empty object and configure it through setter methods. He presents this as offering maximum flexibility in object wiring, allowing incremental setup as collaborators become available. The approach avoids long constructor parameter lists and provides self-documenting method names. The piece is a brief, neutral comparison of initialization strategies rather than a strong advocacy for either approach.
key_points: Setter initialization creates empty objects then configures them incrementally via setter methods
This approach provides flexibility when not all collaborators are available at construction time
Named setter methods are more readable than positional constructor parameters, especially with generic types like strings
Avoids the complexity of multiple constructor overloads and long parameter lists
money_quotes: This approach gives you the maximum flexibility in wiring up objects, allowing you to provide just the collaborators you need for a specific usage.
It frees you from having to set all the values at once - which is handy if some objects are only available at later times.
Setter methods have names that explain their use for the new object.
Constructor parameters with very general types (strings etc) can easily get very cryptic.
posts.key_insight: Setter initialization trades the safety of fully-constructed objects for flexibility and readability when configuring complex object graphs incrementally.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler critiques the absolutist stance of 'Getter Eradicators' who treat all getter methods as encapsulation violations. While acknowledging that getters can enable procedural code in OO languages, he argues the real purpose of encapsulation is hiding design decisions that may change, not just data. He suggests more nuanced heuristics: watch for code calling multiple methods on one object, be suspicious of data-only classes, and remember that behavior allocation is about trade-offs, not rigid rules.
key_points: Encapsulation is fundamentally about hiding design decisions and variability, not just hiding data
The real problem getters enable is procedural code that pulls data out instead of putting behavior in objects (Tell Don't Ask)
A better heuristic than eliminating getters: be wary when code invokes multiple methods on the same object
Data Classes (only fields and accessors) are warning signs that behavior should be moved into the object
Things that change together should be together - sometimes that's data and behavior, sometimes other groupings matter more
money_quotes: For me, the point of encapsulation isn't really about hiding the data, but in hiding design decisions, particularly in areas where those decisions may have to change.
When you are thinking about encapsulation I think it's better to ask yourself 'what piece of variability are you hiding and why' rather than 'am I exposing data'.
The OO community may have 'won' in the sense that modern languages are dominated by objects, but they are still yet to win in that OO programming is still not widely used.
I have a lot of sympathy with this motivation, but I fear that just telling people to avoid getters is a rather blunt tool.
If you ask an object for two bits of data, can you replace this with a single request for the bit of data you're calculating?
Allocation of behavior between objects is the essence of object-oriented design, so like any design, there isn't a hard and fast rule - rather a judging of trade-offs.
A good rule of thumb is that things that change together should be together.
posts.key_insight: Encapsulation is about hiding design decisions that may change, not rigidly avoiding data exposure - better to ask 'what variability am I hiding?' than 'am I exposing data?'

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes his experience visiting ThoughtWorks' London office, highlighting its open-plan layout that encourages communication and reflects the company's flat hierarchy. The office has evolved from handling one or two large projects to managing a dozen smaller clients. He contrasts his isolated home work life with the social atmosphere of the London office and highlights two emerging initiatives: automated deployment work with operations teams integrated from project start, and QuickStart sessions for collaborative system envisioning. The post also notes the London office's strong adoption of retrospectives, championed by Tim Mackinnon.
key_points: The open-plan office design deliberately reinforces ThoughtWorks' non-hierarchical culture, with the managing director sitting at an open desk like everyone else
London's deployment team integrates with development from day one, enabling automated and continuous deployment techniques
QuickStart sessions are intense, visual envisioning activities designed to surface inconsistent viewpoints early and establish collaborative working patterns
The London office has successfully adopted retrospectives across nearly every project, something Fowler struggled to implement in the US
money_quotes: The open plan encourages lots of communication, which is why we like it. It also helps reinforce our lack of hierarchy: Cyndi, the UK managing director, sits at an open desk spot just like everyone else.
I've long been a fan of retrospectives, if nothing else because I've been so frustrated with clients' inability to learn from their own experience.
The idea with QuickStart is to get people used to working in a collaborative manner from the beginning to give people a feel of how a full delivery project will work out.
This allows us to consider deployment issues from the very beginning and also build up automated and continuous deployment techniques.
The sessions are quite intense and focus on surfacing inconsistent viewpoints so they can be discussed and resolved early on.
posts.key_insight: Physical workspace design and early integration of practices like deployment automation and retrospectives directly shape team culture and collaboration quality.

posts.summary: Fowler categorizes code ownership into three models: strong (only owners modify their modules), weak (anyone can modify but owners maintain oversight), and collective (team-wide ownership). He strongly argues against strong code ownership because it creates friction and delays, particularly for simple cross-boundary changes like renaming methods. Weak ownership mitigates these problems while maintaining accountability. The choice between weak and collective ownership depends on team dynamics, though Fowler personally prefers collective ownership in XP contexts.
key_points: Strong code ownership turns all inter-developer interfaces into published interfaces, creating unnecessary overhead for changes
Simple refactoring operations like renaming public methods become problematic when they cross module boundaries under strong ownership
Delays in getting changes from other module owners leads to copying code rather than proper reuse, creating technical debt
Weak ownership provides a middle ground where anyone can make changes but owners maintain oversight
The choice between weak and collective ownership is primarily about team social dynamics rather than technical factors
money_quotes: Of the three the one I really don't like is strong code ownership.
Persuading them to make the change and waiting for the change often takes so long that it leads to delays and deeper problems, this is particularly galling when the change is a simple one.
Essentially you've turned all interfaces between developers into PublishedInterfaces, with all the attendant overheads to change.

Even worse is when you want an implementation change, but because you can't get it quickly enough you make a copy of the foreign code into your module, call your copy of the code and make the change. Of course you intend to sort out the mess later.
Weak code ownership is a good way to mitigate these kinds of problems. People can make changes freely, the code owner just has to keep an eye on things.
The choice between weak and collective ownership has more to do with the social dynamics of the team.
posts.key_insight: Strong code ownership creates more friction than it prevents, forcing developers into workarounds that create technical debt rather than protecting code quality.

posts.summary: Fowler shares an anonymous colleague's experience of shifting from collective to weak code ownership to address quality issues from undisciplined programmers. The change assigned ownership of critical code areas to high-performers while reassigning weaker developers to lower-risk tasks like bug hunting. This resulted in both velocity and morale gains, as strong developers no longer had to constantly fix others' work. The colleague introduced counterbalancing practices like mandatory pairing when working outside owned areas. Fowler uses this as an example of necessary local adaptation within agile methods.
key_points: Collective code ownership isn't absolute—it should be adapted based on team dynamics and skill disparities
Weak ownership can protect critical code areas while still allowing contribution through pairing
Quality problems from some developers were draining velocity and morale from the entire team
The path back to full participation runs through demonstrating quality to code owners
Even strong XP advocates sometimes need to shift from enabling to directing practices
money_quotes: These kind of practices aren't absolute and should always be tempered by local considerations
The result was a gain in velocity since the programmers who now 'own' our key code areas are not constantly reworking sub-par code
We also had a net gain in morale, since everyone but the sub-par producers were getting frustrated by having to watch their every check-in for issues
This change rewarded those who took quality, TDD, non-speculation, etc. seriously
If its in an area other than one where you have 'free play', you need to pair with someone who does
It was really tough for me to introduce a directing instead of an enabling practice, but things have been much improved since
All things being equal my colleague still prefers collective code ownership, but all things are seldom equal
posts.key_insight: Agile practices like collective code ownership are defaults, not dogma—teams must adapt them based on actual conditions, even if it means temporarily restricting freedom to protect quality.

posts.summary: Fowler enthusiastically describes the Squeezebox, a simple network device that streams MP3 files from a server to home amplifiers. He has deployed multiple units throughout his house connected to a central Debian server. The system allows control via remote or web interface and supports playlists and folder browsing. Fowler has automated his listening experience with Ruby scripts that generate themed random playlists nightly, making physical CDs obsolete in his household.
key_points: The Squeezebox streams music from a central server to any room via ethernet or wireless
An open source Perl server handles the streaming backend
Home automation through scripting can eliminate decision fatigue for music selection
Network-based music systems can make physical media storage obsolete
money_quotes: Its job is to take mp3 files streamed from a server and play them through the amplifier.
Every night a ruby script runs on the server and generates themed random playlists.
That way I can have two hours of 18th century classical music followed by an hour of blues without having to make any decisions about what to listen to.
It's certainly made using mp3s a pleasure at home, and now the CDs are relegated to boxes in the basement.
posts.key_insight: Centralizing digital music on a home server with networked players eliminates the friction of physical media and enables creative automation of the listening experience.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes dealing with major flooding in his Melrose, Massachusetts home during a historic New England spring storm. The storm brought nearly ten inches of rain, causing the worst flooding since 1938. His basement flooded to 12 inches, knocking out the boilers and leaving them without heat or hot water. With help from neighbors who lent a pump, they managed to reduce the water level. Fowler notes the timing was fortunate as he was actually home rather than traveling, though he was limited in helping due to recent arm surgery.
key_points: New England experienced its worst flooding since a 1938 hurricane, with nearly ten inches of rain over a weekend
A previous '100 year flood' only brought a couple inches of basement water, making this storm significantly worse at 12 inches
Neighbors provided a pump that helped reduce basement flooding from 12 inches to just over an inch
The flooding knocked out boilers, leaving the household without heat or hot water for several days
money_quotes: People are saying there's been nothing like it since a hurricane in 1938, although it's a minor affair compared to what some other places in the world have suffered in the last few years.
I'm hopeless with handyman tasks so Cindy wears the power tools in the marriage.
But at least I could make tea and keep the internet connection alive.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes being present during a crisis matters more than being useful, even when your contribution is limited to making tea and maintaining internet connectivity.

posts.summary: Fowler recounts his experience building a custom 'Hot Rod' desktop PC based on Ars Technica's system guide, intending to run Ubuntu as the host OS with Windows virtualized via VMWare. What should have been a straightforward build devolved into days of troubleshooting X Window configuration issues, ATI graphics driver problems, and system instability. After upgrading from Ubuntu Breezy to Dapper Drake, he resolved some issues but introduced others, including xemacs breaking and the inability to cleanly shutdown. He concludes that while the setup mostly works, he wouldn't recommend it to others and wonders if he should have just bought a Mac.
key_points: Building custom hardware is easy; configuring Linux graphics drivers and X Window remains painful even with 'user-friendly' installers
Virtualization (running Windows in VMWare on Linux) works well and makes Windows installations portable across machines
Screen real estate significantly impacts developer productivity - larger monitors than you think you need are worthwhile investments
Ubuntu's apt-get package management is a major advantage over Windows' manual download-and-install approach
Early adoption of new OS versions (AMD64 architecture, beta releases) often creates more problems than it solves
money_quotes: One of my favorite answers to 'how do we improve our development productivity' is to tell them to buy larger monitors than they think they need.
This is my big nightmare with Unix desktops - configuring X is one of the worse installation experiences you can go through.
Even when you use a 'user-friendly' X installer, it proceeds to ask you 'Would you like crypto-blue covariance adaptation?' (or something equally meaningful to me).
I certainly have found that I spend much less time faffing with my Mac than I do with either Windows or Linux machines.
Installing software is a guaranteed big time killer on windows - even free downloads have to be individually visited and downloaded.

I do have it working. Since the Dapper update I haven't had the problem of my screens going blank on me while working, so my only issue is the inability to do a clean shutdown. For the moment I can live with that.
posts.key_insight: The promise of customizable, powerful Linux workstations is perpetually undermined by the friction of driver configuration and system integration, making the simplicity of Mac increasingly attractive despite its limitations.

posts.summary: This article addresses the challenges of evolving services in a service-oriented architecture without breaking existing consumers. Fowler introduces the Consumer-Driven Contract pattern, which inverts the traditional approach by deriving provider contracts from the explicit expectations expressed by consumers. The pattern recommends that consumers validate only the specific data they actually need (using tools like Schematron) rather than the entire schema, enabling providers to make changes without coordinated updates across all consumers. This approach excavates hidden coupling between services and provides providers with insight into which contract elements are actually being used, enabling more strategic evolution decisions.
key_points: Traditional schema validation creates hidden coupling where consumers implicitly depend on entire schemas even when they only use portions, making any change expensive
The Robustness Principle (be conservative in sending, liberal in receiving) should guide service consumers to implement 'just enough' validation of only the data they actually process
Consumer contracts express explicit expectations of a provider contract, and when aggregated, they form a consumer-driven provider contract that represents only the mandatory elements
Consumer-driven contracts enable providers to identify truly breaking changes versus changes that affect no actual consumers, allowing safe removal of unused functionality
The pattern is most applicable within a single enterprise or closed community where providers can influence how consumers establish contracts
money_quotes: Contracts enable service independence; paradoxically, they can also couple service providers and consumers in undesirable ways.
In short, the enterprise becomes burdened with services.
Message receivers should implement 'just enough' validation: that is, they should only process data that contributes to the business functions they implement.
Services depend for success on their being consumed.
A service is of value to the business only to the extent it is consumed.
Consumer-driven contracts tie service evolution to business value by asserting the value of exportable service community elements.
Consumer-driven contracts do not necessarily reduce the coupling between services. What the pattern does do, however, is excavate and put on display some of those residual, 'hidden' couplings.
The underlying assumption here is that services, by themselves, are of no value to the business; their value is in being consumed.
posts.key_insight: Service providers should derive their contracts from the explicit expectations of their consumers rather than dictating schemas top-down, because the only valuable parts of a contract are those actually being consumed.

posts.summary: Fowler shares his evaluation of Ruby based on real project experiences from ThoughtWorks colleagues rather than theoretical feature debates. He notes that developers with proven track records in mainstream environments consistently report being significantly more productive in Ruby than Java or C#. The post highlights several non-typical Rails projects including kiosk hardware integration, SQL batch processing, and data munging applications. While acknowledging open questions about long-term maintenance and team changes, Fowler concludes that Ruby deserves serious consideration for projects where speed and productivity matter, though he cautions that mainstream adoption remains uncertain.
key_points: Experience-based evaluation trumps feature-based arguments - 'too many things are hard to judge that way'
Every ThoughtWorker asked reported being significantly more productive in Ruby than Java/C#
Ruby's applicability extends beyond typical Rails web apps to hardware integration, batch processing, and data transformation
Dynamic typing and lack of tools may pose challenges during team changes and later enhancement stages
Development productivity gains can be swamped by political and communication factors on some projects
money_quotes: Too many things are hard to judge that way - hence we spend so much of our time on client projects being slowed down by technology that sounded good on a golf course.
My preference is to make this judgment based on experience - find people who have a track record for delivering in the mainstream environments and who have tried Ruby.
When I ask the question 'do you think you're significantly more productive in Ruby rather than Java/C#', each time I've got a strong 'yes'.
Like many an ex-smalltalker I've long known of more productive platforms than the current mainstream enterprise choices.
There's also plenty of projects where development productivity is swamped by political and other communication factors.
posts.key_insight: Technology choices should be evaluated through the experiences of trusted practitioners with proven track records, not through theoretical debates about language features.

posts.summary: Fowler describes a quick solution for interfacing Ruby with the Ploticus graphing library, built for a personal project. Rather than binding directly to the C API, he pipes commands into Ploticus via standard input. He creates a layered object design with PloticusScripter at the bottom, PloticusClusterBar for specific chart types, and a top-level grapher class. The approach demonstrates the Gateway pattern, transforming a convenient programming interface into the output format Ploticus requires.
key_points: Piping scripts to command-line tools is a pragmatic alternative to C API bindings when you need quick results
The solution uses three layers of abstraction: scripter, chart-type handler, and domain-specific grapher
Ruby's method_missing enables a clean DSL for building Ploticus script commands
Even simple personal projects benefit from composed object designs that separate concerns
The Gateway pattern provides a convenient interface that transforms to match external system requirements
money_quotes: A sophisticated way to drive a C library like ploticus is to bind directly to the C API. Ruby makes this easy, so I'm told, but it's too much work for me (particularly if I want to be done before cocktail-hour).
First a caveat - this is literally something I knocked up one evening. It isn't intended to be robust, performant or otherwise enterprisey.
The PloticusClusterBar class is the gateway with the perfect interface for what I want to do. I make it transform between that convenient interface and what the real output needs to be.
Even for a simple thing like this I find a design of composed objects like this a good way to go. Which may only say how my brain's got twisted over the years.
posts.key_insight: Good object-oriented design habits like the Gateway pattern and layered abstractions remain valuable even for quick personal projects, making code easier to understand and modify.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler responds to Nicholas Carr's claim that Wikipedia is 'dying' due to article protection, arguing this fundamentally misunderstands wiki culture. Drawing on his long experience with Ward Cunningham's original wiki, Fowler contends that Wikipedia is actually remarkably structured compared to its predecessors. The key innovation isn't complete openness but 'after the fact control' - anyone can edit, but changes must be justified afterward. This mirrors how society and agile organizations function: loosening upfront controls while maintaining accountability for outcomes.
key_points: Carr's 'death of Wikipedia' claim over 0.01% protected articles is like 'saying democracy is over when a town hires a policeman'
Ward's original wiki was far more open than Wikipedia - no registration, no version control, truly a whiteboard anyone could modify
Wikipedia's innovation is after-the-fact control rather than prior permission - edits happen freely but must be justified if challenged
The agile movement similarly favors asking forgiveness over permission, but this isn't anarchy - it's minimizing controls while maintaining consequences
Wikipedia proved more useful than Ward's wiki precisely because of its added structure: separate discussion pages, version control, and editor oversight
money_quotes: It's like saying democracy is over when a town hires a policeman.

There's no permission you have to get to change a page before you begin, instead you have to justify what you've done afterward. That's a big shift to many people, particularly in the corporate world.
There's little that really stops me driving down the wrong side of I93 in the morning, yet there would be consequences should I do such a thing.
We try to follow that principle at Thoughtworks, leaving people with lots more latitude than in most organizations - but then following up with those who abuse the system.
Loosening controls isn't the same as anarchy and no control - a misrepresentation that's commonly thrown at agilists too.
It's about asking how we can use a minimum of controls, so that we don't suffocate the good in our desire to protect ourselves from the bad.
posts.key_insight: Effective collaboration comes not from eliminating controls but from shifting to after-the-fact accountability - minimizing upfront barriers while maintaining consequences for abuse.

posts.summary: This is a brief reference entry documenting the historic 2001 meeting at Snowbird, Utah where the term 'Agile' was chosen and the Manifesto for Agile Software Development was created. The post serves primarily as a pointer to a more detailed article about the meeting's background and subsequent developments. It functions as a glossary or bliki entry rather than a substantive article.
key_points: The Snowbird Utah meeting in 2001 was the birthplace of the Agile Manifesto
The meeting decided on the term 'Agile' to describe the movement
This entry links to a more comprehensive article on the agile story
money_quotes: The meeting at Snowbird Utah in 2001 that decided to use the word 'Agile' and began the 'Manifesto for Agile Software Development'.
posts.key_insight: The 2001 Snowbird meeting was the pivotal moment that unified various lightweight software development methodologies under the 'Agile' banner.

posts.summary: Fowler announces Buildix, a free integrated development server created by Thoughtworks' deployment team. The server bundles essential CI tools—source control, continuous integration server, issue tracker, and wiki—into a ready-to-use package. Born from Thoughtworks' frustration with spending weeks configuring build environments on new projects, Buildix packages open-source tools on a Knoppix live CD that can run immediately or be installed permanently. The solution addresses the underestimated complexity of integrating development infrastructure.
key_points: Setting up an integrated CI environment with all necessary tools is harder and more time-consuming than expected
Thoughtworks' deployment team created Buildix after repeatedly spending weeks configuring build servers for new projects
Buildix packages Subversion, Cruise Control, and Trac on a Knoppix live CD with optional VMWare image
The solution is free because it integrates existing open-source software rather than creating new tools
money_quotes: Getting a good environment with all of these things up and integrated together is a tougher job than you might think.
Inevitably we found we would be messing around for a week or so on new projects to get the build server set up with all this stuff.
It's free because it's a collection of open-source software that our deployment wizards have integrated together with some of that magic powder they found in the fridge.
posts.key_insight: The real challenge in continuous integration isn't the individual tools but the non-trivial work of integrating them into a cohesive, ready-to-use development environment.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler recounts the origins and creation of the Agile Manifesto at Snowbird, Utah in February 2001. The meeting grew out of an earlier XP retreat where participants recognized common ground among various 'lightweight methods.' The seventeen attendees chose 'agile' over 'lightweight' because it better captured the adaptiveness central to their approach. They deliberately created a manifesto as a 'call to arms' with clear values and principles that would distinguish agile from watered-down interpretations of iterative development. Fowler emphasizes that the original authors claimed no special status and intentionally 'launched the ship' for others to steer.
key_points: The manifesto emerged from recognizing common ground among XP and other lightweight methods, not from a planned effort to create a movement
The name 'agile' was chosen because 'lightweight' missed the point - adaptiveness and response to change were the core ideas, not merely being light in weight
The authors deliberately claimed no special status and designed the Agile Alliance to spread leadership beyond the original seventeen
