posts.key_insight: Incremental data migration transforms a risky, end-of-project panic into a routine activity while unexpectedly improving domain expert communication through real data.

posts.summary: Fowler explores the relationship between Model-Driven Software Development (MDSD) and Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs), noting that while DSLs are prominent in MDSD circles, they exist and serve similar purposes in traditional programming contexts. He explains that MDSD DSLs require specialized projectional editing tools and language workbenches, which creates pragmatic challenges due to less established tooling support. Fowler observes that DSLs receive proportionally more emphasis in the MDSD community, possibly because that community is smaller with less established practice. He remains skeptical of MDA-based DSLs specifically while acknowledging that model-oriented and textual DSLs share common foundations in semantic models.
key_points: DSLs serve the same role in both MDSD and traditional programming contexts, targeted at specific problems versus general-purpose languages
Model-oriented DSLs require projectional editing and language workbenches, which have less mature tooling ecosystems
MDSD DSLs can work independently within traditional language environments, generating code without requiring a general-purpose MDSD model
The MDSD community's emphasis on DSLs may reflect its smaller size and less established practices rather than superior sophistication
Both textual and model-oriented DSLs share the foundation of working with semantic models, though MDSD typically assumes code generation rather than direct execution
money_quotes: Some MDSD people seem to think that DSLs only exist within the MDSD world.
DSLs exist in both the textual language and MDSD worlds and play pretty much the same role for both.
In order to use model-oriented DSLs you need a different, projectional editing, approach to tooling.
Cynics think this is a result of the MDSD community desperately searching for a way to remain relevant, fans of MDSD regard it as a sign of MDSD's superior sophistication.
I think this is mainly due to the fact that the MDSD community is smaller and has far less in the form of established practice.
A difference is that most MDSD people assume that you'll want to generate code from that model rather than executing the model directly.
posts.key_insight: DSLs are not exclusive to the MDSD world—they serve similar purposes in traditional programming, with the main differences being in tooling approaches and assumptions about code generation versus direct execution.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains Domain Specific Languages (DSLs) for a non-technical audience, defining them as limited-expressiveness programming languages focused on particular domains. He traces their history from Unix and Lisp communities through to their modern resurgence via Ruby and Rails. The piece argues that DSLs primarily benefit developer productivity through improved code comprehension, with the more ambitious goal of making code readable (though not necessarily writable) by business people. Fowler cautions against the dream of non-programmers writing software, instead emphasizing DSLs as collaboration tools between business and technical teams.
key_points: DSLs are not new—they've existed since early Unix and include familiar tools like SQL, CSS, and regular expressions
Internal DSLs are idiomatic code in a host language (fluent interfaces), while external DSLs are separate languages that get parsed
The primary benefit is improved code comprehension and maintainability; the more ambitious benefit is business readability
Business-readable DSLs yield most benefits at far less cost than business-writable ones
DSLs don't add significant complexity beyond existing frameworks—they're essentially fancy facades that can make frameworks easier to use
money_quotes: A Domain Specific Language (DSL) is a computer programming language of limited expressiveness focused on a particular domain.
You get most of the benefit of business facing DSLs by doing enough to allow business people to be able to read the rules.
Getting DSLs to be business readable is far less effort than business writable, but yields most of the benefits.
That was the intent of COBOL, and I don't think there's any reason to think that DSLs will succeed where COBOL (and so many others failed).
What I think is important is that DSLs allow business people and developers to collaborate more effectively because they can talk about a common set of precise rules that are the executable code.
In essence a DSL is nothing more than a fancy facade over a framework.
posts.key_insight: DSLs are most valuable not as tools for non-programmers to write code, but as a shared language that enables business people and developers to collaborate around precise, executable rules.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces the concept of Language Workbenches, a term he coined in 2005 for tools that enable software development through multiple integrated domain-specific languages. These tools support Language-Oriented Programming by allowing developers to define DSL schemas, rich editing environments, and behavioral semantics. The key differentiator is their sophisticated editing environments that go beyond simple text editors, including projectional editors and illustrative programming approaches like spreadsheets. Fowler suggests these tools could significantly change the programming landscape, though they remain far from mainstream adoption.
key_points: Language workbenches enable Language-Oriented Programming by supporting multiple integrated DSLs for different areas of a system
The tools support three key capabilities: defining semantic model schemas, creating rich editing environments, and specifying behavioral semantics through interpretation or code generation
Editing environments are the distinguishing feature, ranging from text-based to projectional editors supporting structured text, diagrams, or both
Illustrative Programming weaves example execution into the editor, with spreadsheets being the most common example of this paradigm
money_quotes: They are one of the few things I feel could significantly change the programming landscape.

People have been making external DSLs for decades, but you edit these in a text editor. Language workbenches look to take this much further, approaching the level of modern PostIntelliJ IDEs, or even beyond.
When you edit a spreadsheet, the primary thing you see isn't the formulae that represents the behavioral definition, instead you see numbers that are calculated from the formulae.
This style of editing is startlingly different from our usual notions of a programming language, and could well be crucial to better engaging domain experts in software development.
The program and example data are woven together so as you edit the program you see the consequences immediately.
posts.key_insight: Language workbenches differentiate themselves from traditional DSL tools through rich editing environments that could fundamentally change how domain experts engage with software development.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that SOA, like all software design, should embrace evolutionary rather than planned design approaches. He challenges the assumption that enterprise service interfaces must be designed upfront because they're hard to change, noting that unpredictable change is inevitable even at enterprise scale. The key question is whether change is predictable—if not, planned design leads to the same chaos it seeks to prevent, often with significant sunk costs. He advocates for approaches like Consumer Driven Contracts and Guerilla SOA that build systems incrementally based on business value. The web itself serves as proof that evolutionary design can work at massive scale despite being messy.
key_points: Planned design only works when change is predictable; in enterprise contexts, unpredictability is doubled
Sunk costs in planned SOA designs often undermine the business value they were meant to produce
Consumer Driven Contracts enable evolutionary thinking about service interfaces
Guerilla SOA builds services through small, value-driven steps rather than big upfront architecture
The web proves evolutionary design can scale far beyond typical SOA efforts
money_quotes: The fundamental question I ask is 'is change predictable?' Only if change is predictable is a planned design approach valid.
If predictability is hard within the context of a single application, it's doubly hard across an enterprise.
If we use planned design in an unpredictable context we find that however good the plans are, they will be undermined by the unpredictable changes, leading to the same mess we are currently in.
The whole point of loose coupling is to make change easier.
The web is built on very loose coupling and lots of unpredictable changes. It is, in many ways, a mess—but it's a mess that works, delivering real value to lots of people every day.
posts.key_insight: Since enterprise change is inherently unpredictable, SOA must embrace evolutionary design—the web itself proves this messy approach delivers real value at scale.

posts.summary: Fowler critiques the traditional waterfall assumption that requirements must be discovered before construction begins. He argues that agile methods intentionally discover requirements during and after development, but leading web companies go even further by observing actual user behavior to generate feature ideas. This approach—watching what users actually do rather than what they say they want—provides more direct information about real needs. Fowler advocates for teams to build monitoring capabilities into their applications and use behavioral data to drive product decisions.
key_points: The word 'requirement' itself embeds waterfall assumptions about upfront discovery
Agile methods deliberately discover requirements during construction and after delivery
Web companies pioneer 'observed requirements' by analyzing user behavior logs to identify features
There's a significant gap between what people say they want and what they actually need and use
Building monitoring and analysis capabilities requires concentrated effort but offers huge potential
money_quotes: The word 'requirement' is inherently waterfallish
Agile methods violate this underlying assumption by intending to discover the 'requirements' during construction and after delivery
Build a new feature and see if people use it
Everyone knows how big the difference is between what people say they want and what people actually need and use
By watching what people actually do with your application, you can find out what actually happens with the software - which can give you much more direct information than other sources
posts.key_insight: Observing actual user behavior provides more reliable requirements information than asking users what they want, representing an evolution beyond even agile's iterative discovery approach.

posts.summary: Model Driven Software Development (MDSD) is a software development approach that uses graphical models and diagrams, typically in UML, to specify systems and generate code automatically. The approach evolved from CASE tools and graphical design notations, with proponents claiming it raises abstraction levels above programming languages. Fowler is skeptical of MDSD's future, arguing that graphical notations aren't inherently higher-level abstractions than code. He notes that projectional editing tools required for MDSD introduce pragmatic problems like source control difficulties. The field is plagued by terminological confusion, with the OMG trademarking many 'Model Driven' phrases, forcing practitioners to navigate naming carefully.
key_points: MDSD centers on building models via diagrammatic notations like UML and generating code from them
The approach evolved from CASE tools and has an ongoing community of dedicated practitioners
Graphical notations are not automatically higher abstractions than programming languages—it depends on specific cases
Projectional editing tools required for MDSD create practical problems, particularly around source control
Terminological confusion abounds due to OMG trademarks on phrases like 'Model Driven Development'
money_quotes: Most fans of MDSD base their enthusiasm on the basis that models are ipso facto a higher level abstraction than programming languages. I don't agree with that argument.
Sometimes graphical notations can be a better abstraction, but not always - it depends on the specific cases.
Although I've been involved, to some extent, in MDSD for most of my career, I'm rather skeptical of its future.
MDSD is surrounded by a terminological mess.
To use MDSD you need tools that support ProjectionalEditing, and these tools currently introduce a number of pragmatic issues in tooling - of which source control is the canonical example.
posts.key_insight: Graphical models are not inherently superior abstractions to code, and the practical tooling problems of model-driven approaches remain a significant barrier to adoption.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler provides an early preview of Microsoft's Oslo project, a Language Workbench announced at PDC 2008. Oslo consists of three components: M (a textual modeling language), Quadrant (a graphical design surface), and a repository for storing semantic models. Fowler is particularly impressed by the textual environment's approach of using compiled grammars that plug into text editors, maintaining true free-text editing rather than structured projectional editing. He notes Oslo occupies similar space to tools like Intentional Software and JetBrains MPS, but with interesting differences in its support for both textual and graphical DSLs. While cautiously optimistic, Fowler acknowledges Microsoft's track record of unfulfilled promises while seeing Oslo as potentially making DSLs much more prevalent.
key_points: Oslo provides a toolkit for defining new languages with three components: M for textual DSLs, Quadrant for graphical DSLs, and a SQL-accessible repository
The textual environment maintains true free-text editing unlike MPS's structured 'looks like text but isn't' approach, which Fowler has come to prefer
Microsoft originally intended only graphical tools but found they didn't work well for many problems, leading them to develop the textual components
The compiled grammar approach that plugs into text editors is praised as elegant compared to code generation approaches
Fowler wisely emphasizes using text files as authoritative source for version control rather than relying on shared repository collaboration
money_quotes: The marketing department will still use the same smarts that replaced 'Avalon and Indigo' with 'WPF and WCF'. I'm just hoping they'll rename 'Windows' to 'Windows Technology Foundation.'
When I first came across it, I rather liked the MPS notion of: 'it looks like text, but really it's a structured editor'. But recently I've begun to think that we lose a lot that way.
In the conversation Rebecca referred to this style as 'anecdotal testing' which is a phrase I must remember to steal.
The notion that we can all collaborate on a single shared database (the moral equivalent of a team editing one copy of its code on a shared drive) strikes me as close to irresponsible.
Code generation is all very well, but it always has this messy feel to it - and tends to lead to all sorts of ways to trip you up.
We do need to remember that all sorts of things were promised about Longhorn that never came to pass.
posts.key_insight: Language workbenches that maintain true free-text editing while providing rich tooling through compiled grammars offer a promising middle ground between the flexibility of external DSLs and the tooling support of structured editors.

posts.summary: A client complained that agile development caused difficulties too early in their project, but Fowler argues this early pain is actually one of agile's greatest benefits. The waterfall process defers problem discovery until late in projects when there's little time or energy to address them effectively. Iterative development intentionally flushes out problems early, giving teams more time to cope or the option to cancel before investing too much. Fowler recommends reflecting on past projects to identify how problems could have surfaced earlier.
key_points: Early difficulties in agile projects are a feature, not a bug—they represent problems being discovered when they can still be addressed
Waterfall's greatest flaw is deferring problem discovery until late in projects when options are limited
Iterative cycles intentionally surface problems early, providing time to either fix them or cancel before overinvesting
Teams should actively reflect on past projects to find ways to make problems appear earlier
money_quotes: This early pain is one of the great benefits of an agile or indeed any iterative development process.
Probably my greatest problem with it is how it tends to defer discovery of problems till late in the project, at which point there's little time or energy to deal with them effectively.
Iterative cycles try to flush out as many problems as possible as early as possible.
This gives you more time to cope, or at least raises the problems early enough to cancel before investing too much money and effort in a problematic project.
The more pain you get earlier, the better.
posts.key_insight: Early pain in software projects is valuable because discovering problems sooner gives you more options to address them or cut your losses.

posts.summary: Fowler describes an enterprise SOA challenge where consumer services need functionality that supplier services haven't yet exposed. The traditional approach creates bottlenecks as supplier teams have competing priorities. He presents a solution observed at a client: treating internal services as open source projects with designated custodians who review patches from consumer developers. This model reduces friction by letting consumer teams write their own changes while custodians maintain quality control. The approach mirrors open source governance, where reviewing patches requires less effort than implementing changes, and trusted contributors can eventually become committers.
key_points: In SOA environments, supplier service teams often can't prioritize features needed by consumer teams, creating organizational friction
The internal open source model lets consumer developers write patches for supplier services, with custodians reviewing and committing changes
Reviewing a patch is much less effort than making a change yourself, significantly reducing bottlenecks
Consumer developers can graduate to committer status once custodians trust their work, further reducing friction
Fancy service registry products can be replaced with simpler humane registries
money_quotes: They took a leaf out of the open source play-book and made all their services into internal open source systems.
Each service has a couple of custodians - people whose responsibility it is to keep the service in a healthy state.
Just like an open-source maintainer, the custodian receives the patch and reviews it to see if it's good enough to commit.
Reviewing a patch is much less effort than making a change yourself.
A consumer developer can be made a committer once the custodians are comfortable.
posts.key_insight: Applying open source governance patterns internally—with service custodians reviewing patches from consumer teams—reduces organizational bottlenecks in service-oriented architectures far more effectively than traditional ownership models.

posts.summary: Fowler critiques the SOA vision of automated service registries where systems magically discover and bind to services, arguing that in reality it's human programmers doing the looking up. He describes a project by colleagues that built a human-centered service registry based on a wiki. The registry combines easy human contribution with automated data gathering from source control, CI, issue tracking, and message buses. The key insight is that the most valuable registry information helps developers find other developers—who built it, who uses it, and who to contact for help.
key_points: Automated service discovery is mostly fantasy; humans are the ones who actually look up and consume services
A useful registry should be oriented around people, not expect manual updates, and make contribution effortless
Wikis enable easy human contribution but should be supplemented with automated data gathering
The most important registry questions are social: who worked on this, who should I contact, who knows the quirks
Combining source control commits, CI feeds, message bus traffic, and dependency data creates a rich picture without manual effort
money_quotes: Well computers may look clever occasionally, but I didn't particularly buy that idea.
While there might the be odd edge case for automated service lookup, I reckon twenty-two times out of twenty it'll be a human programmer who is doing the looking up.
People develop and use services, so orient it around people (sorry UDDI, thank you for playing).
Don't expect people to enter stuff to keep it up to date, people are busy enough as it is.
After all users' opinions are often more useful than providers.

The most important questions the human reader has are about the humans who have worked on the project: who are they, when did they work on this, who should I email, and where do I go for a really good caipirinha?
posts.key_insight: Service registries should be designed for human developers finding other human developers, not for automated system-to-system discovery.

posts.summary: Fowler proposes a method for measuring technical debt by asking teams to estimate how long a feature would have taken in a clean system versus actual effort, with the difference representing 'interest' paid. He acknowledges the technique has flaws—estimates are subjective and based on imaginary states—but argues the visibility it provides to non-technical staff may justify the overhead. The approach can inform decisions about paying down debt by comparing interest payments to the estimated principal cost of cleanup. He suggests capturing these estimates during retrospectives rather than forward-looking planning may be more practical.
key_points: Technical debt interest can be estimated as the difference between actual effort and effort in an ideal clean system
This measurement technique is inherently subjective since it's based on an imaginary system state
The value lies in making technical debt visible to non-technical stakeholders
Comparing accumulated interest to principal cost can inform whether to pay down specific debt
Retrospectives may be a better time to capture these estimates than forward planning
money_quotes: Sadly technical debt isn't like financial debt, so it's not easy to tell how far you are in hock (although we seem to have had some trouble with measuring the financial kind recently).
The statement of how long it would have taken on a clean system is an estimate based on an imaginary state - so is difficult to make objective.
The result may help project a picture of the state of the code-base in a way that's visible to non-technical staff.
I spent an extra day on this feature because of the bad state of the flipper module.
Comparing interest payments with the principal may help inform a decision about whether to pay off the principal.
Doing this estimate against recent completed work may be easier than forward estimates against future stories.
posts.key_insight: While measuring technical debt is inherently subjective, estimating the 'interest' paid on each feature can make the cost visible enough to inform decisions about cleanup investments.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the recurring question of whether DSLs can enable business people to write software without programmers, applying what he calls the 'COBOL inference' - the observation that COBOL aimed for the same goal and failed. He argues that programming requires a particular mindset for giving precise machine instructions and structuring complex programs, which is why disintermediation attempts consistently fail. However, he identifies a 'sweet spot': DSLs should be business-readable rather than business-writable, creating a rich communication channel between developers and domain experts. While business-writable DSLs are possible, the cost of creating proper editing environments and tooling is significantly higher.
key_points: The 'COBOL inference': attempts to eliminate programmers from coding have consistently failed throughout computing history
Programming requires a specific mindset for precise instruction-giving and structuring complex systems that resists disintermediation
The sweet spot for DSLs is business-readable rather than business-writable - programmers write code that business people can understand and review
Business-writable DSLs are possible but require expensive tooling investment for editing, error messages, debugging and testing
Spreadsheets are the notable exception to the COBOL inference, successfully enabling non-programmers to run significant business functions
money_quotes: I like to apply the COBOL inference to this line of thought. That is that one of the original aims of COBOL was to allow people to write software without programmers, and we know how that worked out.

I do think that programming involves a particular mind-set, an ability to both give precise instructions to a machine and the ability to structure a large amount of such instructions to make a comprehensible program.
The sweet spot, however is in making DSLs business-readable rather than business-writable.
If business people are able to look at the DSL code and understand it, then we can build a deep and rich communication channel between software development and the underlying domain.
It's also why many 'non-programming' environments end up breeding their own class of programmers-in-fact.
All over the world suprisingly big business functions are run off the back of Excel. Serious programmers tend to look down their noses at these, but we need to take them more seriously.
posts.key_insight: The value of DSLs lies not in eliminating programmers but in creating readable artifacts that bridge the communication gap between technical and business domains.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that academia's emphasis on publication history actively discourages researchers from gaining practical industry experience, creating a harmful divide. A post-doc explained that spending time in industry would create a fatal gap in his publication record, ruining his academic job prospects. Fowler contends that mining effective techniques from real-world software development experience is valuable academic work, but is dismissed because it lacks novelty. He proposes that academic departments should include faculty with substantial industry experience and encourage regular rotation between academic and industrial roles to improve knowledge transfer and make research more practically relevant.
key_points: Academia penalizes researchers who take industry positions by viewing gaps in publication history as career-ending
The patterns community's focus on documenting proven techniques is dismissed academically because it lacks novelty
Drawing lessons from practical experience should be considered valuable academic work but is currently devalued
Even highly accomplished industry engineers struggle to obtain meaningful academic positions
Regular rotation between industry and academia would improve knowledge transfer and research relevance
money_quotes: A year or two in industry would create a gap in your publication history that would be lethal to your job prospects.
The academics I respect are, I'm told, not highly regarded within academia because the things that I count as useful are usually dismissed by the academic community.
My work, for example, is generally dismissed because all I do is write about stuff that is old hat.
By devaluing it the academic world is ignoring a fruitful avenue to improve the capabilities of our profession.
I find it hard to believe that students wouldn't gain an enormous amount from being taught by people with a long and thoughtful experience in the profession they are entering.
People are the key to good knowledge transfer.
posts.key_insight: Academia's obsession with novelty and publication records creates perverse incentives that prevent researchers from gaining the practical experience needed to produce genuinely useful research.

posts.summary: Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs) are computer languages targeted at particular problem domains rather than general-purpose programming. Fowler distinguishes between internal DSLs, which leverage a host language to create domain-focused syntax (popular in Ruby and Lisp), and external DSLs, which have custom syntax requiring full parsers (common in Unix tradition). DSLs can be textual or graphical, with graphical versions requiring Language Workbenches that some believe could transform programming. Implementation approaches include interpretation and code generation, with interpretation being simpler but code generation sometimes necessary.
key_points: DSLs target specific problem domains rather than general software problems, with examples including CSS, SQL, regular expressions, and build tools like make and rake
Internal DSLs use a host language creatively to feel domain-specific (also called embedded DSLs or Fluent Interfaces), while external DSLs have custom syntax requiring full parsers
XML configurations often become external DSLs despite XML's syntax being poorly suited for this purpose
Graphical DSLs exist but require Language Workbenches, which some believe could profoundly change programming
DSLs can be implemented via interpretation (easier) or code generation (sometimes essential)
money_quotes: The basic idea of a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) is a computer language that's targeted to a particular kind of problem, rather than a general purpose language that's aimed at any kind of software problem.
Internal DSLs are particular ways of using a host language to give the host language the feel of a particular language.
External DSLs have their own custom syntax and you write a full parser to process them.
Many XML configurations have ended up as external DSLs, although XML's syntax is badly suited to this purpose.
Language Workbenches are less common but many people think they have the potential to profoundly improve the way we do programming.
posts.key_insight: DSLs represent a spectrum from internal approaches that bend host languages to feel domain-specific, to external approaches with custom parsers, each with distinct trade-offs in implementation complexity and expressive power.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that domain-specific languages deserve to be treated as a distinct field of study rather than a simplified subset of general-purpose programming language research. He observes that DSLs operate by fundamentally different rules: they don't need user-defined abstractions, they process much smaller inputs, and they prioritize readability for non-programmers over expressiveness. The post advocates for using Semantic Models as intermediate representations and emphasizes the importance of language composability. Fowler positions himself as writing a practitioner-oriented book to fill a gap in existing academic literature.
key_points: DSLs differ fundamentally from general-purpose languages in that they choose abstractions for you rather than letting you define new ones
The smaller scale of DSL scripts changes the trade-offs in parsing tools and implementation approaches
Semantic Models are valuable for separating parsing from semantics, unlike the code-generation-from-grammar approach common in language tooling
DSLs must be designed for readability by non-programmers and ease of parsing, not expressiveness
Language composability is crucial for DSLs since individual DSLs won't solve entire problems
money_quotes: The rules for DSLs are different to the rules for general purpose languages - and this applies on multiple dimensions.
I think the lack of ability to define new abstractions is one of the things that distinguishes DSLs from general purpose languages.
I like topics like this that are well-trodden but not well written about.
DSLs are written with the expectation that each individual DSL won't solve the whole problem at hand and often you need to combine DSLs.
The challenge of DSLs is to write a language that is readable to the intended audience (which may well include non-programmers) and also should be easy to parse.
I think Semantic Models are a really valuable tool in simplifying the use of a DSL, allowing you to separate the parsing from the semantics.
posts.key_insight: DSLs require fundamentally different design principles than general-purpose languages because they optimize for readability and composability rather than expressiveness and abstraction-building.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler examines rules engines as an alternative computational model based on production rules (if-then statements evaluated in any order by the engine). While the model naturally fits certain problems like insurance premium calculations, Fowler expresses skepticism about rules engine products based on his experiences. The promise that business people can specify rules without programmers rarely works in practice. The biggest challenge is that rule interactions, especially with chaining, create implicit program flow that becomes very hard to maintain and debug. Fowler suggests limiting rules, avoiding chaining, rigorous testing, and often building a narrow domain-specific rules engine rather than using a general product.
key_points: Rules engines use a production rule system where conditions and actions can be written in any order, with the engine deciding evaluation order
The promise of enabling business people to write rules without programmers sounds plausible but rarely works in practice
Chaining—where one rule's action affects another rule's condition—creates implicit behavior that is extremely difficult to reason about and debug
Systems with enough rules to need sophisticated algorithms like Rete probably have too many rules to be understood
A domain-specific, hand-rolled rules approach within a narrow context is often preferable to using a rules engine product
money_quotes: The subtlety is that rules can be written in any order, the engine decides when to evaluate them using whatever order makes sense for it

Often the central pitch for a rules engine is that it will allow the business people to specify the rules themselves, so they can build the rules without involving programmers. As so often, this can sound plausible but rarely works out in practice
For all the faults of imperative code, it's relatively easy to understand how it works
With a production rule system, it seems easy to get to a point where a simple change in one place causes lots unintended consequences, which rarely work out well
Any system with enough rules to need sophisticated algorithms to get good performance probably has too many rules to be understood
This is the dark side of leaving the imperative computational model
posts.key_insight: Rules engines trade the explicit control flow of imperative code for implicit behavior that scales poorly—the very sophistication that makes them powerful makes them unmaintainable.

posts.summary: Fowler identifies a pattern where teams adopt Scrum but neglect technical practices, leading to accumulating technical debt and declining productivity. He argues that while Scrum deliberately omits technical practices from its scope, this doesn't mean they're unimportant—prominent Scrum advocates have always emphasized their necessity. The problem may stem from Scrum's popularity rather than inherent flaws, as semantic diffusion affects all successful methodologies. Fowler recommends combining Scrum with XP's technical practices and reminds readers that ultimately teams succeed or fail, not methodologies.
key_points: Scrum's deliberate omission of technical practices doesn't mean they're unimportant, but teams often misinterpret this
Technical debt from poor internal quality will cripple productivity regardless of process
XP's technical practices complement Scrum well and provide a strong foundation
Popularity breeds semantic diffusion—the same fate awaits Lean as it gains traction
Teams succeed or fail based on their execution, not the methodology they nominally follow
money_quotes: If you make that mistake you'll soon find your productivity dragged down because it's much harder to add new features than you'd like.
XPers often joke, with some justification, that Scrum is just XP without the technical practices that make it work.
I always like to point out that it isn't methodologies that succeed or fail, it's teams that succeed or fail.
Teams that fail will probably fail whatever methodology they mis-apply, teams that succeed will build their practices on good ideas.
That doesn't make Lean (or Scrum) worthless, it just reminds us Individuals and Interactions are more valuable than Processes and Tools.
posts.key_insight: Adopting Scrum's project management practices without equally strong technical practices leads to crippling technical debt—process frameworks cannot substitute for engineering discipline.

posts.summary: DSL advocates must recognize that successful DSLs evolve over time, requiring strategies to migrate scripts written in earlier versions. This challenge parallels library versioning but is more acute for external DSLs since refactoring tools cannot help. Fowler presents two migration strategies: incremental migration (writing small migration scripts for each change, similar to evolutionary database design) and model-based migration (maintaining multiple parsers that populate a semantic model, then generating updated scripts). Both approaches have tradeoffs, and migration can be triggered manually or automatically if scripts record their version.
key_points: DSL definitions are essentially published interfaces with the same versioning challenges as libraries
External DSLs face harder migration than internal DSLs because refactoring tools cannot assist
Incremental migration works best with many small migration scripts rather than one large one—ten scripts are faster to write than one
Model-based migration uses multiple parsers feeding a semantic model, then generates updated scripts from that model
Model-based approaches risk losing non-semantic information like comments during transformation
money_quotes: One danger that DSL advocates need to guard against is the notion that first you design a DSL, then people use it.
Your DSL definition is essentially a PublishedInterface and you have to deal with the consequences just the same.
Don't create just one migration script to migrate from version 1 to 2, instead create at least 10 scripts.
Migrations are much easier to write if they are small, and it's easy to chain multiple migrations together.
As a result you'll be much faster writing ten scripts than one.
This is exacerbated if there's too much smarts in the parser, but then the need to migrate this way may help encourage the parsers to stay dumb - which is Good Thing.
posts.key_insight: Successful DSLs must be designed for evolution from the start, with explicit migration strategies that favor many small incremental changes over monolithic version jumps.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes an architectural pattern called Eager Read Derivation, inspired by Greg Young's QCon talk. The pattern separates read and write operations by pre-computing derived data during writes rather than calculating it during reads. When updates occur, the domain model runs all necessary derivation logic and populates specialized reporting databases via message queues. Read requests then hit these pre-populated databases directly without involving domain logic. This approach improves performance and scalability in high-demand distributed systems, though it introduces eventual consistency rather than strong consistency.
key_points: Domain logic can be classified into validations, consequences, and derivations - each applying differently to reads versus writes
Eager read derivation pre-computes all read derivations at write time and stores results in reporting databases structured to match read requests
Read operations bypass the main database entirely, pulling pre-computed data directly from reporting databases with no domain logic involved
The asynchronous message-based approach improves scalability but creates an inconsistency window where immediate reads may not reflect recent updates
In distributed systems, you must choose between consistency and availability - eventual consistency is often the pragmatic choice for high-volume systems
money_quotes: When we use a Domain Model, we use it because it contains complicated domain logic.
Eager read derivation does something quite different. Here reads don't touch the main database at all.
This asynchronous scheme is eventually consistent, but not strongly consistent. But this is the nature of the beast: in a distributed system you can get consistency or availability but not both.
Doing eager evaluation is hardly that new. The technique is older than me (possibly even older than Ron Jeffries) and most high-volume websites populate databases with derived data all the time.
But it's not a technique I see considered as often as it should be, and I liked the aggressive way Greg had used it in his design.
posts.key_insight: Pre-computing derived data during writes and storing it in read-optimized reporting databases can dramatically improve read performance in high-demand distributed systems, at the cost of eventual rather than strong consistency.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on a decade-old Thoughtworks project that has successfully avoided becoming a legacy morass through good design and continuous improvement. The project, which started with J2EE in 1998, has survived EJB removal, offshore transitions, and significant team turnover while remaining productive. The team evolved their acceptance testing approach from story-to-test tracing to a coherent specification-by-example suite. Most notably, new developer onboarding time dropped from one year to three months, demonstrating improved code quality over time.
key_points: Long-term business value delivery enabled by well-designed code is the hallmark of successful software development
Acceptance test suites should describe application behavior holistically rather than trace one-to-one with user stories, reducing duplication and complexity
Developer onboarding time serves as a practical metric for code quality and design health
A ten-year-old codebase can improve rather than decay if the team continuously invests in its design
Good design's purpose is enabling continued rapid development over time, not just initial elegance
money_quotes: The fact that they are still adding useful business value after ten years is a big dollop of kudos.
They are able to rapidly add new features when needed so haven't fallen into the typical morass of a legacy app.

A few years ago, if they wanted to take on someone new they wanted that person committed for at least a year, so they could get contributions that would be worthwhile after coming up to speed on the code base. Now that time is down to three months.
For me the key purpose of good design is that it allows you to continue working rapidly with the code.
Assessing how long it takes a developer to be productive with a code base is a good way to sense this design quality.
posts.key_insight: The true measure of good software design is not initial elegance but whether a codebase remains productive and improvable over years of development and team turnover.

posts.summary: Computer systems often assume data consistency, but some domains require capturing contradictory information. Drawing from healthcare work, Fowler explains how blood type records from different hospitals might conflict, yet both must be preserved for clinical integrity. The solution is modeling observations as first-class objects with metadata about when, who, and how they were recorded. Observations can be rejected but never deleted, maintaining an evidence web that tracks how information influenced decisions. This pattern acknowledges that real-world data is messy and systems must sometimes reflect that complexity rather than enforce artificial consistency.
key_points: Contradictory data should sometimes be preserved rather than resolved, especially when we don't know which record is correct
Observations should be modeled as full objects with provenance metadata, not simple attributes on entities
Rejecting an observation marks it as believed-never-true but preserves it and links to the rejecting evidence
Observations form a web of evidence where rejecting one may cascade questions to dependent observations
Recording absence of something can be as diagnostically important as recording presence
money_quotes: But sometimes computer systems should record contradictory data and help humans deal with that.

Without further investigation we don't know which one is correct. Even if we test again and confirm one of them, we can't just throw away the bad one as it may have been the basis for further clinical action.
The key to handling this issue is to represent my blood type not as an attribute of a person class, but as a fully fledged class in its own right - which we called observation.
Often observing the absence of things is crucial in a diagnostic process.
Rejecting an observation says that we believe it was never true. We never delete the old observation, instead we mark it as rejected.
An important property of information is that it's used to guide behavior.
Observations thus form a web of evidence that we can examine as we learn more about the patient.
We mostly program in a world that we assume is consistent. But there are times where we have to step away from that comfortable assumption.
posts.key_insight: When reality is inherently contradictory or uncertain, systems should model observations as first-class objects with full provenance rather than forcing premature consistency.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces the concept of 'lay programmer' to describe people who program without identifying as programmers, such as heavy spreadsheet users. He argues that professional programmers shouldn't dismiss these domain experts but should instead find ways to better engage them in software development. The post advocates for creating tools that are accessible to lay programmers while still producing well-structured, maintainable code.
key_points: Lay programmers are people who program intensively (like spreadsheet users) without identifying as programmers or investing in programming skills
Professional programmers often look down on lay programmers, but this attitude is counterproductive
Lay programmers possess valuable domain expertise about what software should accomplish
The industry needs tools that bridge accessibility for lay programmers with proper software engineering principles
money_quotes: I use the term lay programmer to mean someone who is programming without thinking themselves as a programmer.
Someone who spends a large part of her day working on spreadsheets is doing programming, often very intense programming.
It's easy for professional programmers to get sniffy at lay programmers, but lay programmers usually are domain experts who know a great deal about what a program should do.

Our challenge is to think of ways to engage them more effectively in software development, and provide tools that are easy for them to use, but also capable of being well structured programs that can evolve efficiently.
posts.key_insight: The gap between domain expertise and programming skill represents an opportunity for better tooling, not a reason for professional programmers to dismiss non-programmers who code.

posts.summary: This brief post defines 'Unforgiven Contract' as a term coined by Neal Ford for contracts with exceptionally harsh penalties. The metaphor references the climactic scene in the film Unforgiven where Clint Eastwood's character threatens extreme consequences to anyone who opposes him. Ford applies this to NDAs and similar legal agreements that impose disproportionately severe repercussions for violations.
key_points: The term 'Unforgiven Contract' describes legal agreements with unusually strong penalties
The metaphor comes from the movie Unforgiven's climactic threat scene
Neal Ford coined the term specifically in reference to NDAs
The concept captures agreements where consequences far exceed typical enforcement
money_quotes: My colleague Neal Ford coined this term to refer to a contract (specifically an NDA) with particularly strong penalties, since he felt it was the legal equivalent to these threats.
posts.key_insight: Some contracts are designed with penalties so severe they function more like threats than legal agreements.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reports on finally seeing Intentional Software's Domain Workbench after years of secretive development, calling it a Language Workbench that enables building comprehensive Domain Specific Languages with projectional editing. The tool allows multiple projections of the same semantic model (XML, custom syntax, Ruby, diagrams) that are all editable and synchronized. A key innovation is combining live execution with program definition, similar to spreadsheets, making it powerful for lay programmers. After a demo at DSL DevCon that drew comparisons to the 'Mother of all Demos,' Fowler cautiously notes that while the technology is impressive, no production systems have been built with it yet, and real validation only comes from deployment.
key_points: The Intentional Domain Workbench is a projectional editing environment that supports multiple synchronized views of the same semantic model, allowing users to switch between XML, custom syntax, diagrams, and code representations
Live execution integrated with program definition—like spreadsheets—may be key to making DSLs accessible to lay programmers
The system handles version control at the tree level rather than text, allowing conflicts to be committed as data structures that can be resolved later by appropriate developers
Representational transformations use many small pipelined transforms rather than monolithic ones, similar to multi-stage compilers
Despite the impressive demo that 'exploded brains' at DSL DevCon, no production systems have been built yet, and real understanding only comes from deploying to production
money_quotes: Only Intentional would make such a release and not bother to tell anyone.
The workbench can handle editable diagrammatic representations.
Combining example execution with program definition is one of the features of spreadsheets - and may be a reason why spreadsheets have become so successful as an environment for LayProgrammers.
Unlike with text files they don't mess up your text - you have a real data structure present, so you can find the conflicts and fix them.
As any agilist knows you never really understand something till you deploy it into production every week.
I don't know whether these workbenches will change the face of programming as we know it, after all I once thought Smalltalk was going to be our future.
Two words to say about the Electronics demo for Intentional Software: HOLY CRAPOLA. That's it, my brain has finally exploded.
This is like seeing a 100-mpg carburetor: OMG someone is going to buy this and put it in a vault!
posts.key_insight: Language workbenches that combine live execution with projectional editing across multiple synchronized representations have transformative potential, but impressive demos must be validated through real production deployment.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler addresses a controversy at the 2009 GoGaRuCo Ruby conference where a presenter used sexually suggestive images of women in a technical talk. While acknowledging the Rails community's valuable anti-corporate edginess, Fowler argues this particular incident crossed a line by alienating women who face real sexism in tech. He criticizes the Rails leadership's dismissive response and warns that tolerating such behavior could create a feedback loop driving talented people away. Fowler calls for a community that maintains its rebellious spirit while being genuinely inclusive of women based on their programming ability, not their tolerance for offense.
key_points: The presenter's intent doesn't matter—what matters is whether the community cares that women felt marginalized and uncomfortable
Most men in software underestimate ongoing sexism; women regularly experience degradation their male colleagues don't witness
The Rails community's anti-corporate edginess is valuable, but this incident targeted the wrong people—women fighting sexism, not suits
Community silence implies acceptance; leadership's dismissive response is more problematic than the original presentation
Creating environments that require 'thick skin' excludes people based on offense tolerance rather than programming ability
money_quotes: I can't choose whether someone is offended by my actions. I can choose whether I care.
The issue is no longer the presentation, but the reaction of the community to this event.
Nearly every one can tell me recent stories where they were clearly expected to feel degraded and belittled because of their gender.
How about a community where women are valued for their ability to program and not by the thickness of their skin?
The consequence of a society that is tolerant of nastiness and bullying is one where only the thick-skinned need apply.
If something questionable happens and people remain silent, that is an implicit acceptance of that event.
You might be surprised by my personal attitude to sexuality. But the point is not about judging various standards, it's about whether we want to make a group of people feel alienated.
posts.key_insight: A community's character is defined not by individual incidents but by leadership's response to them—dismissing legitimate concerns about exclusion signals what behavior is acceptable going forward.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the criticism that dynamic languages like Ruby require so many runtime type checks that developers effectively build their own type system. His team gathered empirical data from 11 real-world Ruby projects to test this claim. The data shows type checks (is_a?, kind_of?, instance_of?) are extremely rare, averaging roughly one per 500-2500 lines of code. Most uses occur when handling 'liberal input' in DSL-style APIs where methods accept multiple types for readability. The conclusion firmly refutes the accusation: explicit type checking is uncommon and was considered bad form even in Smalltalk circles.
key_points: Empirical data from 11 production Ruby projects shows dynamic type checks are extremely rare (1 per 500-2500 LOC)
Type checking in dynamic languages was considered bad form even in historical Smalltalk communities
Most legitimate uses of type checks occur in DSL contexts where methods accept multiple input types for readability
Other potential type-check methods (respond_to?, ===) were found to be no more common than the ones measured
money_quotes: The moral of this data is that you shouldn't expect to see a lot of type check calls in your ruby code base.
It was generally considered bad form in Smalltalk circles I inhabited too.
Most uses are those of dealing with liberal input - eg where a method parameter can be a string, symbol, or array.
These crop up in DSLish situations where you want liberal input for the high readability.
posts.key_insight: The claim that dynamic language programmers end up writing their own type systems through excessive type checks is empirically false—well-written Ruby code rarely uses explicit type checking.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on the distinctive 'we favor x over y' format used in the Agile Manifesto and explains why it works so well. The key insight is that both sides of each value statement must be genuinely valuable—the format loses power if the unfavored item is obviously bad. The best comparative values force hard choices between desirable things, making them effective for articulating what makes a philosophy distinctive. Fowler also emphasizes brevity as crucial to impact, noting the manifesto's four values and twelve principles kept it punchy. He shares an example of comparative values he wrote to describe Thoughtworks' culture, demonstrating the format's versatility for organizational philosophy.
key_points: The unfavored values in comparative statements must also be valuable—'solving world hunger over slavery' carries no weight because no one supports slavery
A good test is whether someone could reasonably and proudly support the reversed position
Brevity amplifies impact—the manifesto's four values had more punch than forty-six would have
Comparative values highlight contrast between philosophies and force hard choices between desirable things
The format works for organizational values too, not just methodology manifestos
money_quotes: The most important of these is that the unfavored values are valuable too.
The format works when both left and right sides of the 'over' are valuable things that most people want.
The harder choice there is between right and left, the better the value statement.
A good mental test is to imagine someone reversing each value statement, indeed reversing all that you have in the set.
There should be people that you can imagine proudly and reasonably supporting that opposite position.
I'm sure the manifesto would have not had the impact it did if it had forty-six value statements.
This usefulness comes directly from the fact that you are making hard choices between things that are all desirable.
posts.key_insight: Comparative value statements derive their power from forcing genuine trade-offs between desirable things, not from contrasting good with bad.

posts.summary: Thoughtworks conducted 41 Ruby projects between 2006-2008, finding it a highly viable platform for web development. Technical leads overwhelmingly approved of choosing Ruby (36 to 5), citing productivity gains of 25-100% compared to Java/.NET, though they cautioned that social resistance from clients was the biggest red flag. While Ruby's execution speed is slow, this proved irrelevant for most I/O-bound web applications. The company learned important lessons about testing strategies, deployment approaches, and managing the meta-programming learning curve.
key_points: Ruby delivered measurable productivity gains, with most projects reporting 25-100% improvement over mainstream platforms, though teams need time to overcome the 'Improvement Ravine' when starting
The biggest barrier to Ruby adoption was social resistance from client organizations, not technical limitations
Ruby's slow execution speed was irrelevant for nearly all projects since web applications are typically I/O-bound by database access
Teams experience a predictable progression with meta-programming: from 'scary and bad' to overuse, eventually settling on judicious application
Ruby works well as a supplementary scripting language for Java/.NET projects, supporting polyglot programming approaches
money_quotes: Ruby has a philosophy of an environment that gives a talented developer more leverage, rather than trying to protect a less talented developer from errors.

The difference is between efficiency risk and delivery risk. A team with dynamic language experience but little Ruby experience will be slower initially (efficiency risk) but a team without any dynamic language experience can produce a knotty code base that could risk the overall delivery.
Choosing a development platform is never a simple choice, particularly since it usually is more of a social choice than a technological choice.
The analogy I like best for these kinds of techniques is that they are like prescription drugs. They are very valuable in small amounts but you need to ensure that you don't overdose.
When learning something new it's common to over-use it at some stage because without crossing the line it's hard to know where that line is.
The point of these frameworks is to improve productivity by making the easier to do common things. It allows a team to focus its effort on the few cases that really matter.
posts.key_insight: Technology platform choices are fundamentally social decisions, and Ruby's value proposition lies in amplifying skilled developers rather than constraining less capable ones.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that spreadsheets represent the most common programming language because they're used by non-programmers for surprisingly complex tasks. He introduces 'Illustrative Programming' as a concept where program execution is fused with its definition, making the output visible while editing. While this approach helps lay programmers understand behavior through concrete examples, it tends to de-emphasize program structure, leading to unmaintainable systems. The challenge for future environments is to maintain good program structure while preserving the illustrative benefits. Fowler sees potential in Language Workbenches that could build illustrative DSLs to engage lay programmers without sacrificing code quality.
key_points: Spreadsheets succeed as programming tools because they fuse program definition with execution, showing calculated results rather than formulae
Illustrative programming differs from REPLs by putting examples front-and-center rather than as an exploration tool
The main weakness is that illustrative environments de-emphasize program structure, leading to copy-paste programming and unmaintainable systems
The inability to create new abstractions in UI builders causes programs to become tangled as they can only illustrate built-in concepts
Language Workbenches offer potential for building illustrative DSLs that could engage lay programmers while maintaining structure
money_quotes: Whenever someone talks about some new environment that's going to allow people to specify complex behavior 'without programming' I mention COBOL, which was originally designed to get rid of programmers.
We've used illustrative programming for years, but we've not paid enough attention to it.
The program retreats to the background, peeping out only when we want to explore a part of the illustration.
They do a good job of revealing what a program does, but de-emphasizes program structure.

The challenge for future illustrative programming environments is to help develop a well structured program behind the illustrations - although the illustrations may also make us rethink what a well structured program is.
APIs in programming languages scale much better, with several orders of magnitude more density before they become hard to navigate.
posts.key_insight: The power of spreadsheets lies in fusing program definition with execution output, but this illustrative approach's emphasis on visible results over program structure creates a fundamental tension that future programming environments must resolve.

posts.summary: The post introduces the concept of a 'Request Stream Map' as a technique for exposing unnecessary complexity in enterprise systems, particularly ESBs. Erik Dörnenburg used this visualization approach to demonstrate how a simple request flows through multiple application layers, printing out pages of code and creating a diagram that made the overhead visible. The technique is analogous to Value Stream Mapping in agile, but applied to technical architecture. The visualization helped convince stakeholders to remove an unnecessary ESB from a system by making its cost apparent.
key_points: ESBs are often unnecessary complexity that teams should work to remove from systems
A Request Stream Map traces how data for a request/response moves through application layers
Visualizing the full code path helps identify excessive layering and architectural waste
Layering is essential but excess layering can be as problematic as too little
When transformations are necessary, prefer multiple small transformations over one large one
money_quotes: Hang around my colleagues at Thoughtworks and you soon get the impression that the only good Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) is a dead ESB.
Jim Webber refers to them as Erroneous Spaghetti Boxes.
Erik printed out all the code that you needed to read to understand how this would work - which ran to several pages.
Layering is an essential tool for building software applications. But like most essential things in life, excess can be almost as much of a problem as too little.
A visualization like this (or the multiple pages of code) can help you find where 'just enough' is.
If you do need to transform data from one form to another, it's usually better to do a few little transformations than one big transformation.
posts.key_insight: Visualizing the full path of a request through your system layers exposes unnecessary complexity and helps you find the 'just enough' level of architecture.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his first impressions of the HTC Magic Android phone after a month of use, having received it at Google IO. He explains why he moved away from his Nokia E61 and why iPhone and Blackberry weren't options for him. Overall he finds the Android device satisfactory, praising its physical design, battery life, app ecosystem, and Google service integration. However, he notes frustrations with local HTML browsing limitations, timezone handling in the calendar, and the soft keyboard compared to physical keys.
key_points: The phone's physical design, screen quality, and battery life meet his needs for mobile web browsing
Google service integration (Gmail, Calendar) works seamlessly with the device
The inability to browse local HTML files via file:// URLs is a significant workflow limitation
Timezone handling in calendar apps remains a universal unsolved problem for travelers
The soft keyboard is inferior to physical keyboards for typing
money_quotes: The short statement is that I do like the htc magic android.
Like every other calendar app on the planet, Google calendar suffers from TimeZoneUncertainty.
I tried a Blackberry storm for a few days, but (how about this for irony) the email was no good for me.
I'm not allowing myself any such fun until I get the book finished.
posts.key_insight: Early Android phones offered a viable alternative to iPhone for users locked into incompatible carrier plans, though they came with their own set of workflow limitations.

posts.summary: Fowler documents his procedure for squashing multiple Mercurial commits into a single commit using the mq (mercurial queues) extension. He was reorganizing files and folders, wanting to checkpoint his work along the way but present a clean single commit in the final history. The post walks through cloning repositories, importing commits as patches, folding them together, and pulling the result back. He notes this preserves file move history, which was important for his use case. The procedure involves multiple repository clones as a safety measure before editing history.
key_points: Mercurial encourages using multiple repositories where other systems would use branches - it's 'the Mercurial way of working'
Git-style diffs in Mercurial help track file moves properly, which is important for maintaining history across reorganizations
Cloning before editing history provides a safety net until you verify the squash worked correctly
Version control attention has evolved from audit (going back to old revisions) to collaboration to narrative (how a codebase changes over time)
money_quotes: Making a single commit makes it easier to understand what happened - particularly since moving files tends to complicate looking at repository logs.
It's important to clone at this point because I was about to edit history, so wanted to keep the original history handy until I knew it had worked.
Mercurial encourages different repositories where other systems, eg git or svn, would use different branches.
Early on the primary and only purpose was audit - to be able to safely go back to older revision - mainly to diagnose problems.
Now there's more attention to using them to provide a narrative of how a code base changes - hence the desire for history rewriting commands like this.
This need is built on top of the other two, but introduces new capabilities and new tensions.
posts.key_insight: Version control has evolved beyond audit and collaboration to serve as a narrative tool for understanding how codebases change, creating new capabilities and tensions around history rewriting.

posts.summary: This brief post documents and attributes the famous Phil Karlton quote about the two hardest problems in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things. Fowler explains he created this page because he couldn't find a satisfactory URL for the quote. The post has evolved over time to collect clever variations on the joke from various programmers on Twitter. It serves primarily as a reference page and celebration of programming humor rather than a technical analysis.
key_points: The original quote by Phil Karlton identifies cache invalidation and naming as computing's two hardest problems
The joke format has spawned numerous variations that poke fun at different programming challenges
The distributed systems variation cleverly demonstrates message ordering problems through its own structure
The earliest internet attribution traces to Tim Bray's blog around 1996-7
The meta-commentary suggests the proliferation of variations hints that programming genuinely is difficult
money_quotes: There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.
There are 2 hard problems in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-1 errors.
There are only two hard problems in distributed systems: 2. Exactly-once delivery 1. Guaranteed order of messages 2. Exactly-once delivery
there's two hard problems in computer science: we only have one joke and it's not funny.
There are so many variations on the 'there are only two hard problems in computer programming...' joke that I'm starting to suspect that programming isn't actually very easy.
posts.key_insight: The enduring popularity and endless variations of this joke reveal a truth about software development: naming things well and managing cached state remain genuinely difficult problems that every programmer encounters.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler applies Kent Beck's Composed Method pattern to regular expressions, arguing that complex regexes should be broken into well-named smaller pieces rather than left as monolithic strings or merely commented. He demonstrates progressive refactoring of a hotel points regex, showing how extracting named constants for logical chunks makes the pattern readable without diving into regex syntax. The post explores variations including separating whitespace tokens and creating a helper method to join patterns. Fowler concludes that code should be readable without mental parsing, and composition works whether using raw regex or fluent interfaces.
key_points: The Composed Method pattern that works for methods applies equally well to regular expressions
Comments are not bad, but self-documenting structure through naming is usually a better option
Breaking regexes into named string constants allows readers to understand structure before diving into syntax details
Programming abstractions like helper methods can further clean up repetitive patterns like whitespace handling
Named capture groups and fluent interfaces help but don't replace the fundamental benefit of composition
money_quotes: People can read your programs much more quickly and accurately if they can understand them in detail, then chunk those details into higher level structures.
Comments are not bad - but there are often better options. I always try to write code that doesn't need comments, usually by good naming and structure.
Code should not need to be figured out, it should be just read.
The point of composition is that it breaks the overall regex into small pieces that are easier to understand.
I've broken down the pattern into logical chunks and put them together again at the end.
posts.key_insight: The same composition principle that makes methods readable—breaking them into well-named smaller pieces—applies to regular expressions and other code structures that tend toward unreadable complexity.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler analyzes how software and internet tools contributed to Barack Obama's 2008 presidential victory, particularly his primary win over Hillary Clinton. The post traces the evolution of political organizing from Howard Dean's 2004 campaign through Obama's innovative fusion of peer-to-peer volunteer networks with mass-organization directed from campaign leadership. Key software systems included Blue State Digital's MyBarackObama.com for volunteer coordination and The VAN (Voter Activation Network) for voter data management and canvassing. The campaign's organizational innovation was shifting paid staffers from direct organizing to recruiting and training volunteer organizers, dramatically expanding reach. Fowler concludes that these software-enabled changes in organizing represent a meaningful way to increase everyday citizens' influence on politics.
key_points: The Obama campaign fused peer-to-peer volunteer organizing with mass-organization leadership direction, combining grassroots energy with strategic focus
The VAN voter database enabled sophisticated canvassing by tracking voter information, preferences, and voting history while simplifying turf cutting and walk packet preparation
Paid staffers shifted from organizing volunteers directly to recruiting and training volunteer organizers - what Zack Exley calls 'splitting the atom' of campaign reach
Neighbor to Neighbor extended organizing tools to volunteers in non-battleground states, enabling 'activist arbitrage' where California volunteers could call Florida voters
The campaign's 13-million-person email list became a powerful tool, with emails providing background context and reasoning rather than just asks
money_quotes: What really did it for Dean was the 2nd quarter of 2003 - he raised more ($8m) than the Big Money candidates Kerry ($5m) and Edwards ($5m). It was the watershed moment when online supporters learned their power to put an insurgent ahead of the front runner.

Along comes this campaign to take back the country for ordinary human beings, and the best way you can do that is through the Net. We listen. We pay attention. If I give a speech and the blog people don't like it, next time I change the speech.
Opponents don't want to waste their time doing insignificant damage to the other side. They'd rather go do something for their own candidate.
Democracy relies on the engagement of everyday people in the way the country is run. It's too easy for layers of bureaucracy and money to get between The People and their government.
Agile software people like me argue that open access to project plans enable everyone to get involved - which boosts both the effectiveness of the planning and the motivation of those doing the work.
It's just about telling people what to do, but also why.
posts.key_insight: Software tools that democratize organizing capabilities - allowing volunteers to do work previously reserved for paid staff - can fundamentally shift the power dynamics in political campaigns and potentially in democracy itself.

posts.summary: A self-initializing fake is a test double pattern for remote services that records responses on first call and replays them subsequently. Unlike traditional stubs that require manual setup, this pattern captures real service responses automatically. The approach handles cache invalidation by simply not attempting it—saved responses remain static. When remote data changes, teams can either accept stale data for tests or create validation suites that verify saved responses still match the live service.
key_points: Self-initializing fakes record real service responses on first call and replay them for subsequent tests, eliminating manual stub setup
The pattern avoids cache invalidation entirely by treating saved responses as permanent fixtures
When remote services change, teams can run validation suites comparing saved responses against live data
Unimportant parameters that vary between calls can be stripped from lookup keys to improve cache hits
Build pipelines can run fast stages against fakes and slow final stages against real services
money_quotes: Remote services are usually slow and often unreliable, so using a double is a good way to make your tests faster and more stable.
In a sense this is like a cache, but with the important difference that there is no attempt to handle cache invalidation, which is handy as that's one of the TwoHardThings.
The distinction here is that a stub needs setting up when you build the fixture, while fakes are autonomous.
Automated tests rely on getting the same answers to the same questions.
Usually tests don't care whether the data is up to date or not, so saving an old value worked just fine.
posts.key_insight: Self-initializing fakes sidestep cache invalidation complexity by treating recorded responses as permanent test fixtures, trading data freshness for test stability and speed.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that the useful distinction in technical debt isn't whether messy code qualifies as 'debt' but rather whether the debt is prudent versus reckless, and deliberate versus inadvertent. He introduces a quadrant framework for categorizing technical debt along these two dimensions. Responding to Uncle Bob's claim that 'a mess is not a debt,' Fowler contends the metaphor remains valuable for communicating design problems to non-technical stakeholders regardless of how the debt arose. He highlights that even excellent teams inevitably accumulate inadvertent debt as they learn what the design should have been through the process of building software.
key_points: The debt metaphor's value lies in communicating design problems to non-technical people, not in precisely categorizing what counts as 'real' debt
Technical debt should be categorized along two axes: prudent vs reckless, and deliberate vs inadvertent
Reckless deliberate debt often stems from teams underestimating how quickly good design pays off - clean code makes you go faster, not slower
Prudent inadvertent debt is inevitable even for excellent teams, as understanding the best design often only comes after building the system
The decision framework of paying interest versus paying down principal applies to all four quadrants
money_quotes: To my mind, the question of whether a design flaw is or isn't debt is the wrong question.
The whole point of good design and clean code is to make you go faster - if it didn't people like Uncle Bob, Kent Beck, and Ward Cunningham wouldn't be spending time talking about it.
It's often the case that it can take a year of programming on a project before you understand what the best design approach should have been.
The moment you realize what the design should have been, you also realize that you have an inadvertent debt.
Even the best teams will have debt to deal with as a project goes on - even more reason not to recklessly overload it with crummy code.
