A mess is a reckless debt which results in crippling interest payments or a long period of paying down the principal.
posts.key_insight: Technical debt should be understood through a quadrant of prudent/reckless and deliberate/inadvertent dimensions, with the recognition that even excellent teams inevitably accumulate debt as they learn what the design should have been.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains his philosophy on providing talk materials to audiences who request slide copies. He argues that well-designed presentation slides serve as a visual channel to complement spoken words, not as standalone documents. Since his slides are graphic, minimal, and animation-heavy, they become meaningless without his narration. Instead of distributing 'slideuments,' he directs audiences to written articles and books that cover the same topics in a format designed for reading. The post then provides a comprehensive index of reading materials corresponding to his various conference talks on topics like NoSQL, Agile, DSLs, and continuous delivery.
key_points: Slides designed for presentations make poor handouts because they're meant to complement speech, not replace it
Bullet-heavy 'slideuments' distract audiences by competing with the speaker for attention
Written articles and books are more effective standalone resources than presentation slides
The post serves as a curated bibliography linking Fowler's talks to corresponding written material
money_quotes: When I use slides, I design them to be a VisualChannel: an accompaniment to my words, not as a stand-alone 'Slideument'.
Such slideuments are very distracting when talking, as reading the bullet points takes the audience's attention away from the words being spoken.
Such slides are meaningless without me speaking.
A written piece is a much more effective stand-alone document.
I can write this to make sense without audio, and use an approach designed for reading rather projecting on a screen.
posts.key_insight: Effective presentation slides and effective written documentation require fundamentally different design approaches, and speakers should provide purpose-built written materials rather than exporting their slides.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses a common misconception that user stories flow one-way from product owners to developers, with business defining 'what' and developers determining 'how'. He argues this 'decreed stories' model fundamentally misunderstands agile development, which should be built on ongoing conversation between customers and developers. Developers should actively participate in refining, creating, and improving stories using their technical knowledge. While the product owner retains final decision-making authority on prioritization and business value, this shouldn't prevent collaborative story creation across the entire team.
key_points: The one-way flow of stories from product owner to developers is a misunderstanding of agile, not a feature
Stories should always be refined through conversation, with developers actively participating in their definition
Developers bring unique value: spotting gaps, suggesting cheaper alternatives, and splitting stories for better planning
The 'Negotiable' principle in INVEST means any team member can create or modify stories
Product owners have final say on prioritization but should never operate in a 'decreed' model
money_quotes: This notion of product owners coming up with DecreedStories is a profound misunderstanding of the way agile development should work.

When we were brainstorming names at Snowbird, I remember Kent suggesting 'conversational'. This emphasized the fact that the heart of our thinking was of an on-going conversation between customers and developers.
Any member of an agile team can create stories and suggest modifications.
Having a final decision maker should never stop others from participating, and should not lead people astray into a decreed model of stories.
Developers should play an active role in helping that definition.
posts.key_insight: User stories are not requirements handed down from business to developers, but living artifacts shaped through ongoing conversation where technical knowledge actively informs what gets built.

posts.summary: Fowler briefly critiques an anti-pattern where user stories are written in isolation by product owners or analysts and handed off to developers as mandates. He argues this approach fundamentally misunderstands agile principles, which emphasize collaboration over documentation handoffs. He advocates instead for conversational stories where requirements emerge through dialogue between all parties.
key_points: Stories written by product owners and 'passed to developers' represents a command-and-control mindset incompatible with agile
This approach treats developers as implementers rather than collaborative problem-solvers
Conversational stories are the preferred alternative, emphasizing ongoing dialogue over upfront specification
money_quotes: I see this as a profound misunderstanding of agile thinking
posts.key_insight: User stories should emerge from conversation between product owners and developers, not be decreed from on high.

posts.summary: Blue-green deployment is a technique for achieving zero-downtime releases by maintaining two identical production environments and switching between them via router configuration. The approach enables rapid rollback if issues arise, as the previous environment remains available. Fowler explains that both environments cycle between serving live traffic, acting as rollback targets, and staging the next release. Database schema changes require special handling by separating schema migrations from application deployments. The technique also doubles as disaster recovery testing since it validates hot-standby procedures with every release. Despite being well-established, Fowler notes the technique remains underused in practice.
key_points: Blue-green deployment uses two identical production environments with a router switch to achieve zero-downtime releases and instant rollback capability
The environments continuously cycle roles: live production, rollback target, and staging for next release
Database schema changes require careful sequencing—deploy schema changes that support both versions first, then deploy the application
The approach inherently tests disaster recovery procedures with every release since it validates the hot-standby mechanism
Implementation varies widely: hardware separation, virtual machines, partitioned zones, or even web server bouncing instead of router switching
money_quotes: Automating your deployment helps reduce the frictions and delays that crop up in between getting the software 'done' and getting it to realize its value.
One of the challenges with automating deployment is the cut-over itself, taking software from the final stage of testing to live production.
Blue-green deployment also gives you a rapid way to rollback - if anything goes wrong you switch the router back to your blue environment.
An advantage of this approach is that it's the same basic mechanism as you need to get a hot-standby working.
I hope that you release more frequently than you have a disaster.
The trick is to separate the deployment of schema changes from application upgrades.
This technique has been 'out there' for ages, but I don't see it used as often as it should be.
posts.key_insight: Blue-green deployment transforms the risky cut-over moment into a simple router switch between identical environments, providing both zero-downtime releases and instant rollback capability.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses whether Toyota's quality failings undermine the case for lean software development. He argues that Toyota's problems don't negate fifty years of manufacturing success that transformed the company and raised industry-wide quality standards. More importantly, he contends that lean manufacturing's record in software should be judged independently since the application of lean ideas to software is a metaphorical adaptation, not a direct transfer. The techniques should be evaluated based on their effectiveness in software, not their history in manufacturing.
key_points: Toyota's recent problems don't erase half a century of proven success in manufacturing
Lean techniques were widely copied and credited with raising overall car quality industry-wide
Lean manufacturing and lean software development are separate domains connected by metaphor, not direct application
Ideas borrowed from other industries should be judged by their effectiveness in their new context
money_quotes: Lean manufacturing techniques were the underpinning of Toyota's rise from an insignificant company in the 1950's to a global giant in the 2000's.
I would be very surprised if the recent problems at Toyota are enough negate that half-century of success.
Lean manufacturing is about manufacturing not software.
Lean ideas can help us come up with better ideas for software development, and as such are valuable.
In the end their usefulness lies with how they are used in software and they should be judged on their record here.
Their history in manufacturing, both good and bad, is another industry.
posts.key_insight: Ideas borrowed from other industries through metaphor should be judged by their effectiveness in their new context, not by the successes or failures of their original domain.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler presents results from an internal ThoughtWorks survey on version control tools, conducted in early 2010 with 99 respondents. The survey asked developers to rate VCS tools from 'Best in Class' to 'Dangerous.' Results show a clear cluster of highly-approved tools (git, Mercurial, Subversion with 97-99% approval) versus poorly-regarded ones (ClearCase, TFS, VSS with 0-5% approval). Git notably received the most 'Best in Class' votes, while VSS and ClearCase received the most 'Dangerous' ratings. Fowler emphasizes that the meaningful takeaway is the large gap between leading and lagging tools, not small differences within clusters.
key_points: Git, Mercurial, and Subversion form a clear high-approval cluster (93-99%), with git receiving notably more 'Best in Class' votes (65 vs 20-33)
VSS, TFS, and ClearCase received near-zero approval, with VSS and ClearCase garnering the most 'Dangerous' ratings
The survey had 99 responses from ThoughtWorks' internal software development mailing list, making it an opinion snapshot rather than rigorous research
Small numerical differences between similarly-ranked tools shouldn't be overinterpreted due to survey noise
Tool opinions are often colored by organizational processes rather than the tools themselves
money_quotes: I realized that I could add some spurious but mesmerizing numbers to my analysis by doing a survey.
It's also clear that there's a big divide in approval between those three, together with Bazaar and Perforce, versus the rest.
Opinions of tools are often colored by processes that are more about the organization than the tool itself.
I've never managed to get my ThoughtBot opinion-control software to work reliably.
The important point is the big approval gap between the leading tools (Subversion, git, and Mercurial) and the laggards.
Don't read too much into small differences as I'm sure they aren't significant.
posts.key_insight: The meaningful insight from tool surveys is the large gap between clearly superior and inferior options, not the small differences between tools in the same tier.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the Richardson Maturity Model (RMM), a framework developed by Leonard Richardson that describes the progression toward RESTful API design in three levels. Starting from Level 0 (HTTP as a tunnel for RPC), systems evolve through Level 1 (individual resources with URIs), Level 2 (proper use of HTTP verbs and status codes), and finally Level 3 (hypermedia controls/HATEOAS). Fowler emphasizes that while Level 3 is a precondition for true REST according to Roy Fielding, the model is best used as a learning tool rather than an assessment mechanism. He notes the practical benefits of each level, from caching enabled by proper GET usage to the discoverability and flexibility that hypermedia controls provide.
key_points: Level 0 uses HTTP purely as transport for RPC-style calls to a single endpoint, ignoring web mechanisms
Level 1 introduces addressable resources with individual URIs, similar to object identity in OOP
Level 2 leverages HTTP verbs (GET for safe operations, POST/PUT for state changes) and response codes, enabling caching and standardized error handling
Level 3 adds hypermedia controls (HATEOAS) that tell clients what actions are available and where to perform them, enabling URI scheme changes without breaking clients
Roy Fielding considers Level 3 a precondition for REST, not just a nice-to-have
money_quotes: The web is an existence proof of a massively scalable distributed system that works really well, and we can take ideas from that to build integrated systems more easily.
To an object guy like me this is like the notion of object identity. Rather than calling some function in the ether and passing arguments, we call a method on one particular object.

HTTP defines GET as a safe operation, that is it doesn't make any significant changes to the state of anything. This allows us to invoke GETs safely any number of times in any order and get the same results each time.
The point of hypermedia controls is that they tell us what we can do next, and the URI of the resource we need to manipulate to do it.
One obvious benefit of hypermedia controls is that it allows the server to change its URI scheme without breaking clients.
Roy Fielding has made it clear that level 3 RMM is a pre-condition of REST.

I don't think we have enough examples yet to be really sure that the restful approach is the right way to integrate systems, I do think it's a very attractive approach and the one that I would recommend in most situations.
posts.key_insight: The Richardson Maturity Model provides a practical progression for understanding REST, where each level—resources, HTTP verbs, and hypermedia controls—addresses a distinct design challenge: complexity management, standardization, and discoverability.

posts.summary: This is a personal travel diary recounting a week's vacation on Italy's Amalfi Coast. Fowler describes staying at an authentic agriturismo farm, the challenges and rewards of driving narrow coastal roads, and visits to various towns including Ravello and Sorrento. He offers practical advice about using local buses instead of rental cars and recommends hiking with good maps. The post is entirely about leisure travel with no connection to software development or technology.
key_points: The Amalfi Coast combines dramatic mountains and sea with villages clinging to mountainsides
Agriturismos vary in authenticity - Serafina was a genuine working farm unlike many tourist-oriented alternatives
Local buses are recommended over rental cars due to difficult parking and narrow roads
Ravello has beautiful gardens but feels exclusive to the wealthy
Capri is touristy but offers good hiking away from the boutique center
money_quotes: The Amalfi Coast also has that dramatic combination of mountains and sea that marks so many of my favorite places
I'm convinced that I'm neither rich enough nor beautiful enough to fit in there
The twisty, narrow roads, precipitous drops, and infamous Italian drivers make a very intimidating combination for driving
I quickly got used to it as long as I got more laid-back, started to enjoy the antics of my fellow drivers, and lost my inhibitions about being on the wrong side of the road
posts.key_insight: A practical and personal account of Italian coastal travel with no relevance to software engineering topics.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains his decision to decline participation in SEMAT, an initiative by Ivar Jacobson, Bertrand Meyer, and Richard Soley to 'refound software engineering' on solid theory. After investigating, Fowler found the core idea was creating a meta-method-kernel for composing software processes. He rejected this based on his own 1980s-90s experience with similar ideas and Alistair Cockburn's insight that people's inherent unpredictability makes such formalization fundamentally doomed. Cockburn's subsequent withdrawal from SEMAT after attending their inaugural meeting reinforced Fowler's skepticism.
key_points: SEMAT's goal of creating a rigorous, composable meta-method-kernel for software processes is not new—Fowler explored similar ideas decades ago
The fundamental flaw is that people are the central element in software development, and people are inherently non-linear and unpredictable
Fowler finds SEMAT's 'call for action' ironic—it derides fads while reading like a fashion statement itself
Software process is too multi-faceted to be usefully captured by a meta-method-kernel
money_quotes: Although it derided fads and fashions, it seemed very much like a fashion statement itself.
Since people are the central element in software development, and people are inherently non-linear and unpredictable - such an effort is fundamentally doomed.
Or at least it is until people become predictable agents that can be described with tractable mathematics - for which event I'm not holding my breath.
I spent a fair bit of time in the 80's and 90's mucking around with this idea. In the end I decided that it was too hard and of limited value.
posts.key_insight: Attempts to formalize software methodology into rigorous, composable systems are fundamentally doomed because they cannot account for the inherent unpredictability of the people at the center of software development.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reviews the Canon S90 compact camera after several months of use. He praises its pocket-friendly size combined with serious photography features like manual controls, RAW support, and an f2 lens. The camera excels in low light thanks to good high ISO performance and image stabilization. Fowler finds it complements his DSLR well, filling the gap when he doesn't want to carry bulky equipment. The only minor complaint is the rear control ring being too easy to turn accidentally.
key_points: The S90 offers serious photography features (manual controls, RAW, f2 lens) in a truly pocket-sized form factor
Low light performance is excellent due to the combination of good high ISO and stabilized f2 lens
The front control ring around the lens is a well-designed handling feature
The camera resolves Fowler's micro 4/3 regret by pairing well with his Canon DSLR system
Minor ergonomic issue with the rear control ring being accidentally turned
money_quotes: It's small enough to fit in your pocket, but has the kind of things that people with pretensions to seriousness like: full manual controls, RAW file support, a good sensor, and an f2 lens.
The combination of reasonable high ISO performance and a stabilized f2 lens, is very potent.
Even my DSLR can't give me stabilization and an f2 lens.
The S90 has salved my micro 4/3 angst.
I feel happy about the combo of DSLR and S90, which gives me a good choice between capability and bulk.
posts.key_insight: A high-quality pocket camera with manual controls can effectively complement a DSLR system by providing serious capability without the bulk.

posts.summary: This is a brief travel tip about getting to Heathrow Airport from nearby hotels. Fowler notes the frustration of hotels near Heathrow lacking walkable access to the airport. While Heathrow's official 'Hoppa' shuttle service exists, regular public buses offer more frequent service at lower cost. The post was updated to note that the Free Travel Zone around the airport was discontinued in 2021.
key_points: Hotels near Heathrow lack walkable access to the airport terminals
The official Heathrow Hoppa service is expensive and infrequent
Regular public buses are a better alternative - more frequent and cheaper
The Heathrow Free Travel Zone that made local buses free was discontinued in 2021
money_quotes: If I stay near at a hotel near to Heathrow Airport, I've always been annoyed that there's no way to walk into the airport.
Fortunately regular buses also run, and they run far more frequently for a fraction of the cost.
posts.key_insight: Regular public buses offer better value and frequency than Heathrow's official hotel shuttle service.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on his early experience with the iPad, explaining how it fits his lifestyle of reading and browsing away from his desk better than a laptop. He praises the form factor, battery life, screen quality, and touch interface while noting concerns about ebook lock-in, web app limitations, and wifi restrictions. Despite not considering himself an Apple fanboy, he compares the sensation of using the iPad to first experiencing the World Wide Web in 1995, suggesting it represents a paradigm shift in how we consume content.
key_points: The iPad's form factor and battery life solve the awkwardness of using laptops for casual reading and browsing
Ebook platform lock-in is a significant concern, with Amazon's Kindle potentially being more portable than iBooks
Web apps designed with hover interactions don't translate well to touch interfaces
Apple's curated software approach has both drawbacks and benefits compared to open systems
The iPad represents a potential paradigm shift similar to the emergence of the World Wide Web
money_quotes: After a few hours with the iPad, I realized that I was feeling a sensation I'd only remembered once before: back in 1995 when someone showed me the World Wide Web.
There's no dramatic new technology, we've seen tablets before just as we'd seen hypertext before. But the overall package was game-changer.
I don't know if the iPad will be The Device, but I do think that this kind of device will make huge difference to how we read and watch things in the future.
Given how much of my life I've lost to sorting out software incompatibilities I'm not sure whether to like curation or not.
I certainly need to work at making sure what I produce in the future is built with these kinds of devices in mind.
posts.key_insight: Transformative technology products aren't necessarily built on dramatic new inventions, but rather on combining existing technologies into a package that fundamentally changes user behavior.

posts.summary: The post advocates for a U-shaped desk arrangement (u-pod) in team rooms as an alternative to the traditional central desk layout. The u-pod configuration places developers in the middle of a U-shaped table arrangement, which facilitates easier collaboration by allowing team members to wheel over to view each other's monitors. While the layout does sacrifice some wall space for whiteboards, practitioners find the improved interpersonal communication worth the tradeoff.
key_points: U-pods allow team members to more easily wheel chairs over to glance at colleagues' monitors
The layout encourages more casual conversations and better 'ear contact' between team members
U-pods can be configured tightly or wider with room for a snack table or central working table
The main disadvantage is reduced access to wall space for whiteboards
Monitors should never be placed in corners as this inhibits pairing and collaboration
money_quotes: The biggest advantage of u-pods, is that they allow people to more easily wheel their chairs over to glance at another team member's monitor.
They also seem to encourage more casual conversations and generally better ear contact.
On the whole, people I've talked find the easier inter-personal communication worth this cost.
Don't put your monitor in a corner, as was common in the days of big CRT monitors. That way only one person can easily work at it - which is terrible both for pairing and collaboration generally.
posts.key_insight: Physical workspace layout directly impacts team collaboration, and optimizing for easy movement and visual access to colleagues' work outweighs concerns about wall space efficiency.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler advocates for agile teams to work in shared, open team rooms rather than private offices, arguing that software development's collaborative nature benefits from constant informal communication. He addresses noise concerns by noting that team rooms differ from generic open-plan offices because everyone shares a common purpose. The post provides extensive practical advice on room design, including space requirements, lighting, furniture, and accommodating remote workers. Fowler emphasizes that teams should control their own space configuration as part of agile self-organization principles.
key_points: Open team rooms promote informal, deep communication and serendipitous overhearing of useful information
Team rooms differ from regular open-plan offices because shared purpose makes conversation less distracting
Teams should have complete control over their space configuration as part of agile self-organization
Physical environment details matter: natural light, 50 sq ft per person, quality chairs, abundant monitor space, and wall space for information radiators
Invest in good chairs and skip expensive modular furniture; simple moveable tables work best
money_quotes: Software development is an intense exercise in collaboration.
Often you get serendipitous communication, where you overhear something that's really useful.

I suspect the reason it's not that distracting is because the team has a common purpose around a collaborative activity. It isn't comparable to an open-plan office where everyone is doing something different.
An important part of agile thinking is that the team is responsible for how it works, and how it organizes its space is part of that.

The place to spend money on furniture is for good quality chairs. Programmers spend a lot of time sitting, and any physical damage due to poor posture will have a direct effect on the team's productivity.
Clever software with multiple virtual workspaces is a great feature, but nothing is faster than just moving your eyes.
Software development is inherently creative, so expect to see lots of baubly distractions.
It's hard to be creative when you have to pay for awful coffee.
posts.key_insight: Open team rooms work for agile teams not because open offices are inherently good, but because shared purpose transforms ambient conversation from distraction into valuable ambient awareness.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on his experience at Agile 2010 in Orlando, noting the conference maintained reasonable attendance despite the recession. He observes the audience was mostly newcomers to agile with an unusually high proportion of women, but the overwhelming 214 talks across 16 tracks had variable presentation quality. The main tension he addresses is criticism that the conference over-emphasized soft skills at the expense of programming topics, which he sees as a valid concern but argues against splitting the community.
key_points: Conference attendance held steady around 1400 despite the Great Recession, suggesting agile's continued relevance
Most emphasis was on team collaboration and soft skills, drawing criticism from the programming-focused community
XP's strength lies in blending technical excellence with collaboration rather than treating them as separate concerns
Conferences like JAOO and QCon do better at balancing technical and collaborative content with stronger quality control
There's a real tendency for programmers to look inward and obsess on technical issues rather than engaging with those outside programming
money_quotes: Programming is part of a very interconnected system which involves lots of pieces - and making these interconnections work requires exactly these kinds of soft and woolly skills.
There is a real tendency for programmers to look inward and obsess on technical issues rather than engaging with those outside programming.
One of the things I like about XP is that it recognizes this: blending technical excellence with collaboration.
I would hate to see programming detached from the agilexx conference with programmers going to different events.
Our crucial task is to engage in excellence in the programming side without cutting off the essential collaboration.
Certainly programming plays a central role in software development and attempts to marginalize it correlate well with dead-ends.
posts.key_insight: The agile community must maintain spaces where technical excellence and collaborative soft skills intermingle, rather than allowing programmers to retreat into purely technical silos.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his impressions from attending and speaking at Agile Australia 2010 in Sydney/Melbourne. He observes that despite being geographically isolated, the Australian agile community is just as mature as their global counterparts, facing similar challenges. The conference had strong IT leadership attendance, including Suncorp's CIO. Fowler highlights his visit to Lonely Planet as a trip high-point, noting their successful adoption of agile practices beyond just software development. He delivered his keynote as a suite of talks, and when given the choice between Q&A or a third talk, the audience almost unanimously chose the additional content.
key_points: The Australian agile community, though small and geographically isolated, demonstrates equal maturity to the global community
Jim Highsmith joining Thoughtworks was announced, someone Fowler values for helping people see software development from new perspectives
Strong IT leadership presence at the conference, exemplified by Suncorp's CIO attending
Lonely Planet successfully applied agile techniques beyond software development throughout their organization
Fowler's keynote audience overwhelmingly chose a third talk over Q&A when given the option
money_quotes: The Australian agile community is small and somewhat disconnected from the rest of the world (for a good reason, it's a long flight from anywhere). But however small and disconnected they are, they are no less mature.
I've always found he has a way of helping people look at software development from a new set of perspectives.
It was good to see how much the organization had gained from the techniques we agilists have talked about.
As I keep saying, I like being in Australia, but hate going there. 28 hours of travel isn't fun, even with an upgrade.
posts.key_insight: Geographic isolation doesn't limit the maturity of agile communities, and agile practices provide value beyond software development when adopted organization-wide.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains feature flags as an alternative to feature branches for managing incomplete features across release cycles. The technique involves configuration-controlled toggles that hide pending features from users while allowing continuous integration on mainline. He categorizes flags into release toggles, experiment toggles, ops toggles, and permissioning toggles. Fowler emphasizes minimizing toggle points and retiring flags promptly to avoid technical debt. Crucially, he argues that release flags should be a last resort—teams should first try breaking features into smaller releasable pieces or using keystone interfaces.
key_points: Feature flags enable continuous integration by hiding incomplete features through configuration rather than code branches
Toggle points should be minimized to entry points only—don't protect every code path, just the user-facing access points
Testing doesn't require combinatorial explosion: test with expected-release flags on, and with all flags on
Release flags must be retired once features ship, or you accumulate unmaintainable toggle debt
Feature flags should be the last choice after small releases and keystone interfaces
money_quotes: Your first choice should be to break the feature down so you can safely introduce parts of the feature into the product.
Don't try to protect every code path in the new feature code with a flag, focus on just the entry points that would lead users there and toggle those entry points.
It's very important to retire release flags once the pending features have bedded down in production.
In one memorable example I heard of, it required making a special recompilation of the linux kernel to handle enough command line switches.
Only if you can't do small releases or a Keystone Interface should you employ release flags.
The advantages of doing this are the same ones as any strategy based on small, frequent releases.
posts.key_insight: Feature flags are a useful technique for hiding incomplete features, but they should be your last resort after attempting to break features into smaller releasable pieces or using keystone interfaces.

posts.summary: Fowler recounts his experience upgrading from Mac OS X Leopard to Snow Leopard, a task he had postponed despite needing it for Aperture 3. The upgrade itself went smoothly after careful backups, though Aperture ironically required a reinstall to work. The main complication was MacPorts breaking, leading to repeated failed rebuilds with cryptic errors. After Twitter followers recommended Homebrew as an alternative, he switched package managers successfully. The post captures the typical friction of OS upgrades and the value of community recommendations.
key_points: OS upgrades are typically painful enough that people postpone them even when beneficial software awaits
Extra-paranoid backups before major system changes are a common ritual even when redundant
MacPorts proved fragile across the OS upgrade, failing with inscrutable errors
Twitter served as an effective channel for getting peer recommendations during technical struggles
Switching tools entirely is sometimes easier than fixing broken ones
money_quotes: Operating system upgrades are usually such a pain.
I always get extra-paranoid around moments like this.
The biggest yak so far was software I'd installed via MacPorts.
I tried several times to get MacPorts to rebuild its software but kept failing after long compiles and inscrutable error messages.
There's something compelling about sharing your pain on Twitter.
The only thing I could think of to fix MacPorts was to completely eradicate and reinstall it.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes abandoning a broken tool for an alternative is more practical than debugging inscrutable failures.

posts.summary: Fowler explores motion graphics tools for creating animated diagrams in presentations. He evaluates Adobe After Effects and Apple Motion (both expensive at ~$1000), with After Effects offering a trial version while Apple frustratingly doesn't. He experimented with Blender for 3D animation but found the cognitive overhead of 3D thinking and Blender's idiosyncratic UI made it slower than After Effects. His main frustration is that all these tools are overloaded with features he doesn't need, making them harder to use for his specific purpose of animating presentation diagrams.
key_points: Professional motion graphics tools (After Effects, Motion) are expensive at ~$1000 and may be overkill for presentation diagrams
Apple's lack of trial versions for software is a frustrating barrier to evaluation
3D animation with Blender proved slower due to cognitive overhead and poor UI design
Feature-bloated tools create friction when you only need a subset of their capabilities
money_quotes: Infuriatingly I can't do the same with Motion since Apple don't provide trial versions of their software.
Thinking in 3D is more difficult, and I'm not sure the results are usually worth the extra effort involved.
The UI for blender is very idiosyncratic, which made it frustratingly difficult to do even simple things.
All the tools I've been looking at so far do lots of things that I'm not so interested in, which makes it harder to use them for my main purpose.
posts.key_insight: Tools designed to do everything often become harder to use for any specific purpose, creating friction for users with focused needs.

posts.summary: Fowler explains his return to using slides after a decade of slideshow-free keynotes, inspired by colleague Neal Ford's effective techniques. He advocates treating slides as a visual channel that complements rather than duplicates the audio channel of spoken words. The key is using simple diagrams and purposeful animations that reinforce what's being said, rather than bullet points or loosely connected stock photos. He emphasizes that effective slides should be meaningless without the accompanying speech, deliberately refusing to share decks since they fail as standalone documents.
key_points: Slides should function as a visual channel complementing the audio channel of speech, not duplicating it
Stock photos have become the lazy replacement for bullet points - often tenuously connected to the actual content
Simple diagrams with strategic positioning of graphics and words are more effective than text-heavy slides
Animations should be semantically meaningful, timed to match spoken words rather than gratuitous visual sizzle
Good presentation slides are intentionally useless as handouts - trying to serve both purposes creates a failed 'Slideument'
money_quotes: Stock photos are the bullet points of the 21st century.
The visual channel needs to make the same point, but in a different way.
Extraneous pictures interfere with the learners ability to make sense of the material.
I've no problem in using sizzle, but I try to always match an animation to what I'm saying, so that the animation can be part of that semantically rich visual channel.
That role is to keep the eyes engaged in an activity that supports my words without distracting from them.
If someone asks for a copy of my slide deck, I always refuse as the slides do not make any sense on their own (and can even mislead).
posts.key_insight: Effective presentation slides should function as a complementary visual channel that reinforces spoken words through diagrams and purposeful animation, not as a parallel text track competing for attention.

posts.summary: This is a personal travel blog post about a diving vacation in India's Andaman Islands, not a technical article. Fowler shares practical travel tips from his trip to Havelock Island, including diving conditions, accommodation reviews, and recommendations for fellow travelers. The post covers logistics like dive operators, resorts, and getting around the islands. It concludes with a brief mention of Port Blair and the historically interesting Ross Island ruins.
key_points: Diving conditions were decent but not exceptional due to low visibility and coral bleaching from warm waters
Dive India operator receives strong recommendation for being friendly and well-organized
The tour operator Welcome Andaman Travels is explicitly not recommended due to poor service
Location matters for accommodation - staying away from the main resort area created transportation difficulties
Ross Island's colonial ruins being reclaimed by nature was a highlight worth visiting
money_quotes: The diving was good, but not as good as we've had in Saba.
They were one of the best dive operations we've been with - both friendly and well-organized.
We were not happy with their service and do not recommend them.
Yet another reason to continue with our usual preference for independent travel.
The colonial buildings are now ruins being spectacularly reclaimed by epiphytic plants.
posts.key_insight: This is a personal travel post with no technical content - it documents a diving vacation with practical recommendations for other travelers considering the Andaman Islands.

posts.summary: Fowler describes migrating his website's XML transformation system from REXML to Nokogiri, a faster Ruby XML library. After years of using REXML for his custom blog-to-HTML pipeline, performance needs and API preferences drove the change. He outlines a three-step migration strategy using an insulating layer to enable gradual transition while keeping the site buildable. The approach allows incremental change rather than a risky all-at-once swap, demonstrating practical refactoring principles on his own codebase.
key_points: REXML was adequate but slow compared to libxml-based alternatives like Nokogiri, which offered both speed (10 seconds vs 1 minute) and a better API
The migration strategy uses an insulating layer between application code and the XML library, allowing gradual transition while maintaining a working build
The three-step approach: introduce insulation matching REXML's interface, implement Nokogiri behind the same interface, then refactor to Nokogiri's native API
He deliberately chose not to keep the insulating layer permanently, preferring to pay migration costs later rather than maintain unnecessary abstraction now
Functional testing by rebuilding the site and diffing against the released version provides confidence during the refactoring
money_quotes: While I got pretty good at programming XSLT I came to the conclusion that I was not enough of a masochist to want to continue using it.
Replacing the XML library in a program that mainly does XML processing is often seen as a fraught task.
Switching over to Nokogiri in one fell swoop is too big a change, instead I can gradually implement it while my site can still build fine with the REXML version.
I'd rather pay that price then than have to pay the price of dealing with an unnecessary layer now.
Since I'm the only programmer I can be more casual than if I was working on a team with this, but I still follow much the same habits.
posts.key_insight: Large library migrations become manageable through insulating layers that enable gradual, reversible change while keeping the system functional throughout the transition.

posts.summary: Contract tests verify that test doubles accurately represent external services, addressing the fundamental question of whether your mocks reflect reality. When communicating with services maintained by other teams, test doubles prevent slow and unreliable tests, but they create a risk of drift from the actual service contract. Contract tests run separately from the main deployment pipeline, typically on a daily cadence tied to external service changes rather than your own code changes. Failures should trigger either code updates or conversations with the service team, and ideally teams adopt Consumer Driven Contracts where suppliers run your contract tests in their pipeline.
key_points: Test doubles solve the problem of slow and unreliable external services but create the risk of representing outdated contracts
Contract tests should run on a separate cadence from deployment pipelines, based on external service change rhythms rather than your own code changes
Contract test failures should trigger communication with service suppliers, not just code fixes
Consumer Driven Contracts reduce surprise breakages by letting suppliers run your contract tests in their build pipeline
Contract tests verify data format consistency, not necessarily exact data values
money_quotes: Testing against a double always raises the question of whether the double is indeed an accurate representation of the external service, and what happens if the external service changes its contract?
Your regular pipeline is based on the rhythm of changes to your code, but these tests need to be based on the rhythm of changes to the external service.
A failure in a contract test shouldn't necessarily break the build in the same way that a normal test failure would.
Just as likely it will trigger a conversation with the keepers of the external service to talk about the change and alert them to how their changes are affecting other applications.
You can facilitate this by letting the supplier team have copies of your contract tests so they can run them as part of their build pipeline.
Contract tests check the contract of external service calls, but not necessarily the exact data.
posts.key_insight: Contract tests bridge the gap between the convenience of test doubles and the reality of external services by verifying your mocks still match the actual service contract, running on a cadence tied to external changes rather than your own.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces Gerard Meszaros's taxonomy for test doubles, addressing the confusion around terms like stubs, mocks, and fakes. The term 'Test Double' serves as an umbrella concept (like a stunt double) for any object that replaces a production component during testing. Fowler outlines five distinct types: Dummy, Fake, Stub, Spy, and Mock, each serving different testing purposes. This vocabulary provides clarity for practitioners who often use these terms interchangeably or inconsistently.
key_points: Test Double is the generic term for any object replacing a production object in tests, analogous to a stunt double in film
Dummies fill parameter lists but are never used; Fakes have working implementations but take shortcuts unsuitable for production
Stubs provide canned responses while Spies additionally record information about how they were called
Mocks are the most sophisticated—pre-programmed with expectations that verify both the calls received and can fail if unexpected calls occur
This taxonomy comes from Gerard Meszaros's xUnit patterns work, which Fowler endorses as worth spreading
money_quotes: Test Double is a generic term for any case where you replace a production object for testing purposes.
Dummy objects are passed around but never actually used. Usually they are just used to fill parameter lists.
Mocks are pre-programmed with expectations which form a specification of the calls they are expected to receive.
Fake objects actually have working implementations, but usually take some shortcut which makes them not suitable for production.
posts.key_insight: Clear vocabulary matters in testing—distinguishing between dummies, fakes, stubs, spies, and mocks helps teams communicate precisely about their test strategies.

posts.summary: Fowler expresses skepticism toward claims that new technologies will eliminate the need for programmers by enabling users to write their own software. He points out that COBOL was originally designed with this exact goal, yet it obviously failed to make programmers obsolete. He argues that effective programming requires specific skills and mindsets—particularly the ability to precisely describe behavior and organize large bodies of such descriptions. These skills typically lead to full-time programming work, and technologies marketed for end-user programming inevitably develop their own communities of dedicated programmers rather than true business users.
key_points: COBOL was originally intended to let users write software without programmers—a goal it clearly failed to achieve
Programming requires specific skills: precisely describing behavior and organizing large bodies of such descriptions into malleable form
These programming skills are not universal and typically lead to full-time specialization
End-user programming technologies tend to spawn their own programmer communities separate from actual business users
money_quotes: When I hear this I like to remember that this was the aim COBOL - and we know how that worked out.

I think there are specific skills and mindsets that make an effective programmer - the available to figure out how to precisely describe behavior and organize large bodies of such descriptions into a malleable form.
That's not a skill everyone has - and it usually leads to a full-time job.
Often when I have seen technology introduced for end-user programming, it tends to develop it's own community of programmers who are not truly business users.
posts.key_insight: Technologies promising to eliminate programmers by empowering end users inevitably create new programmer communities instead, because the fundamental skills of programming remain specialized regardless of the tools.

posts.summary: Fowler responds to debates about software craftsmanship by arguing that while the movement valuably refocuses attention on technical practices neglected by Scrum and Lean, it risks creating a new problem by neglecting customer relationships. He traces craftsmanship's rise as a reaction to agile's shift away from Extreme Programming's technical focus. The core concern is the 'yawning crevasse of doom' between developers and customers, which organizational habits and programmer preferences both widen. Fowler's ideal programmer combines technical craft with genuine engagement in understanding customer domains.
key_points: The software craftsmanship movement emerged as a reaction to Scrum and Lean's neglect of technical practices that were central to early Extreme Programming
Debates about whether software development is a craft, art, or trade are fundamentally tedious and miss the point
Senior developers tend to focus more on technical issues and disengage from understanding the business domain they work in
Both management-focused agile (neglecting technical skills) and craftsmanship (neglecting relationship skills) risk widening the divide between developers and customers
The ideal programmer is skilled in craft but also energized by learning about the domain and communicating with domain experts
money_quotes: I'm fundamentally uninterested in whether software development is a craft, an art, a trade, or a dessert topping.

The dominant agile strains are Scrum and Lean, which don't care very much about programming - and thus those people who primarily self-identify as programmers feel a large part of their life is no longer important in the agile world.

A large part of the blame for the crevasse lies in organizational habits that are founded on the notion programmers and customers are such different creatures that they can't communicate (and shouldn't try).
As developers get more senior they tend to focus more on technical issues and don't tend to engage in understanding the domain they are working in.
The software shouldn't be at the center of a programmer's world, instead a programmer should focus on the benefit that the software is supposed to deliver.
I fear craftsmanship in turn may make the crevasse worse by neglecting the relationship skills.
posts.key_insight: A programmer's excellence should be measured not just by technical craft, but by their ability to bridge the divide between developers and customers through genuine domain engagement.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces the concept of 'subcutaneous testing' - tests that operate just beneath an application's UI layer. This approach is valuable for functional end-to-end testing when the UI itself is difficult to test. While faster and avoiding presentation layer complexities, it risks missing important behavior if logic leaks into the UI layer.
key_points: Subcutaneous tests operate immediately below the UI, enabling end-to-end functional testing without UI complexity
This approach is faster than UI testing and avoids hard-to-test presentation technologies
The technique only works safely if you maintain strict separation of logic from UI code
Subcutaneous testing is a pragmatic middle ground between unit tests and full UI tests
money_quotes: I use subcutaneous test to mean a test that operates just under the UI of an application.
Subcutaneous testing can avoid difficulties with hard-to-test presentation technologies and usually is much faster than testing through the UI.
The big danger is that, unless you are a firm follower of keeping all useful logic out of your UI, subcutaneous testing will leave important behavior out of its test.
posts.key_insight: Subcutaneous testing offers a pragmatic balance between speed and coverage, but its effectiveness depends entirely on disciplined separation of business logic from the UI layer.

posts.summary: Fowler argues against framing internal software quality as something tradable for speed or features. While external quality (user-facing attributes) can legitimately be traded off, internal quality (code design and structure) follows different economics. He contends that the 'tradable quality hypothesis' is a trap: once you accept that framing, you've already lost the argument. Instead, internal quality should be understood through the 'Design Stamina Hypothesis' - good design is what enables sustained development speed, not a luxury that slows you down.
key_points: External quality (UI, user experience) can legitimately be traded against features, but internal quality cannot
The tradable quality hypothesis is self-defeating: if design quality provides no benefit, there's no logical reason to invest in it at all
Internal quality follows the Design Stamina Hypothesis - poor design slows you down, making quality an enabler of speed rather than an obstacle
The 'software craftsmanship' metaphor is counterproductive because 'craft' implies higher costs, reinforcing the tradable quality mindset
The short-term speed gains from ignoring design quality operate over a far shorter time horizon than most people realize
money_quotes: When I hear this I know that the developers, management and their customers have already lost.
The trouble with doing this, is that if internal quality is tradable, then it makes no sense to put any effort into internal quality at all.
Reducing internal quality slows us down.
As soon as you frame internal quality as tradable, you've lost.
It's vital to focus on the true value of internal quality - that it's the enabler to speed.
The purpose of internal quality is to go faster.
The word 'craft' reinforces the tradable quality hypothesis - and that's a crippling disadvantage to those of us who know that speed requires good design.
posts.key_insight: Internal software quality is not a trade-off against speed - it is the mechanism that enables speed, and framing it otherwise guarantees losing the argument.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains his decision to upgrade from a Canon Rebel XTi/400D to the Canon 60D after several years of waiting for the right technology improvements. He initially bought a cheaper camera body knowing technology would advance, preferring to invest in lenses. The articulated screen was the feature that finally pushed him to upgrade, despite serious photographers dismissing its importance. He shares his initial impressions of the new camera, finding unexpected delight in the improved viewfinder.
key_points: Strategic purchasing: buy cheaper camera bodies initially and invest in lenses, knowing technology will improve
The articulated screen, though dismissed by 'serious photographers' as only useful for video, has legitimate use cases for awkward shooting angles
The biggest unexpected benefit was the brighter, bigger viewfinder rather than the anticipated articulated screen
Lighter weight from the non-magnesium body is a feature, not a bug, despite criticism from some photographers
money_quotes: I deliberately bought a cheaper camera - a Canon Rebel XTi/400D. I did this partly to put more money to lenses, but also because I knew that technology would move on and I'd be looking to replace the body in a few years.
Serious photographers tend to dismiss the importance of an articulated screen, saying that it's only worthwhile for video work.
While I use the viewfinder for 98.37% of my shots, from time to time I find shooting angles that are just too awkward or impossible to do.
The biggest surprise plus for me is (ironically) the viewfinder which is much brighter and bigger.
Some people bemoan dropping the magnesium body from the 50D, but I'm happy with a lower-weight option.
The articulated screen gets very occasional but very much appreciated use.
posts.key_insight: Smart technology purchasing means buying cheaper now while investing in lasting components, then upgrading bodies when specific meaningful improvements arrive rather than chasing every iteration.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that software certifications are almost universally worthless because they lack correlation with actual competence. A useful certification would make hiring easier by reliably indicating skill, but current schemes fail this basic test. The situation is worsened by the financial incentives that corrupt certification programs. While competent people may need certifications to get past hiring gates, Fowler believes they should actively work to educate others about certification uselessness and help remove this 'canker' from the profession.
key_points: For certification to be useful, it must correlate with competence - something most software certifications fail to demonstrate
The only reliable way to assess programming ability is having other good programmers evaluate candidates, which is difficult and time-consuming
Certification schemes are prone to corruption because recognition creates money-making opportunities regardless of actual usefulness
Competent people may reasonably acquire useless certifications as entry gates to interviews, but should never imply these certifications mean anything
Training courses that offer certifications reinforce the problem but are understandable since many organizations require certification for course attendance
money_quotes: For a certification to be useful, it needs a correlation with competence in the thing that it certifies.
The reason we have disdain for most software certification programs is because we've not seen such a correlation (indeed sometimes we feel there's a negative correlation).
I tend to judge such schemes as guilty until proven innocent.
At the moment the only way you can tell if someone is a good programmer is to find other good programmers to assess their ability.
Sadly there doesn't seem to be much correlation between a certification's ability to make money and its usefulness.
Lousy certifications are a canker to our profession and we should work as much as we can to remove them.
posts.key_insight: Software certifications are mostly worthless because they lack the essential quality that would make them useful: a reliable correlation between holding the certification and being competent in the certified skill.

posts.summary: Fowler clarifies the distinction between being an author of the Agile Manifesto (one of 17 original authors from the Snowbird meeting) versus being a signatory, noting that over 10,000 people have signed it. He traces the history of the manifesto website, from Ward Cunningham's initial hosting to the addition of signature collection in October 2001. The post includes data visualizations showing signature growth patterns, revealing a significant increase from 2004 to 2008. Fowler also mentions the translation effort coordinated by Henrik Kniberg, with 22 languages completed and 15 in progress as of writing.
key_points: The Agile Manifesto has over 10,000 signatories, far exceeding Ward Cunningham's initial estimate of 'dozens or even hundreds'
The manifesto values were published in late February 2001, but the principles took another month to finalize and were published in April
Chet Hendrickson was the first person to sign the manifesto on October 10, 2001
Signature growth increased significantly from 2004 to 2008, reflecting agile's mainstream adoption
The manifesto has been translated into 22 languages with 15 more in progress, each translation sparking debate
money_quotes: From time to time I get introduced as a 'signatory of the Agile Manifesto'. Usually what they mean is that I'm an author of the Manifesto for Agile Software Development, and thus one of its initial signatories.
Ward still audits the signatures himself with the help of some ruby/cgi code that Prag Dave whipped up all those years ago.
There's never been any particular push to add signatures. People have just signed up as they have come across the web page.
Each translation sparks a lively debate and discussion.
posts.key_insight: The organic growth of Agile Manifesto signatories from Ward Cunningham's modest expectation of 'dozens or even hundreds' to over 10,000 reflects agile's grassroots adoption without any organized promotional effort.

posts.summary: Resource pools provide an efficient way to manage expensive-to-create resources like database connections and threads. Rather than creating resources on demand, clients borrow them from a pre-initialized pool and return them when finished. Pools can be configured to grow dynamically when demand exceeds capacity, with limits to prevent unbounded growth. The pattern also requires strategies for handling resource leaks, with testing techniques to detect clients that fail to return resources.
key_points: Resource pools manage expensive resources (database connections, threads) by lending them to clients rather than creating new ones each time
Pools can respond to exhaustion by throwing exceptions, blocking clients, or dynamically growing to meet demand
Dynamic pool growth should still have upper limits, with optional shrinking when resources sit idle
Resource leaks can be detected by setting pool limit to 1 during testing and throwing exceptions when exceeded
money_quotes: Many programs need to make use of resources that are expensive to create and maintain.
When a client needs to use a database connection, rather than create one itself, it asks the pool to give it one.
Often the best response, if possible, is to grow the pool by creating further connections.
One problem you often run into with resource pools is clients not handing back the resources - which is a resource leak.
A good way of combating that is to set the pool limit to 1 during testing, and configure the pool so that asking for a resource at limit raises an exception.
posts.key_insight: Resource pools transform expensive resource creation into a borrowing pattern, with the key challenge being leak detection rather than allocation strategy.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler recounts a vacation to Savannah and Charleston, two historic Southern cities he found well worth visiting. He notes the contrasting atmospheres - Savannah's quiet squares under live oaks versus Charleston's more open feel. The post covers practical travel tips including timing (early April for azaleas), accommodation via VRBO house rentals, and nearby day trips. He also offers restaurant recommendations and reading suggestions for understanding Savannah's culture.
key_points: Early April offers ideal weather and blooming azaleas for visiting these Southern cities
Savannah and Charleston have distinctly different atmospheres that complement each other well for a week trip
Renting houses through VRBO proved preferable to hotels for someone who travels frequently
Nearby attractions like Middleton Place, Jekyll Island, and Beaufort make excellent day trips
Reading 'Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil' is considered essential preparation for visiting Savannah
money_quotes: Both are well worth a couple days of wandering around.
Savannah reveled in quiet squares under canopies of live oak and spanish moss; Charleston felt more open.
I spend too much of my life in hotels.
It seems mandatory to read The Book before you go to Savannah.
We gladly spent a full day there.
posts.key_insight: Savannah and Charleston offer complementary historic charm - Savannah intimate and shaded, Charleston open and airy - making them ideal paired destinations for a week's exploration.

posts.summary: Non-deterministic tests—those that pass and fail randomly without code changes—are among the most damaging problems in automated testing. Fowler argues they're useless as regression tests and, worse, they infect entire test suites by training developers to ignore failures. He prescribes quarantining flaky tests immediately while working to eliminate them, then details the root causes: lack of test isolation, improper handling of asynchronous behavior, unstable remote services, direct system clock calls, and resource leaks. The key is treating non-determinism as an emergency that requires immediate attention rather than a nuisance to tolerate.
key_points: Non-deterministic tests are worse than no tests because they teach developers to ignore test failures, eventually compromising the entire test suite
Quarantine flaky tests immediately to preserve the integrity of your healthy test suite, but enforce limits on quarantine size or duration
Test isolation is essential—each test should be able to run independently in any order, preferably by rebuilding state from scratch rather than relying on cleanup
Never use bare sleeps for async operations; use callbacks or polling with high timeouts and short polling intervals
Always wrap system clocks and external services with test doubles to ensure deterministic behavior
money_quotes: A test is non-deterministic when it passes sometimes and fails sometimes, without any noticeable change in the code, tests, or environment.
Non-deterministic tests have two problems, firstly they are useless, secondly they are a virulent infection that can completely ruin your entire test suite.
Once you start ignoring a regression test failure, then that test is useless and you might as well throw it away.
As you gain confidence in your bug detector, you gain the courage to make big changes knowing that when you goof, the bug detector will go off and you can fix the mistake quickly.
Place any non-deterministic test in a quarantined area. (But fix quarantined tests quickly.)
Never use bare sleeps to wait for asynchronous responses: use a callback or polling.
Any advantage you gain by talking to the real system is overwhelmed by the need to stamp out non-determinism.
posts.key_insight: Non-deterministic tests don't just fail to provide value—they actively destroy the value of your entire test suite by training developers to ignore failures.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the Uniform Access Principle, coined by Bertrand Meyer, which states that clients should use the same notation to access module services regardless of whether they're stored or computed. The principle is fundamental to encapsulation, allowing objects to change their implementation without affecting clients. Despite being essential to object-oriented programming, few languages properly support it, with most relying on accessor conventions. Fowler argues against naming conventions that betray implementation details and dismisses performance concerns as a special case that can be addressed through caching.
key_points: The Uniform Access Principle means clients should neither know nor care whether a value is stored or computed
This principle is fundamental to encapsulation and data-hiding, yet remarkably few OO languages truly follow it
Naming conventions like 'getAge' vs 'calcAge' subvert the principle by revealing implementation details
Performance concerns about slow computations should be treated as special cases, not reasons to abandon the principle
The principle applies to both getters and setters, though computed accessors are often read-only
money_quotes: All services offered by a module should be available through a uniform notation, which does not betray whether they are implemented through storage or through computation.
It effectively means that a client of the person should neither know nor care whether the age is stored or computed.
Although it's a fundamental feature of object-oriented programming, remarkably few OO languages really follow it.
Often you'll see conventions that subvert uniform access. An example of this might be saying that data accessors are named getAge while computed accessors are named calcAge.

I prefer to treat this as a special case - follow the uniform access principle unless there is a particularly slow computation (and that should be one that's been measured to be slow, not just expected to be slow).
posts.key_insight: Good API design hides implementation details so completely that clients cannot distinguish between stored data and computed values, preserving the freedom to change implementations without breaking callers.

posts.summary: Fowler presents a humorous 'law' observing that software demonstrations fail more spectacularly when the stakes are highest. He notes this principle serves both as an observable truth and as a face-saving explanation after a demo disaster. The law applies to both the likelihood and severity of problems during important presentations.
key_points: Demo failures correlate positively with audience importance
The 'law' serves as a consolation when things go wrong
Both probability and severity of problems increase with stakes
money_quotes: The chances of a demo going wrong are directly proportional to the importance of the audience.
It also provides something of a sop when you explain it after everything has gone pear-shaped.
posts.key_insight: High-stakes demos have a perverse tendency to fail precisely when success matters most, a phenomenon so universal it deserves recognition as a law.

posts.summary: Fowler describes using UML class diagrams as personal note-taking while exploring unfamiliar code bases, particularly domain models. He observes that UML has fallen out of fashion but continues to find it valuable for his own use. He explicitly rejects reverse-engineering tools that auto-generate diagrams, arguing they produce useless output by including everything rather than highlighting what matters. The key distinction he draws is between diagrams as personal notes versus diagrams for communicating with others—different purposes requiring different approaches and tools.
key_points: UML remains useful as a personal note-taking tool even as it has fallen out of fashion in the industry
Reverse-engineering tools that auto-generate class diagrams are useless because they don't distinguish important from unimportant elements
Good diagrams highlight important stuff and ignore what's not—selective curation is the value
Speed matters for note-taking; Fowler prefers Umlet for its text-based wiki-ish markup
Diagrams for personal notes require different effort and tooling than diagrams meant to explain code to others
money_quotes: For me the value of a good diagram is that it highlights the important stuff, and ignores what's not.
I don't use reverse-engineering tools for this. Although this is quite straight-forward to do technically, the result is rather useless.
People who are less familiar with the syntax shouldn't worry about it in this mode of working.
With notes I'm creating reminders for myself, with explanatory diagrams I'm trying to communicate different things to another audience.
Although this isn't good for me financially, I can't say I'm displeased to see a lot of rather dodgy UMLisms going away.
posts.key_insight: The value of a diagram lies not in completeness but in selective curation—highlighting what matters and ignoring what doesn't.

posts.summary: Fowler argues against using cross-platform mobile toolkits, drawing parallels to their failure on desktop platforms. He identifies two core problems: the technical difficulty of either abstracting native controls or emulating them convincingly, and the deeper challenge that different platforms have fundamentally different user experience expectations. When his team tried porting an iPhone app to Android, they found they had to completely redesign the experience from scratch. His recommended alternative is using web apps that embrace being web apps rather than pretending to be native, or investing in true platform-native development when the platform warrants it.
key_points: Cross-platform toolkits face an impossible choice between lowest-common-denominator native controls or emulation that falls into an 'uncanny valley' of almost-right behavior
The bigger problem isn't UI controls but overall experience design—mobile platforms have even greater disparity in interaction patterns than desktops did
Web apps succeed as cross-platform solutions precisely because users expect them to behave like web apps, not native apps
If it's worth building a native app, it's worth building it properly with platform-specific experience design
Libraries without UI work well cross-platform; the problem is specifically with graphical interfaces
money_quotes: It's easy to get trapped in an uncanny valley where things work mostly like the native controls but there are just enough tiny differences to throw users off.
With UI controls you have to be really anal to get the behavior 'just right'.
Our initial experiments showed us that we had to completely rethink the experience design from scratch to get a decent Android app.
If it's worth building a native app, it's worth building it properly, including an individual experience design for that platform.
When you do the web app, don't try to make it look and feel like a native app - make it look like a mobile web app.
The key to its success here is that people expect web apps to behave like web apps, so they don't expect them to be like native apps.
posts.key_insight: Cross-platform mobile toolkits fail not primarily due to technical limitations, but because different platforms have fundamentally different user experience expectations that cannot be abstracted away.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler draws a parallel between medieval heraldry and modern presentation design, specifically around the challenge of distinguishing symbols from a distance. Medieval knights used the Rule of Tincture in heraldry to ensure their emblems were visible on battlefields. This rule divides colors into metals (white/silver, yellow/gold) and colors (everything else), requiring that metals not be placed on metals, nor colors on colors. Fowler suggests this principle translates well to PowerPoint design, where projector quality often limits contrast, making readability a common challenge.
key_points: Medieval heraldry and modern presentations share the need for clear visual distinction at a distance
The Rule of Tincture divides heraldic colors into metals (white, yellow) and colors (everything else)
You cannot place a metal on a metal or a color on a color in proper heraldry
This principle helps ensure text on colored backgrounds remains readable in slides
Many projectors have poor contrast, making this historical rule surprisingly relevant
money_quotes: PowerPoint wasn't invented in the Middle-Ages, when knights rushed around battlefields in full armor. But the slide decks of today share a common characteristic with those knights of old.
We may not have mud and dust, but many projectors aren't all that great at contrast.
The rule of tincture says that you cannot put a metal on a metal, or a color on a color.
If you have a blue (color) square, any text you put on it should be a metal (white or yellow).
I've found it a useful guideline to keep my slides easy to read.
posts.key_insight: Ancient heraldic principles for visual contrast remain directly applicable to modern presentation design.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on his conversion from paper book skeptic to ebook enthusiast, driven primarily by the convenience of traveling with unlimited reading material. He explores the implications for writers, noting that future books must be designed with multiple digital formats in mind, affecting everything from diagrams to section numbering. The piece examines the evolving relationship between publishers, distributors, and readers, highlighting how companies like Pragmatic Programmers are pioneering direct-to-reader models. Fowler argues that while the physical form of books is changing, the essential value—organizing and presenting a large body of material coherently—remains unchanged.
key_points: The shift to digital requires rethinking book design fundamentals, from color in diagrams to section numbering for cross-references
Video will not replace text because text allows faster consumption, easier skimming, and better navigation—critical for learning complex topics
The Pragmatic Programmers model of selling content access rather than individual representations (epub, kindle, PDF) represents a superior approach for readers
Publishers face pressure to automate production processes with semantic source files to efficiently generate multiple output formats
Self-publishing economics are shifting—lower prices with higher author percentages may become viable as distribution disintermediates
money_quotes: What makes a book hard to write, at least for me, is pulling together and organizing a large body of material. This is why a good book is more than a couple of dozen articles.
Video is really such a poor medium for communicating things in depth. I can read text far faster than I can watch a video of someone talking.
As with most changes we'll regret what we lose twice as much as appreciate what we'll gain until we get to the new status quo, whatever that becomes.
While it's nice to focus on logical structure in a way that's independent of its presentation, there are always cases like this that disturb the comfortable abstraction.
When I buy an ebook from them I am buying access to the content of the book - and I can take as many representations (epub, kindle, etc) as I like.

In the future I firmly believe this kind of automation will become more of an imperative, particularly if we want to push the boundaries of what a book should look like in a world of multiple tablets.
posts.key_insight: The book as a form survives not because of its physical medium but because of its unique value in organizing and synthesizing large bodies of material—something neither articles nor videos can replicate.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that web services should follow Postel's Law—being liberal in what they accept—to enable graceful evolution of service interfaces. Schema-driven binding approaches like generating code from XSD definitions create unnecessary brittleness, breaking clients even for non-breaking changes like adding fields. Instead, services should use tolerant readers that extract only needed data while ignoring unknown elements, using flexible queries like `//order` rather than rigid paths. Wrapping payloads in Data Transfer Objects provides additional insulation, and sharing reader code with providers enables Consumer-Driven Contracts for safe evolution.
key_points: Schema-driven code generation from service definitions creates unnecessary coupling that breaks on benign changes
Tolerant readers should extract only needed elements and make minimal assumptions about structure
Data Transfer Objects provide an additional abstraction layer protecting code from payload changes
Even binary protocols benefit from tolerance—use generic collections instead of direct object serialization
Sharing your reader code with service providers enables Consumer-Driven Contracts and safer evolution
money_quotes: be conservative in what you do, be liberal in what you accept from others.

Although there are some people who believe that you should just get your service definitions right first time so you never need to change them, my regular readers will not be surprised to find me absent from their parties.
Look ma, no programming. It works well until the provider needs to make any change to the interface, such as adding a field.
Adding field to an interface like this shouldn't be a breaking change for anyone - but often does break these schemes.
Your aim should be to allow the provider to make any change that ought not to break your code.
Rather than use an XPath search like /order-history/order-list/order use //order.
posts.key_insight: Service consumers should be maximally tolerant in parsing responses, extracting only what they need and ignoring the rest, to allow providers freedom to evolve their interfaces without breaking clients.

posts.summary: Fowler describes Thoughtworks' three-pillar model, borrowed from Ben & Jerry's, which balances Sustainable Business, Software Excellence, and Social Justice.
