He argues that while these pillars create tensions—such as being difficult consultants who push clients hard or refusing to build features that reinforce poor practices—they ultimately reinforce each other long-term. The Software Excellence pillar extends beyond internal practice to improving the broader software industry through public knowledge sharing. The Social Justice pillar encompasses both work for good causes and ensuring core commercial work benefits society. Fowler emphasizes that revenue is like oxygen: necessary for survival but not the purpose of existence.
key_points: The three pillars (Sustainable Business, Software Excellence, Social Justice) must be balanced rather than optimized individually
Thoughtworks deliberately chooses to be 'difficult consultants' who push for significant changes, prioritizing excellence over client comfort
The company refuses lucrative opportunities that would reinforce poor software practices, including certain product features and vendor partnerships
Public knowledge sharing about techniques is part of the mission to improve the entire software industry, despite the commercial cost
Long-term thinking reveals that the pillars reinforce each other more than they conflict
money_quotes: Revenue is like oxygen - you need it in order to live but it isn't what you live for.
Thoughtworks did a much better job of software delivery than Accenture, but clients were much happier with Accenture than with us.
One colleague likened us to a hard-driving personal trainer, which isn't always an easy relationship.
Many consultancies gain considerable revenue with product vendors whose products require lots of consulting support but provide little value to users.
I'm very happy to make money, but I believe that revenue should come from providing something valuable to customers and society.

Improving the operations of the used-car market in the USA isn't as socially glamorous as helping to find children after a natural disaster, but I still think it's part of the path that's led to humanity's greater prosperity.
When you look at the long-term, the pillars reinforce each other much more than they fight.
posts.key_insight: A company can sustain commercial success while refusing to compromise on professional excellence and social responsibility, because in the long run these values reinforce rather than undermine each other.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the common problem of presentation slides having text and diagrams too small for audience members to read, especially those sitting further back. He offers a simple, practical technique: compose presentations at 50% view size. If you can't easily read your own slides at half size, your audience will struggle during the actual presentation. This serves as a quick self-check before presenting.
key_points: Small text and diagrams are a widespread problem in presentation decks
People in the back of the room suffer most from undersized content
Composing at 50% view size simulates audience viewing conditions
If you struggle to read it at half size, your audience will too
money_quotes: A common problem in people's presentation decks is that they make text and diagrams so small that only the people in the front of the room can properly see them.
If I can't read it easily at 50%, then the audience will struggle too.
posts.key_insight: Test your presentation readability by composing at 50% view size—if you can't read it easily, neither will your audience.

posts.summary: Flag arguments are function parameters that change the function's behavior based on their value, such as a boolean determining premium vs regular booking. Fowler advocates replacing flag arguments with separate, clearly-named methods that communicate intent more explicitly. When implementation logic is tangled between the flag states, he suggests keeping a private flag-based method hidden behind public explicit methods. The exception is when the flag value comes directly from a data source like a UI checkbox, where passing through the boolean may be cleaner than wrapping it in conditionals.
key_points: Flag arguments obscure caller intent - book(martin, false) is less clear than regularBook(martin)
For tangled implementations with shared logic, use a private hidden method with explicit public wrapper methods
When behavior is derived from object state (like customer status), the caller shouldn't need to specify the flag
Boolean setters face the same tradeoff: prefer setOn()/setOff() unless the caller is passing through a boolean from a data source
API design should prioritize making things easier for the caller, considering where the data originates
money_quotes: My general reaction to flag arguments is to avoid them.
I can easily read regularBook(martin).
The point here is that only the regular and premium book methods should call hiddenBookImpl.
I like to telegraph this by using an ugly name - which also has the bonus that, if you have to, you can easily add a regex probe to ensure nobody else is calling it.
You only want different methods when the caller needs to specify which one she wants.
This is an example that an API should be written to make it easier for the caller.
posts.key_insight: Replace flag arguments with explicitly-named methods to make caller intent self-documenting, unless the flag value flows directly from an external data source.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his experience upgrading his home media server to an HP N36L. The upgrade was motivated by two main factors: switching from IDE to SATA drives for easier maintenance, and reducing power consumption from 80W to 20W. He installed Ubuntu 10.04 LTS and experimented with Puppet for configuration management. The post is a brief, practical hardware recommendation for home server enthusiasts.
key_points: The HP N36L offers easy hot-swappable SATA drive access through a front door design
Power consumption dropped dramatically from 80W to 20W idle, though ROI at $6/month savings is minimal
The compact, quiet form factor suits various home environments
Using Puppet for a home server is overkill but provides useful DevOps learning experience
money_quotes: At $6 a month, I'm not going to be making a great ROI on this, but at least I'll feel more virtuous about leaving it on all the time.
I also enjoyed the humor of the vendor in including a 160GB drive in the package. (I put in something rather larger.)
I took the opportunity to use puppet - overkill for a home server but useful to give me some devops creds.
Overall something worth recommending but nothing too dramatic - but then I don't like drama in a server.
posts.key_insight: A good home server should be boring and reliable—dramatic improvements in power efficiency and maintainability matter more than flashy features.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler documents his home server upgrade project, replacing an aging 2005 Debian box with an HP N36L server and adding a WD TV for video streaming. He walks through his requirements including samba shares, backups, music streaming via Squeezebox, and DHCP services, while also wanting to reduce power consumption and add Netflix capability. After evaluating options like Mac Mini, all-in-one HTPC builds, and NAS solutions, he chose to keep server and video functions separate for independent upgradeability. The post serves as a practical log of his decision-making process rather than a technical tutorial.
key_points: Separating server and video functions into distinct devices allows for independent upgrades and flexibility
RAID mirroring provides limited protection compared to regular automated backups, which also protect against user errors like accidental deletion
Familiarity with a platform (Debian/Unix) is a valid factor when choosing home server solutions
Hot-swapping capabilities in NAS units are often unnecessary for home use where drive changes are rare
Starting with simpler solutions (WD TV over Boxee) allows for future upgrades if needs change
money_quotes: I also see little point in RAID mirroring. It does protect you from a hard drive failure - but that's not the only thing that can go wrong.
RAID won't protect you from rm important_file.
So my preference is regular, automated, backups. This also allows the backup hard drive to be well away from the server box.
I ended up not fancying having it all in a single machine - preferring the ability to upgrade them independently.
I felt the WDTV would do for now and I could always change later should I need to.
posts.key_insight: Regular automated backups provide better protection than RAID mirroring because they guard against a wider range of failures including human error, not just hardware failure.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that companies should buy packages for utility (non-differentiating) business processes and build custom software for strategic advantages. The critical mistake is heavily customizing packages, which creates massive technical debt because packages lack proper development infrastructure like version control, testing, and deployment pipelines. This leads to upgrade nightmares—Gartner estimated $500 billion to bring corporate systems current. Instead, companies should either accept the package's way of working, use APIs to build custom interfaces alongside packages, or recognize that package customization platforms are poor development environments that cost far more than expected.
key_points: Buy packages for utility processes but accept their workflows; build custom for competitive advantage
Most packages lack 'deliverability'—version control, testing, deployment pipelines—making large customizations brittle
Package customization creates hidden costs that often exceed building custom software from scratch
Customizable packages are effectively another language/platform with the same adoption costs but worse hiring prospects
Prefer single-vendor solutions over best-of-breed to reduce integration complexity for utility functions
money_quotes: If the business process you are supporting is part of your competitive advantage you should build custom software, if not you should buy a package and adjust your business process to fit the way the package works.
The fact is that most packages aren't designed in such a way as to make customization really viable, at least not on a significant scale.
Recently a colleague came across a package customization that ran to 300KLOC of customization code.
The real cost is how much money has been wasted in customizations that weren't worthwhile or could have been cheaper with a purely custom route.
We must remember that many of these customizable packages are, in effect, another language or platform.
The nature of much package customization work deters able people, which makes it even harder to find good people.
Package environments usually provide a very poor platform for software development.
posts.key_insight: Package customization appears cheaper than custom development but creates compounding technical debt because packages fundamentally lack the tooling and practices needed for sustainable software development at scale.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that IT projects fall into two distinct categories—utility and strategic—requiring fundamentally different management approaches. Utility projects like payroll should 'just work' with minimal cost and customization, while strategic projects enable competitive differentiation and demand rapid iteration. The key distinction is whether the underlying business function is a differentiator. Fowler critiques the popular 'Bimodal IT' concept as a flawed interpretation that wrongly separates systems by technical layer rather than business value, and falsely assumes speed requires accepting defects.
key_points: The utility vs strategic distinction is about the business function, not the technology—strategic functions differentiate you from competitors
For utility projects, buy packages and adapt your process to match; for strategic projects, build custom software to enable differentiation
Risk profiles differ fundamentally: utility projects fear catastrophic failure, strategic projects fear being outpaced by competitors
The 80/20 rule applies—perhaps 95/5—meaning very few projects are truly strategic, but organizations often misclassify too many as strategic
Bimodal IT is a flawed model because it separates by technical layer instead of business value, and falsely trades quality for speed
money_quotes: If how you do this function is a crucial part of what makes you better than the competition, then the software that supports this function needs to be as good as you can make it.
For strategic projects, the biggest risk is not doing something before your competitors do.
For a strategic function you don't want the same software as your competitors because that would cripple your ability to differentiate.
It's like expecting the same people who design warehouses to design an arts museum.
High quality (and low defects) are a crucial enabler for rapid cycle-time.
'Shades of grey' give license to pile things into the wrong category; things that are really utility will be given an inflated importance.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental question for any IT initiative isn't about the technology itself, but whether the underlying business function differentiates you from competitors—and this distinction should drive radically different approaches to staffing, budgeting, and methodology.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler discusses his decision to add a Canon 70-300mm telephoto lens to his photography kit. After weighing options between upgrading his mid-range zoom or telephoto capabilities, he chose the 70-300 for its lighter weight, faster autofocus, and lower cost compared to alternatives. The purchase changed his shooting habits - he now typically carries the ultra-wide 10-22 and the 70-300, finding the mid-range gap less problematic than expected since he can crop wider shots. He keeps a compact camera as backup for spontaneous moments when lens changes would slow him down.
key_points: The desire for better equipment is a constant temptation for amateur photographers, even when current gear produces satisfying results
Most photos end up at resolutions far below what camera sensors capture, making theoretical sharpness improvements less meaningful in practice
Switching to a two-lens setup (ultra-wide and telephoto) with a mid-range gap proved more workable than expected
Cropping in post-processing can compensate for not having the ideal focal length available
Carrying a compact camera provides insurance against missing shots during lens changes
money_quotes: But, as most obsessive amateurs like me know, there's always that nagging desire for better equipment.
The counter is that most photos only end up on my own screensaver with a resolution that is way less that what my camera sensor will dish up.
But finally the new-lens demons began to gain control.
I think I am getting some sharper tele pictures out of it, but that could easily be a rationalization.
Cropping on the computer to adjust framing is one of the big changes to an ex-slide shooter like me.
posts.key_insight: Equipment upgrades often matter less than we convince ourselves they do, but the psychological satisfaction and workflow changes they enable can still be worthwhile.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains his approach to choosing digital SLR equipment as a self-described dabbler photographer. He chose Canon over Nikon somewhat arbitrarily, prioritized spending on lenses over camera bodies, and selected a mega-zoom as his primary lens despite purists' objections. His lens collection grew to include a fast 50mm for portraits, an ultra-wide 10-22mm for landscapes, and a 100mm for conference photography. Throughout, he emphasizes practical considerations like weight, convenience, and matching equipment to actual usage patterns rather than pursuing maximum quality.
key_points: System choice (Canon vs Nikon) is the critical irreversible decision, but the difference between them is minor enough that a trifling factor can decide it
Spend money on lenses rather than camera bodies because lenses stay current longer while bodies get upgraded frequently
A mega-zoom lens sacrifices some quality but the convenience of not changing lenses is worth it for casual shooting situations
Equipment choices should match actual usage patterns - most photos end up on screensavers or web pages, not gallery prints
Weight and portability matter enormously for photographers who shoot while doing other activities rather than dedicated photo expeditions
money_quotes: We geeks like photography because it provides the veneer of an artistic endeavor while allowing us to indulge in lots of technical details and spend money on expensive toys.
Once you pick your system, you'll then commit money to it by buying lenses and the cost of switching is more than a dabbler like me can go with.
The whole point of SLRs is to have good lenses, so I'd rather concentrate my limited dollars there.
Serious photographers will, probably rightly, turn their noses up at this lens. But I'm a dabbler.
Most of my photographs are taken while I'm doing something else, often with others around. I don't go out much to just shoot.
In that situation even changing lenses can be a significant deterrent to getting a shot.
posts.key_insight: Equipment decisions should be driven by how you actually use gear in practice, not by pursuing theoretical maximum quality that exceeds your real needs.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on his experience with social networks as Google+ launches, describing himself as a 'misanthropic hermit' who nonetheless found value in Twitter and Facebook. He uses Twitter as a professional, public-facing channel for his 35,000 followers while reserving Facebook for personal connections with people he knows. He praises Twitter's seemingly accidental design decisions like the 140-character limit and asymmetric following. While acknowledging Google+ Circles as a sensible feature, he's skeptical about adoption since network effects mean his choice depends entirely on where his contacts go, not which platform is technically superior.
key_points: Twitter's design constraints (140 chars, asymmetric following, mentions) work better in practice than they would sound in theory
The author maintains strict separation between Twitter (professional/geek content) and Facebook (personal/social content)
Google+ Circles address an obvious social need that Facebook ignored due to their ideology that people should be public
Network effects trump product quality - the best platform is worthless if your contacts don't use it
Low-bandwidth social updates work well for maintaining global friendships without the friction of emails or phone calls
money_quotes: At heart I'm a misanthropic hermit with enough intelligence to put on a veneer of sociability.
The 140 character limit is one of those silly technology fiats, yet it works well.
Favorite Buzz feature: I'm not restricted to 140 chars. Least fav Buzz feature: everyone else not restricted to 140 chars.
It doesn't matter what I think or do, it's what all my contacts do that counts.
I think the fact that Facebook didn't do this is that they are fixation in their view of the world that people should be public - which also explains their regular missteps over privacy.
It's rather like Wave was - a cool idea but of little use if nobody else is using it.
posts.key_insight: Network effects are the ultimate arbiter of social platform success - superior features mean nothing if your contacts aren't there.

posts.summary: LMAX is a retail financial trading platform that processes 6 million orders per second on a single thread, defying conventional wisdom about concurrent programming. The architecture centers on a Business Logic Processor that runs entirely in-memory with no database, using Event Sourcing for durability and recovery. Input and output operations are handled by custom concurrent components called Disruptors, which avoid traditional queue bottlenecks through careful attention to CPU cache behavior. The team arrived at this design after discovering that actor-model approaches spent more time managing queues than processing business logic, leading them to embrace 'mechanical sympathy' with modern hardware.
key_points: Single-threaded business logic processing eliminates concurrency complexity while achieving 6 million TPS through cache-friendly design and elimination of locks
Event Sourcing enables in-memory processing by making state fully recoverable from input events, with nightly snapshots allowing full restart in under a minute
The Disruptor component solves concurrent IO without queues by using a ring buffer with sequence counters, following the single-writer principle
Traditional queues fail high-performance systems because write contention causes locks, context switches, and cache invalidation
The asynchronous programming model requires splitting operations at external service boundaries but provides better error handling and resilience
money_quotes: In fact they ended up by doing all the business logic for their platform: all trades, from all customers, in all markets - on a single thread.

The phrase Martin Thompson likes to use is 'mechanical sympathy'. The term comes from race car driving and it reflects the driver having an innate feel for the car, so they are able to feel how to get the best out of it.
To get the best caching behavior, you need a design that has only one core writing to any memory location.
What they found was that the processors spent more time managing queues than doing the real logic of the application. Queue access was a bottleneck.
Even good programmers are very good at constructing performance arguments that end up being wrong, so the best programmers prefer profilers and test cases to speculation.
These days, you can get more main memory into your servers than us old guys could get as disk space.
The LMAX team certainly feels that the days of the coordinating transactional database are numbered.
posts.key_insight: By understanding how modern CPUs actually work and following the single-writer principle, you can achieve massive throughput with simple single-threaded code that outperforms complex concurrent systems.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reviews 'The Presentation' by Andrew Abela, which advocates for a distinct approach to small-group persuasive presentations. The book distinguishes between 'ballroom' presentations for large audiences and 'conference room' presentations aimed at convincing small groups to take specific actions. The core technique uses a SCoRE narrative structure (Situation, Complication, Resolution, Example) that cycles through problems and solutions. Rather than projected slides, Abela recommends detailed printed materials that pass the 'squint test' for overall structure while containing supporting evidence. Fowler finds the approach valuable while noting it's one of many presentation styles, and compares it to a military approach using prose handouts read before meetings.
key_points: Conference room presentations differ fundamentally from ballroom presentations - they aim to convince small groups to take specific action rather than inform large audiences
The SCoRE narrative structure (Situation, Complication, Resolution, Example) creates tension and resolution cycles that build toward a recommended decision
Printed slides with detailed evidence are preferable to projected slides for this presentation style, as they allow fine print while maintaining clear overall structure
The presenter should eventually back out of the conversation to let the audience discuss and decide
An alternative approach uses short prose handouts read before the meeting, with discussion replacing formal presentation
money_quotes: What you are trying to do is convince a small number of people to make a specific decision and to take a particular action
He characterizes this as taking people from a current situation to a preferred future situation
The point of the example is both to provide a concrete case for the audience to latch onto and to pace the rhythm of complication/resolution, which otherwise would be jarring
Each slide should have an easily grasped overall structure, which he explains as passing the squint test - you should be able to squint at the slide and immediately recognize its overall structure
The detail is the reason you shouldn't project them, since slides don't do well with lots of detail
I like the notion of thinking about different styles of presentation and how this leads to different ways of approaching them
posts.key_insight: Effective persuasive presentations for small groups require a fundamentally different approach than large-audience talks, using narrative tension cycles and detailed printed materials rather than projected slides.

posts.summary: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) splits a system's conceptual model into separate models for updates (commands) and reads (queries), rather than using a single CRUD-based model for both. While this separation can be valuable for complex domains or high-performance applications with disparate read/write loads, Fowler strongly cautions that CQRS adds significant complexity and should only be applied to specific bounded contexts where the benefit justifies the cost. He notes that most systems fit a CRUD model well, and the majority of CQRS implementations he's encountered have caused serious difficulties rather than improvements.
key_points: CQRS separates the conceptual model into distinct command (write) and query (read) models, often running in different processes or using different databases
The pattern naturally complements event-based architectures, eventual consistency, event sourcing, and domain-driven design
CQRS provides benefits for complex domains where command and query requirements differ significantly, and for high-performance systems needing independent read/write scaling
Most systems should use simple CRUD approaches; CQRS should only be applied to specific bounded contexts, not entire systems
A reporting database offers a simpler alternative for demanding queries without requiring full CQRS adoption
money_quotes: For some situations, this separation can be valuable, but beware that for most systems CQRS adds risky complexity.
The rationale is that for many problems, particularly in more complicated domains, having the same conceptual model for commands and queries leads to a more complex model that does neither well.
CQRS is a significant mental leap for all concerned, so shouldn't be tackled unless the benefit is worth the jump.

While I have come across successful uses of CQRS, so far the majority of cases I've run into have not been so good, with CQRS seen as a significant force for getting a software system into serious difficulties.
Using CQRS on a domain that doesn't match it will add complexity, thus reducing productivity and increasing risk.
You should be very cautious about using CQRS.
CQRS is a pattern that's good to have in the toolbox, beware that it is difficult to use well and you can easily chop off important bits if you mishandle it.
posts.key_insight: CQRS is a powerful pattern for specific complex domains and high-performance scenarios, but its overuse on systems that fit a simpler CRUD model has caused more harm than good in most real-world implementations.

posts.summary: Fowler explains his counterintuitive principle that painful activities become easier when done more frequently rather than avoided. Using integration as the primary example, he shows how the pain-to-time relationship is exponential, meaning frequent small integrations hurt far less than infrequent large ones. He identifies three mechanisms behind this effect: decomposition into manageable chunks, faster feedback loops for course correction, and practice leading to process improvement and automation. The principle applies broadly across agile practices including testing, refactoring, database migrations, and releases.
key_points: Pain from activities like integration grows exponentially with time between occurrences, so doing them more often drastically reduces total pain
Complex tasks become manageable when broken into small chunks that compose easily, as seen in database refactoring
Frequent execution creates tighter feedback loops, enabling faster learning and course correction in complex processes like software development
Practice improves process quality and creates motivation and opportunity for automation, which increases speed and reduces errors
money_quotes: If it hurts, do it more often.
If you have this kind of exponential relationship, then if you do it more frequently, you can drastically reduce the pain.
It did hurt, so you did it more often, and now it no longer hurts.
Specifying a large database migration involving multiple tables is hard and error prone. But if you take it one small change at a time it's much easier to get each one correct.
In a complex process, like software development, you have to frequently check where you are and make course corrections.
Once you've done something a few times, it's both easier to see how to automate it, and you are more motivated to automate it.
Whenever you're faced with a painful activity, ask yourself if these forces apply. If so increasing the frequency can make you more effective and remove a source of stress.
posts.key_insight: Painful development activities often have exponential pain-to-delay relationships, so doing them more frequently transforms them from dreaded ordeals into routine non-events through decomposition, feedback, and practice.

posts.summary: Fowler critiques the JavaScript/jQuery convention of using the same function name for both getting and setting values, a pattern borrowed from Smalltalk. He argues that retrieving data is fundamentally different from setting it, so methods should have distinct names. The overloaded approach creates ambiguity, especially when getters take arguments, making it unclear whether a call is reading or mutating state. He prefers languages like C# and Ruby that use property syntax with assignment operators to clearly signal mutation. For JavaScript, he recommends bare getters with prefixed setters (e.g., height() and setHeight()). While acknowledging the importance of following language conventions, he notes JavaScript lacks strong conventions, making this a case where deviation is justified.
key_points: Getting and setting are fundamentally different operations that deserve clearly different names
The overloaded pattern creates confusion when getters take arguments, as the extra argument acts as a flag argument
Property syntax with assignment operators (C#, Ruby) best signals mutation intent
For languages without property syntax, a bare getter with prefixed setter is preferable to overloading
Language conventions matter, but JavaScript's weak conventions make deviation acceptable
money_quotes: My principal objection is that the act of retrieving data is fundamentally different to that of setting a value, so the names should be more clearly different.
The presence of the extra argument acts as a FlagArgument, which is usually a Bad Thing.
The point here is that use of assignment clearly signals mutation.
I'm not proposing that Java's ugly getHeight() / setHeight(100) convention is better.
JavaScript, however, isn't noted for having strong conventions, so here I'd prefer to avoid this convention, even if it is used by jQuery.
posts.key_insight: APIs should make the distinction between reading and mutating state syntactically obvious, because these operations have fundamentally different consequences.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his recommended podcasts across various genres, from storytelling and science to economics and software engineering. He highlights This American Life and Radio Lab as joint favorites with his wife, praising their storytelling craft and innovative audio design. For professional development, he recommends Software Engineering Radio as a way to stay current with industry developments. The post also covers educational podcasts on economics, history of ideas, and literature, reflecting Fowler's broad intellectual interests beyond software.
key_points: This American Life and Radio Lab excel at storytelling and making complex topics accessible to general audiences
Planet Money emerged from This American Life's success with 'The Giant Pool of Money' episode on the mortgage crisis
Software Engineering Radio provides valuable industry insights through interviews with leading figures despite the limitation of no code examples
In Our Time and A History of the World in a Hundred Objects offer intellectual enrichment on the history of ideas
Personal recommendations span far beyond technology, reflecting diverse interests in literature, economics, and cricket
money_quotes: The show does a really good job of the storytelling aspect in this, which makes it such a pleasure to listen to.
Radio Lab focuses on science stories, especially cognitive science, doing a really good job of explaining the material.
It's also remarkable for a very innovative approach to their audio design, which involves a lot audio tricks to help bring out the science.
Stochasticity helps explain how people really don't understand randomness.
Despite the understandable lack of code examples, I've found the podcast has provided a good way to keep on top of a range of developments in our industry.
The podcast takes a hundred of these and weaves them into a narrative of human development, one that manages to avoid much of the Euro-centric perspective of the history I grew up with.
posts.key_insight: Quality podcasts that excel at storytelling and explanation provide valuable ways to stay informed across both professional and personal interests.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that while modern version control tools have made textual merging easier, they cannot solve semantic conflicts—situations where code merges cleanly but behaves incorrectly. He illustrates this with examples like method renaming and extracting side effects, where parallel development creates integration bugs that won't be caught by merge tools. The post advocates for self-testing code and frequent integration as the primary defenses against semantic conflicts, reinforcing his long-standing position against feature branching.
key_points: Modern VCS tools solve textual conflicts but cannot detect semantic conflicts where merged code compiles but behaves incorrectly
Method renames and side-effect extractions create subtle integration bugs that slip through automated merging
Self-testing code is the primary defense for detecting semantic conflicts during integration
Frequent merging (continuous integration) reduces semantic conflict severity by catching changes within hours rather than days
DVCS advocates who promote feature branching based on easy merging are missing the semantic conflict problem
money_quotes: Branching is easy, but merging is hard.
By a semantic conflict I mean a situation where Jez and I make changes which can be safely merged on a textual level but cause the program to behave differently.
However powerful your tooling is, it will only protect you from textual conflicts.
The particularly annoying point is that semantic conflicts are harder to spot and harder to fix.
We can't automatically resolve semantic conflicts. Maybe some day tools will be able to tackle some of them, but I suspect some gnarly ones will always be with us.
Tests are effectively probing our code to see if their view of the code's semantics are consistent with what the code actually does.
Jez's difficulties are much less if he discovers my change in a few hours rather than in a few days.
posts.key_insight: Version control tools can only solve textual conflicts; semantic conflicts require self-testing code and continuous integration to catch, making feature branching inherently risky regardless of tooling sophistication.

posts.summary: Fowler evaluates version control tools based on developer experience, recommending Subversion, Git, and Mercurial as the only tools that pass his 'recommendability threshold.' He criticizes ClearCase and TFS for poor capability, and Visual Source Safe as particularly dangerous. The choice between centralized (Subversion) and distributed (Git/Mercurial) depends on team skill and discipline, with DVCS requiring more maturity to avoid 'merge doom.' Between Git and Mercurial, Git offers more power but less usability, while GitHub's ecosystem gives Git a significant advantage. Ultimately, practices and workflows matter more than tools themselves.
key_points: Three tools pass the recommendability threshold: Subversion, Git, and Mercurial—all open source
DVCS flexibility can be dangerous without team maturity; it encourages branching that can lead to merge problems
Git wins on power and merging capability; Mercurial wins on usability and discoverability
GitHub's ecosystem is a major factor favoring Git over Mercurial
Dual-VCS strategies work well when corporate standards mandate inferior tools—use a capable VCS locally and sync to the corporate system periodically
money_quotes: Visual Source Safe, or as I call it: Visual Source Shredder
DVCS encourages quick branching for experimentation... but that flexibility can be dangerous if you don't have the maturity to use it well
There is this amazingly powerful thing writhing around in there that will basically do everything I could possibly ask of it if only I knew how
Git seems to be attractive more to those who enjoy learning the internals while mercurial seems to appeal more to those who just want to do version control
Although modern VCS have almost magic tools to merge different people's changes, these merges are still just merging text. Continuous Integration is still necessary to get semantic consistency
I'd rather use a poor tool with CI than a good tool without
Tools can certainly make it much easier to use a good set of practices, but in the end it's up to the people to use an effective way of working
posts.key_insight: The choice of version control tool matters less than the practices and workflows teams adopt—Continuous Integration with any capable tool beats a powerful tool used poorly.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that while patents were historically one of humanity's most valuable inventions—enabling the Industrial Revolution by incentivizing innovation—software patents have become an 'unmitigated disaster.' He traces patents' original purpose through William Rosen's analysis of how they fueled unprecedented economic change in 18th century England. However, software patents have abandoned the core principle of novelty, routinely granting monopolies on obvious or already-existing techniques. The result is a system that stifles rather than encourages innovation, weaponized for legal battles and shakedowns against smaller companies, and Fowler concludes we'd be better off eliminating software patents entirely than continuing with the current broken system.
key_points: Patents were a key enabler of the Industrial Revolution by providing financial incentive for inventors beyond just the wealthy
Software patents have abandoned the novelty requirement, routinely covering obvious techniques or ideas already in use
Patent terms are too long for fast-moving software, and patents are far too broad and vague rather than narrow and specific
Patents have become weapons for legal battles rather than tools for incentivizing innovation, particularly harming small companies
Programmers who cooperate in obtaining baseless patents undermine the profession's claim to be treated as professionals
money_quotes: While patents (even software patents) are a good idea in principle, in practice they have turned into an unmitigated disaster and would be better scrapped.
The biggest change the Industrial Revolution unleashed was change itself.
The core problem with software patents is that this key principle has been tossed aside.
Narrow patents encourage innovation by incenting people to think of different ways to solve the same problem, broad patents snuff that innovation out.
The result of all this debasement is a world where patents no longer incentivize and communicate new inventions, but where they are weapons to be used to fight legal battles.
The tragedy is that patents have become a source of reinforcing existing powers.
I do think that any programmer who cooperates in getting a baseless patent should be ashamed of themselves.
A world without software patents would be better than the mess we're currently in.
posts.key_insight: Software patents have been so thoroughly debased from their original purpose of rewarding genuine novelty that they now actively impede the innovation they were designed to encourage.

posts.summary: Fowler explores 'memory image' as an alternative to traditional database persistence, where application state lives entirely in memory and is rebuilt from event logs rather than stored in a database. The approach uses event sourcing as its foundation—capturing every state change as a persistent event that can be replayed. He examines three implementations: LMAX's high-performance trading system, EventPoster architectures for analytics, and Smalltalk's development environment. While memory images offer performance gains and eliminate database mapping complexity, they require careful handling of event migration and concurrency. Fowler suggests that increasing memory sizes and the NOSQL movement may drive renewed interest in this historically niche pattern.
key_points: Event sourcing is the key enabler—capturing all state changes as events allows full state reconstruction without maintaining a synchronized database
Memory images eliminate database mapping code and synchronization concerns, trading them for event storage reliability and snapshot management challenges
Concurrency handling shifts from database transactions to application-level solutions, as demonstrated by LMAX's single-threaded approach
Event log migration is a critical consideration—serialization formats must handle schema evolution gracefully to avoid being trapped by old event structures
The historical barrier of memory size has largely disappeared as commodity servers now have more RAM than previous generations had disk space
money_quotes: But there's another question to consider: 'should we use a database at all?'
The events are then the primary persistence mechanism.
Using a memory image allows you to get high performance, since everything is being done in-memory with no IO or remote calls to database systems.
Perhaps more importantly it means you can get rid of database mapping code, or worrying about synchronizing between in-memory state and database state.
They found the simplification in programming model to be equally important. They don't have to worry about concurrency as its all about a single thread.
Like many of these kinds of ideas, it's an approach that's been used and reinvented many times, but never got mainstream traction.
For a long time, a big argument against using a memory image was size, but now most commodity servers have more memory than we were used to having in disk.
posts.key_insight: Memory images offer a compelling alternative to databases by keeping all application state in memory and using event sourcing for persistence, trading database complexity for event management concerns.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes his ongoing project with his wife and neighbors to watch every Shakespeare play in every available video version. For A Midsummer Night's Dream, they watched six versions chronologically, comparing the BBC's solid baseline, Hollywood's 1935 Busby Berkeley-style spectacle, Peter Hall's star-studded 1960s RSC production, Hoffman's lavish 1999 film, and Noble's imaginatively staged 1996 RSC version. The Noble production emerged as their clear favorite due to its creative staging (light bulbs for trees, a blank stage) combined with strong acting and direction. Fowler finds comparing different interpretations of the same fixed text to be as fascinating as Shakespeare's plays themselves.
key_points: The BBC Shakespeare cycle provides a reliable baseline with excellent actors and reasonable production values
Hollywood productions (1935 and 1999) prioritize visual spectacle and star power, with varying degrees of success in handling Shakespeare's language
Non-literal staging approaches, like Noble's abstract forest design, can be more compelling than traditional woodland settings
The fixed nature of Shakespeare's text makes comparing directorial visions particularly interesting
Strong acting and imaginative direction can triumph over limited budgets
money_quotes: I really enjoy watching multiple versions of a play like this. Every production has the single text to work from, that fixes what can be said, with only cuts and occasional rearrangements to play with.
To that fixed text they then put their own vision into the staging and the way the actors perform the roles.
In many ways I find the different way people approach Shakespeare to be every much as interesting as the plays themselves.

It isn't like a stage play that's been filmed, it definitely had a first rate movie direction, with lots of memorable visual sequences, yet it kept the feel of a stage play. That's a rare thing to pull off.
We like our Shakespeare served with a twist.
posts.key_insight: The constraints of a fixed text make comparing different productions illuminating, as each interpretation reveals as much about the creative vision of its makers as about Shakespeare himself.

posts.summary: Fowler explains why he avoids creating video content despite its growing popularity and commercial viability. While acknowledging that video suits his public speaking abilities and that audiences clearly value it, he finds the medium personally unappealing for both learning and creating. His core objection is that writing serves as a thinking tool—he figures things out through the act of writing, whereas video feels like it requires pre-formed ideas. The essay format allows for exploration and discovery that video production doesn't seem to support.
key_points: Reading is faster than listening and allows for easy navigation—skipping ahead or jumping back
Audio has a niche advantage over video: it can be consumed while walking or driving
Many people publish video talks but miss the opportunity to provide audio-only versions
Writing is a tool for thinking, not just explaining already-formed ideas
The word 'essay' comes from French 'essayer' meaning 'to try'—writing is trying out ideas
money_quotes: I can read much faster than I can listen.
More than once I've been frustrated by people who publish videos of talks, but miss the opportunity to do an audio-only version.
When I'm writing, I'm not usually explaining things I've already figured out, I'm doing the figuring out as I write.
The act of writing something down has often helped me understand a topic, indeed I think that's a large part of why I write as much as I do.
I enjoy trying to understand things, and writing for others is a vital tool to help me do that.
posts.key_insight: Writing is not merely a way to communicate ideas you've already formed, but a tool for discovering and refining those ideas in the first place.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler recounts highlights from the 2011 goto conference in Aarhus, focusing on emerging technologies and architectural trends. He discusses Google's new Dart language as a cleaner replacement for JavaScript, noting its optional typing system. The post examines a case study of migrating Denmark's prescription system from MySQL to Riak for better scalability. Fowler observes that the 'DatabaseThaw' is gaining momentum, with organizations increasingly choosing NoSQL solutions when appropriate. He also highlights the Moose code analysis tool, praising its embodiment of the Lisp/Smalltalk philosophy of malleable, user-extensible tools.
key_points: Dart offers a cleaner alternative to JavaScript with optional typing that doesn't affect compilation but enables IDE support and type checking
The shift away from relational databases is accelerating as organizations realize no single storage solution fits all cases
Event Interception migration patterns allow testing new data stores alongside existing systems before full transition
Malleable tools that encourage users to modify and extend them (like Moose and emacs) embody a powerful but underutilized philosophy
Scala is emerging as a serious option for organizations developing on the JVM platform
money_quotes: Type checkers are lobotomized theorem provers.
Even well-written javascript was not clear enough code.
Relational databases were built with a mindset of a single big server, but increasingly we are seeing clusters of commodity servers - and thus a rethink of data storage.
Increasingly people are beginning to realize that data storage is no longer automatically the province of SQL databases, but is now a choice that demands thought.
No single data storage solution is the right one for all cases, so you have to pick the one that fits your circumstances.
This philosophy sees the user of a tool as someone who is expected to tinker with the very guts of the tool in order to mould it to her needs.

Malleable tools haven't caught on that much. They do require an eagerness from the user to learn how to make the tool truly sing, but this effort comes with the reward of a tool that can truly be tailored to an individual's needs.
posts.key_insight: The era of automatic reliance on relational databases is ending as distributed systems demand thoughtful selection of data storage solutions matched to specific use cases.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that refactoring should be an ongoing, opportunistic practice rather than a scheduled phase in the development lifecycle. He introduces the 'camp site rule' - always leave code better than you found it - as a guiding principle for continuous improvement. The post identifies several natural opportunities for refactoring: before implementing features, while adding functionality, and after getting something working. Fowler acknowledges the risk of falling down rabbit holes but emphasizes that good judgment and incremental improvement ultimately accelerate development rather than slowing it down. He warns against organizational practices like strong code ownership and long-lived feature branches that create friction for opportunistic refactoring.
key_points: Refactoring should be continuous and opportunistic, not scheduled into dedicated phases or iterations
The 'camp site rule' means everyone should leave code better than they found it through small, regular contributions
Natural refactoring opportunities arise before, during, and after implementing features - but always with green tests
Avoid rabbit holes through good judgment - incremental improvement across frequent visits is more effective than perfection in one pass
Development practices like strong code ownership and feature branches that discourage opportunistic refactoring are organizational smells worth addressing
money_quotes: At any time someone sees some code that isn't as clear as it should be, they should take the opportunity to fix it right there and then - or at least within a few minutes.
Always leave the code behind in a better state than you found it.
The whole point of refactoring is that it makes the code base easier to work with, thus allowing the team to add value more quickly.

If you always make things a little better, then repeated applications will make a big impact that's focused on the areas that are frequently visited - which are exactly the areas where clean code is most valuable.
There's often a temptation to leave a change in another part of a code base to another day - but another day often doesn't come.
From the beginning I've always seen refactoring as something you do continuously, as regular and indivisible a part of programming as typing if statements.
A team that's using refactoring well should hardly ever need to plan refactoring.
posts.key_insight: Refactoring works best as an opportunistic habit woven into daily programming rather than as a planned activity, with small continuous improvements preventing the accumulation of technical debt.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that adding people to software teams too quickly causes more harm than good, even when organizations are willing to pay for faster delivery. He explains that team productivity doesn't scale linearly with headcount—his rough estimate is that doubling a team yields only about 50% more productivity due to communication overhead. The real danger lies in ramping up before the codebase and team culture can absorb new members, leading to architectural inconsistency, code duplication, and broken communication patterns. He advises starting large projects with small teams who establish the design foundations, then growing carefully—never more than doubling at once—with settling periods between additions. The core message is that scaling teams is often not worth the cost and complexity it introduces.
key_points: Productivity gains from adding people follow roughly a square root relationship—doubling a team yields about 50% more output, not 100%
Rapid team growth causes codebase breakdown: duplication, competing frameworks, and the Broken Windows effect where inconsistency breeds more inconsistency
New team members create a temporary productivity dip as existing staff must help them onboard, often showing no net gain for weeks
Large projects should start with small teams (around a dozen) who establish design foundations before any significant scaling
Before ramping up, actively remove bad code patterns since new people will copy whatever exists in the codebase
money_quotes: You don't get a linear benefit, doubling a team won't double its productivity because the communication and coordination costs kick in and blunt the increase.
I've come across projects who added too many people too quickly. This manifests itself in a breakdown of the cohesion of the code base itself.
Several friends of mine know about the project that had three object-relational mapping frameworks within a single application.
People will naturally copy stuff that's already there, so you should ensure that what's there is all going to make a good platform for further development.
I've rarely come across a large team where there isn't a feeling that the team could be significantly cut without reducing its productivity.
Scaling agile is the last thing you want to do.
posts.key_insight: The urgency to deliver faster by adding people often backfires because teams need time to absorb new members, and premature scaling destroys the codebase coherence and team communication that make productivity possible in the first place.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that the long-standing dominance of relational databases is beginning to thaw, analogous to the earlier 'nuclear winter' in programming languages caused by Java's dominance. He attributes relational databases' grip not to their data management superiority but to their role in integration via SQL as a common query language. The rise of web services and HTTP-based integration is breaking this grip by enabling application databases rather than integration databases. This shift allows developers to choose data models suited to their specific needs rather than defaulting to RDBMS.
key_points: Relational database dominance parallels the earlier 'nuclear winter' in programming languages, but represents a longer and deeper freeze
The RDBMS grip stems more from SQL's role as an integration lingua franca than from relational superiority in data management
Shared database integration creates tight coupling between applications - 'you can't get bigger breach of encapsulation than that'
HTTP/web services as an integration protocol enables the shift from integration databases to application databases
This architectural shift liberates applications to choose appropriate data models rather than defaulting to relational
money_quotes: I've never liked the approach of multiple applications tightly coupled through a common database - you can't get bigger breach of encapsulation than that.
The role of SQL as mostly-standard query language has been central to the dominance of relational databases.
If you switch your integration protocol from SQL to HTTP, it now means you can change databases from being IntegrationDatabases to ApplicationDatabases. This change is profound.
If you integrate through HTTP it no longer matters how an application stores its own data, which in turn means an application can choose a data model that makes sense for its own needs.
I don't think this means that relational databases will disappear - after all they are the right choice for many situations.
posts.key_insight: The dominance of relational databases was never really about data management—it was about integration—and the rise of HTTP-based integration finally frees applications to choose databases suited to their actual needs.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that enterprises should move beyond the assumption that relational databases are the default choice for data persistence. Drawing on Neal Ford's concept of polyglot programming, he proposes that organizations should use different storage technologies for different types of data based on how that data will be manipulated. While acknowledging the complexity costs of managing multiple persistence technologies, he contends the benefits—reduced development drag and better scalability—make this shift worthwhile for strategic projects. Fowler predicts this 'database thaw' will progress steadily over the coming decade, though companies will adopt it conservatively.
key_points: For new strategic enterprise applications, relational persistence should no longer be the default assumption—alternatives must be seriously evaluated
Polyglot persistence will appear even within single applications, with different components using different storage technologies based on data manipulation needs
The costs include learning new interfaces, understanding performance characteristics, and grappling with consistency/availability tradeoffs on clusters
Strategic projects suffering from development drag or intensive data needs are the best candidates for alternative persistence
This shift will be gradual due to natural conservatism around data storage, but the trend is inevitable
money_quotes: I'm confident to say that if you starting a new strategic enterprise application you should no longer be assuming that your persistence should be relational.
Increasingly we'll be first asking how we want to manipulate the data and only then figuring out what technology is the best bet for it.
The transactional single point of truth will no longer hold sway (although its role as such has often been illusory).
When relational databases are used inappropriately, they exert a significant drag on application development.
Polyglot persistence will come at a cost - but it will come because the benefits are worth it.
A component boundary is a good way to wrap a particular storage technology chosen for the way its data in manipulated.
posts.key_insight: The question is no longer whether to use a relational database, but how you want to manipulate your data—and only then choosing the storage technology that best fits.

posts.summary: Fowler reviews his experience with Apple's Thunderbolt Display after purchasing it as a docking station solution for his MacBook Pro. While he encounters a persistent sleep/wake bug requiring cable reconnection and finds the glossy screen reflective, he concludes the display is worth the investment. The large, high-resolution screen has unexpectedly shifted his work habits, causing him to migrate from his Ubuntu desktop to the Mac for writing tasks. He particularly values the display for fitting multiple windows side-by-side and for improved video calls.
key_points: The display has a recurring bug where it fails to wake from sleep about 25% of the time, requiring cable reconnection
Glossy screens cause annoying reflections depending on ambient lighting
The high resolution and large size accommodate multiple windows side-by-side, improving productivity for writing and photo editing
Quality displays can shift work habits - the superior experience drew him away from his Ubuntu desktop
Built-in camera and speakers make video calls significantly better
money_quotes: Sensing this laptop reached across the network to my Ubuntu desktop, seduced it, and persuaded it to kill one of its monitors.
I've always believed its worth spending money on a BigScreen - and this one, despite the sleep problem, is a winner for me.
The surprising thing is that it's shifted my work habits.
There's a serious danger that my Ubuntu box is going to become unused. (Which, I guess, is one way to cope with Unity.)
posts.key_insight: Investing in high-quality display hardware can unexpectedly reshape work habits by making certain environments more pleasant to use.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler and Jez Humble wrote an open letter protesting Pearson's support for the SOPA/PIPA legislation, which they viewed as fundamentally flawed despite sympathizing with its copyright protection intent. They argued the bills lacked due process protections, proposed technically unsound approaches that would undermine internet security, and were crafted without adequate engineering input. The letter called on Pearson to reconsider its support and take responsibility as a technology education company. Over 100 prominent technical authors, including Joshua Bloch, James Gosling, Grady Booch, and Eric Evans, co-signed the letter in solidarity.
key_points: The legislation's provisions for blocking websites lack due process, risking punishment of innocent parties, especially smaller organizations with limited legal resources
The technical approach of manipulating DNS protocols undermines internet security and contradicts efforts to improve it, while being easily circumvented by determined actors
Congressional proceedings showed no engineering expertise and were heavily tilted toward bill supporters
As a technology education company, Pearson has a responsibility to ensure copyright protection efforts are balanced and technically sound
The letter gathered signatures from over 100 influential technical authors, demonstrating broad industry opposition
money_quotes: Even the most serious criminals need to be treated with due process, otherwise we risk innocent people facing punishment.
Not just do these approaches undermine internet security, they are also ineffective to moderately determined people who can work-around the blocking that's being done.
We don't expect members of congress to understand software engineering issues, but we do expect them to listen to those that do.

It is important for those that wish to protect copyrighted works to ensure they take actions that balance the need to protect intellectual property with fair procedures in sympathy with the way the internet works.
The contrast between Pearson and O'Reilly has been particularly stark.
posts.key_insight: Copyright protection legislation must be crafted with engineering input and due process safeguards, otherwise it risks undermining the very internet infrastructure it seeks to protect.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes how ThoughtWorks colleagues have organized code jam events where developers volunteer to build software for charitable organizations like RapidFTR and OpenMRS. Starting from a one-off event in New York in 2010, a small group began meeting weekly to contribute to open-source projects for NGOs. Fowler explains that while one-off events rarely produce much useful software, they generate awareness and can seed regular meetups. The post provides practical advice for organizing successful code jams, emphasizing the importance of champions, small tasks, easy setup, and shared food.
key_points: One-off code jams rarely produce much useful software but generate awareness and can seed regular meetups
Regular code jams with a committed core of people can make significant contributions to a codebase
Success requires champions who organize, tasks small enough to complete in one session, and easy development setup
People participate to work on different technologies with different people for an audience beyond just developers
The open-source model fits well for NGO software projects
money_quotes: Whatever people may say and hope, they'll rarely work on the project outside code jam hours, and the schedule is too infrequent to want half-done things hanging over.
One-off code jams like this don't usually produce much useful software, since they are too short to really accomplish much. But they still have value.
People come because they get to work on some different technologies, with a different group of co-developers, to an audience that (unlike most open-source projects) isn't just other developers.
Small tasks allow teams to make perceptible progress each jam - which helps keep motivation high.
As any XPer knows, sharing food when working is an important part of gelling a team.
âCode-jamâ is a problematic name for these events. The events I describe here are the utter opposite of competitive programming, on many levels, but have attracted the same name.
posts.key_insight: Regular volunteer code jams succeed through realistic expectations about contributor time, small completable tasks, dedicated organizers, and the motivating appeal of building software for meaningful causes rather than just other developers.

posts.summary: This brief post introduces a PDF infodeck co-authored with Pramod Sadalage that explains NoSQL databases to technical managers. The authors describe why relational databases dominated for so long and what forces disrupted that dominance. Rather than positioning NoSQL as a replacement, they emphasize that relational databases still have an important role, advocating for polyglot persistence—using different data storage technologies for different needs.
key_points: NoSQL databases represent an important shift in data storage that technical managers need to understand
Relational databases dominated for valid reasons, and specific forces led to NoSQL emerging as an alternative
The future isn't NoSQL replacing relational databases, but polyglot persistence—choosing the right storage technology for each use case
The content targets technical managers rather than developers, focusing on strategic understanding
money_quotes: We discuss the principal benefits of a NoSQL approach, but stress that relational databases still have a large role to play, thus our emphasis on polyglot persistence.
posts.key_insight: The rise of NoSQL doesn't mean abandoning relational databases, but rather adopting polyglot persistence—strategically choosing different data storage technologies for different needs.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler identifies common presentation anti-patterns that reduce the effectiveness of conference talks. He argues against putting sentences on slides, as they compete with the speaker's voice for the audience's attention. He criticizes repetitive elements like corporate logos on every slide, unnecessary pointer movements that should be built into slides, backtracking through slide decks, and mandatory slide titles. However, he concludes that content quality matters most—good content can overcome poor presentation technique, while great slides cannot save weak content.
key_points: Sentences on slides force audiences to parse written and spoken words simultaneously, breaking the flow of attention
Repetitive elements like logos and legal text either get tuned out (making them useless) or distract the audience
If you repeatedly point to the same things or backtrack to earlier slides, your slide sequence needs restructuring
Slide titles should be questioned rather than assumed mandatory—removing them often improves the visual channel
Content quality ultimately trumps presentation technique; audiences forgive poor slides if the ideas are interesting
money_quotes: Reading sentences breaks that up as the audience is both trying to parse the written word and the spoken words at the same time.
The audience either tunes out the floodmark (which makes them useless) or is distracted by them (which is worse).
Are your slides really so amazing that you'll lose a fortune if someone copies them?
If you find yourself going back to a previous slide, that indicates the slide sequence isn't right.
If the content isn't solid, or you don't really care about it, then even outstanding slidecraft isn't going to save you.
Conversely an audience will forgive all sorts of crummy presentation techniques if you have interesting things to say.
posts.key_insight: Slides should serve as a visual channel that complements rather than competes with the speaker's words, but ultimately no amount of slide optimization can substitute for having something worthwhile to say.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that test coverage is a misunderstood metric that's useful for finding untested code but poor as a measure of test quality. He criticizes organizations that mandate specific coverage percentages, noting that high coverage is easily achieved with low-quality tests. Instead of chasing numbers, he proposes that sufficient testing means you rarely have production bugs and aren't afraid to change code. The post warns against both under-testing and over-testing, with the latter manifested in tests that slow down development or require excessive changes for simple code modifications.
key_points: Test coverage helps find untested code but doesn't measure test quality
Mandating coverage targets leads to low-quality tests that hit numbers without meaningful assertions
True test sufficiency means few production bugs AND confidence to change code without fear
Over-testing shows up as tests that slow you down or require disproportionate changes
Coverage analysis is a technical tool with little value for management without context
money_quotes: Test coverage is a useful tool for finding untested parts of a codebase. Test coverage is of little use as a numeric statement of how good your tests are.
I expect a high level of coverage. Sometimes managers require one. There's a subtle difference.
If you make a certain level of coverage a target, people will try to attain it. The trouble is that high coverage numbers are too easy to reach with low quality testing.
I would be suspicious of anything like 100% - it would smell of someone writing tests to make the coverage numbers happy, but not thinking about what they are doing.
You rarely get bugs that escape into production, and you are rarely hesitant to change some code for fear it will cause production bugs.
If a part of your test suite is weak in a way that coverage can detect, it's likely also weak in a way coverage can't detect.
posts.key_insight: Test coverage is valuable for discovering untested code, but chasing coverage percentages as a target inevitably produces low-quality tests that satisfy metrics without providing real confidence.

posts.summary: Fowler observes that content management systems have become problematic tools that hinder development, particularly due to their single-copy architecture. He argues that editing and publishing have fundamentally different data access patterns—editing involves few people doing frequent updates, while publishing serves many readers doing mostly reads. The solution is to separate these concerns into independent modules, with the editing module optimized for updates and workflow, and the publishing module optimized for serving content at scale. This separation enables benefits like different storage technologies, format transformations, staging previews, and independent scaling strategies.
key_points: CMS tools are becoming invasively overused beyond their core purpose, hindering development
Editing and publishing have fundamentally different access patterns that warrant architectural separation
The publishing module can be freely replicated across clusters while editing should remain centralized
Format transformation (e.g., markdown to HTML) and page composition can happen at publish time rather than read time
Even user-generated content like wikis benefit from this separation since readers vastly outnumber writers
money_quotes: They aren't usually seen as helpful, indeed there is a clear sign that they are becoming a worryingly invasive tool - used for more than their core purpose in such a manner that they hinder overall development.

The idea of keeping a single copy of some piece of data is a common one. It's the underlying principle behind the relational concept of normalization, and enterprise architects often try to ensure that critical data has a single authoritative copy.
Yet for a CMS there's a clear downside - the data access patterns for editing and publication are very different.
The publishing module treats the article as largely read-only, updated rarely and only by the editing module.
The publishing module can be freely replicated across many nodes in a cluster, while it's usually better for the editing module to be centralized on a single node.
Separating these modules can also help with the editing workflow. Often people want to preview changes before publishing to the world.
posts.key_insight: When data access patterns differ significantly between use cases—like editing versus publishing—architectural separation into specialized modules yields better performance, scalability, and developer experience than the intuitive single-copy approach.

posts.summary: The test pyramid is a framework for balancing automated testing, advocating for many more unit tests than UI-based end-to-end tests. Fowler explains that while UI test automation seems attractive initially due to ease of recording, it quickly becomes problematic: slow, brittle, expensive, and prone to non-determinism. The pyramid recommends an intermediate layer of subcutaneous tests that operate through service/API layers, capturing end-to-end benefits without UI complexity. Fowler emphasizes that high-level tests should serve as a second line of defense, and any bug they catch should be replicated with a unit test before fixing.
key_points: UI-based end-to-end tests are brittle, slow, expensive to write, and prone to non-determinism
Record-playback tools are almost always a bad idea because they resist changeability and obstruct useful abstractions
Subcutaneous tests through API/service layers provide end-to-end benefits without UI framework complexity
Teams often conflate end-to-end tests, UI tests, and customer-facing tests, which are actually orthogonal characteristics
High-level test failures indicate both a functional bug and a missing unit test
money_quotes: You should have many more low-level unit tests than high level broad-stack tests running through a GUI.
Record-playback tools are almost always a bad idea for any kind of automation, since they resist changeability and obstruct useful abstractions.
Tests that run end-to-end through the UI are: brittle, expensive to write, and time consuming to run.
If my high level tests are fast, reliable, and cheap to modify - then lower-level tests aren't needed.
High-level tests are there as a second line of test defense.
If you get a failure in a high level test, not just do you have a bug in your functional code, you also have a missing or incorrect unit test.
Before fixing a bug exposed by a high level test, you should replicate the bug with a unit test. Then the unit test ensures the bug stays dead.
posts.key_insight: A well-balanced test portfolio should favor many focused unit tests over fewer broad end-to-end tests, with high-level tests serving as a safety net that reveals gaps in unit test coverage.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler defends Object/Relational Mapping tools against widespread criticism, arguing that while ORMs are genuinely complex and provide leaky abstractions, the underlying problem they solve is inherently difficult. He contends that rolling your own ORM is typically worse than using established tools, and that the real issue is inflated expectations—ORMs handle 80-90% of mapping problems, which is valuable even if imperfect. The alternatives (using relational models in memory or switching to NoSQL) have their own significant limitations and aren't always viable. Fowler concludes that ORMs deserve more respect given the genuinely hard problem they address.
key_points: The object/relational mapping problem is fundamentally hard because you're synchronizing between two quite different data representations, complicated by bidirectional changes and concurrency
Critics who dump on ORMs rarely state viable alternatives—you either solve the problem differently or avoid it, and both approaches have significant flaws
Rolling your own ORM almost always leads to a quagmire; the 1990s were littered with failed custom frameworks that seemed promising early but became traps
ORMs are leaky abstractions by necessity, but a tool that handles 80% of repetitive boilerplate code is still worthwhile—the problem is expecting 100%
NoSQL can avoid the mapping problem when the fit is good, but many situations require relational databases due to technical suitability or organizational constraints
money_quotes: The object/relational mapping problem is hard. Essentially what you are doing is synchronizing between two quite different representations of data.
If you're going to dump on something in the way many people do about ORMs, you have to state the alternative.
Usually you'd get enough early success to commit deeply to the framework and only after a while did you realize you were in a quagmire.
However much you may hate using an ORM, take my word for it - you're better off.
Many people treat the relational database like a crazy aunt who's shut up in an attic and whom nobody wants to talk about.
A framework that allows me to avoid 80% of that is worthwhile even if it is only 80%. The problem is in me for pretending it's 100% when it isn't.
If you are writing an application backed by a relational database you should damn well know how a relational database works.
They aren't pretty tools, but then the problem they tackle isn't exactly cuddly either.
posts.key_insight: ORMs are imperfect solutions to a genuinely hard problem, and criticizing them without acknowledging the difficulty of the alternatives or the value of handling even 80% of the mapping work is intellectually dishonest.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler and colleagues present a framework for organizations entering mobile development, identifying a fundamental trade-off between platform coverage and user experience quality. They describe two strategic extremes: the Laser strategy (single platform, exceptional experience) and Cover-Your-Bases strategy (all platforms, simpler experience). The article argues that mobile's importance stems from user experience expectations, as exemplified by Apple's market success. Organizations should view their initial mobile approach as an 'opening gambit' that evolves toward supporting all platforms with high-quality experiences, with hybrid web technologies offering promising paths forward.
key_points: Mobile commerce will eclipse web commerce, just as web commerce eclipsed offline commerce
The fundamental strategic tension is between platform coverage breadth and user experience depth
