The Laser strategy suits products where the app IS the product and experience is paramount
Cover-Your-Bases strategy works best for organizations with existing user bases seeking new channels
HTML5 and hybrid approaches provide viable paths for evolving from either strategy toward comprehensive coverage
money_quotes: Mobile is the new web: in 2000 businesses were realizing that the future of commerce and customer relations was on the just-exploding web.
If my store is your favorite store and it's always in your pocket, why do I need to know what you had for dinner last night?
Mobiles fit into a person's lifestyle, moving with them, rather than demanding people meet them on the technology's terms.
Apple has established a strong position in the mobile market almost exclusively based upon providing a higher quality experience.

Cross-platform frameworks and the like work by offering a lowest-common denominator approach. This makes app development consistent, but it places a ceiling on the quality of the experience you can provide.
Unlike the web before it, mobile is making user experience matter.
Can you run your mobile development group as a lean startup? Can you build a culture of rapid pace and learning into this group?
posts.key_insight: Your initial mobile strategy should be viewed as an opening gambit in a longer evolution, requiring intentional trade-offs between platform coverage and experience quality while preparing hybrid technology paths for future growth.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces communal dashboards as a workplace practice where display monitors in semi-public spaces show live data visualizations to create organizational awareness. Unlike individual analytics tools, these dashboards aim to educate and create emotional engagement with business operations rather than drive specific decisions. He provides examples from Redbox showing rental counts, geographic expansion maps, and creative display mediums like floor projections. While acknowledging these are crude visualizations lacking detail or interactivity, Fowler notes they can connect office staff to distant day-to-day activities and serve as conversation starters with business partners. He concludes with a caution that dashboards alone may not trigger engagement without human facilitation.
key_points: Communal dashboards are a subtype of information radiator connected to live data feeds, distinct from static information displays
The purpose is creating awareness and emotional engagement rather than enabling specific decisions
Cheap flat-screen monitors have made communal dashboards much more economically feasible
Creative presentation formats (maps with pushpins, tetris-style falling icons, floor projections) help grab attention
Dashboards alone may not drive engagement—often a human touch like a leader asking questions is needed to spark conversations
money_quotes: This is less about helping people make decisions, more about educating people about what's happening, providing background information and context for their regular work.
One person put it as creating an emotional engagement with how the business is running.

These are crude visualizations, they can't show much detail, nor can they be interactive. However they can generate an awareness of what's happening, creating a connection between office staff and a otherwise distant day-to-day activity.
The display of information on a screen like this makes a natural entry point for a conversation that can help oil some deal-making.
To get people to engage with this information you often need a human touch, such as a leader asking questions based on what's being displayed.
posts.key_insight: Communal dashboards create organizational awareness and emotional connection to business operations, but require human facilitation to truly spark engagement and conversation.

posts.summary: Thoughtworks partnered with AutoTrader UK to test Google BigQuery's capability to analyze large datasets, using 750 million rows of historical car advertisement data. The proof-of-concept aimed to help dealers determine car pricing and selling timeframes. While data transfer from Amazon EC2 to Google Cloud Storage was slow (12 hours), once loaded, BigQuery delivered query results in 7-10 seconds. The team built a web frontend using Google App Engine and Charts for visualization. Overall, BigQuery proved viable for organizations wanting Big Data insights without expensive infrastructure investments.
key_points: Transferring 750 million rows from Amazon to Google Cloud Storage was the slowest part, taking 12 hours, while parallel loading into BigQuery could have reduced the 8-hour import to 15 minutes
Query performance stabilized at 7-10 seconds after an initial slow period, possibly due to internal data distribution processes
BigQuery offers a low-investment option for testing theories on large datasets, even for organizations with existing enterprise data warehouses
The proof-of-concept encountered practical issues including timestamp format incompatibilities, JSON format mismatches between BigQuery and Charts, and Python version conflicts
Data usage costs can accumulate quickly during development since fast query returns mask the large result sets being processed
money_quotes: For organisations longing to gain insights into very large datasets, BigQuery could be a viable option that requires no purchases of specialised software or additional infrastructure.

Even for organisations that already have an enterprise data warehouse, BigQuery is an option for testing out theories, or for other instances where the keepers of the data warehouse place restrictions on use.
Queries come back so fast, you forget that return sets can be many gigabytesâand they add up fast.
We were able to experiment with different ways of visualising and querying a very large volume of data with a relatively low investment in terms of time and without needing expensive infrastructure.
Initially these queries were taking a few minutes, but the next morning things took around 7 to 10 seconds and this remained reasonably consistent for the remainder of the exercise.
posts.key_insight: BigQuery enables rapid experimentation with Big Data analytics at low cost, making it valuable both as a primary solution for organizations lacking infrastructure and as a sandbox for those with existing data warehouses.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler examines modern mocking tools like JMockIt that can bypass Java's language constraints to mock static methods and direct instantiations without modifying production code. While initially impressed by this capability for testing legacy code, he demonstrates that applying traditional refactoring techniques (instance delegators, method extraction, testing subclasses) produces cleaner, more maintainable tests. The article walks through testing a poorly-designed currency conversion method both ways, showing that the refactored approach yields simpler tests while also improving the production code. Fowler ultimately concludes that while powerful mocking tools have legitimate uses, they can short-circuit the feedback loop that drives developers to write better code in the first place.
key_points: Modern mocking tools like JMockIt can replace static methods and intercept 'new' calls at the bytecode level, enabling tests for otherwise untestable code without modifying production code
The example currency conversion method violates multiple principles: Single Responsibility, Dependency Inversion, and unnecessary temporal coupling between validation and parsing
Traditional legacy refactoring techniques (instance delegators, method extraction) took only 3 minutes but produced cleaner, more maintainable tests than the JMockIt approach
Powerful mocking tools can remove the pain associated with poor design decisions, potentially short-circuiting the feedback loop that helps developers learn and improve
While JMockIt is valuable for characterization testing of legacy code, it requires more discipline during new development because it doesn't force addressing underlying design problems
money_quotes: In Working Effectively with Legacy Code Michael Feathers defines legacy code as code without automated tests.
If I think something is cool, then it may not be appropriate for real development. JMockIt makes my 'cool spider senses' tingle on overdrive.
I believe test-ability is more important than design. That's actually a false dichotomy but it sounds controversial so I like to say it anyway.
What happens when tools allow us to not learn from our mistakes? I'm concerned that powerful tools might loosen or remove the pain associated with failure, which will lead to stagnation.
If we are not always striving to make new stupid mistakes, then we aren't really learning, right?
Notice, a little bit of refactoring makes the final JMockIt test much better. JMockIt did not force this.
While parsing does follow validation in time, it should not therefore follow that validation must be required to allow us to verify parsing.
posts.key_insight: Powerful tools that remove friction from testing poorly-designed code may inadvertently short-circuit the learning feedback loop that drives developers toward better design.

posts.summary: This post presents the distilled key points from the book 'NoSQL Distilled' by Pramod Sadalage and Martin Fowler. It covers why NoSQL emerged as an alternative to relational databases, particularly for handling large data volumes on clusters. The content explains different data models (aggregate-oriented, graph), distribution patterns (sharding, replication), consistency tradeoffs, and practical concerns like schema migrations. The overarching message emphasizes polyglot persistence—using the right database technology for each specific need rather than one-size-fits-all. The authors caution that most applications should stick with relational databases until NoSQL matures further.
key_points: NoSQL emerged primarily from the need to handle large data volumes across clusters, which relational databases weren't designed for efficiently
Aggregate-oriented databases (key-value, document, column-family) organize data around units of interaction, making cluster distribution easier but cross-aggregate relationships harder

Consistency in distributed systems involves tradeoffs: you can choose between availability and consistency during network partitions (CAP theorem), and between consistency and latency in normal operations
Polyglot persistence—using different storage technologies for different needs—is the most important outcome of the NoSQL movement
Most applications should stick with relational technology until NoSQL ecosystems mature, and any NoSQL adoption should be tested against actual productivity and performance expectations
money_quotes: The vital factor for a change in data storage was the need to support large volumes of data by running on clusters. Relational databases are not designed to run efficiently on clusters.
NoSQL is an accidental neologism. There is no prescriptive definition—all you can make is an observation of common characteristics.
The most important result of the rise of NoSQL is Polyglot Persistence.
Schemaless databases allow you to freely add fields to records, but there is usually an implicit schema expected by users of the data.
To get good consistency, you need to involve many nodes in data operations, but this increases latency. So you often have to trade off consistency versus latency.
Adding more data storage technologies increases complexity in programming and operations, so the advantages of a good data storage fit need to be weighed against this complexity.
Most applications, particularly nonstrategic ones, should stick with relational technology—at least until the NoSQL ecosystem becomes more mature.
posts.key_insight: NoSQL's real contribution isn't replacing relational databases but enabling polyglot persistence—matching storage technology to specific data needs while acknowledging the complexity tradeoffs involved.

posts.summary: Fowler reflects on the 2012 goto conference in Aarhus, highlighting several emerging technology trends. He praises Netflix's openness in sharing their Cassandra learnings and tools, contrasting it with the secrecy that often hampers industry progress. The post focuses heavily on JavaScript's growing dominance despite its flaws, discussing TypeScript as a promising solution. He also notes Microsoft's significant shift toward open-source collaboration, marking a change from their historically hostile stance. Finally, he emphasizes SVG's potential to transform web visualization by making vector graphics accessible through the DOM.
key_points: Netflix exemplifies valuable industry openness by sharing tools and lessons from their large-scale Cassandra usage
JavaScript's centrality is growing despite being deeply flawed, making TypeScript an important development
Microsoft has shifted significantly toward open-source collaboration, with teams like ASP.NET/Azure and TypeScript embracing openness
The 'new data panorama' offers opportunities for developers to be active participants rather than passive recipients of requirements
SVG represents a profound shift by creating DOM-accessible vector elements that enable new possibilities for data visualization and interactivity
money_quotes: Too often our profession's progress has been held back by secrecy that's born more out of vanity than any real competitive advantage.
I'm one of those who despairs that a language with such deep flaws plays such an important role in computation.
It also provides an opportunity to push back against the sad tendency to see developers as a passive recipient of requirements.
We must take javascript seriously as a first-class language and concentrate on how to limit the damage its flaws cause.
My sense is that Microsoft is very much a factional organization, with many factions still deeply inimical to the open-source world.
SVG is more than a standard to get vector graphics on the web, the more important impact of SVG is that it creates vector shapes as elements in the DOM.
posts.key_insight: Industry progress depends on companies sharing their learnings openly, and developers must engage actively with emerging technologies rather than passively receiving requirements.

posts.summary: The Technology Radar is Thoughtworks' biannual publication that categorizes emerging technologies into four recommendation levels: Adopt, Trial, Assess, and Hold. Volume 33 (November 2025) focuses heavily on AI infrastructure and agentic workflows, highlighting how GPU orchestration has become essential for AI workloads and how the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is transforming how agents operate. The Radar also examines AI coding workflows and their impact on software development, while warning about emerging AI antipatterns that teams should avoid. This edition reflects the industry's rapid evolution around AI tools and practices, emphasizing that human judgment remains critical even as AI accelerates engineering work.
key_points: GPU-aware orchestration has become table stakes for AI workloads, with topology now a first-class scheduling concern for multi-GPU training and inference
MCP (Model Context Protocol) has become the ultimate integration protocol for powering agents and enabling semi-autonomous agentic workflows
AI coding workflows are embedding across the entire software value chain, from understanding legacy codebases to forward engineering
Emerging AI antipatterns require vigilance, including AI-accelerated shadow IT, naive API-to-MCP conversion, and complacency with AI-generated code
AI must amplify entire teams rather than just individual contributors, driving practices like curated shared instructions
money_quotes: GPU‑aware orchestration is becoming table stakes — topology is now a first‑class scheduling concern.
In many ways, MCP has become the ultimate integration protocol for powering agents and enabling them to work efficiently and semi-autonomously.
While AI can accelerate engineering, human judgment is still indispensable.
AI must amplify the entire team, not just individual contributors.
Teams should stay vigilant for patterns that appear effective at first but degrade over time and slow feedback, undermine adaptability or obscure accountability.
Complacency with AI-generated code remains a shared concern.
posts.key_insight: As AI reshapes software development infrastructure and workflows, organizations must balance rapid adoption with vigilance against emerging antipatterns while ensuring AI amplifies entire teams rather than just individuals.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the practice of reading the Retrospective Prime Directive at the start of every retrospective, as recommended by Pat Kua. While his gut reaction is revulsion—associating it with empty rituals and motivational posters—he acknowledges that psychological research on priming suggests such rituals may genuinely help prime participants for the open mindset needed in retrospectives. He concludes that despite his distaste, the evidence suggests he should accept the practice. A 2017 update notes that priming research has faced serious replication problems, casting doubt on the scientific foundation for this advice.
key_points: Pat Kua recommends reading the Prime Directive aloud at every retrospective, claiming teams that skip it get less valuable outputs
Fowler's visceral reaction is revulsion, seeing parallels to empty rituals and forced ceremonies
Psychological priming research suggests that focusing attention on certain ideas can meaningfully influence subsequent behavior and mindset
The priming evidence convinced Fowler to accept a practice he emotionally dislikes
A significant update reveals the priming studies Kahneman cited have serious replication problems, undermining the scientific basis for the recommendation
money_quotes: This kind of advice generates in me a feeling of deep revulsion.
Immediately it brings to mind images of motivational posters, school prayers, and two-minutes hate.
Empty rituals, designed to quench curiosity and innovation, presided over by those whose minds are too empty to share my distaste.
I find Pat's advice for the Prime Directive revolting, yet have to say he's almost certainly correct.
So however much I hate it, I should just bite my tongue and put up with it.
Recent research finds these to be serious, enough that we should be very skeptical about the studies cited in that chapter of Kahneman's book.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes we should follow practices that feel intellectually revolting if evidence suggests they work—though we must also update our beliefs when that evidence crumbles.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler uses the 2012 US election and Nate Silver's probabilistic forecasting as a lens to examine widespread probabilistic illiteracy. He argues that people fundamentally misunderstand probability - conflating an 85% chance with certainty, believing single events can validate probabilistic models, and demanding binary answers rather than ranges. Fowler connects this to software development, where stakeholders want firm estimates rather than probability distributions. He contends that as data becomes more central to decision-making, software professionals must both understand statistics themselves and educate consumers of their data to distinguish signal from noise.
key_points: An 85% probability is not a prediction of certainty - it means there's still a meaningful 15% chance of the alternative outcome, like rolling a 6 on a die
Single events cannot validate or invalidate probabilistic models; you would need many repetitions to test the underlying distribution
People's demand for certainty over probabilistic ranges leads to serious errors in project planning and decision-making
The difference between those who embrace their biases versus those who strive for objectivity is meaningful, even if absolute objectivity is impossible
Software developers have a responsibility to help users understand probability and statistics as data becomes more central to our lives
money_quotes: Silver is saying his model forecasts an 85% chance of an Obama victory, which is not at all the same thing as predicting an Obama victory.
One event cannot say much about an underlying distribution. You'd have to hold many tens of elections to really test the model.
I've developed a strong distrust of certainty, to the point that the more certain someone seems to be, the less I'm inclined to believe them.
People want to hear a binary answer rather than a probabilistic one. We see this, of course, in project planning where people want firm numbers rather than ranges.
That difference between 85% and 100% can lead to some serious errors.
While absolute objectivity is impossible, if you make the effort it is possible to get closer to objectivity than just wallowing in your prejudices.
An important part of this is helping people to see the difference between signal and noise.
posts.key_insight: Understanding probability is essential for making sense of data, and software professionals must lead in both developing this literacy themselves and educating others to distinguish signal from noise.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces the concept of 'infodecks' - slide decks designed purely for reading rather than presentation, distinguishing them from the problematic 'slideuments' that try to serve both purposes. He argues that infodecks have legitimate advantages: spatial layout aids explanation, they discourage unread long prose, and diagrams become primary communication elements. While acknowledging that infodecks are often dismissed as unserious by both casual users and professional writers, Fowler advocates taking them seriously as communication vehicles, especially as tablet computing becomes more prevalent. He shares his experiments with both PDF and HTML formats, ultimately preferring HTML for its dynamic capabilities despite tooling challenges.
key_points: Infodecks differ from slideuments because they serve a single purpose (reading) rather than trying to work for both presentation and reading
The rise of tablets and screen-based reading makes diagram-heavy, colorful documents more practical than paper-optimized prose
A 'lede-and-details' approach works well: large-font summary sentences let readers skim, with detailed text blocks available for depth
HTML offers advantages over PDF for infodecks (animation, web integration) but existing tools are designed for live presentations, not reading
Most presentation tools load entire decks into memory, which crashes memory-limited devices - a problem Fowler solved by building custom tooling
money_quotes: Slideuments are bad because they try to do two different things, but an infodeck is there only for reading, so can be done well.

Most users look at them as something they just knock up with PowerPoint, and writers with pretentions to seriousness (such as myself) consider infodecks to be beneath us. I'm beginning to think we should take them more seriously.
People like me can snark about managers these days being unable to read anything that doesn't look like a bullet point, but there are advantages to these infodecks.
Modern computers have bright multi-color screens, and aren't worried about conserving paper.
The idea is that the reader can get a good sense of what the deck says by skipping through the slides just reading the ledes.
I think of them as short paragraphs rather than bulleted sentences.
posts.key_insight: Infodecks deserve serious attention as a communication form because they're optimized for a single purpose - reading on screens - rather than being compromised slideuments that fail at both presentation and documentation.

posts.summary: A slideument is a hybrid between a slide deck and a document that attempts to serve both as presentation visuals and standalone reading material. Fowler argues this approach fails because the two purposes have conflicting requirements: documents need enough context to stand alone, while good slides should be a visual channel that accompanies rather than duplicates spoken words. When audiences read dense slides while listening to a speaker, they concentrate properly on neither. Despite audience expectations for slide handouts, Fowler advocates providing separate, purpose-built documents alongside or instead of slide copies.
key_points: Slideuments fail because they try to satisfy two contradictory requirements - standalone readability and visual accompaniment to speech
Good slides should function as a visual channel that reinforces but doesn't duplicate the speaker's words
Audiences reading word-heavy slides while listening end up not concentrating properly on either
Despite audience expectations for slide copies, a separate document designed for reading is more valuable
Fowler recommends providing talk notes or relevant articles rather than trying to make slides serve as documentation
money_quotes: A slideument is a cross between a slide deck and a document. The idea is that you can use a single slide deck both for slides during your presentation and as a handout for people to read afterwards.
The trouble is that those two needs lead to very different requirements on your slides, so you can't satisfy them both.
If the speaker is there, and speaking, then the audience ends up both reading and listening - and usually not concentrating properly on either.
A good slide deck is a VisualChannel, it provides an accompaniment to the spoken words, reinforcing but not duplicating what is being said.
If people want a stand-alone document, then you can provide them with one - but it should be a different document, one that's designed for reading and not speaking.
People do expect slides, even when they don't really make any sense, and often it's easiest just to run off a PDF.
posts.key_insight: Slides and documents serve fundamentally different purposes, and trying to combine them into a single artifact means failing at both.

posts.summary: Fowler explores the concept of 'internal reprogrammability' - the ability to modify software from within its own environment without external toolchains or restarts, as exemplified by Emacs and Smalltalk. He contrasts this with modern plugin architectures that limit customization surface area. While acknowledging challenges like instance fragmentation and upgrade complexity, he argues this capability represents a fundamentally different relationship with tools - one where users craft software to fit their needs rather than accepting fixed products. He questions why this philosophy hasn't been extended more broadly, especially to non-programmers, noting it was central to Alan Kay's dynabook vision.
key_points: Internal reprogrammability means extending software using the same language and environment used to build its core - no separate toolchain or restart required
Modern extensibility through plugins differs fundamentally from true reprogrammability because it limits customization surface area
Fragmentation is the main challenge - personal modifications create custom versions tightly coupled to individual configurations
The Unix command line offers a pale reflection of this philosophy, where custom commands become indistinguishable from built-in ones
Kay's dynabook vision saw users as active programmers of their environment, not passive consumers - a vision largely unrealized
money_quotes: There's a palpable difference between extensions in most of these environments and the kind of reprogramming you do in Emacs or Smalltalk.
Adding a new elisp function is exactly how Emacs is programmed itself - there is no difference between how you program little extensions and the core programming of the software.
Internally reprogrammable tools allow you to add or change any part of your software, allowing you to craft your tools to exactly fit your metaphorical hand.
A reprogrammable system is incredibly powerful. Abusing the power is always possible and it's a point of principle in a reprogrammable system that people *must* be able to abuse it.
For many people the software you use is a relatively fixed product. Even plugins add a relatively limited menu of options.
A large part of why modern dynabooks lack the internal reprogrammability of Kay's vision is because it hasn't been made a high-enough priority.
posts.key_insight: True software extensibility means users program their tools using the same mechanisms that built the core system, creating a fundamentally different and more empowering relationship between people and their software.

posts.summary: This is a travel blog post about Saba, a small Caribbean island near St Martin that the author loves for its lack of typical tourist amenities. The island has no beaches, golf courses, or casinos, which has kept mass tourism away. The main attraction is excellent diving in the surrounding marine park, along with hiking trails including the tallest peak in the Netherlands. The author describes the island's quirky history, including a road built by locals after Dutch engineers said it was impossible, and one of the shortest airport runways in the world.
key_points: Saba's appeal lies in what it lacks - no beaches, casinos, or mass tourism infrastructure
The island offers excellent diving with coral reefs starting at 30 feet depth, operated by small professional outfits
Hiking trails include the highest point in the Netherlands at around 3000 feet
The island has remarkable engineering stories - locals built an 'impossible' road after taking a correspondence course
The airport has the shortest landing strip visitors will likely ever experience, yet 30 years without accidents
money_quotes: In many ways the best things about Saba are the things it doesn't have.

They got Dutch civil engineers to survey a road early this century and were told it couldn't be built. So one of the islanders took a correspondence course in civil engineering and the islanders built the road themselves.
Sit by right hand window and you'll swear that that wing will scrape the mountainside going in. But in 30 years there's never been an accident.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most appealing destinations are defined not by what they offer, but by what they've avoided becoming.

posts.summary: Fowler distinguishes 'transparent' compilers like CoffeeScript and SCSS from other source-to-source compilers by their design intention: the output must remain readable and debuggable by developers. This constraint fundamentally shapes what the source language can do, requiring it to follow the target language's semantics and program structure. Despite these limitations, transparent compilation provides significant value through useful language features and improved code readability. The tradeoff is worthwhile because cleaner source code is easier to understand even when debugging the generated output.
key_points: Transparent compilation differs from regular source-to-source compilation by its intention that developers will read and debug the output code
The transparency constraint limits language design freedom - you must follow the target language's semantics and maintain clear structural correspondence
Superset languages like SCSS and TypeScript make the correspondence even clearer by ensuring any target code is also valid source code
The value of transparent compilation lies in adding missing language features and improving readability, even when debugging happens in the target language
An experienced JavaScript programmer concluded CoffeeScript was worthwhile because the source is easier to read, even when debugging generated JavaScript
money_quotes: This is the essence of what makes the compilation process transparent - the intention that you will work in the output language.
If you're going to generate JavaScript for the browser, this ignorance is hard to live with.
It's this difference in intention which is what separates the transparent style of transpilation from the more common approach.
The fact that you have to work at keeping the compilation transparent puts limits on what you can do in the source language.
These constraints haven't been widely talked about as a feature of language design.
It's easier to understand what is going on when you're reading the CoffeeScript, even when you're debugging in the generated JavaScript code.
The transformation may not look like such a big deal for small fragments, but it makes a big difference once you're up to hundreds of lines of code.
posts.key_insight: Transparent compilation succeeds not despite the constraint of readable output, but because that constraint forces a clear correspondence that makes both source and target code easier to understand.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes his evolution in keynote speaking, moving from extemporary talks without slides to a new 'suite of talklets' format. He found keynotes awkward because broad audiences require broader topics than focused conference sessions. After being inspired by Neal Ford's effective use of slides as a visual channel rather than bullet points, Fowler developed a hybrid approach: three twenty-minute slide-backed segments that can be mixed and matched. This preserves his flexibility to customize talks for specific audiences while gaining the benefits of prepared visual materials.
key_points: Keynotes present a unique challenge because speakers must appeal to diverse audiences rather than self-selected attendees interested in a specific topic
Slides should function as a visual channel supporting the audio, not as bullet points or stock photos ('Stock photos are the bullet points of the twenty-first century')
Twenty minutes is an optimal length for idea delivery—long enough to develop a concept, short enough to maintain energy
The modular talklet approach preserves spontaneity while allowing prepared visual materials
money_quotes: Stock photos are the bullet points of the twenty-first century.
My strategy for keynotes is to give a vague title and then decide what to talk about depending on what I think the audience would find most useful to hear, and what I have most energy about saying.
I quite enjoyed the way it jolted my thinking and pushed me into trying some things that I thought would be useful.
I figure out how to do what I do with any good idea - steal it.
Watching them, it struck me that this was enough time to get an idea out without sagging in the way that an hour-long often does.
Keeping to twenty minutes is certainly a challenge for someone as loquacious as me, but it strikes me as a worthwhile discipline.
posts.key_insight: Modular presentation formats can preserve the spontaneity advantages of extemporary speaking while gaining the clarity benefits of prepared visual materials.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that organizations misuse metrics by conflating targets, performance measures, and best practices into a single number, which drives counterproductive behavior. When metrics become disconnected from their underlying goals, people optimize for the number rather than the actual objective—leading to single-loop learning where teams work harder without questioning whether they're solving the right problem. The article proposes four guidelines: explicitly link metrics to goals, track trends rather than absolute numbers, use shorter tracking periods, and retire metrics when they stop driving meaningful change. True improvement comes from double-loop learning, where teams examine and change the system itself rather than just working harder within a broken system.
key_points: Overloading metrics as targets, performance measures, and implied best practices causes people to game the system rather than achieve real goals
Single-loop learning (working harder at the same approach) is less effective than double-loop learning (questioning and changing the underlying system)
Tracking trends over shorter periods provides actionable feedback and creates opportunities for course correction, unlike annual targets that only allow end-of-period evaluation
Management cannot devise effective metrics in isolation—they must work with people closest to the work who understand the system's complexities
Metrics should be explicitly linked to goals so people can challenge their relevance and make pragmatic decisions toward the actual objective
money_quotes: Tell me how you measure me and I will tell you how I behave.
The cost of simplifying complexity comes at the cost of losing sight of the real end goal, and ends in a suboptimal result.
Metrics are simplifications of much more complex attributes.
Strong incentives tied to strong metrics force people to concentrate on just one part of the work, neglecting other contributing factors that might make a goal more successful.
It is easy to monitor activity (how much time they sit at their computer) yet it is hard to observe the value they produce.
Changing the system that people work in often has a much greater impact than focusing on the individual's efforts to work harder or faster.
Metrics have a purpose and a place in organizations and teams. They cannot be used as a substitute for thinking.
posts.key_insight: Metrics should serve as instruments for understanding and improving systems, not as substitutes for thinking or as tools for pressuring individuals to hit arbitrary numbers.

posts.summary: An integration database serves as a shared data store for multiple applications, contrasting with application-specific databases. While this approach eliminates the need for separate integration services and keeps data synchronized across applications, it creates significant architectural problems. The shared schema must accommodate all client applications, becoming overly general or complex. Most critically, the database becomes a deep coupling point between applications, making changes risky and evolution difficult. Fowler concludes that respected software architects recommend avoiding integration databases entirely.
key_points: Integration databases unify what should be separate bounded contexts, forcing schema compromises
Database changes require negotiation between a central database group and multiple application teams
The synchronization benefit (immediate data availability across apps) comes at a high coupling cost
Deep coupling through shared databases significantly increases change risk and hampers application evolution
money_quotes: An integration database needs a schema that takes all its client applications into account.
The resulting schema is either more general, more complex or both - because it has to unify what should be separate BoundedContexts.

The database usually is controlled by a separate organization to those that develop applications and database changes are more complex because they have to be negotiated between the database group and the various applications.
On the whole integration databases lead to serious problems because the database becomes a point of coupling between the applications that access it.
This is usually a deep coupling that significantly increases the risk involved in changing those applications and making it harder to evolve them.
Most software architects that I respect take the view that integration databases should be avoided.
posts.key_insight: Sharing a database between applications creates deep coupling that makes evolution difficult, and the convenience of synchronized data access rarely justifies the architectural costs.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the trend of development teams using NoSQL databases to bypass centralized DBA groups and their slow change-order processes. While acknowledging the legitimate frustrations with weeks-long waits to add a database column, he notes that choosing technology for social rather than technical reasons can lead to poor outcomes. He observes that many legacy databases are essentially brittle, untested applications at the center of complex dependencies, which explains DBA caution. Fowler advocates for a devops-style engagement approach where developers and data professionals collaborate rather than work around each other.
key_points: Development teams increasingly use NoSQL to circumvent slow DBA approval processes, often framing it as a 'datastore' rather than 'database' to avoid oversight
Central database groups exist for valid reasons but create ceremony incompatible with modern evolutionary design practices
Legacy integration databases are often brittle and untested, making DBAs justifiably cautious about changes
Choosing technology for social reasons (avoiding people) rather than technical merit leads to suboptimal architecture
The devops-style engagement model of breaking down barriers between groups is preferable to the end-run strategy
money_quotes: I often hear complaints about change orders that take weeks to add a column to a database.
So application development groups tell me of using NoSQL databases to do an end-run around the DBAs.
It helps that they are using a 'mere datastore' here, not a 'proper database'. That way the DBAs can be kept out of the loop.
Using a technology for social reasons can often lead to a poor use of the technology.
Shared databases are a poor integration mechanism.
A large part of the slow response to change requests is that many incumbent databases are in essence complex legacy applications at the centre of a large web of dependencies.
It's the barriers between groups that cause much of the ceremony, few data groups are populated by devils.
posts.key_insight: When developers choose technology to avoid organizational friction rather than for technical merit, the real problem—broken collaboration between teams—remains unsolved and often worsens.

posts.summary: C3 (Chrysler Comprehensive Compensation) was the payroll project at Chrysler that birthed Extreme Programming. Started in 1993 and rebooted under Kent Beck in 1996, it became the first project to combine all XP practices. The software went live in 1997 paying around ten thousand people, but new development stopped in 1999 and eventually reverted to COBOL. While C3's early success inspired XP's spread, its eventual cancellation demonstrates that XP is no guarantee of success. Fowler notes that most public analyses of C3 lack factual basis and contain misleading information.
key_points: C3 was rebooted under Kent Beck in 1996, becoming the birthplace of Extreme Programming by combining practices Kent had used on previous projects
The system successfully went live in 1997 paying ~10,000 people before being canceled in 1999 and eventually reverted to COBOL
C3's early success inspired other projects to adopt XP, which continued XP's development even after C3's cancellation
Most public analyses of C3, including Wikipedia, are misleading and not based on actual knowledge of the project
Many C3 team members went on to successful XP work, including one notable VeryLowDefectProject
money_quotes: C3's cancellation, however, also proves that XP is no guarantee of success.
C3's early success was a great inspiration for XP, and C3 had an important role in blazing the trail for XP.
By the time the project was canceled, enough other projects had replicated C3's early success to continue XP's development.
I haven't seen any analysis based on much knowledge of the facts.
In particular the entry in Wikipedia is misleading and incomplete, much of its comments seem to be based on a paper from a determined XP critic whose sources are unclear.
posts.key_insight: C3's significance lies not in its ultimate success or failure, but in being the crucible where Extreme Programming practices were first combined—and its cancellation reminds us that no methodology guarantees project success.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the debate within the agile community about whether estimation is valuable or harmful. He argues that estimation is neither inherently good nor bad—its value depends entirely on whether it informs a significant decision. He identifies legitimate uses including resource allocation decisions, coordination between teams, and as a forcing function for team discussions. However, he warns that estimates misused as fixed commitments lead to unrealistic expectations, feature devotion, and quality sacrifices. Teams at higher agile fluency levels often find they can work effectively without formal estimation.
key_points: Estimation is valuable only when it helps make a significant decision—if you can't identify the decision, the estimate is likely wasteful
Misused estimates create unrealistic expectations and encourage 'feature devotion' where ticking off features matters more than real outcomes
Plans and estimates have a limited shelf life—'good for a couple of days, rather wilty after a week, and unrecognizable after a couple of months'
Estimation meetings often provide value as a forcing function for team conversations about implementation approaches and architecture
As teams progress in agile fluency, they first struggle with estimation, then get good at it, then reach a point where they often don't need it
money_quotes: An estimate is a guess in a clean shirt.
If it's worth doing well, it's worth asking why on earth you're doing it at all.
Estimation is valuable when it helps you make a significant decision.
Plans and estimates were like a lettuce, good for a couple of days, rather wilty after a week, and unrecognizable after a couple of months.
Be wary of anyone who tells you they are always needed, or never needed.
The way that estimates set expectations is up to us—it is poor project management that results in a client who thinks estimates are fixed.
I, in fact, try to practice delivering bad news on a weekly basis with my key client, even when things are travelling as expected.
posts.key_insight: The value of estimation depends entirely on whether it informs a meaningful decision—always ask what decision an estimate serves before investing effort in creating it.

posts.summary: A component test deliberately limits scope to a portion of a system, contrasting with broad-stack tests that exercise the full system. The distinction is one of degree rather than absolute difference, with component tests using internal interfaces and test doubles for isolation. While component tests are faster and easier to maintain, bugs tend to hide in component interactions. Fowler recommends combining many component tests with fewer broad-stack tests following the Test Pyramid approach.
key_points: Component tests scope to portions of a system while broad-stack tests exercise the whole system
The difference between test types is one of degree, not an absolute distinction
Component tests use internal interfaces and test doubles for isolation
Bugs lurk in component interactions, so component coverage alone is insufficient
The Test Pyramid recommends many component tests with fewer broad-stack tests
money_quotes: The difference between broad-stack and component tests is one of degree rather than an absolute difference.
Component tests deliberately neglect parts of the system outside the scope of the test.
In theory a system with excellent component test coverage should be free of bugs, but in practice bugs like to lurk in the interactions between components.
posts.key_insight: Component tests trade completeness for speed and maintainability, but must be combined with broad-stack tests since bugs hide in component interactions.

posts.summary: This post introduces JavaScript promises as objects that represent pending results of asynchronous operations. Fowler explains how promises provide a clean interface for scheduling callbacks after async tasks complete, with handlers for success, failure, and either outcome. He highlights that promises compose well, allowing multiple async operations to be combined. The post acknowledges the fragmented landscape of promise implementations in JavaScript, noting jQuery's Deferred Object as the most common, and points readers to Trevor Burnham's resources for deeper learning.
key_points: Promises represent pending results of asynchronous operations and allow scheduling callbacks via done, fail, and always handlers
Promises compose naturally, enabling multiple async operations to be coordinated with constructs like $.when()
The JavaScript ecosystem has multiple promise implementations with subtly different APIs and vocabularies
The concepts of promises, futures, and deferreds appear across many languages, often for both concurrency and asynchrony
money_quotes: In Javascript, promises are objects which represent the pending result of an asynchronous operation.
As well as providing a clear interface to schedule activity with asynchronous tasks, they also compose.
There are various forms of promises in javascript, annoyingly they have subtly different APIs and vocabularies.
These concepts appear in many languages, not just javascript, often with concurrency in mind as much as asynchrony.
posts.key_insight: Promises provide both a clean callback interface and composability for managing asynchronous JavaScript operations, though the ecosystem suffers from fragmented implementations.

posts.summary: This post defines the Aggregate pattern from Domain-Driven Design, explaining it as a cluster of domain objects treated as a single unit. Using the example of an order with its line-items, Fowler describes how aggregates have a root object through which all external references must pass, ensuring integrity. He emphasizes that aggregates are the basic unit for data storage operations and that transactions should not cross their boundaries. The post also clarifies the common confusion between DDD aggregates and generic collection classes.
key_points: A DDD aggregate is a cluster of domain objects that can be treated as a single unit
All external references should only go through the aggregate root, which ensures integrity of the whole
Aggregates are the basic element of data storage transfer - you load or save whole aggregates
Transactions should not cross aggregate boundaries
DDD aggregates are domain concepts, not to be confused with generic collection classes
money_quotes: A DDD aggregate is a cluster of domain objects that can be treated as a single unit.
Any references from outside the aggregate should only go to the aggregate root.
The root can thus ensure the integrity of the aggregate as a whole.
Aggregates are the basic element of transfer of data storage - you request to load or save whole aggregates.
Transactions should not cross aggregate boundaries.
DDD aggregates are domain concepts (order, clinic visit, playlist), while collections are generic.
posts.key_insight: Aggregates define transactional and consistency boundaries in your domain model, with a single root object controlling all access to ensure integrity.

posts.summary: Broad-stack tests (also called end-to-end or full-stack tests) exercise most parts of a large application, in contrast to component tests that focus on isolated parts. The distinction between broad-stack and component tests exists on a continuum rather than as a clear boundary. Tests can be broad-stack even without UI manipulation if they exercise most of the software. Fowler recommends using test doubles for remote systems rather than calling them directly. While broad-stack tests excel at finding bugs in component interactions, they are slower and harder to maintain, leading to the Test Pyramid recommendation of using fewer of them.
key_points: The difference between broad-stack and component tests is a continuum, not a clear line
Tests can qualify as broad-stack even without UI manipulation (subcutaneous tests) if they exercise most of the system
Remote system calls should be replaced with test doubles and verified with contract tests to avoid slow, brittle tests
Broad-stack tests find interaction bugs that component tests miss, but are harder to maintain and slower to run
The Test Pyramid suggests using fewer broad-stack tests in favor of more component tests
money_quotes: The difference between a broad-stack test and a component test is a continuum rather than a clear line.
Many people, including myself, think that tests that call remote systems are unnecessarily slow and brittle.
It is usually better to use TestDoubles for these remote systems and check the doubles with ContractTests.

Broad-stack tests have the advantage of exercising the application with all its parts connected together and thus can find bugs in the interaction between components in the way that component tests cannot.
Broad-stack tests also tend to be harder to maintain and slower to run than component tests.
posts.key_insight: Broad-stack tests are valuable for catching integration bugs but should be used sparingly per the Test Pyramid, with remote dependencies replaced by test doubles verified through contract tests.

posts.summary: Business-facing tests are designed to communicate with non-programming stakeholders like customers and business analysts, describing system behavior in domain terms rather than technical architecture. When automated, these tests often use domain-specific languages via tools like Cucumber to bridge the gap between programmers and business people. While commonly implemented as broad-stack black-box tests, component-level implementations offer maintenance and speed benefits. Fowler emphasizes that manual testing techniques like exploratory and usability testing remain essential complements to automation.
key_points: Business-facing tests serve as communication tools with non-technical stakeholders and often function as acceptance criteria
Domain-specific languages help both non-programmers understand tests and programmers step back from code details
While naturally suited to broad-stack testing, business-facing tests can benefit from component-level implementation
Manual testing techniques like exploratory and usability testing are irreplaceable parts of a balanced test portfolio
Story tests and user journey tests are common forms of business-facing tests
money_quotes: A business-facing test is a test that's intended to be used as an aid to communicating with the non-programming members of a development team.
When automated, they describe the system in domain-oriented terms, ignoring the component architecture of the system itself.
Having such tests pass indicates the system provides the functionality that the customer expects.
There are significant advantages to implementing business-facing tests as ComponentTests since this often results in easier maintenance and faster execution.
It's important to recognize that manual tests play a significant role in business-facing testing.
Techniques such as exploratory testing and usability testing are inherently manual activities and are essential parts of a well-balanced test portfolio.
posts.key_insight: Effective business-facing tests prioritize communication with stakeholders over technical implementation details, using domain language to ensure the system meets customer expectations.

posts.summary: Story tests are business-facing tests that verify software delivered as part of a user story, serving as acceptance criteria and forming a regression suite. While user stories offer a simple workflow where each story adds new tests, this approach often leads to problems. The accumulating tests create significant duplication, making updates painful when behavior changes in later iterations. Additionally, broad-stack story tests are slow to execute, violating the Test Pyramid principle. Fowler suggests using fewer user journey tests combined with business-facing component tests as a better alternative.
key_points: Story tests act as acceptance criteria for user stories and provide traceability from requirements to system behavior
The simple workflow of adding tests per story leads to a large, duplicated test suite over time
Duplication in tests becomes painful to update when behavior changes in later iterations
Broad-stack story tests are slow and violate the Test Pyramid principle
A recommended alternative is combining few user journey tests with business-facing component tests
money_quotes: Story tests are BusinessFacingTests used to describe and verify the software delivered as part of a UserStory.
When a story is elaborated the team creates several story tests that act as acceptance criteria for the story.
Regularly adding story tests leads to a large body of tests, often with significant duplication between them.
When behavior needs to change in later iterations of the project, duplication in tests can take a painful amount of time to update.
Broad-stack story tests take a long time to execute, which is why having a lot of them violates the TestPyramid.
posts.key_insight: While story tests provide valuable traceability and acceptance criteria, their tendency toward duplication and slow execution makes a leaner approach combining user journey tests with component tests preferable.

posts.summary: User-journey tests simulate a typical user's complete interaction with a system to achieve a goal, functioning as a single path through a use case. As broad-stack tests, they are slow and brittle, so teams should keep suites small rather than comprehensive. These tests exercise system integration with usually just one happy-path scenario per use case, leaving behavioral variations to more focused test styles. Unlike story tests, user-journey tests aren't tied to individual user stories but are modified incrementally as stories change system behavior.
key_points: User-journey tests cover a user's entire interaction to achieve a goal, representing one path through a use case
Being broad-stack tests, they are slow and brittle, so suites should be kept small rather than comprehensive
Typically only the happy path is tested per use case, with behavioral variations verified by other test styles
User-journey tests are independent of user stories and are modified as stories change behavior rather than created anew for each story
money_quotes: User-journey tests are a form of BusinessFacingTest, designed to simulate a typical user's 'journey' through the system
Suites of user journey tests usually aren't built to be comprehensive tests of a system's behavior
Usually you will have only a few user journey tests to exercise the integration of the system as a whole - probably only one path for each use case (usually the happy path)
Verification of all the variations in behavior is left to tests done in different styles, usually with more focused coverage
When you play a story you look at the existing user journey tests and modify them to support any change in behavior implied by the user story
posts.key_insight: User-journey tests should be few in number, covering only happy paths for system integration, while other test styles handle comprehensive behavioral verification.

posts.summary: Yesterday's Weather is an estimation principle from Extreme Programming that suggests planning to accomplish as much in the current iteration as was completed in the previous one. Fowler recounts the term's origin from his collaboration with Kent Beck on their XP book. The name derives from a story about an expensive weather prediction system that achieved only marginally better accuracy than simply predicting today's weather will match yesterday's. The post argues that this crude but effective heuristic rivals more sophisticated planning approaches in accuracy.
key_points: Plan to accomplish as much this iteration as you did last iteration
The term originated during Fowler's collaboration with Kent Beck on their Extreme Programming book
Simple heuristics can match the accuracy of far more expensive and complex systems
Sophistication in estimation often adds complexity without proportional accuracy gains
money_quotes: This is the principle that says you'll get as much done today as you got done yesterday.
Somebody then figures out that in this country if you predict today's weather will be the same as yesterday's weather you will be 69.5% accurate.
While Yesterdays Weather is a crude mechanism, it ends up being not significantly less accurate than more sophisticated (i.e. complicated) ways of doing it.
posts.key_insight: Simple estimation based on past performance often matches the accuracy of sophisticated prediction systems while avoiding their complexity and cost.

posts.summary: This article explores practical applications of the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) through real-world examples from production systems. The author distinguishes DIP from Dependency Injection and Inversion of Control, explaining that DIP is about raising the abstraction level of dependencies to match your domain. Through four examples—simplifying logging APIs, hiding database details behind repositories, abstracting away messaging infrastructure, and controlling time in tests—the article demonstrates how inverting dependencies toward higher-level domain concepts reduces risk, improves testability, and creates more maintainable systems.
key_points: DIP is distinct from Dependency Injection and IoC: DI is about wiring, IoC is about direction, and DIP is about the shape and abstraction level of dependencies
Interfaces and abstract classes are not automatically good abstractions—they must exist at a level appropriate to your domain, not expose irrelevant implementation details
Flexibility in APIs often leads to inconsistent use; narrowing interfaces to domain-specific needs reduces cognitive burden and prevents misuse
External constraints like messaging protocols or database choices should be hidden behind domain-relevant abstractions rather than allowed to diffuse throughout the system
Even seemingly fixed concepts like time can and should be controlled through abstraction when they cause testing or operational difficulties
money_quotes: A dependency is a risk.
Nothing + Nothing + Nothing … eventually equals something.
The larger the surface area of a class, the more likely it becomes easier to use the class incorrectly than it is to use it correctly.
DI is about wiring, IoC is about direction, and DIP is about shape.
Confusing a solution with the problem is a common mistake.
Make the implementation of the abstraction do the work once rather than all consumers of a lower-level abstraction duplicating the effort.
If something is causing problems, control it.
All software is a liability.
posts.key_insight: The DIP is not about using interfaces or abstract classes, but about ensuring your code depends on abstractions that exist at the same conceptual level as your domain, hiding lower-level implementation details behind boundaries you control.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler identifies a unifying concept across key-value, document, and column-family NoSQL databases: they all organize data around aggregates, which are rich structures of closely related data. This aggregate orientation emerged from the need to store data effectively on large clusters, where aggregates serve as natural units for sharding and distribution. While this model simplifies programming by avoiding the decomposition required for relational storage, it creates challenges when you need to access data in ways that cut across aggregate boundaries. Fowler argues this trade-off supports polyglot persistence—using aggregate-oriented databases for clear aggregates and relational or graph databases when flexible data access is needed.
key_points: Key-value, document, and column-family databases share a common characteristic: they use aggregates (in DDD terms) as their fundamental unit of storage
Aggregate orientation arose from cluster computing needs—aggregates make natural units for sharding since related data stays together
The programming model benefit is significant: storing a screenful of information as an aggregate is simpler than decomposing it into relational rows
The major downside appears when access patterns cut across aggregates, like analyzing product sales across orders
Map-reduce and materialized views can reorganize data for different readers, but require more work than relational queries
money_quotes: In DDD terms, this group of data is an Aggregate.
Storing aggregates as fundamental units makes a lot of sense for running on a cluster.
An aggregate makes for a much simpler mapping - which is why many early adopters of NoSQL databases report that it's an easier programming model.
The advantage of not using an aggregate structure in the database is that it allows you to slice and dice your data different ways for different audiences.
Order entry naturally stores orders as aggregates, but analyzing product sales cuts across the aggregate structure.
posts.key_insight: NoSQL databases (excluding graph) share an aggregate-oriented model that aligns well with both cluster distribution and application programming, but sacrifices the flexible querying that relational databases provide.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the complaint that object-oriented languages create unnecessary overhead when flowing JSON data through servers. Rather than converting JSON to full object graphs and back, he proposes wrapping generic data structures (parsed JSON) inside thin object wrappers that provide encapsulated access methods. This 'embedded document' pattern preserves the efficiency of working with raw data structures while maintaining the benefits of object-oriented encapsulation. The approach works best when data is stored and served in similar formats but requires some server-side manipulation. As processing complexity increases, converting to a full object graph may become worthwhile.
key_points: The perceived overhead of OO languages with JSON isn't a problem with objects—it's a failure to understand encapsulation
Generic data structures (lists, dictionaries) can be wrapped in objects that provide methods for manipulation without full deserialization to domain objects
Object wrappers on embedded documents have different semantics than normal objects, particularly around equality
The sweet spot is when input and output formats match but some manipulation is needed
Performance arguments against object conversion should always be backed by measurements
money_quotes: I agree with the point about waste, but I argue that it's not a problem with objects but a failure to understand encapsulation.
The point of encapsulated data is the hiding of the data structure, so that users of the object don't know or care about the internal structure.
I would certainly not accept a performance argument against converting to an object graph unless it was accompanied by measurements.

One of my pet annoyances is hearing some functional fans say that this style of code isn't object-oriented. While it may seem foreign to those with a C++/Java background, this style is perfectly natural to smalltalkers.
You can think of the object as providing a namespace for manipulating the generic data structures.
posts.key_insight: Encapsulation doesn't require converting data to rich object graphs—wrapping generic data structures in thin objects provides the benefits of OO while avoiding unnecessary transformation overhead.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that companies make a fundamental mistake when they set out to 'build a mobile application' as a separate project. Instead, organizations should think about building a single transmedia application that presents itself across multiple devices—phone, tablet, desktop, TV—each adapted to its usage context. The user experience should be seamless across these presentations, allowing users to switch between devices naturally. This isn't new thinking; it's basic systems analysis applied to the modern multi-device world, where software must meet users wherever they are.
key_points: Thinking of mobile, web, and tablet as separate applications is a fundamental framing error—they should be different presentations of one unified application
Each device is used in different contexts with different needs, so the experience must be designed with those contexts in mind while maintaining coherence
The iPod's success came from being 'software above the level of a single device'—seamlessly connecting handheld, web backend, and PC as one product
Transmedia thinking must permeate all aspects of the project: user experience, software architecture, project management—not just development
This approach is essentially traditional systems analysis: understanding the domain process and how software can best support it across all touchpoints
money_quotes: Even though you want users to interact with a mobile device, you should never think of building a mobile application.
It's as though the single screens of yesterday have been shattered and the shards have been embedded into our pockets, our environment, and the products we use.
The fact that the company commissioning the software wants to send out the web site and mobile app to different software houses is less relevant than 1923's baseball scores.
I consider the desire to create a mobile application to be a Wrong Thought.
You need to start with the user, figuring out what it is that they want to do and how software can help them do that.
The application, and the project team that builds it, need to be thought of as a single whole, all dedicated to making their users happy.
posts.key_insight: Don't build a mobile app—build one application with multiple device-appropriate presentations, designed from the user's perspective rather than the technology's.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler examines the limitations of automated configuration management tools like Chef, Puppet, and CFEngine. While these tools promise to prevent configuration drift by continuously synchronizing server state to specifications, in practice they cannot manage every configurable element of a system. The work required to specify, test, and maintain recipes grows non-linearly with complexity, and managing unwanted elements is even harder than managing desired ones. Teams typically apply the 80/20 rule, automating only the most critical 5-20% of configuration. This practical limitation leads Fowler to recommend Phoenix Servers or Immutable Servers as more effective approaches to preventing drift.
key_points: Configuration synchronization tools promise to prevent drift but cannot practically manage 100% of a server's configurable surface area
The work to specify each configuration element grows non-linearly due to interactions, integrations, and dependencies between them
Software package dependencies are particularly challenging since tools like apt-get and yum install implicit dependencies that may change without notice
Managing unwanted elements is harder than managing desired ones, since there are effectively infinite things you don't want on a server
Phoenix Servers and Immutable Servers offer better solutions by keeping server lifespans short rather than fighting drift over time
money_quotes: In practice, however, it isn't feasible to keep servers completely consistent using current automated configuration management tools, so over time configuration synchronization leads to inconsistency.

Each element of server configuration managed by an automated tool requires work to write, test, and maintain the recipe or manifest. It simply isn't reasonable to attempt to manage every single element of a typical server using these tools.
If managing the stuff you want to have on your server is difficult, managing the things you don't want is worse, given there are an effectively infinite number of them.
Teams apply the 80/20 rule to automated configuration, focusing 80% (or more like 95%) of their attention on defining that 20% (or 5%) of the system which is most relevant to their particular needs.
The longer a server stays up, the more it may deviate from other servers, especially newer ones.
posts.key_insight: Configuration management tools cannot practically achieve complete drift prevention, making short-lived Phoenix or Immutable Servers a more effective strategy than fighting entropy through synchronization.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the concept of Immutable Servers as a logical evolution from Phoenix Servers, where servers are never modified after deployment but simply replaced with new instances. The approach builds on automated configuration tools but shifts focus from managing running servers to managing base images. All changes are made to the base image, tested through automated pipelines, and deployed as fresh instances. This pattern aligns with Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery principles, treating server instances like compiled artifacts that should be built once and deployed consistently. Per-instance configuration should be minimized and externalized through metadata or central registries.
key_points: Immutable servers eliminate configuration drift by never modifying running instances, only replacing them with new ones built from tested base images
The pattern shifts configuration management focus from running servers to base image management and automated testing
Per-instance configuration should be minimized and externalized through metadata services or central registries like Zookeeper
Data persistence requires special consideration—logs can be shipped to central servers, and storage can use mountable devices or managed services
Netflix pioneered this approach at scale, driven by the need for fast instance creation to enable automatic scaling and recovery
money_quotes: An Immutable Server is the logical conclusion of this approach, a server that once deployed, is never modified, merely replaced with a new updated instance.
In practice it's not possible to manage a server's configuration completely, so there is considerable scope for configuration drift, and unexpected changes to running servers.
Any change to a running system introduces risk.
Once you've spun up a server instance from a well-tested base image, you shouldn't run configuration management tools, since they create opportunities for untested changes to the instance.
With an immutable server, you make each change to a base image, and then you know that all instances created from that image are consistent.
If servers are disposable, the data that lives on them often is not.
posts.key_insight: Treating servers as immutable artifacts that are replaced rather than modified eliminates configuration drift and brings the reliability benefits of continuous delivery to infrastructure management.

posts.summary: Fowler introduces the concept of 'Phoenix Servers' - the practice of regularly destroying and rebuilding servers from scratch rather than maintaining long-running instances. He argues this approach combats configuration drift, where ad hoc changes accumulate and go unrecorded, eventually creating unique 'snowflake servers' that are difficult to reproduce. While configuration management tools like Puppet and Chef can re-apply known configurations, they only catch drift in areas they explicitly control. Phoenix servers, by starting fresh each time, ensure complete alignment with source configuration.
key_points: Configuration drift - unrecorded ad hoc changes - leads to fragile, irreproducible 'snowflake servers'
Phoenix servers are regularly destroyed and rebuilt from scratch, ensuring clean state
Configuration management tools only catch drift in areas they explicitly control, missing unknown changes
Both re-applying configuration (faster) and phoenix rebuilds (more thorough) are valuable strategies
Netflix's Chaos Monkey exemplifies this philosophy by randomly destroying servers to test resilience
money_quotes: The certification assessment would consist of a colleague and I turning up at the corporate data center and setting about critical production servers with a baseball bat, a chainsaw, and a water pistol.
A server should be like a phoenix, regularly rising from the ashes.
Drift is the name of a street that leads to SnowflakeServers, and you don't want to go there without a big plough.
Since phoenixes start from scratch, however, they will pick up any drift from the source configuration.
It's valuable to use both strategies to fight away the snowflakes.
posts.key_insight: Regularly destroying and rebuilding servers from scratch is the most thorough way to prevent configuration drift and ensure infrastructure remains reproducible.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes the 'snowflake server' anti-pattern where production servers become unique, manually-configured systems that are difficult to reproduce, understand, or modify. These servers accumulate cruft and undocumented changes over time, making them fragile and resistant to upgrades. The solution is to define server configurations as automated recipes using tools like Puppet or Chef, stored in version control. This approach enables reproducibility, auditability, easier testing environments, and supports continuous delivery practices.
key_points: Snowflake servers are unique configurations created through manual, ad-hoc changes that become impossible to reliably reproduce
The fragility of snowflakes manifests most severely when changes are needed - upgrades cause unpredictable knock-on effects and debugging becomes stressful
Automated configuration recipes (Puppet, Chef) provide both reproducibility AND understandability, which disk images alone cannot offer
Forcing all configuration changes through version-controlled recipes creates an excellent audit mechanism for regulated environments
Avoiding snowflakes enables test environments to be true clones of production, reducing configuration-related bugs
money_quotes: The result is a unique snowflake - good for a ski resort, bad for a data center.
Another metaphor I've heard for this is that you should treat your servers like cattle and not like pets.
Snowflakes soon become hard to understand and modify. Upgrades of one bit software cause unpredictable knock-on effects.
You're not sure what parts of the configuration are important, or just the way it came out of the box many years ago.

The point of using a recipe is not just that you can easily rebuild the server (which you could also do with imaging) but you can also easily understand its configuration and thus modify it more easily.
This fragility leads to long, stressful bouts of debugging.
This is one reason why you often see important software running on ancient operating systems.
posts.key_insight: Server configurations should be defined as version-controlled, automated recipes rather than accumulated manual changes, because reproducibility without understandability still leaves you with a fragile system.

posts.summary: Fowler tackles the surprisingly difficult task of defining what constitutes a NoSQL database, noting the term emerged from a 2009 San Francisco meetup without any formal definition, trademark, or standards body behind it. He argues that interpreting NoSQL as 'Not Only SQL' for individual databases renders the term meaningless, preferring to reserve that interpretation for the broader ecosystem (which he calls Polyglot Persistence). Rather than a strict definition, he proposes NoSQL refers to a particular wave of recent databases sharing common but non-definitional characteristics: non-relational, open source, cluster-oriented, web-scale focused, and schema-free. Despite his frustration with another blurry industry term, he concludes that these databases represent an important addition to application development regardless of definitional clarity.
key_points: The term NoSQL originated from a June 2009 meetup in San Francisco, not from any formal standards process or manifesto
Interpreting NoSQL as 'Not Only SQL' for individual databases is meaningless—that interpretation should apply only to the broader persistence ecosystem
