NoSQL databases share common characteristics (non-relational, open source, cluster-based, web-scale, schema-free) but none of these are strictly definitional
The lack of clear definition is a minor annoyance compared to the practical value these databases provide
money_quotes: There's no strong definition of the concept out there, no trademarks, no standard group, not even a manifesto.
When we say 'x is a NoSQL database' I think it's silly to interpret NoSQL as 'Not Only' because that would render the term meaningless.
You could then reasonably argue that SQL Server (say) is a NoSQL database.
While I'm used to the blurry lines of definitions in the software industry, I confess my heart sinks at yet another one.
A lack of a clear definition will be no more than a gnat bite on their future successes.
posts.key_insight: NoSQL is best understood not through a rigid definition but as a label for a particular wave of databases sharing common characteristics, with the 'Not Only SQL' interpretation reserved for the broader polyglot persistence ecosystem.

posts.summary: Ideal time was an early Extreme Programming estimation technique where developers estimated focused work time without interruptions, then applied a 'load factor' to convert to actual elapsed time. The load factor was determined empirically by measuring velocity across iterations. The approach acknowledged that developers face many interruptions (meetings, interviews, support) that reduce productive coding time. However, the technique has largely been replaced by Story Points, which proved easier to calculate, harder to misuse, and equally accurate for planning purposes.
key_points: Ideal time estimates assume focused, uninterrupted work on a task
Load factor converts ideal time to elapsed time by measuring actual productivity ratios
Velocity measurement from recent iterations provides empirical data for the load factor
Story Points replaced ideal time because they're simpler and less prone to abuse
money_quotes: With ideal time you specifically estimate in terms of focused quality time âif I'm doing nothing else, how long would this take meâ.
Using idea time fell out of favor since StoryPoints were easier to calculate, less likely to be abused, and every bit as accurate.
posts.key_insight: Estimation techniques should account for the gap between focused work time and actual elapsed time, but simpler abstractions like Story Points achieve this without the complexity of calculating load factors.

posts.summary: Story counting is an alternative to story points for agile planning, where teams simply count the number of stories delivered per iteration rather than estimating relative sizes. Fowler explains that some experienced teams have found their story point estimates are no more accurate than raw story counts over time, making the estimation effort unnecessary. The technique works because larger and smaller stories tend to cancel each other out statistically. Teams should still ensure stories are within an order of magnitude of effort and watch for distribution imbalances. Fowler notes this may be better suited for more advanced teams who have already mastered story points.
key_points: Some teams find story point estimates no more accurate than simple story counts over project timelines
Story counting works because bigger and smaller stories cancel each other out over time
Teams should still ensure stories are roughly within an order of magnitude of effort (1-8 point range equivalent)
T-shirt sizing can be used as a lightweight check for distribution imbalances without full estimation
Story counting may be a technique better suited for more advanced teams already skilled with story points
money_quotes: I've heard several teams look at their history and find that over the course of the project, their estimates from using story points are no more accurate than if they had simply counted how many stories were in each iteration.
Given that, the effort of calculating story points isn't worth doing.
Stories can still vary in size, but over time the bigger and smaller stories will cancel each other out, hence a simple count ends up the same.
One of the benefits of estimating with story points is that it helps identify poorly understood stories.
So far the teams I've come across using story counting are teams that have already been good at using story points, so it may be that story counting is a technique for more advanced teams.
posts.key_insight: When story point estimates prove no more accurate than simple counts over time, the estimation effort may not be worth the overhead for experienced teams.

posts.summary: Story points are a relative sizing technique for estimating work in agile projects, used in combination with velocity to forecast when stories can be completed. Fowler explains that teams struggled with traditional staff-hour estimates, finding relative comparison more effective. The technique involves comparing new stories to previously estimated ones of similar size, using a small range of values like Fibonacci sequences. The process should be quick, with discussion only arising when team members have contrasting views, which often reveals unclear requirements. Fowler notes that story counting is an equally valid alternative, leaving the choice to individual teams.
key_points: Story points estimate work through relative sizing rather than absolute time, comparing new stories to previously estimated ones
Teams use small ranges like 1,2,4,8 or Fibonacci sequences, with the top number often representing 'too big to estimate'
The estimation process should be rapid, with discussion only when contrasting views reveal unclear story requirements
Story points combined with velocity enable planning forecasts, though story counting works equally well as an alternative
money_quotes: We found the most effective way to estimate was to size stories relative to each other, and then use past experience to determine how much could be done in an iteration.
Discussion should only break out when people have contrasting views on the estimate, in which case its useful to have a discussion as it usually means that something about the story isn't clear.
Using the top number as too big is saying that a story sized at '8' really means '8 or more'. If you do this beware of using this top number when making forecasts of things like completion time.
It's usually better to explicitly say its too big to be estimated rather than use a false marker number.
I don't have a preference between the two - both seem to work equally well so it's up to the team to try out and go with whichever suits them best.
posts.key_insight: Story points succeed not by measuring absolute effort but by enabling relative comparison between work items, with past velocity translating those abstract units into actionable forecasts.

posts.summary: Velocity is a planning calibration tool that measures how much work a team completes in a time period, typically determined by averaging recent iterations following the 'Yesterday's Weather' principle. Originally from Extreme Programming, it's now used widely across agile methodologies. The post emphasizes that velocity should only be used for forecasting, not as a productivity measure, since different teams use different baselines making comparisons meaningless. Using velocity as a productivity metric undermines the very agility it's meant to support.
key_points: Velocity should be determined by measuring past performance, typically averaging the last three iterations
Velocity is a calibration tool for planning, not a measure of productivity
Comparing velocities between teams is meaningless since there are no standard story points
Velocity works with both fixed iterations and Kanban-style planning
Using velocity as a productivity measure actively kills agility
money_quotes: Velocity is a tool for calibrating estimations for YesterdaysWeather, it is not a measure of productivity.
Different teams will use different baselines for their velocity units, so it's stupid to compare teams based on their velocities: there's no such thing as StandardStoryPoints.
Using velocity as a productivity measure kills agility.
Velocity is a useful tool for estimation, one that's considerably less complicated to use than techniques I observed in the 80s.
posts.key_insight: Velocity exists purely to help teams forecast their own delivery timelines based on past performance, and corrupting it into a productivity metric destroys its usefulness and undermines agile principles.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explores the challenge of allowing users to define their own fields in data structures, a common need in applications like address books. He explains that in-memory solutions using hashmaps (Variable State pattern) are straightforward, but persistence presents the real challenge. The article surveys five database approaches: Serialized LOB (storing fields as JSON/XML in a text column), attribute tables (key-value pairs in a separate table), pre-defined custom fields (schema columns like custom_field_1), and dynamic schema (using ALTER TABLE to add columns at runtime). Fowler notes that schemaless databases handle this naturally, while relational databases require tradeoffs between querying ease and flexibility.
key_points: In-memory user-defined fields are easily implemented with hashmaps; the real complexity lies in persistence
Serialized LOB stores custom fields as JSON/XML documents, with increasingly good database support for indexing within the blob
Dynamic schema (ALTER TABLE at runtime) is an often-overlooked approach that Mingle successfully used, providing native indexing and querying
Pre-defined custom fields and attribute tables both make querying and indexing awkward
User-defined fields are related to non-uniform types (sparse tables), but differ in that developers never know what fields users will create
money_quotes: Often the trickiest part of this is figuring out how to persist this.
A dynamic schema is an approach that's often overlooked.
Remember that schemaless doesn't mean you don't have a schema.

The difference between non-uniform types and user-defined fields is that non-uniform types have all the potential fields known to developers, while user-defined fields allow fields to be created that developers will never know about.
If you want common user defined fields, you can use a class variable to keep a list of common keys for the hashmap.
posts.key_insight: While in-memory user-defined fields are trivial to implement, the persistence layer—particularly with relational databases—requires choosing between querying convenience and schema flexibility, with dynamic schema being an underappreciated option that provides both.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that privacy protection isn't primarily about individuals with 'nothing to hide,' but about safeguarding those who challenge power in a democracy. Journalists investigating corruption and activists pushing for social change require privacy to do their work effectively. Those in power can exploit surveillance data to identify sources, discredit opponents, and suppress dissent. While acknowledging privacy isn't absolute and surveillance has legitimate uses against criminals, Fowler contends that without protecting the privacy of 'bothersome people,' democracy loses a vital pillar.
key_points: Privacy's democratic value lies in protecting journalists and activists who challenge power, not ordinary citizens with 'nothing to hide'
Metadata access allows those in power to identify sources, map support networks, and find vulnerabilities to exploit against their critics
Even imperfect people doing important work can be discredited through selective exposure of personal failings
Surveillance tools are always liable to misuse, requiring careful design of controls
Today's 'fringe' activists may be leading changes that become self-evident to future generations, as with abolitionists and suffragettes
money_quotes: The value of privacy to us isn't primarily about our privacy but about those who play a more active role in the operation of a democratic system of government.
Such activity often involves bothering people who have power, and those with power are likely to use their power to suppress the bothersome. But without all that bothering, democracy withers.
With this information you can find out who your tormentors are talking to, where their sources of information are, who is supporting them with encouragement and funds.
A journalist's substance abuse problem may not be stopping her expose corruption, but you can use it to derail her efforts.
Without good journalists I can't understand what is really happening and thus I can't cast a meaningful vote.
If we can't protect the privacy of those who bother the powerful, then we lose a vital pillar of a democratic society.
posts.key_insight: Privacy protection matters not for those with nothing to hide, but because democracy depends on journalists and activists being able to challenge power without surveillance-enabled retaliation.

posts.summary: The Expression Builder pattern solves a key problem with Fluent Interfaces: methods designed for fluent chaining look awkward on domain objects. Methods like 'with', 'skippable', and 'priorityRush' make sense in a DSL context but violate expectations of standard Command-Query APIs, where each method should make sense individually. An Expression Builder is a separate object that hosts the fluent interface and translates those calls into regular API calls on the underlying domain objects. This separation keeps domain classes clean while still enabling expressive DSL-style code.
key_points: Fluent interface methods often violate Command-Query API conventions, where methods should make sense individually and follow CommandQuerySeparation
Expression Builder is a separate object that provides the fluent interface while delegating to a regular API underneath
You can use either a single builder or a structure of builders, with tradeoffs between state management complexity and delegation overhead
The pattern allows domain objects to maintain clean, conventional APIs while still supporting expressive DSL-style construction
money_quotes: Methods like with, skippable, and priorityRush don't sit well on the Order class.
Mixing fluent-style methods with command-query methods may lead to confusion because fluent methods can violate the expectations of what most APIs should look like.
An expression builder is a separate object on which we define the fluent interface that then translates the fluent calls to the underlying regular API calls.
In a command-query API each method makes sense individually in any context.
posts.key_insight: When fluent interfaces clash with clean domain object design, introduce a separate Expression Builder object to host the DSL methods and translate them to conventional API calls.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces the concept of 'fluent interfaces,' a term coined during a workshop with Eric Evans, describing an API design style that reads like a domain-specific language. The approach prioritizes readability and natural flow over conventional method patterns, using techniques like method chaining and carefully chosen return types. While fluent APIs require significantly more design effort than simple constructor/setter approaches, they create code that expresses intent more clearly. Fowler acknowledges the style violates conventions like CommandQuerySeparation but argues the fluency benefits justify the exception. He notes the pattern is most commonly applied to value object configurations and calls for more experimentation given limited real-world experience.
key_points: Fluent interfaces are essentially internal domain-specific languages designed for readability and natural flow
The approach requires more upfront design effort but produces more expressive, readable code
Method chaining is a common technique but true fluency involves more than just chaining
Fluent interface methods often don't make sense in isolation - they derive meaning from context
The pattern works particularly well with value objects where you can freely create new values from old ones
money_quotes: The API is primarily designed to be readable and to flow.
The price of this fluency is more effort, both in thinking and in the API construction itself.
Good fluent APIs take a while to build.
The key test of fluency, for us, is the Domain Specific Language quality.
One of the problems of methods in a fluent interface is that they don't make much sense on their own.
I haven't seen a lot of fluent interfaces out there yet, so I conclude that we don't know much about their strengths and weaknesses.
Many people seem to equate fluent interfaces with Method Chaining. Certainly chaining is a common technique to use with fluent interfaces, but true fluency is much more than that.
posts.key_insight: Fluent interfaces are internal DSLs that prioritize readable, flowing code over conventional API patterns, requiring more design effort but yielding more expressive results.

posts.summary: Given-When-Then is a structured approach to writing tests developed by Daniel Terhorst-North and Chris Matts as part of Behavior-Driven Development (BDD). The pattern breaks tests into three sections: Given (pre-conditions), When (the behavior being tested), and Then (expected outcomes). While popularized through Cucumber and BDD, the pattern is essentially a reformulation of existing testing conventions like the Four-Phase Test and Arrange-Act-Assert. The approach works for both formal business-facing tests and informal unit test comments.
key_points: Given-When-Then divides tests into pre-conditions (Given), behavior (When), and expected changes (Then)
The pattern is a reformulation of the Four-Phase Test (Setup, Exercise, Verify, Teardown) and Arrange-Act-Assert conventions
While associated with BDD and Cucumber, the style can be applied to any kind of test including unit tests
The 'given' section describes state but frameworks interpret it as commands to establish that state
Teardown is omitted from BDD style because it doesn't add to the communication aspect of specification by example
money_quotes: Given-When-Then is a style of representing tests - or as its advocates would say - specifying a system's behavior using SpecificationByExample.
The given part describes the state of the world before you begin the behavior you're specifying in this scenario.
Since we're talking about using examples as specifications, it makes sense to show this with an example.
You can use the Given-When-Then style with any kind of tests.
I've characterized the given as a description of the pre-condition state because that's the way I prefer to think of it.
posts.key_insight: Given-When-Then is not unique to BDD but rather a communication-focused reformulation of established test structuring patterns that can be applied across any testing context.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that Specification By Example—using concrete tests as specifications—is a powerful and practical alternative to formal pre/post conditions and traditional requirements documents. While examples don't cover all cases like general specifications, they're much easier to create, especially for non-technical stakeholders. The approach provides a valuable double-check mechanism by expressing requirements differently in tests versus code. Fowler acknowledges this technique is incomplete and must be combined with other practices like conversation and Domain Driven Design, but sees this incompleteness as a feature that prevents teams from thinking communication is 'done' after writing specs.
key_points: Examples are often easier to write than formal pre/post conditions, especially for non-programmers, even though they don't cover all cases
TDD involves a valuable double-check principle—saying things twice in different ways (code and tests) increases error detection
Specification By Example makes verification easy compared to formal methods, being 'less valuable in theory but more valuable in practice'
The incompleteness of example-based specs is actually beneficial because it forces teams to maintain ongoing communication rather than treating requirements as 'done'
This approach only works when both sides are collaborating rather than fighting—examples trigger abstractions while keeping them grounded
money_quotes: Rather like if someone offered me a million dollars to hike up a mountain. I may say that the main purpose of the hike is the enjoyment of nature, but the side-effect to my wallet is hardly trivial.
I've found that in many situations it's as hard to write the pre and post conditions as it is to write the solution.
They make a very strong point of saying things Once and Only Once, but in fact they always say things twice: once in the code and once in the tests.
Another case of Specification By Example being less valuable in theory but more valuable in practice.
My flippant answer to this is that if you are concerned about this then the issue of tests versus Design by Contract is the least of your worries.
One of the most dangerous things about a traditional requirements specification is when people think that once they've written it they are done communicating.
Specification By Example is a powerful tool, perhaps my most used tool, but never my only tool.
posts.key_insight: Specification By Example succeeds not despite its incompleteness but partly because of it—forcing teams to maintain ongoing collaboration rather than treating written specs as the end of communication.

posts.summary: Fowler traces the evolution of software process from chaotic 'code and fix' through rigid plan-driven methodologies to agile approaches. He argues that agile methods differ fundamentally from traditional approaches in two ways: they are adaptive rather than predictive, and people-oriented rather than process-oriented. The essay explains why software development cannot follow the civil engineering model of separating design from construction, since in software the source code itself is the design document and construction (compilation) is essentially free. Fowler concludes that while agile requires committed teams and collaborative customers, it offers superior control over inherently unpredictable software projects through iterative development and continuous feedback.
key_points: Software construction is so cheap as to be free; all software effort is design work requiring creative people, making predictability an often impossible target
Agile methods are adaptive (welcoming change) rather than predictive (resisting change), and people-oriented rather than process-oriented
The traditional engineering metaphor fails for software because design cannot be reliably separated from construction as it can in civil engineering
Iterative development with tested, integrated systems provides honest feedback that documents and untested code cannot hide
Measurement-based management leads to dysfunction in software; delegatory management where developers decide how to do their work is more effective
money_quotes: In software: construction is so cheap as to be free. In software all the effort is design, and thus requires creative and talented people.
A late change in requirements is a competitive advantage.
There is nothing like a tested, integrated system for bringing a forceful dose of reality into any project.
If you believe you can be predictable when you can't, it leads to situations where people build a plan early on, then don't properly handle the situation where the plan falls apart.
The mistake in this approach is that 'people' are highly variable and non-linear, with unique success and failure modes. Those factors are first-order, not negligible factors.
Creative processes are not easily planned, and so predictability may well be an impossible target.
A good predictive project will go according to plan, a good agile project will build something different and better than the original plan foresaw.
posts.key_insight: Software development is fundamentally a design activity where construction is essentially free, making traditional engineering's separation of design and construction inapplicable and requiring adaptive, people-centered processes instead.

posts.summary: Tell-Don't-Ask is a principle that encourages moving behavior into objects rather than extracting data and acting on it externally. The principle helps programmers remember that object-orientation bundles data with functions that operate on that data. While many find it useful for improving code design by co-locating tightly coupled elements, Fowler personally doesn't emphasize it because it can lead to extreme positions like eliminating all query methods. He notes that sometimes objects collaborate effectively by providing information, and good design requires balancing multiple trade-offs rather than following any single principle dogmatically.
key_points: Tell-Don't-Ask means telling objects what to do rather than asking for their data and acting on it externally
The principle promotes co-locating data and behavior because they are tightly coupled - changes in one cause changes in the other
Taken too far, the principle can lead to 'Getter Eradicators' who eliminate all query methods unnecessarily
Sometimes objects collaborate more effectively by providing information through queries
Good design involves trade-offs, and co-locating data and behavior is just one factor among many
money_quotes: Tell-Don't-Ask is a principle that helps people remember that object-orientation is about bundling data with the functions that operate on that data.
Things that are tightly coupled should be in the same component.
But personally, I don't use tell-dont-ask.
I've seen it encourage people to become GetterEradicators, seeking to get rid of all query methods.
There are times when objects collaborate effectively by providing information.
I've seen code get into convolutions of only telling where suitably responsible query methods would simplify matters.
Good design is all about trade-offs, and co-locating data and behavior is just one factor to bear in mind.
posts.key_insight: While Tell-Don't-Ask usefully reminds us to co-locate data and behavior, treating it as an absolute rule leads to unnecessarily convoluted code - good design requires balancing multiple trade-offs.

posts.summary: Page objects are a design pattern that wraps HTML pages or UI fragments with an application-specific API, shielding tests from brittle dependencies on UI implementation details. The pattern allows test code to interact with pages at a meaningful abstraction level—using strings for text fields, booleans for checkboxes, and action-oriented methods for buttons—rather than manipulating HTML directly. Fowler advocates for assertion-free page objects that provide access to page state while leaving assertion logic to test scripts. The pattern applies beyond web testing to any UI technology and represents a classic application of encapsulation that improves both maintainability and test readability.
key_points: Page objects should model what makes sense to users, not mirror the technical structure of pages—often meaning multiple page objects per page for significant UI elements
A good rule of thumb: imagine changing the concrete control; if your page object interface would need to change, the abstraction is leaky
Page objects should return fundamental types or other page objects, never exposing raw WebDriver or HTML manipulation to tests
Assertions belong in test scripts, not page objects—duplication concerns can be addressed through assertion libraries instead
Patterns that move logic out of UI (Presentation Model, Passive View) reduce the need for page objects by making UI testing less necessary
money_quotes: The basic rule of thumb for a page object is that it should allow a software client to do anything and see anything that a human can.
If you have WebDriver APIs in your test methods, You're Doing It Wrong.
A good rule of thumb is to imagine changing the concrete control - in which case the page object interface shouldn't change.
Page objects are a classic example of encapsulation - they hide the details of the UI structure and widgetry from other components.

The name 'page object' is misleading because it makes you think you should have just one page object per page. Something like 'panel object' would be better - but the term 'page object' is what's become accepted.
Ask yourself 'how can I hide some details from the rest of the software?'
It makes the client (test) code easier to understand because the logic there is about the intention of the test and not cluttered by UI details.
posts.key_insight: Page objects apply encapsulation to UI testing by hiding structural details behind an application-meaningful API, making tests both more maintainable and more expressive of their actual intent.

posts.summary: A deployment pipeline addresses the tension between fast feedback and comprehensive testing in automated build environments by breaking the build into sequential stages. Each stage provides increasing confidence at the cost of additional time, with early stages catching most problems quickly while later stages perform more thorough validation. The pipeline's broader purpose is to detect any changes that could cause production problems, including performance, security, and usability issues. Fowler recommends modeling your current delivery process as a pipeline to identify bottlenecks and automation opportunities.
key_points: Deployment pipelines solve the speed-vs-thoroughness tradeoff by breaking builds into stages with increasing confidence levels
Early stages provide fast feedback on most problems; later stages offer slower but more comprehensive validation
Pipelines detect production-threatening changes across multiple dimensions: performance, security, usability
Stages can be automatic or require human authorization, and may be parallelized for speed
Modeling your current delivery process as a pipeline reveals bottlenecks and collaboration opportunities
money_quotes: Each stage provides increasing confidence, usually at the cost of extra time.
Early stages can find most problems yielding faster feedback, while later stages provide slower and more thorough probing.
The deployment pipeline's job is to detect any changes that will lead to problems in production.
A deployment pipeline should enable collaboration between the various groups involved in delivering software and provide everyone visibility about the flow of changes in the system.

A good way to introduce continuous delivery is to model your current delivery process as a deployment pipeline, then examine this for bottlenecks, opportunities for automation, and collaboration points.
posts.key_insight: Deployment pipelines resolve the fundamental tension between fast feedback and comprehensive validation by structuring builds as progressive stages of increasing confidence.

posts.summary: A threshold test is a deployment pipeline test that compares a measurable value in the current build against a defined threshold, failing the build if the threshold is exceeded. Performance testing is a common use case, where teams time representative operations and fail builds that show significant degradation. The key benefit is catching problems at the commit that introduced them, making issues easier to diagnose and preventing gradual accumulation of problems. Threshold tests can be combined with ratcheting to progressively improve values over time. Teams may sometimes consciously decide to relax a threshold, but the test ensures this is a deliberate decision rather than silent degradation.
key_points: Threshold tests catch measurable degradations (like performance) at the exact commit that caused them, narrowing the search space for fixes
Preventing problem accumulation is crucial—when many issues already exist, new ones are harder to detect
Ratcheting combines with threshold tests to drive gradual improvement by tightening thresholds after each improvement commit
Relaxing a threshold when a test fails is sometimes appropriate, but the test forces this to be a conscious team decision
money_quotes: By having a threshold test you can spot this kind of problem when it's first introduced.
This makes it easier to fix, since you know it's this commit which caused the failure - which cuts down where you have to search for the problem.
If you have many of these poor queries, then it's easier for more to creep in since their effects are obscured by the ones already present.
Using threshold tests with ratcheting is particularly helpful when you begin in a poor place and starting a program of improvement.
The threshold test helps here by making this decision a conscious one.
posts.key_insight: Threshold tests transform silent, gradual degradation into visible, actionable failures at the moment problems are introduced.

posts.summary: The post argues that most JavaScript code isn't intrinsically asynchronous—it merely supports async behavior through callbacks or promises. This distinction matters for testing because async-supporting code can be tested synchronously by stubbing external APIs. The author demonstrates testing patterns for both callback and promise-based code, strongly advocating for promises as they produce cleaner, more readable tests. Truly asynchronous tests should be aggressively limited because they're hard to maintain and often indicate code smell. The key technique is flattening potentially async operations into synchronous execution during tests.
key_points: Most 'async' JavaScript code isn't inherently async—it just uses async-capable APIs like callbacks or promises
Async-supporting code can be tested synchronously by stubbing dependencies to resolve immediately
When testing callbacks, you must verify the callback was actually invoked (Mocha's done() pattern helps)
Promises lead to cleaner tests because they reify async control flow as objects that can be passed around
Truly asynchronous tests should be rare—isolate inherently async code and test it separately with integration tests
money_quotes: Another way to think about this is that method calls which operate synchronously are a special case of the more general asynchronous case.
If the tests look bad or are hard to write then there's probably something wrong with the way the code under test was designed.
I like to think of Promises as an object-oriented version of callbacks, with some bonus features tacked on.
Essentially promises allow us to separate the concern of invoking an operation from the concern of handling the result of that operation.
Aggressively limit the number of truly asynchronous tests you write and maintain.

If you do find yourself writing significant portions of inherently async code then it's likely you need to take a step back and identify a small, containable area of your code which handles all that icky async stuff.
posts.key_insight: Most JavaScript code isn't inherently asynchronous, just async-compatible, which means unit tests can stub dependencies and execute synchronously rather than requiring complex async test infrastructure.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his enthusiasm for strategic hobby board games, particularly Eurogames, which are distinct from mass-market games like Monopoly. He maintains an active blog on BoardGameGeek called 'Martin's Seventh Decade' where he documents his gaming experiences. The page serves as an index to his board game writings, covering play reports, game reviews, and analytical pieces about game design concepts. His interests center on economic and route-building games like Brass, Age of Steam, and Power Grid, with recent exploration into the 18xx genre.
key_points: Strategic hobby games are fundamentally different from mass-market games - Fowler hasn't played Monopoly in decades and has no interest in returning to it
BoardGameGeek serves as the primary platform for his gaming writing, though it lacks good indexing capabilities
His gaming analysis extends beyond reviews to conceptual pieces on game design (tightness, victory points, player interaction, theme vs setting)
Economic and route-building games dominate his preferences, particularly the Brass and Age of Steam families
He's recently been exploring 18xx games after having a 'love-hate relationship' with the genre
money_quotes: It's been many decades since I've played Monopoly, and I have no interest in ever playing it again.
The kinds of board games I play are what I call strategic hobby games. These are a long distance from the mass market board games that you might find in most stores.
Brass is a hugely successful family of games, and if forced to pick a favorite game, it's the one I'd pick.
I've long had a love-hate relationship with 18xx games. There's so much that appeals to me about an economic game with a strong tie to history.
Victory points are almost omnipresent in board gaming, yet often they encourage games to become unfocused.
posts.key_insight: Board gaming as a hobby involves strategic depth and thoughtful design analysis that mass-market games simply cannot provide.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his experience with the Google Nexus 7 tablet after several months of use. He purchased it to replace an aging iPad and to experiment with both Android and the smaller 7-inch form factor. While he finds Google's base UI slightly superior to Apple's and appreciates Android's keyboard completion approach, he has mixed feelings about the smaller screen size. He concludes that despite using the 7-inch more at home, he would choose a 10-inch tablet if limited to one device due to the screen size requirements for certain content.
key_points: Android's UI has advantages over iOS, particularly the cross-application back button and word completion without automatic spacing
The 7-inch form factor is lighter and more convenient most of the time, but the 10-inch screen is essential for certain content like PDFs, code, and complex websites
If forced to choose one tablet size, the 10-inch wins despite the 7-inch being used more frequently at home
iOS still has an edge in application quality and availability, though this may be partly due to familiarity
money_quotes: I miss the cross-application back button when I go back to the iPad.
There are times - some websites, pdf documents, or books with code or graphics - when that bigger screen is essential.
If I could only have one tablet, it would have to be a 10â, but I do use the 7â more often at home.
I like to wait until I've used a device for a while before I post my experiences of it.
posts.key_insight: When choosing between tablet sizes, occasional strong needs for a larger screen outweigh the frequent mild preference for smaller, lighter devices.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains why Thoughtworks uses the term 'historically-discriminated-against' to focus diversity efforts on groups that have faced systemic oppression. He argues that underrepresentation of such groups—like women in tech—signals that historical wrongs haven't been corrected, unlike imbalances affecting groups without discrimination histories. The post rejects 'color-blind' or 'gender-blind' policies as inadequate, noting that centuries of discrimination cannot be undone in years. Fowler dismisses biological explanations for women's underrepresentation as both unsupported and historically weaponized to justify discrimination.
key_points: The term 'historically-discriminated-against' helps distinguish which diversity imbalances deserve priority attention
Historically-discriminated-against groups aren't always minorities (e.g., Black South Africans) or visibly different (e.g., religious groups, homosexuals)
Gender-blind and race-blind policies are fallacious because they ignore that centuries of wrongs can't be corrected in years
Biological arguments for underrepresentation are 'treacherous'—unsupported by evidence and historically used to justify discrimination
Until representation is corrected, claims of meritocracy are undermined by the reality of imbalance
money_quotes: Humanity has a consistently sad record of pushing groups of humans down.

It takes many generations to undo the effect of centuries of discrimination, so just because the law and society are beginning to catch up doesn't mean the work of supporting historically-discriminated-against groups can stop.
This is the fallacy of people who advocate for gender-blind and race-blind policies.
There's no evidence to support that women are less capable programmers (other than the circular one of their lack of numbers).
Such biological and cultural arguments have a long history of being used to justify discrimination.
I think it's wise to consider that an underrepresentation of a historically-discriminated-against group is a sign that we haven't yet finished the task of correcting a long-running wrong.
posts.key_insight: Underrepresentation of historically-discriminated-against groups should be treated as evidence that systemic wrongs remain uncorrected, not as a neutral statistical anomaly.

posts.summary: This article argues that successful enterprise REST integration depends far more on engineering practices than on REST's technical nuances. The author contends that large legacy replacements are among IT's hardest challenges, and organizations often underestimate integration complexity. The piece advocates for logical environment isolation, disciplined deployment pipelines with consumer-based testing, domain-driven bounded contexts for data modeling, and epic-level program management. The conclusion emphasizes that scaling RESTful integration requires addressing social and organizational issues rather than obsessing over hypermedia mechanics.
key_points: Logical environments should be defined per need, allowing developers isolation and control over release schedules rather than forcing everyone into shared, inflexible environments
Versioning should be a last resort—techniques like Postel's Law, tolerant deserialization, and semantic versioning can delay or avoid the complexity of maintaining multiple service versions
Consumer-based testing, where consumers write tests for producers, catches integration problems early and enables deployable artifact sets that are proven to work together
Bounded contexts from Domain-Driven Design prevent the disaster of trying to create universal data models—each business unit should have its own definition of common entities
Epics rather than user stories should be the primary metric for tracking velocity in large SOA projects, as story-level metrics can give a false sense of progress
money_quotes: Large-scale legacy replacement is the hardest job in the entire IT industry.
Some people, when confronted with a problem, think 'I know, I'll use versioning.' Now they have 2.1.0 problems.
Automatic deserialization usually falls into the WSDL pitfall of coupling consumers and producers by duplicating a static class structure in both.
Defining data boundaries poorly is one of the most expensive mistakes an architect can make.
In any large system, some of it will be poorly designed. Our principal goal of integration, therefore, is to ensure that we insulate ourselves from another subsystem's design.
Ease of deployment and environment management should be one of the criteria by which packages are selected.
The symptom to watch for is when velocity burnups show the program delivering on time, but nothing seems to work.
posts.key_insight: Success in enterprise REST integration depends far less on understanding HATEOAS and far more on deployment strategies, consumer-based testing, bounded contexts, and epic-level coordination.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces Datensparsamkeit, a German privacy concept meaning 'data minimization' that opposes the prevailing Big Data mentality of capturing everything possible. He argues that the 'capture-it-all' approach creates serious privacy risks, as every data store becomes a target for criminals or surveillance agencies. Germany's history with government surveillance led to strong privacy laws embodying this principle. Fowler advocates that companies should only capture data they genuinely need, be transparent about their practices, and bear the burden of demonstrating necessity. He concludes that even from a pure risk management perspective, holding unnecessary data creates liability exposure.
key_points: The Big Data mentality of capturing all possible data 'just in case' creates unnecessary privacy and security risks
Germany's experience with surveillance regimes led to privacy laws requiring data minimization by design
Technical solutions exist to practice Datensparsamkeit, such as hashing IP addresses or storing only partial information
The default assumption that captured data becomes a company's commercial property needs to change—burden of demonstrating need should fall on collectors
Even without ethical concerns, holding unnecessary data creates legal and reputational liability when breaches occur
money_quotes: We might not have an immediate use for the contacts our users store in their address books, but we'll ask for it anyway in case it comes in useful later.
Even if we trust ourselves to not abuse the data we collect, each data store represents a target for criminals or government surveillance agencies.
You should always ask yourself why you are capturing or storing data, and look to handle only the minimum amount of data you need for your purpose.
The default attitude at the moment is that any data you generate is not just freely usable by the capturer but furthermore becomes their valuable commercial property.
Companies should only capture what they need and the burden of demonstrating need should fall on them.
If you hold data that you don't need, and someone steals it and causes damage, shouldn't you be liable for that damage?
posts.key_insight: Data you collect but don't need isn't a free asset—it's a liability that creates privacy risks, security vulnerabilities, and potential legal exposure.

posts.summary: Branch by Abstraction is a technique for making large-scale changes to software systems incrementally while maintaining continuous delivery capability. The approach involves creating an abstraction layer between client code and a component you want to replace, gradually migrating all clients to use this abstraction, then building a new implementation behind it. This allows the old and new implementations to coexist during migration, with the system remaining deployable throughout. The technique is particularly valuable as an alternative to long-lived version control branches when replacing frameworks or major dependencies.
key_points: Create an abstraction layer that captures interactions between client code and the component being replaced
Gradually migrate all client code to use the abstraction, improving test coverage along the way
Build the new implementation behind the same abstraction, allowing both old and new to coexist
Use feature flags to compare behavior between implementations in test environments
The system remains buildable and deployable throughout the entire migration process
money_quotes: Use an abstraction layer to allow multiple implementations to co-exist in the software system.
Ensure that the system builds and runs correctly at all times, so you can continue to use Continuous Delivery while you are doing the replacement.
Look for as many ways as possible to make changes gradually.
Use the notion of one abstraction and multiple implementations to perform the migration from one implementation to the other.
While we are building the new feature we can use FeatureFlags to run the new supplier in test environments and compare its behavior to the flawed supplier.
posts.key_insight: Large-scale system changes can be made safely and incrementally by introducing an abstraction layer that allows old and new implementations to coexist, enabling continuous delivery throughout the migration.

posts.summary: Bounded Context is a central pattern in Domain-Driven Design that addresses the challenge of building unified models across large systems. Rather than attempting total unification—which DDD recognizes as neither feasible nor cost-effective—the pattern divides large systems into distinct contexts, each maintaining its own internally consistent model. Different contexts may share concepts like 'Customer' or 'Product' but represent them differently, with explicit mapping mechanisms handling integration. The boundaries between contexts are typically drawn along human cultural lines, since models serve as a Ubiquitous Language that changes across organizational groups.
key_points: Total unification of domain models for large systems is neither feasible nor cost-effective, necessitating bounded contexts
Polysemes—words like 'meter,' 'customer,' or 'product' that mean subtly different things to different groups—cause confusion that can be smoothed over in conversation but not in software
Bounded contexts can share concepts but with completely different models, requiring explicit mapping mechanisms for integration
Context boundaries are primarily drawn by human culture and language differences, not just technical considerations
Context maps help visualize and manage relationships between bounded contexts
money_quotes: DDD deals with large models by dividing them into different Bounded Contexts and being explicit about their interrelationships.
As you try to model a larger domain, it gets progressively harder to build a single unified model.
These subtle polysemes could be smoothed over in conversation but not in the precise world of computers.
Total unification of the domain model for a large system will not be feasible or cost-effective.
Usually the dominant one is human culture, since models act as Ubiquitous Language, you need a different model when the language changes.
Different contexts may have completely different models of common concepts with mechanisms to map between these polysemic concepts for integration.
posts.key_insight: Large systems require multiple bounded contexts with explicit interrelationships because the precision software demands cannot accommodate the natural polysemy of language across organizational boundaries.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler addresses the common problem of 'jQuery soup' in single-page web applications, where application logic, DOM manipulation, and server access become tangled together. He proposes Segregated DOM as a modularization tactic that isolates all DOM manipulation into dedicated JavaScript objects. Using his eurogames catalog page as an example, he demonstrates how a controller class can manage application logic while communicating with DOM gateway classes through DOM-independent interfaces. This separation improves code reasoning, maintainability, and crucially enables testing the controller logic without a browser or mock browser environment.
key_points: Segregated DOM separates all DOM manipulation into dedicated JavaScript objects, keeping the controller free of jQuery and HTML structure concerns
DOM objects act as Gateways between bounded contexts - translating application vocabulary into HTML DOM operations
The pattern enables testing controller logic in Node.js without PhantomJS or browser dependencies by using simple test doubles
Applying the Humble Object pattern by shifting logic out of DOM gateways maximizes testable code
Good modular design correlates directly with testability - each class can be tested in relative isolation
money_quotes: Single-page web applications often turn into jQuery soup, where application logic, DOM manipulation, and server access are all mixed together.
The principal benefit of doing this is to make it easier to reason about each class by keeping it focused on a single task.
Like any gateway the DOM object has an interface that speaks the vocabulary of its client (the application) and an implementation that translates this into the barbaric (HTML DOM) land beyond.
A good modular design tends to correlate to testability.
A particular advantage with a Segregated DOM is that I can test the controller without using a browser or mock browser such as PhantomJS.
By shifting as much logic out of the DOM gateway as possible, I can increase how much testing I can do without resorting to PhantomJS and the like.
posts.key_insight: Treating DOM manipulation as a Gateway between bounded contexts enables cleaner separation of concerns and dramatically improves testability by allowing application logic to be tested without browser dependencies.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the alienating atmosphere in the tech community that contributes to diversity imbalance, particularly affecting women and historically discriminated-against groups. He acknowledges that as a middle-class white man, he rarely experiences this alienation, while accomplished women colleagues still feel unwelcome at tech gatherings. The post explores how sexual jokes, online harassment, and the accumulation of small slights create a hostile environment. Fowler argues that individuals can choose whether to care about offending others, and that the tech community should actively work to combat this atmosphere through awareness, codes of conduct, and speaking up against inappropriate behavior.
key_points: The alienating atmosphere is experienced very differently depending on one's identity - what feels neutral to majority groups can signal 'you're not welcome here' to others
Small individual incidents accumulate into significant harm through their sheer quantity (microaggressions)
While you cannot control whether someone is offended, you can choose whether you care about their reaction
The standard you walk past is the standard you accept - silence enables the problem to persist
Fear of online backlash creates its own form of censorship, silencing articulate people who don't want to deal with harassment
money_quotes: I can't choose whether someone is offended by my actions. I can choose whether I care.
The standard you walk past is the standard you accept.
Anyone driven away from expressing innovation or writing excellent code is a loss to all of us, however unoffended we think they should be.
Their reaction is a result of their life experiences, which will be different to mine.
While I cannot be responsible for what my ancestors did, I can take responsibility to play what small part I can in cleaning up their mess.
Ironically the bilers often complain about censorship infringing their right to make sexual jokes, but not acknowledging that fear of the intermobs imposes its own censorship.
Correcting these long-term discriminations takes time, and can't be solved by just saying 'we're all equal now'.
posts.key_insight: Creating an inclusive tech community requires those in positions of privilege to actively care about and address behaviors that alienate others, even when those behaviors seem harmless from the majority perspective.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that the software industry's diversity imbalance—particularly the underrepresentation of women—cannot be explained by 'natural' differences in aptitude or inclination. He systematically dismantles this 'natural balance hypothesis' on two grounds: lack of evidence and its troubling historical use to deny equal rights. The post contends that diversity imbalance matters for moral reasons (equal opportunity), practical reasons (wasted talent and missed innovation), and professional credibility. Fowler concludes that software's diversity problems undermine its potential to be a forward-thinking model for human collaboration.
key_points: The 'natural balance hypothesis' (women lack aptitude/inclination for programming) has no supporting evidence and relies on circular logic
Historical precedent shows men have used similar arguments for centuries to deny women opportunities—and been proven wrong repeatedly
Diversity imbalance wastes talent, reduces innovation through homogeneous thinking, and handicaps the profession's ability to meet growing demand
The software profession's meritocratic self-image is undermined when it systematically excludes qualified candidates from underrepresented groups
Left unchecked, imbalances worsen through self-reinforcing exclusion and 'culture fit' screening
money_quotes: Personally I find it troubling when software professionals, who ought to be good at logical thinking, can reach so easily for such circular logic.
Unless someone comes up with decent evidence that there is a relevant biological difference, we must operate on the assumption that women are equally well suited to programming.
Critics of efforts to fix the diversity imbalance often fret that we risk failing to hire a well-qualified male, when we habitually fail to hire well-qualified females.
How can we claim to be forward thinking when our diversity looks it comes from where the rest of the world was a couple of generations ago?
Diversity imbalances are a cancer to that position.
A diversity imbalance suggest that there are many women, who would have good careers as programmers, who are not getting the opportunity to do so.
posts.key_insight: The burden of proof lies with those claiming diversity imbalance is natural—absent evidence, we must assume it results from systemic barriers that harm both individuals and the profession.

posts.summary: The Circuit Breaker pattern prevents cascading failures in distributed systems by wrapping remote calls in a monitoring object that trips after a failure threshold is reached. When open, the breaker fails fast without attempting the call, protecting both client resources and struggling servers. The pattern can self-reset by periodically attempting trial calls in a 'half-open' state. Fowler demonstrates the implementation in Ruby, progressing from a simple breaker requiring manual reset to a self-resetting version with three states: closed, open, and half-open. The pattern is valuable not just for fault tolerance but also as a monitoring and alerting mechanism for operational awareness.
key_points: Remote calls differ fundamentally from in-memory calls because they can fail or hang, potentially causing cascading failures across systems
The circuit breaker monitors failures and trips at a threshold, returning errors immediately without attempting the protected call
Self-resetting breakers use a half-open state to periodically test if the underlying service has recovered
Circuit breakers should be integrated with monitoring systems and expose their state for operational visibility
Clients must implement fallback strategies when breakers trip, such as queuing operations or showing stale data
money_quotes: One of the big differences between in-memory calls and remote calls is that remote calls can fail, or hang without a response until some timeout limit is reached.
What's worse if you have many callers on a unresponsive supplier, then you can run out of critical resources leading to cascading failures across multiple systems.
You wrap a protected function call in a circuit breaker object, which monitors for failures.
This is a reasonable approach with electrical circuit breakers in buildings, but for software circuit breakers we can have the breaker itself detect if the underlying calls are working again.
You avoid waiting on timeouts for the client, and a broken circuit avoids putting load on a struggling server.
Circuit breakers are a valuable place for monitoring. Any change in breaker state should be logged and breakers should reveal details of their state for deeper monitoring.
Breaker behavior is often a good source of warnings about deeper troubles in the environment.
posts.key_insight: Circuit breakers transform unpredictable remote call failures into predictable, fast-failing responses that protect system resources and provide operational visibility into service health.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes his text-based book writing toolchain, developed over two decades of technical authorship. He moved from WYSIWYG tools like Framemaker to a custom XML-based system that uses semantic markup and transforms to Docbook for multiple output formats. The approach enables automatic code import from live programs, eliminating error-prone copy/paste. While non-geeks might find it primitive, the system integrates well with version control and collaborative workflows, including his publisher's production staff working directly in the repository.
key_points: Text-based source formats enable version control and programmatic processing that WYSIWYG tools cannot match
Semantic markup (like <term> instead of <bold>) separates meaning from presentation and enables richer transformations like automatic indexing
Automatic code import from live, tested programs eliminates the error-prone copy/paste step that plagued his earlier books
Docbook serves as an excellent transformation target even if it's too verbose as a source format, providing paths to HTML, PDF, ePub, and print
The entire toolchain treats writing like software development: text files, version control, automated builds, and collaborative workflows
money_quotes: I'm a geek after all, and it's easy for me to write tools to process text-based files.
Now with my current scheme, all code is automatically imported into the book as part of the build process. So if I update my code, the book automatically becomes up to date.
I prefer this because it forces me to focus on semantics rather than formatting.
I prefer XML because it gives me more flexibility in markup - I can introduce whatever tags I like, including specialist tags for just this book.
While I don't like Docbook as a source document format, it is really good as a transformation target.
Like any programmer I value version control highly.
posts.key_insight: Treating book writing like software development—with text-based source files, semantic markup, automated builds, and version control—solves the unique challenges technical authors face with code examples and collaboration.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reflects on his choice to use 'enterprise application' as terminology for a specific class of software systems—those dealing with persistent data, concurrent access, complex integrations, and business logic. He identifies key characteristics: heavy reliance on databases, concurrent multi-user access, numerous UI screens, integration with legacy systems across decades of technology, conceptual dissonance between systems, and complex business rules that defy logical simplification. While acknowledging the term has acquired unfortunate connotations associating it with large corporations rather than the technical characteristics he intended, he concludes it remains the best available terminology.
key_points: Enterprise applications are defined by technical characteristics (persistent data, concurrency, integration needs) rather than company size
Business logic in enterprise applications is inherently 'illogical'—accumulated special cases from thousands of sales deals that can't be simplified
Integration with other systems spanning decades of technology creates irreducible complexity, with each rationalization attempt adding more layers
The term 'enterprise' has unfortunately become associated with large corporations and even used as an insult in some communities like Ruby on Rails
Conceptual dissonance between applications means the same entity (like 'customer') has different meanings across different organizational contexts
money_quotes: This data will usually be longer lasting and more valuable than the applications that process it.
Despite high levels of concurrency, many enterprise application developers don't think much about critical regions, race conditions and other elements of classic concurrent programming.
Every so often there will be an attempt to rationalize all this communication technology, but they never entirely succeed leaving behind more complexity in their wake.
If you want to change them you need sixty-seven meetings and three vice-presidents retiring.
Their insanity derives from a good reason, each one is a case where salesman could close a particular deal by offering some special one-off condition.
Big systems make a lot of noise when they go wrong, yet the cumulative effect of small systems can have a surprising effect on an enterprise's health.
Just don't tell DHH I said so or he'll turn me into a hood ornament.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise applications are defined not by the size of the company using them but by their technical characteristics: persistent data, concurrent access, complex integrations, and business rules that accumulate irrationality for good commercial reasons.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that enterprise applications should separate their reporting needs from operational databases into dedicated reporting databases. The operational and reporting requirements differ significantly in schema design and data access patterns, making a unified approach suboptimal. A reporting database can be denormalized, use different technology, and store derived data without impacting operational performance. The main tradeoff is keeping data synchronized, though overnight batch updates often suffice since most reporting doesn't require real-time data.
key_points: Operational and reporting database needs differ fundamentally in schema design and access patterns, justifying separation
Reporting databases can be freely denormalized and duplicated since they're read-only, making queries easier to write
Development teams can refactor operational databases independently without breaking reports
Data synchronization is the main challenge, but overnight batch updates work well for most reporting needs
Near-real-time analytics is often overvalued and can lead to 'information panic' from reacting to rapidly changing data
money_quotes: You don't need to normalize a reporting database, because it's read-only. Feel free to duplicate data as much as needed to make queries and reporting easier.
The development team can refactor the operational database without needing to change the reporting database.
Often when analyzing data trends you don't need to react right away, and your thinking improves when you give it time for a proper mulling.
Reacting too quickly leads to a form of information panic, where you react badly to data that's changing too rapidly to get a proper picture of what's going on.
I'm skeptical of the value of near-real time analytics.
posts.key_insight: Separating reporting from operational databases isn't just a performance optimization—it's an architectural boundary that enables independent evolution of both systems while acknowledging that most analytical thinking benefits from slightly stale data anyway.

posts.summary: Self-Testing Code refers to writing comprehensive automated tests alongside functional software, enabling developers to invoke a single command that illuminates any bugs in the codebase. Fowler traces the concept from an OOPSLA talk through his collaboration with Kent Beck and the creation of JUnit. The practice forms a foundational pillar of Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery. While Test-Driven Development is one excellent way to achieve self-testing code, the key outcome is having the tests regardless of when they're written. The greatest benefit isn't avoiding production bugs but gaining confidence to change and refactor code safely, breaking the cycle of fear that paralyzes legacy codebases.
key_points: Self-testing code creates a 'bug detector' that catches defects when tests are run frequently, making bugs easy to find in recent changes
The biggest benefit is confidence to change code safely, not merely avoiding production bugs
TDD and self-testing code are related but distinct concepts - you can achieve self-testing code by writing tests after code
When production bugs occur, teams should first write a test that exposes the bug before attempting to fix it
Self-testing code is essential for true Continuous Integration - without it, you're not really doing CI
money_quotes: Our attitude is to assume that any non-trivial code without tests is broken.
Old codebases are often terrifying places, where developers fear to change working code.
The biggest benefit isn't about merely avoiding production bugs, it's about the confidence that you get to make changes to the system.
With that safety net, you can spend time keeping the code in good shape, and end up in a virtuous spiral where you get steadily faster at adding new features.
You aren't really doing continuous integration unless you have self-testing code.
The attitude should be that any bug isn't just a failure in the code, it's equally a failure in the testing screen.
No programming episode is complete without working code and the tests to keep it working.
posts.key_insight: Self-testing code's primary value lies not in catching bugs before production, but in providing the confidence to continuously improve and refactor the codebase without fear.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the surprisingly ill-defined term 'unit test,' tracing its evolution through XP and xunit frameworks. He identifies common elements—small scope, programmer-written, and fast execution—while noting significant disagreements about what constitutes a 'unit.' The central distinction he explores is between 'solitary' tests (using test doubles for collaborators) and 'sociable' tests (using real collaborators), representing classic versus mockist schools. He argues there's no absolute right answer on test speed or isolation, emphasizing that the real goal is running tests frequently enough to catch bugs while the context is fresh.
key_points: Unit testing terminology is poorly defined despite widespread use, with at least 24 different definitions acknowledged by testing experts
The solitary vs. sociable distinction is more important than defining the unit—mockists insist on isolation while classicists allow real collaborators
XP revolutionized testing by having programmers write their own tests, contradicting the previous orthodoxy that programmers should never test their own code
Test suite speed requirements vary dramatically between practitioners (sub-second to several minutes), but the key is running frequently enough to localize bugs quickly
Test doubles are valuable for removing non-determinism and awkward collaborations, but aren't absolute requirements for classic-style testers
money_quotes: Like most software development terminology, however, it's very ill-defined, and I see confusion can often occur when people think that it's more tightly defined than it actually is.
In the first morning of my training course I cover 24 different definitions of unit test.
The team decides what makes sense to be a unit for the purposes of their understanding of the system and its testing.
We write the tests assuming everything other than that unit is working correctly.
If I've introduced the defect with my last change it's much easier for me to spot the bug because I don't have far to look.
Your test suites should run fast enough that you're not discouraged from running them frequently enough.
posts.key_insight: The value of unit testing lies not in rigid definitions of isolation or unit boundaries, but in enabling frequent test runs that catch bugs while the code changes are still fresh in mind.

posts.summary: This article documents a series of public conversations between Martin Fowler, Kent Beck, and David Heinemeier Hansson about whether TDD is 'dead.' Sparked by DHH's provocative RailsConf keynote, the discussions explore TDD's value, costs, and appropriate contexts. The participants find substantial common ground: all value self-testing code highly, agree TDD works well in some contexts, and emphasize that developers must thoughtfully adapt techniques to their situations rather than following dogma. The conversation serves as a 'reboot' to first principles after years of accumulated interpretations and misapplications of TDD practices.
key_points: TDD and self-testing code are often conflated but are distinct concepts - TDD is one way to achieve self-testing code, not the only way
Test-induced design damage often stems from excessive mocking and isolation, not from TDD itself - it's about design decisions, not the testing workflow
The value of testing involves trade-offs around frequency, fidelity, overhead, and lifespan - there's no universal right answer
Over-testing is a real concern: tests with zero delta coverage should be deleted, and the ratio of test code to production code is a bogus metric
Both TDD and the broader testing culture benefit from periodic 'reboots' to first principles after accumulating cruft and misinterpretations over time
money_quotes: Programmers deserve to feel confident that their code works, TDD is one (not the only) way to reach that.
Ascribing test-induced damage to TDD was like driving a car to a bad place and blaming the car for it.

You don't have enough tests (or good enough tests) if you can't confidently change the code, and the sign of too much is whenever you change the code you think you expend more effort changing the tests than changing the code.
The on-call is the feedback loop that teaches you what tests you didn't write.
Most people can't leave good ideas the fuck alone.
You either die a hero or become the villain.
TDD isn't dead, but is glad David set fire to it so it could come out like a phoenix.
If you're involved in software development you have to be thoughtful about it, you have to build up what practices work for you and your team, you can't take any technique blindly.
posts.key_insight: TDD remains valuable but must be applied thoughtfully based on context - the real goal is self-testing code that gives developers confidence, and achieving that matters more than adherence to any particular workflow.

posts.summary: Parallel change (or expand and contract) is a three-phase pattern for making backward-incompatible interface changes safely: expand the interface to support both old and new versions, migrate all clients to the new version, then contract by removing the old version. The post illustrates this with a Grid class example where x,y coordinates are refactored to a Coordinate object. This pattern is essential for continuous delivery because code can be released at any phase. It applies broadly to refactoring, database migrations, deployments (canary/blue-green), and REST API evolution.
key_points: Breaking interface changes into expand-migrate-contract phases separates the concerns of implementing change from updating usages
During expand, both old and new versions coexist; during migrate, clients incrementally adopt the new version; during contract, the old version is removed
Even with full control over clients, this pattern prevents spreading breakage across the entire codebase at once
The pattern applies to refactoring, database design, deployment strategies, and remote API evolution
Feature flags can control which version clients use during migration, decoupling supplier and client releases
money_quotes: Making a change to an interface that impacts all its consumers requires two thinking modes: implementing the change itself, and then updating all its usages.
Parallel change is a pattern to implement backward-incompatible changes to an interface in a safe manner, by breaking the change into three distinct phases: expand, migrate, and contract.
This pattern is particularly useful when practicing ContinuousDelivery because it allows your code to be released in any of these three phases.
Even when you have control over all usages of the interface, following this pattern is still useful because it prevents you from spreading breakage across the entire codebase all at once.
The migrate phase can be short, but it is an alternative to leaning on the compiler to find all the usages that need to be fixed.
The downside of using parallel change is that during the migrate phase the supplier has to support two different versions, and clients could get confused about which version is new versus old.
posts.key_insight: Backward-incompatible changes become safe and manageable when decomposed into expand, migrate, and contract phases that allow incremental client migration while maintaining a releasable codebase throughout.

posts.summary: Fowler distinguishes between 'published' and 'public' interfaces, arguing that a published interface is one used outside its defining codebase, making it fundamentally different from merely public code. The key insight is that public interfaces within a single codebase can be freely refactored since all callers are accessible, while published interfaces require special handling because you cannot update external consumers. Fowler contends this distinction matters more than the traditional public/private divide.
key_points: Published interfaces are used outside their defining codebase, beyond what language visibility modifiers indicate
The public/published distinction is more important than public/private
Internal public interfaces can be freely refactored with modern tools since all callers are reachable
Published interfaces require 'more complicated treatment' because you cannot update external calling code
money_quotes: The distinction between published and public is actually more important than that between public and private.
With a non-published interface you can change it and update the calling code since it is all within a single code base.
Anything published so you can't reach the calling code needs more complicated treatment.
posts.key_insight: The critical API design distinction isn't what's visible to code, but what's visible to codebases you don't control—published interfaces constrain evolution in ways that mere public interfaces do not.

posts.summary: This comprehensive article uses the 2014 'goto fail' and Heartbleed security vulnerabilities as case studies to argue for universal adoption of unit testing in software development. Fowler demonstrates through proof-of-concept tests that both catastrophic bugs could have been prevented with basic unit testing practices. He traces how Google's Testing Grouplet transformed a resistant development culture into one where unit testing became the norm, emphasizing that the benefits—reduced fear of change, faster feedback loops, improved code quality, and executable documentation—far outweigh the startup costs. The article concludes with detailed guidance on changing development cultures, drawing from the author's experience driving adoption across a large organization.
key_points: Both goto fail and Heartbleed were straightforward programming errors that well-crafted unit tests would have caught before deployment, not inevitable consequences of software complexity

Unit testing produces benefits beyond bug detection: faster feedback loops, reduced fear of change, improved code quality through design pressure, and executable documentation that accelerates team onboarding

Google's Testing Grouplet transformed company culture through grassroots efforts like Testing on the Toilet, the Test Certified program, and strategic Fixits—proving that cultural change is achievable without top-down mandates
Code duplication and untested code are symptoms of development cultures that tolerate poor practices, not one-time programmer errors
Linus's Law has a dark corollary: not all eyes examining open source code belong to those who will report bugs for the public good
