This is what makes a good antipattern something separate to just a bad thing to point and laugh at.
The fact that it looks like a good solution is its essential danger.
Since it looks good, sensible people will take the path - only once you've put a lot of effort into it will you know it's bad result.
It's useful to remember that the same solution can be a good pattern in some contexts and an antipattern in others.
posts.key_insight: An antipattern's defining characteristic is not that it's bad, but that it's deceptively attractive—it looks like a good solution until you've invested heavily in it.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler advocates for the classic presentation-domain-data layering pattern as an effective way to modularize information-rich applications. The primary benefit is reducing cognitive scope—allowing developers to focus on one concern at a time without being overwhelmed by the entire system. While the pattern supports substitutability and testability, Fowler emphasizes that the cognitive benefit alone justifies the approach. He warns against organizing development teams along these layer boundaries, arguing that while individual developers need not be full-stack, cross-functional teams are essential for effective collaboration.
key_points: The main benefit of layering is reducing scope of attention, allowing focused thinking on one concern at a time
Layers are logical separations, not physical tiers—they can be deployed in various configurations
As applications grow, don't use presentation-domain-data as top-level modules; instead create domain-oriented modules that are internally layered
Organizing development teams by layers is an antipattern that adds friction and reduces cross-layer understanding
Substitutability of data sources is often cited as a benefit but rarely actually exercised in practice
money_quotes: It's biggest advantage (for me) is that it allows me to reduce the scope of my attention by allowing me to think about the three topics relatively independently.
While substitutability and testability are certainly benefits of this layering, I must stress that even without either of these reasons I would still divide into layers like this.
I have to mention that while I often hear about data access substitution being a driver for separating the data source layer, I rarely hear of someone actually doing it.
Developers don't have to be full-stack but teams should be.
Once any of these layers gets too big you should split your top level into domain oriented modules which are internally layered.
Team boundaries add considerable friction, as well as reducing an individual's motivation to develop the important cross-layer understanding of a system.
posts.key_insight: The primary value of presentation-domain-data layering is cognitive—it narrows the scope of attention—and this benefit alone justifies the pattern even without substitutability or testability gains.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explores the enduring but elusive goal of building software through component assembly rather than hand-crafted code. He adopts Ralph Johnson's customer-centric definition: components are things that are independently replaceable and upgradeable, allowing users to mix, match, and upgrade on their own schedule. Fowler identifies two modern forms of components: libraries (code linked into processes) and services (separate processes accessed via interprocess communication). The key distinction from ordinary modules is that components must allow independent replacement without forcing coordinated upgrades across the system.
key_points: Components are defined by independent replaceability and upgradeability, not by technology
The component vision serves customer needs: buying software piecemeal and upgrading on their own schedule
Libraries and services are the two modern manifestations of software components
Components are a specialized form of module with the additional property of independent replacement
For services to be true components, you should never need to coordinate upgrades between services
money_quotes: Components are not a technology. Technology people seem to find this hard to understand.
Components are about how customers want to relate to software.
They want to be able to buy their software a piece at a time, and to be able to upgrade it just like they can upgrade their stereo.
This is a very reasonable requirement. It is just hard to satisfy.
Software components are things that are independently replaceable and upgradeable.
For services to be components, you should never need to coordinate the upgrade of one service with another service.
posts.key_insight: Software components are defined not by their technology but by their property of independent replaceability—enabling customers to upgrade and mix pieces on their own terms.

posts.summary: A required interface is defined by the client of an interaction, specifying what a supplier component needs to provide to participate. Unlike typical interfaces where suppliers decide what to expose, required interfaces flip this relationship by letting clients specify their needs. The Comparable interface in Java exemplifies this pattern, where sort functions define what objects must implement to be sortable. When integrating independently-developed modules, adapters bridge the gap between a supplier's actual interface and a client's required interface, even when method signatures happen to match but types don't align.
key_points: Required interfaces are defined by clients rather than suppliers, inverting the typical interface ownership model
The Comparable interface demonstrates this pattern - sort functions define what objects need to implement
Type systems enforce required interfaces even when method signatures accidentally match across unrelated classes
Adapters serve as bridges when supplier components don't directly implement the required interface
Thinking about what clients require leads to more useful interfaces and role-based interface design
money_quotes: A required interface is an interface that is defined by the client of an interaction that specifies what a supplier component needs to do so that it can be used in that interaction.
Often people think about interfaces as a decision by the supplier about what to expose to clients. But required interfaces are specified (and often defined) by the client.
You often get more useful interfaces by thinking about what clients require - leading towards thinking about RoleInterfaces.
Even though the activity has a method whose signature happens to match the required interface's, I (rightly) can't create a task list of activities because the type definitions don't match.
In this usage an adapter is an object that maps one object to the required interface of another.
posts.key_insight: Better interfaces emerge when you design from the client's perspective, defining what they require rather than what suppliers choose to expose.

posts.summary: This article demonstrates how to refactor a monolithic codebase into well-separated modules using presentation-domain-data layering. Fowler walks through a practical example of extracting calculation logic (Gondorff number) from both UI code and data access code, then explores different approaches for managing dependencies between these modules. He compares linker substitution, passing dependencies as parameters, service locator pattern, and dependency injection, showing the trade-offs of each approach. The key insight is that splitting service configuration from service use enables both testability through substitution and cleaner code organization. He also introduces the Split Phase refactoring pattern as a general technique for separating computation into distinct stages with intermediate results.
key_points: Module boundaries should be drawn so that code that changes together lives in the same or nearby modules - this is more important than following rigid architectural rules
Presentation-domain-data layering is useful but has limitations; adding a data field often requires changes across all three layers, making it unsuitable for team boundaries
Service Locator and Dependency Injection both solve the same problem of separating configuration from use, but DI removes the component's dependency on locator machinery
Split Phase is a powerful refactoring pattern: extract configuration into its own phase that passes results to later phases via intermediate data structures
Creation dependencies (knowing how to instantiate) are more intimate than usage dependencies (just calling methods), and good architecture minimizes creation dependencies in business logic
money_quotes: Perhaps the most important part of drawing good module boundaries is paying attention to the changes you make and refactoring your code so that code that changes together is in the same or nearby modules.

This illustrates the advantage of keeping a program factored into small pieces - it allows substitution of those pieces, even if the original writer didn't have any substitutions in mind. It enables unforeseen customization.
The more serious problem is that now every time I have an application module that uses gondorff I have to ensure I can create and configure the data source too.
Using a Required Interface like this is helpful because it makes explicit what functions gondorff is expecting from its data source.
Splitting computation like this into separate phases is a useful refactoring because it allows us to think separately about the different needs in each phase.

Like many great patterns it has that notion of obviousness - I feel like it's just putting a name to something that I've been doing for decades. But such a name isn't a small thing, once you name an oft-used technique like this, it makes it easier to talk to other people about.
The heart of the idea is that you should be able to write components like the gondorff object without needing to know about any special conventions or tools for configuring dependent components.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental goal of module dependency refactoring is separating service configuration from service use, which enables both testability and clean component boundaries regardless of whether you use service locator or dependency injection patterns.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that remote versus co-located work isn't a simple binary choice, but rather a spectrum of distribution patterns including single-site, multi-site, satellite workers, and remote-first teams. While he believes most people are more productive when co-located due to richer communication, he contends that remote teams can often be more productive overall because they can draw from a wider talent pool. The key is understanding the trade-offs of each pattern and investing in communication accordingly. He concludes that organizations able to make remote working effective will have growing competitive advantage as top developers increasingly resist relocation and commuting demands.
key_points: There are multiple distribution patterns (single-site, multi-site, satellite, remote-first), each with distinct trade-offs and techniques
Most individuals are more productive co-located, but remote teams can be more productive overall by accessing better talent
Satellite workers are the hardest pattern to make effective—they tend to become increasingly detached from the main team
Multi-site teams should divide work by autonomous components, not by layer or activity, due to Conway's Law
Remote-first requires going all-out—all communication online, regular in-person meetups, and caution with junior staff
money_quotes: A remote team may be less productive than that same team if it were co-located, but may still be more productive than the best co-located team you can form.
The key point is the point where you find it easier to send an email than walk over to talk.
Such a rule means you aren't able to hire the best person for the job, you can merely hire the best person who is prepared to relocate.
I've heard a few people argue that agile software development is incompatible with remote working. That's poppycock.
It's very difficult to get satellite workers to be effective. With most people co-located, most communication will happen within the co-located team.
I sense a growing reluctance amongst the best developers to accept the location and commuting disadvantages of single-site work.
posts.key_insight: The productivity question isn't whether remote or co-located is better in isolation, but whether the talent advantages of remote work outweigh the communication advantages of co-location for your specific situation.

posts.summary: Fowler demonstrates how to refactor imperative conditional logic into an Adaptive Model—a data-driven approach where business rules are encoded in JSON and executed by a simple interpreter engine. The motivating problem is cross-platform duplication: the same recommendation logic must run on Node.js servers, iOS, and Android apps, making synchronized changes painful. By extracting the logic into a portable JSON structure using a Production Rule System pattern, the same rules can be loaded and interpreted anywhere. The refactoring proceeds incrementally, converting one conditional at a time while keeping tests passing, and introducing patterns like Range Picker to handle numeric thresholds elegantly.
key_points: Adaptive Models separate business logic (data) from execution (interpreter engine), enabling portability across platforms and languages
Production Rule Systems organize computation as condition-action pairs, naturally fitting conditional business logic
Refactoring to an adaptive model should proceed incrementally—convert one rule at a time, extending the model's expressiveness as needed
The trade-off is increased code size and complexity; the benefit is a single source of truth that's portable and often easier to modify
Sometimes the reverse refactoring is warranted—adaptive models can be harder for teams to maintain than straightforward imperative code
money_quotes: When I'm looking to represent some imperative code with a data structure, my first task is to figure out what kind of model I should use to structure that data.
A good choice of model can do much to simplify the logic, indeed there are times when using an adaptive model is worth it when the only reason is to make the logic easier to follow.
With a larger set of rules, imperative code's flexibility can let it get more tangled easily, while the limited expressivity of an adaptive model can help keep logic easier to follow.
At any point I can stop and leave some logic in the model and some in imperative code.
It's common for people to get all excited with the possibilities of representing code as data when they first come across it, and consequently over-using it.

Replacing an adaptive model with imperative code is a similar process to its inverse, in that you first set things up so you can compose the outcome of the model with imperative code, then move logic into the imperative code in small chunks, testing as you go.
posts.key_insight: When business logic must be portable across platforms, refactoring it from imperative code into a data-driven adaptive model traded off code simplicity for a single, shareable source of truth—but this transformation should be incremental, reversible, and justified by real needs.

posts.summary: This post addresses enterprise architects facing organizational transitions to lean/agile practices, arguing that their role must shift from centralized decision-making to influence and enablement. Fowler contends that EAs should stop making technology choices for teams and instead help teams make informed choices themselves. The article outlines practical approaches: establishing and sharing an architectural vision, building partnerships with development teams rather than mandating compliance, finding strategic opportunities for gradual change, and fostering learning communities. The core message is that EAs remain essential but must earn influence through collaboration rather than authority.
key_points: Enterprise architects must shift from making decisions to helping others make the right decisions and radiating that information across the organization
Architectural visions should be developed collaboratively with developers, displayed visibly, and treated as tools for common understanding rather than mandates
When teams want to adopt new technologies, EAs should engage in genuine discussion and time-boxed proofs of concept rather than reflexively blocking change
Architectural change must happen gradually within the context of existing projects, framed in terms of business value and risk reduction
Building learning communities and treating developers as partners generates the goodwill necessary for EAs to remain influential
money_quotes: Your role is no longer to make choices, but to help others make the right choice and then radiate that information.
If you treat them like subordinates they will find ways around you and put your vision and strategy at risk.
Their active engagement with the vision is more valuable than getting the vision exactly the way you'd like it.
Try to avoid making the architecture a mandate, instead use it as a tool for common understanding with developers and their teams.
Avoid the temptation to immediately say no or assume that they are selecting the new tool just because it is new and fun.
Keep in mind that generating business value is your primary goal, so avoid purely technical projects that are interesting but not valuable.
Remember that to be an EA in a Lean Enterprise is to build relationships between development teams and the business.
posts.key_insight: In agile organizations, enterprise architects derive their effectiveness from influence earned through partnership and collaboration, not from authority to mandate decisions.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that web publishing should embrace iterative revision rather than mimicking print magazine conventions. Despite the web's ability to update content, most sites still treat articles as static, time-stamped artifacts. He advocates publishing articles in installments, then consolidating them into single evolving resources that improve over time. Using his microservices and collection pipelines articles as examples, he demonstrates how articles can be extended and revised as understanding deepens. The key shift is viewing web articles as living information resources rather than fixed publications.
key_points: Most web publishing still follows print-era assumptions where articles are static and organized by publication date
Publishing in installments allows shorter reads and multiple opportunities to reach readers, while the final form should be a single cohesive article
Articles should evolve through revision as understanding increases, with the web enabling updates that print never could
Feed entries should announce updates to articles rather than being permanently coupled to a single publication moment
Revision history and occasional archiving of original versions preserves intellectual history while prioritizing current best content
money_quotes: Yet these are constraints of a paper medium, where updating something already published is mostly impossible.
A reader doesn't care that I revised the article to improve it, she just wants to read the best explanation of the topic at hand.
We should consider web articles as information resources, resources that can and should be extended and revised as our understanding increases and as time and energy allow.
We shouldn't let the Print Age notions of how articles should be constructed dictate the patterns of the Internet Age.
This suggests to me that the publisher isn't thinking of the series as an article that's going to be valuable in the long-term.
Letting an article evolve like this is the kind of thing that's difficult in a print medium, but exactly the right thing to do on the web.
posts.key_insight: Web articles should be treated as living resources that evolve through revision and extension, not as static artifacts constrained by print-era conventions.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the ubiquitous pattern of representing data structures as composites of lists and hashmaps, noting their presence across virtually all modern programming languages. He explains how these structures enable flexible, schemaless data representation while acknowledging the implicit schemas that exist in practice. The post highlights the power of generic operations and collection pipelines for manipulating these structures without knowing specific keys. Fowler also discusses serialization options, favoring JSON over XML, while noting that richer tree representations with XPath-style navigation sometimes offer advantages these simpler structures lack.
key_points: Lists and hashmaps together can represent most hierarchical data structures and have become standard equipment in modern languages
Schemaless structures are flexible but nearly always have an implicit schema that developers expect when manipulating them
Generic operations parameterized by keys allow powerful manipulation without coupling to specific data shapes
JSON is the preferred serialization format over XML, which is verbose and has the unnecessary attribute/element distinction
Richer tree representations with path-based navigation (XPath, CSS selectors) are sometimes preferable for complex documents
money_quotes: A list 'n' hash structure is by default schemaless, the lists can contain disparate elements and the hashes any combination of keys.
We nearly always have an implicit schema when we manipulate a schemaless data structure, in that we expect certain data to be represented with certain keys.
A strength of the list and hash structure is that you can manipulate it with generic operations which know nothing of the actual keys present.
JSON is a particularly effective form of serialization for such a data structure, and is my default choice for this.

The presence of rich lists and hashes as standard equipment in modern languages has been one of the definite improvements in my programming life since I started programming in Fortran IV, but there's no need to stop there.
I find it awkward that there's no generally accepted, cross-language term for this kind of data structure.
posts.key_insight: While list-and-hash composites have become the lingua franca of data representation across programming languages, we should remember that schemaless flexibility always comes with implicit schemas, and richer tree abstractions still have their place.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the collection pipeline pattern, a powerful technique for processing data through a sequence of operations where each step takes a collection as input and emits a collection as output. He traces its origins from Unix pipes through Smalltalk to modern languages like Ruby and Clojure, demonstrating how pipelines enable clear, composable code for filtering, transforming, and aggregating data. The article covers key operations (map, reduce, filter, group-by, flat-map), compares pipelines to loops and comprehensions, and addresses practical concerns like debugging, laziness, parallelism, and immutability. Fowler argues that while functional programmers often claim collection pipelines as their domain, Smalltalkers used them widely, and the pattern only declined in OO because popular languages like Java lacked lambdas until recently.
key_points: Collection pipelines compose operations that each take a collection as input and return a collection as output, enabling complex transformations through simple, chainable steps
The pattern appears across paradigms—Unix pipes, Smalltalk methods, Ruby chains, and functional languages like Clojure—with the core concept remaining consistent despite syntactic differences
Key operations include map (transform elements), reduce (combine to single value), filter/select (keep matching elements), group-by (partition by key), and flat-map (map then flatten)
Pipelines work best when linear and small; larger ones should be factored into separate functions following the principle of extracting methods when 'what' differs from 'how'
Without good lambda support, collection pipelines become too syntactically messy to be worthwhile—this is why they declined in early Java and C++
money_quotes: A collection pipeline lays out a sequence of operations that pass a collection of items between them. Each operation takes a collection as an input and emits another collection.
The reason people say that collection pipelines aren't a feature of OO programming is that the popular OO languages like C++, Java, and C# didn't adopt Smalltalk's use of lambdas.
Terse, powerful code is nice - but terseness is only valuable in the service of clarity.
Extract a method whenever there's a difference between what a block of code does and how it does it.
Without good language support for clean lambda expressions, this pattern usually ends up being more trouble than is worth it.

Transformations like this are usually much easier to reason about when you break the full transformation down into little pieces and string them together - which is the whole point of the collection pipeline pattern.
It's often useful to treat hashmaps as lists of key-value pairs.
posts.key_insight: Collection pipelines enable complex data transformations through simple, composable operations—their power lies not in any single step but in how naturally the steps chain together.

posts.summary: Fowler examines different strategies for loading and processing JSON documents in Java, arguing against the common practice of creating full object hierarchies when only partial data is needed. He demonstrates through progressive refactoring examples how to encapsulate JSON handling, use sparse object structures, store raw JSON alongside needed objects, and enrich documents without full databinding. The key principle is that code should only declare and process the data structures it actually uses, creating more resilient and maintainable systems.
key_points: Encapsulation means hiding design decisions (including serialization mechanisms), not just data - turn decisions into secrets hidden from outside code
When you only need a subset of JSON data, declare only the properties you need to create a Tolerant Reader that's resilient to upstream changes
You can store both raw JSON strings and partial object structures together when you need full JSON output but only partial Java API access
Document enrichment can be done by traversing Java objects and modifying a tree model, avoiding the need to declare unnecessary intermediate classes
The choice of data representation should be based on how the data will be used, not on what the source document contains
money_quotes: The essence of encapsulation is turning a design decision into a secret
If I'm defining ten classes and 200 public methods, and only using five of them, that's a sign that something is wrong
If I only need part of the JSON data, there's no point in importing it all
By defining only the structure I use, I'm creating a Tolerant Reader, which makes my program much more resilient to changes in the input data
We should not expect that a module's interface should correspond to its internal storage
I don't prefer this in Java. Having and using the java objects is generally the best place to put behavior, particularly as it grows in volume and complexity
posts.key_insight: Your code's internal data structures should reflect what you actually need to process, not mirror the shape of incoming documents - encapsulate serialization decisions and declare only what you use.

posts.summary: The article argues that database design must evolve alongside application development in agile methodologies, rather than being fixed upfront. Fowler and Sadalagi describe techniques developed over a decade at Thoughtworks for managing database schema changes through small, versioned migration scripts that are treated like application code. The approach treats every database change as a refactoring—combining schema modifications, data migration, and access code changes into atomic units. Key enablers include close DBA-developer collaboration, individual database instances for each developer, continuous integration of database changes, and automation tools. The techniques have scaled to projects with 600+ tables, 100+ developers, and 24/7 uptime requirements.
key_points: Database changes should be captured as versioned migration scripts stored in the same repository as application code, not made ad-hoc through schema editing tools
Every developer should have their own database instance to experiment freely, with automation making it trivial to create, destroy, and rebuild databases
Database refactorings have three dimensions that must change together: schema, data migration, and database access code
Transition phases using views and triggers allow destructive changes to coexist with legacy systems during migration periods
Frequent small integrations are exponentially easier than infrequent large ones—the pain of integration increases exponentially with size
money_quotes: On an agile project you assume that you cannot fix the requirements of the system up-front.

Instead of thinking of design as a phase, which is mostly completed before you begin construction, you look at design as an on-going process that is interleaved with construction, testing, and even delivery.
Database environments should be phoenixes—regularly burnt down and rebuilt at will.
The pain of integration increases exponentially with the size of the integration, so doing many small changes is much easier in practice, even though it appears counter-intuitive to many.
Being schemaless doesn't free us from worrying about schemas, there is still an implicit schema—one that's implied by any code that accesses the database.
If you are determined to automate every task, you can handle lot of work with much less people.
posts.key_insight: Database schema evolution becomes manageable when every change is captured as a small, versioned migration script that travels through the same continuous integration pipeline as application code.

posts.summary: This comprehensive guide explores daily stand-up meetings as more than a simple ritual, documenting patterns that distinguish effective stand-ups from wasteful ones. Fowler presents stand-ups as solutions to team coordination problems: sharing goals, coordinating efforts, surfacing problems, and building team identity. The article organizes patterns around key questions—who attends, what to discuss, speaking order, timing, energy management, and autonomy. It emphasizes that stand-ups should serve the team rather than management, with work items being the true focus rather than individual status reports. The piece concludes that while following patterns matters, the ultimate measure is whether teams are energized, sharing, focused, and working together.
key_points: Stand-ups solve four coordination problems: shared goals, effort coordination, problem sharing, and team identity formation
The 'Work Items Attend' pattern shifts focus from people being busy to work actually progressing—treating stories as the speakers rather than individuals
Walking the board (right-to-left, top-to-bottom) provides better visibility into workflow status than person-by-person updates
Multiple anti-patterns signal dysfunction: Reporting to the Leader, Story Telling, Problem Solving during meetings, and obstacles not being raised or removed
Team autonomy matters more than rigid structure—patterns like rotating facilitators and breaking eye contact prevent dependence on single leaders
money_quotes: If the stories are so important to the project, they ought to be the ones speaking in the standup
Standups keep everyone busy. Walking the board keeps everyone focused on the most important things.
A 'team' where each team member is working toward different goals tends to be ineffective.
The meeting is for the whole team, not for any particular individual.
With the exception of a blaming environment, the surest way to stop people from raising obstacles is to not remove them.
It is very difficult to psychologically identify with a group if you don't regularly engage with the group.
Tell the headline, not the whole story.
The stand-up should be more of a Huddle than a meeting.
posts.key_insight: Effective stand-ups focus on progressing work items rather than reporting individual busyness, requiring patterns that shift attention from runners to the baton and empower teams to self-organize.

posts.summary: Fowler addresses the overuse and vagueness of the term 'cloud' by pointing readers to NIST's authoritative definition. He notes that overhyped terms typically lack clear definitions, making meaningful discussion difficult. The NIST document defines cloud computing as access to rapidly provisionable computing resources with minimal overhead. Fowler highlights the definition's clarity and brevity as notable, given the typical opacity of standards documents.
key_points: Overhyped technology terms tend to lose meaningful definition
NIST provides a clear, concise official definition of cloud computing
Cloud computing centers on rapid provisioning of pooled computing resources
The definition encompasses five characteristics, three service models, and four deployment models
money_quotes: One of the characteristics of over-hyped words is that they have little or no definition to them
It's available by a wonderfully short and easy to understand standards document (no, I'm not kidding)
NIST defines cloud as access to a pool of computing resources that can be rapidly provisioned and made available with minimal overhead
posts.key_insight: When industry buzzwords become meaningless through overuse, returning to authoritative definitions like NIST's can restore clarity to technical discussions.

posts.summary: Continuous Delivery is the practice of building software that can be released to production at any time through extensive automation and collaboration. It requires maintaining deployable software throughout its lifecycle, using deployment pipelines, and fostering DevOps culture. The post distinguishes Continuous Delivery from Continuous Deployment—the former means you can deploy frequently, while the latter means every change automatically goes to production. Key benefits include reduced deployment risk through smaller changes, more believable progress metrics, and faster user feedback loops.
key_points: Software must be deployable throughout its lifecycle, with teams prioritizing deployability over new features
Continuous Delivery requires a deployment pipeline with automated testing and increasingly production-like environments
Continuous Deployment is a subset of Continuous Delivery—all Continuous Deployment requires Continuous Delivery, but not vice versa
DevOps culture must extend beyond developers and operations to include testers, database teams, and everyone involved in delivery
User feedback is the greatest risk mitigator, as it reveals whether you're building something actually useful
money_quotes: The key test is that a business sponsor could request that the current development version of the software can be deployed into production at a moment's notice - and nobody would bat an eyelid, let alone panic.
Your team prioritizes keeping the software deployable over working on new features
If 'done' means 'developers declare it to be done' that's much less believable than if it's deployed into a production (or production-like) environment.
The biggest risk to any software effort is that you end up building something that isn't useful.
Since you are deploying smaller changes, there's less to go wrong and it's easier to fix should a problem appear.
posts.key_insight: True readiness for production deployment—not just developer sign-off—is the only believable measure of software progress and the key to reducing both technical and business risk.

posts.summary: Infrastructure as code defines computing and network infrastructure through source code that can be version-controlled, tested, and delivered continuously. Fowler contrasts the old 'Iron Age' of months-long hardware provisioning with the 'Cloud Age' where servers spin up in seconds. The approach treats infrastructure configuration files as first-class software artifacts, eliminating manual server adjustments that create snowflake configurations. Key practices include using definition files exclusively, versioning everything, continuous testing, making small incremental changes, and maintaining service availability. This methodology becomes essential when adopting microservices at scale.
key_points: All infrastructure configuration should be defined in executable files (scripts, playbooks, manifests) with no manual server adjustments allowed
Version control enables auditability, reproducible builds, and the ability to diagnose and revert problematic changes
Small, frequent infrastructure updates are safer than large batches because errors are easier to detect and fix
Code-defined configuration eliminates snowflake servers with subtle differences that cause hard-to-debug faults
Infrastructure as code is a necessary capability for serious microservices adoption at scale
money_quotes: At no time should anyone log into a server and make on-the-fly adjustments.
This is a dynamic infrastructure where software commands are used to create servers, provision them, and tear them down, all without going anywhere near a screwdriver.
The bigger the infrastructure update, the more likely it is to contain an error and the harder it is to detect that error, particularly if several errors interact.

With manual provisioning different interpretations of imprecise instructions (let alone errors) lead to snowflakes with subtly different configurations, which often leads to tricky faults that are hard to debug.
Most importantly using configuration code makes changes safer, allowing upgrades of applications and system software with less risk.
When changing infrastructure FrequencyReducesDifficulty.
posts.key_insight: Treating infrastructure configuration as version-controlled, testable code eliminates the configuration drift and manual errors that plague traditional server management, making it essential for cloud-native and microservices architectures.

posts.summary: Reproducible builds are a foundational but often unstated assumption of Continuous Integration. While frequent releases with solid Continuous Delivery might seem to eliminate the need for reproducible builds, they remain essential for debugging scenarios where bugs cannot be reproduced on the current codebase. The ability to recreate past builds enables proper bug verification, ensures no tests are missing, and facilitates diff debugging to track down regressions. Even in modern release environments, reproducible builds provide critical debugging capabilities that forward-only releases cannot replace.
key_points: Reproducible builds are considered so obvious in CI that they're rarely explicitly stated as a key practice
Even with weekly releases and solid CD pipelines, reproducible builds remain valuable for debugging
When bugs can't be reproduced on head, you need to build the released version to properly understand and test the fix
Diff debugging uses reproducible builds to bisect between known-good and known-bad versions to isolate when a bug was introduced
Modern version control systems like Git and Mercurial have bisect features specifically to support this debugging approach
money_quotes: By this we mean that at any point you should be able to take some older version of the system that you are working on and build it from source in exactly the same way as you did then.
I think that's because it's an underlying assumption - one that's considered so obvious there's no need to explain it.
What happens when you get a bug report and you can't reproduce it. Do you just declare it fixed and move on? I wouldn't be happy with that response.
To be confident in reproducing the bug, I'd need to reproduce the build.
Furthermore even if I'm confident that the bug got fixed en passant during recent development, I'd still argue that there's at least one test missing.
Such bugs can hide a long time before they wake up and wave their feelers at you.
posts.key_insight: Reproducible builds aren't just for supporting old releases—they're essential debugging tools that let you verify bug understanding and use bisection to isolate when regressions were introduced.

posts.summary: This article traces how the programmer stereotype—antisocial, mathematically-inclined, white males—was artificially constructed rather than natural. Beginning with the ENIAC 'Computer Girls' who were the first programmers, the piece examines how 1960s hiring practices, particularly the IBM Programmer Aptitude Test and SDC's personality profiles emphasizing 'disinterest in people,' created a self-fulfilling prophecy. The author explores how this stereotype, combined with the 1980s home computer boom that favored boys, continues to filter out talented people who don't match the narrow image. Drawing from Nathan Ensmenger's 'The Computer Boys Take Over' and Margolis and Fisher's 'Unlocking the Clubhouse,' the article argues that the 'born to code' myth damages diversity and that teams should actively challenge these assumptions.
key_points: The first programmers were women ('Computer Girls') who invented fundamental practices like pair programming, but their work was dismissed as 'mechanical' rather than intellectual
In 1967 alone, over 700,000 people took the IBM Programmer Aptitude Test, creating industry-wide selection for a specific personality type
Psychologists in 1966 defined programmers as having 'disinterest in people,' which became a hiring template for the company that 'trained the industry'
The 1980s home computer boom coincided with a sharp decline in female CS students, as computers were disproportionately placed in boys' rooms
Research shows that believing abilities are fixed ('born for it') versus developable significantly impacts performance, especially for minorities facing stereotypes
money_quotes: The primary selection mechanism used by the industry selected for antisocial, mathematically inclined males, and therefore antisocial, mathematically inclined males were overrepresented in the programmer population

Betty Snyder and I, from the beginning, were a pair. And I believe that the best design and all that stuff is done by pairs because you can criticize each other, find each other's errors, and use the best ideas

If the ENIAC's administrators had known how crucial programming would be to the functioning of the electronic computer and how complex it would prove to be, they might have been more hesitant to give such an important role to women
If we are using a single model to identify potential programmers, we will miss many potential students
There are people who are born to do this, and I am not one of them. And it's definitely not one of those things that, like, 'Oh, with practice, you will become one who is born to do it'

It might be an innocent little comment that you don't mean anything by, but the person you are saying it to might be hearing this for the 500th time, and your comment might be the last straw to make them think that they indeed do not belong
posts.key_insight: The programmer stereotype of antisocial male geniuses was manufactured by flawed 1960s hiring practices, not natural selection, and continues to filter out talented people who don't match this arbitrary image.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler revisits the video store example from his 1999 refactoring book, this time implementing it in modern JavaScript ES6. He demonstrates how to break down a single long function into smaller, well-named functions, then explores four different architectural approaches for organizing the code to support multiple output formats (text and HTML): parameter dispatch, top-level functions, classes, and data transformation. The article shows that refactoring is about improving code in a direction that suits your team's coding style, and JavaScript's flexibility allows multiple valid approaches where Java would naturally push toward OO.
key_points: The first step in any refactoring of a long function is decomposition into smaller, well-named functions using Extract Method, Replace Temp with Query, and eliminating mutable variables

Four distinct organizational patterns emerge: parameter dispatch (format as argument), top-level functions (pure functions with explicit parameters), classes (common context via object instances), and data transformation (intermediate data structure)
Side effects in extracted functions make code hard to reason about - prefer returning values over modifying parent-scoped variables
Top-level functions suffer from repetitive parameter passing that obscures common context; classes and transforms make shared context explicit
Using literal values to dispatch to different behaviors is a code smell - let callers use named functions instead of recreating language dispatch mechanisms
money_quotes: Poorly factored code is a problem because it's hard to understand. Code that's hard to understand is hard to modify, whether to add new features or to debug.
Such side-effects make code hard to reason about, so I alter it so that it has no side-effects in its body.
Using literal values to indicate choice of function is a smell. Instead of this API, let the caller say what they want as part of the function name.
One of the difficulties in programming in JavaScript is that there is little consensus on what is good style.
Small functions, providing they are well-named, can be combined and manipulated to support varied needs both at the same time and over time.
Common contexts suggest grouping logic together, while much of the art of programming is deciding how to separate concerns into a clear set of such contexts.
posts.key_insight: Different programming styles (functional, OO, transformational) are often observably equivalent implementations of the same computation - understanding what unites them (small well-named functions, explicit common context, clear separation of concerns) matters more than dogmatic style preferences.

posts.summary: Activity-oriented teams organize around functions like development, testing, and UX design, promising benefits like standardization and skill development. However, this structure causes teams to optimize for their own metrics rather than delivering useful software. It also increases batch sizes between handoffs, lengthens feedback loops, and limits team autonomy. The post argues that outcome-oriented teams with cross-functional membership are more effective for modern software development, where market responsiveness matters more than cost efficiency. Communities of practice can provide the mentoring and standardization benefits without the organizational downsides.
key_points: Activity-oriented teams optimize for their own metrics (like velocity) rather than overall software value delivery
Separating activities increases batch sizes and lengthens feedback loops between teams
Specialization itself isn't problematic—organizing teams along lines of specialization is
Market responsiveness and time-efficiency matter more than cost-efficiency in today's business climate
Communities of practice can provide mentoring and standardization without activity-oriented reporting structures
money_quotes: Activity-oriented teams are prone to optimize for their own activity and not for the bigger picture of delivering useful software.
If they are only tasked with delivering scope, they will not think about whether it is going to solve the problems it was meant to.
Specialization isn't the problem; organizing along lines of specialization is.
A separate team of testers won't accept one story at a time from a team of developers. They'd rather test a release worth of stories or at least all stories in a feature at a time.
Activity-oriented organization may be attractive from the perspective of maximizing utilization but it comes in the way of maximizing value-addition and end-to-end responsiveness.
In today's business climate, market responsiveness (time-efficiency) is more important than cost-efficiency.
posts.key_insight: Organizing teams around activities like development or testing creates local optimization that undermines the cross-functional collaboration and fast feedback loops essential to effective software delivery.

posts.summary: Outcome-oriented teams are mandated and equipped to deliver business outcomes rather than just completing activities or delivering scope. Unlike activity-oriented teams that can only perform single specialized functions, outcome-oriented teams are cross-functional and self-sufficient. True outcome realization requires iterative collaboration between problem-understanders and solution-builders, which fails when product management is separated from development. While outcome orientation works better than activity orientation for large projects, the project-centric funding model limits its effectiveness by disbanding teams before outcomes fully materialize.
key_points: Business sponsors care about outcomes (lower effort, better conversion, customer satisfaction) not development metrics like velocity or deployment frequency
Outcome-oriented teams require cross-functional composition with all capabilities needed to deliver results, unlike mono-functional activity teams
Delivering scope is easy; delivering outcomes requires iterative collaboration where solution attempts improve problem understanding
Communities of practice with dedicated leads help mitigate the mentorship gap when people of the same specialty are distributed across teams
Project-centric funding undermines outcome orientation by disbanding teams before outcomes fully materialize in the business
money_quotes: People who sponsor development of software usually aren't very interested in development metrics such as velocity or frequency of deployment to production.
Scope delivery is easy, relatively speaking. Outcome realization requires real collaboration between those who understand the problem and those who can fashion various levels of solution for it.
Initial attempts at solution lead to a better understanding of the problem which leads to further attempts at better solutions.
This doesn't work where the product management organization is separate from the development (scope-delivery) organization.
A mandate to deliver a business outcome is very different from a mandate to deliver a certain amount of scope.
They are disbanded at the end of the project which means we lose sight of the outcome until another release in the same area is funded with another team.
posts.key_insight: True outcome delivery requires cross-functional teams with both the mandate and capability to iterate on solutions, which traditional activity-oriented and project-centric structures inherently prevent.

posts.summary: This brief, humorous post coins the term 'YAaaS' (Yet Another as a Service) as a meta-term for the proliferation of 'as a Service' terminology in the tech industry. Fowler credits colleague Birgitta Böckeler with the coinage. The post playfully demonstrates the term's usage by applying it to FaaS (Function as a Service).
key_points: The tech industry has spawned countless 'as a Service' terms requiring a meta-term to describe the pattern
YAaaS stands for 'Yet Another as a Service'
The term can be used to categorize new aaS offerings, e.g., 'FaaS is a YAaaS for Function'
money_quotes: These days everything seems to need to be 'as a service', so we need a meta-term for this linguistic trend.
FaaS is a YAaaS for 'Function'
posts.key_insight: The explosion of 'as a Service' terminology in tech has become so pervasive that it warrants its own self-referential meta-term.

posts.summary: This comprehensive article defines Serverless architecture as the combination of Backend as a Service (BaaS) and Functions as a Service (FaaS), where organizations reduce server management by leveraging third-party services and ephemeral, event-triggered compute containers. Fowler examines how Serverless shifts responsibility from central server orchestration to choreographed, distributed components, drawing parallels to microservices patterns. The piece methodically weighs benefits—including reduced operational costs, automatic scaling, faster time to market, and environmental efficiency—against significant drawbacks like vendor lock-in, cold start latency, testing complexity, and loss of server-side state. Fowler concludes that while Serverless isn't universally applicable, its reduced feedback loop for delivering new features represents its most compelling advantage for lean development approaches.
key_points: Serverless encompasses two distinct concepts: BaaS (third-party services for auth, databases, etc.) and FaaS (ephemeral, event-triggered function execution), with FaaS being the newer and more architecturally transformative approach
The shift to Serverless moves from orchestration to choreography—instead of a central server managing flow and security, distributed components become architecturally aware and self-managing
FaaS provides automatic, transparent horizontal scaling with pay-per-use economics, but requires externalizing all persistent state since function instances are ephemeral
Vendor lock-in is a legitimate concern but often overstated—the costs of multi-cloud abstraction frequently exceed the benefits of fully exploiting a single vendor's capabilities
Integration testing Serverless applications is significantly harder than traditional systems, and teams must embrace cloud-based testing rather than local simulation
money_quotes: Serverless is, at its most simple, an outsourcing solution.
If your PaaS can efficiently start instances in 20ms that run for half a second, then call it serverless.
Serverless doesn't mean 'No Ops'—though it might mean 'No sysadmin' depending on how far down the Serverless rabbit hole you go.
The sysadmin is still happening—you're just outsourcing it with Serverless.
With a Serverless approach we no longer make such capacity decisions ourselves—we let the Serverless vendor provision just enough compute capacity for our needs in real time.
While the cost benefits are the most easily expressed improvements with Serverless, it's this reduction in lead time that makes me most excited.
Serverless is not the correct approach for every problem, so be wary of anyone who says it will replace all of your existing architectures.
While there are riches—of scaling and saved deployment effort—to be plundered, there also be dragons—of debugging and monitoring—lurking right around the next corner.
posts.key_insight: Serverless architecture's greatest value lies not in cost savings but in dramatically reducing the feedback loop for deploying new application components, enabling continuous experimentation and lean product development.

posts.summary: This post defines serverless architectures as internet-based systems that replace traditional server processes with third-party services, client-side logic, and Functions as a Service (FaaS). The architecture shifts control flow from server-side controllers to rich client applications that coordinate multiple services via API calls. Custom code components have much shorter lifecycles, activating per-request rather than running continuously. While serverless offers cost savings for bursty traffic and easier scaling, it introduces conceptual overhead from service composition, harder local development and testing, and new operational challenges when third-party services experience outages.
key_points: Serverless replaces traditional servers with third-party services, client-side logic, and FaaS, shifting control flow to rich JavaScript or mobile applications
Custom code lifecycles shrink from long-running processes to per-request activation, enabling pay-per-use pricing models
Cost savings are most significant for applications with bursty traffic patterns that would otherwise require over-provisioned servers
The conceptual overhead of weaving applications from multiple services is significant and increases with service variety
Operations don't disappear—teams must still handle cascading impacts from third-party service outages, downtime, and slowdowns
money_quotes: In serverless applications, custom code components have a lifecycle that is much shorter, closer to the timeline of a single HTTP request/response cycle.
The code activates when a request arrives, processes the request and becomes dormant as soon as the activity dies down.
The cost of having a beefy server run cold the majority of the time in order to accommodate the bursts is both wasteful and expensive.
The conceptual overhead of splitting a single application into something that woven from a fabric of services is significant.
Local development and unit testing is also harder when applications have significant parts implemented and running in external services.
This doesn't mean teams can forget about operations. It still falls on development teams to deal with the problems caused by service outage, downtime, decommissioning and slowdowns.
posts.key_insight: Serverless architectures trade traditional server management for new complexities around service composition, testing, and third-party dependency management—operations shift but don't disappear.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler critiques Bimodal IT, a Gartner-promoted approach that divides systems into fast-moving 'systems of engagement' and slow, reliable 'systems of record.' He argues this distinction is fundamentally flawed for two reasons: first, rapid innovation in front-office systems inevitably requires changes to back-office systems too. Second, the approach falsely assumes quality must be traded for speed, when in fact Thoughtworks' experience shows high quality enables faster delivery with fewer defects. Fowler recommends instead organizing around business capabilities and distinguishing between utility and strategic functions.
key_points: Bimodal IT wrongly separates systems by technical role rather than business activity, ignoring that back-office changes are needed to support front-office innovation
The approach rests on the false premise that quality can be traded for speed, when evidence shows high quality actually accelerates delivery
Thoughtworks achieved both rapid feature delivery and dramatically fewer production defects using agile approaches
Organizations should instead take a business-capability-centric approach, distinguishing utility from strategic capabilities
money_quotes: Bimodal IT is the flawed notion that software systems should be divided into these two distinct categories for management and control.
You can't come up with clever pricing plans without modifying the systems of record that support them.
The bimodal idea is founded on the TradableQualityHypothesis, the idea that quality is something you trade-off for speed. It's a common notion, but a false one.
High quality (and low defects) are a crucial enabler for rapid cycle-time.
By not paying attention to quality, people following a bimodal approach will actually end up slowing down their pace of innovation in their 'systems of engagement'.
I find it hard to resist calling it 'Bipolar IT.'
posts.key_insight: Quality and speed are not trade-offs—high quality is actually a prerequisite for rapid innovation, making Bimodal IT's core premise fundamentally wrong.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that techniques and tools are often unfairly blamed for failures when the real problem is improper application. Using the metaphor of his mother's overcooked carrots, he explains how stored procedures blamed for version control problems or TDD blamed for brittle designs are examples of misuse rather than inherent flaws. He acknowledges the tension between this view and the 'no methodology has ever failed' defense, noting that techniques require skill and judgment rather than rigid adherence. The key is to dig deeper into reported failures to understand whether the technique itself was flawed or simply misapplied.
key_points: Techniques often fail not because they're inherently bad, but because they're applied incorrectly or incompletely
Some techniques are 'fragile' - harder to apply correctly, which legitimately limits their applicability to more skilled teams
When evaluating failures, ask whether context was appropriate, pieces were missing, or people focused on cargo-cult outward signs
There's tension between 'boiled carrot' thinking and the unfalsifiable 'it wasn't done right' defense of methodologies
Success ultimately depends on practitioner skill - techniques can inspire and guide but cannot guarantee success
money_quotes: Often a technique or tool is like the poor carrot - blamed for being awful when the real problem is that the technique is being done incorrectly.
If we discard them without taking their usage into account we lose useful tools from our toolbox.
Remember that software development is a very human activity, often techniques aren't suitable for a context because of culture and personality.
It's worth remembering Paracelsus's principle that the difference between a medicine and a poison is the dosage.

While some teams can develop components to careful specifications that can integrate together late in the day, in practice few teams are able to pull that off, and late integration ends up being like Fugu.
Such techniques aren't rigorously describable, just as Spaghetti Carbonara does not have one precise recipe that can be followed without thinking.
In the end what really counts is the dish, the technique to prepare it can inspire and guide a good chef but cannot guarantee success to someone without a certain degree of skill.
posts.key_insight: Before dismissing a technique as fundamentally flawed, investigate whether it was applied correctly, in the right context, and with all necessary supporting practices in place.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains aliasing bugs, which occur when multiple references point to the same memory location and unexpected mutations affect all references. He demonstrates the problem with a date example where modifying a party date accidentally changes a retirement date. Fowler distinguishes between reference objects (where sharing is intentional) and value objects (where sharing causes bugs). He advocates making value objects immutable to prevent aliasing bugs, while acknowledging that immutability introduces its own complexity when changes are genuinely needed.
key_points: Aliasing bugs occur when the same memory location is accessed through multiple references, causing unexpected shared mutations
The distinction between reference objects (deliberately shared) and value objects (should not share updates) is crucial for avoiding these bugs
Making value objects immutable is an effective way to prevent aliasing bugs
Functional languages prefer immutability by default, treating shared changes as the exception
While immutability prevents bugs, it introduces complexity when things genuinely need to change - it's not a free lunch
money_quotes: Aliasing occurs when the same memory location is accessed through more than one reference. Often this is a good thing, but frequently it occurs in an unexpected way, which leads to confusing bugs.
It's common to want to share records like this, and then if it changes, it changes for all references.
A good way to avoid shared updates of value objects is to make value objects immutable.
Immutability is a handy property, one that makes it harder to create several kinds of bugs.
But when things do need to change, immutability can introduce complexity, so it's by no means a free breakfast.
posts.key_insight: The key to avoiding aliasing bugs is distinguishing between reference objects (where sharing is intentional) and value objects (which should be immutable to prevent unexpected shared mutations).

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the concept of value objects—compound objects whose equality is determined by their property values rather than memory references. He contrasts them with reference objects (entities), demonstrates implementation across JavaScript, Java, and Clojure, and emphasizes that value objects should be immutable to avoid aliasing bugs. The post argues that while small objects like points and money are obvious candidates, larger structures can also benefit from value object treatment. Fowler concludes that value objects are often overlooked but can dramatically simplify codebases when properly identified and implemented.
key_points: Value objects are equal when their properties match, while reference objects are equal when they share identity (same memory reference or unique identifier)
Value objects should be immutable to prevent aliasing bugs where changes in one place have unexpected consequences elsewhere
Whether to model something as a value or reference object depends on context—a postal address might be a value object in one system and a reference object in another
Replacing primitive types with domain-specific value objects improves type safety, enables validation, and prevents inappropriate operations
Small value objects are often overlooked but can serve as powerful refactoring focal points that dramatically simplify systems
money_quotes: Value objects should be immutable.
I find that value objects, particularly small ones, are often overlooked - seen as too trivial to be worth thinking about.
It's often a good idea to replace common primitives, such as strings, with appropriate value objects.
I often run into code bases where domain-specific value objects like this can act as a focus for refactoring, leading to a drastic simplification of a system.
Whether to treat a concept as a reference object or value object depends on your context.
As with most modeling problems, different contexts lead to different solutions.
There is robust competition for the worst feature of the pre-Java-8 date and time system - but my vote would be this one.
posts.key_insight: Value objects—immutable compounds compared by their properties rather than identity—are deceptively simple yet serve as powerful refactoring leverage points that can dramatically simplify complex codebases.

posts.summary: Fowler breaks his rule against book reviews to recommend 'The Thrilling Adventures of Lovelace and Babbage' by Sydney Padua. The graphic novel combines entertaining steampunk alternate history with genuine Victorian-era computing history. After depicting the real collaboration between Ada Lovelace and Charles Babbage, the book enters a pocket universe where Lovelace survives and they complete the Analytical Engine. Fowler particularly praises the extensive footnotes that connect the fictional adventures to real history, and notes this is a book that works best in physical form.
key_points: The book effectively combines graphic novel storytelling with substantive historical information about early computing pioneers
It uses an alternate universe premise where Lovelace lives and the Analytical Engine gets built, enabling creative fictional adventures
The footnotes (with their own endnotes) provide the real historical context that makes the fictional elements meaningful
Fowler considers it ideal for his technical readership as an enjoyable diversion
The physical book format is essential to the reading experience
money_quotes: As a rule, I don't do book reviews. My main area of activity is software development, and I know too many authors.
It opens with a comic book narrative of the collaboration between Countess Lovelace and Charles Babbage, which explains why we refer to Countess Lovelace as the first computer programmer.
The book is imaginative, funny, and also very informative about the real Victorian universe.
All in all it's a great book for dipping in and out of, and I think most people who read my serious stuff will find it a most excellent diversion.
It's also a book that screams to only be a physical book.
posts.key_insight: A graphic novel about computing pioneers can be both genuinely entertaining and historically informative, making it an ideal diversion for technically-minded readers.

posts.summary: Hidden precision occurs when data contains more precision than expected, leading to subtle bugs. The post illustrates this with two common examples: date/time handling where creating a 'date' actually creates an instant at midnight, causing range checks to fail unexpectedly, and floating point arithmetic where values like 0.10 don't represent exactly, causing sums to miss expected thresholds. Fowler argues these issues stem from poor naming and modeling in APIs. The solution for money is to use integers representing the smallest currency unit, while floating point comparisons generally require explicit precision tolerances.
key_points: Date APIs often create instants (with time components) when you think you're creating dates, causing off-by-one-day bugs in range checks
Floating point numbers cannot exactly represent many decimal values, leading to invisible precision errors in calculations
Money should never be represented with floating point; use integers on the fractional value instead
Test framework assertions include precision parameters for floating point comparisons precisely because of these issues
Hidden precision problems stem from poor API naming and inadequate modeling of domain concepts like dates
money_quotes: Sometimes when I work with some data, that data is more precise than I expect. One might think that would be a good thing, after all precision is good, so more is better. But hidden precision can lead to some subtle bugs.
I didn't actually specify dates, but instants in time, so I'm not marking the end date as November 8th, I'm marking the end as the time 00:00 on November 8th.
Hidden precision is a common problem with dates, because it's sadly common to have a date creation function that actually provides an instant like this.
It's an example of poor naming, and indeed general poor modeling of dates and times.
You should be extremely wary of representing money with a floating point number.
The more general conclusion is that floating point is tricksy when it comes to comparisons.
posts.key_insight: When APIs provide more precision than their names suggest—dates that are really instants, decimals that are really approximations—the hidden precision creates subtle bugs that only surface at the worst times.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that the right question about function length isn't about line counts or reuse, but about separating intention from implementation. If you need to spend effort figuring out what code does, extract it into a well-named function. He advocates for very small functions—typically just a few lines—noting that half the methods in his own codebase are two lines or less. Performance concerns about function calls are largely outdated, and the approach only works with careful attention to naming.
key_points: The key principle is separation between intention and implementation—extract code when you have to think about what it's doing
Function names can be longer than their implementations and that's perfectly fine if it bridges the intention-implementation gap
Performance concerns about small functions are largely obsolete; modern compilers often optimize shorter functions better
Good naming is essential—without it, small functions don't work; with it, code becomes remarkably self-documenting
Fowler's own codebase shows half of methods are two lines or less, with functions over six lines being suspicious
money_quotes: If you have to spend effort into looking at a fragment of code to figure out what it's doing, then you should extract it into a function and name the function after that 'what'.
The name of the method was longer than its implementation—but that didn't matter because there was a big distance between the intention of the code and its implementation.
Any function more than half-a-dozen lines of code starts to smell to me, and it's not unusual for me to have functions that are a single line of code.
Small functions like this only work if the names are good, so you need to pay good attention to naming.
Larger scale functions can read like a story, and the reader can choose which functions to dive into for more detail as she needs it.
posts.key_insight: Extract functions not based on length or reuse, but whenever there's a gap between what code does and how it does it—then name the function after the 'what'.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares news about the second edition of the Dominion board game, which updates the base game and Intrigue expansion with replacement cards. He recommends new buyers get the second edition and existing owners purchase the $12 update packs while they're still available. The post provides practical purchasing advice and links to Amazon for the various editions and update packs.
key_points: Second edition replaces half-a-dozen cards in both the base game and Intrigue expansion
Update packs ($12 each) are available for existing owners but only for a limited time
New Intrigue is packaged as expansion-only without base cards
The second edition is considered an improvement based on Fowler's limited play
money_quotes: It's simple to learn, but has great variety and subtlety.
From my limited play the second edition is certainly an improvement, so get that.
The update packs will only be around for a while, until they feel most folks have upgraded, so I'd suggest grabbing them soon.
posts.key_insight: If you own or are buying Dominion, be aware of the second edition upgrade and grab the update packs while they're still available.

posts.summary: This comprehensive guide covers fundamental web security practices that every developer should implement. Fowler and colleagues argue that security is often deprioritized until breaches occur, yet most vulnerabilities stem from predictable, preventable mistakes. The article walks through essential defensive techniques: validating and rejecting unexpected input, properly encoding output to prevent XSS, using parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection, enforcing HTTPS everywhere, hashing passwords with proper salts and slow algorithms, implementing secure session management, and building robust authorization systems. The core philosophy is 'trust nothing'—every input from browsers, users, and even upstream services should be treated as potentially malicious.
key_points: Input validation should use whitelisting (positive validation) rather than blacklisting, rejecting unexpected data entirely rather than trying to sanitize it
Output encoding must be context-aware—HTML, JavaScript, and URLs each require different encoding strategies to prevent XSS attacks
SQL injection remains prevalent despite being easily preventable through parameter binding; stored procedures and ORMs don't automatically protect you
HTTPS should be used for everything, not just sensitive pages, and HSTS headers enforce this policy in browsers
Password storage requires slow hashing algorithms (bcrypt, scrypt) with unique salts per user—never store passwords in plaintext or use fast hashes like MD5/SHA
money_quotes: That is, until something goes wrong, until the system you build is compromised, then suddenly security is, and always was, the most important thing.

HTML forms can create the illusion of controlling input. The form markup author might believe that because they are restricting the types of values that a user can enter in the form the data will conform to those restrictions. But rest assured, it is no more than an illusion.
Remember: if you don't control it, you can't trust it. If it violates the contract, reject it!
The developer is always one errant angle bracket away from running in a very different execution context than they intend.
Injection into the database tier continues to plague the modern web application even though it's relatively easy to prevent!

There is no limit to what the attacker can do, including stealing the user's session or their personal information, injecting malicious code that will be executed by the browser in the context of the website, or altering data the user is sending to the server.
Among the most critical mistakes a programmer can make is hiding capabilities rather than explicitly enforcing authorization on the server.
posts.key_insight: Security is not a feature to be bolted on later but a fundamental discipline requiring systematic distrust of all external input and rigorous application of defensive coding practices at every layer of the stack.

posts.summary: Fowler presents Jez Humble's simple three-question test for genuine Continuous Integration, revealing that most teams who claim to practice CI actually don't. The test asks: does everyone commit daily to a shared mainline, does each commit trigger automated builds/tests, and when builds fail, are they fixed within ten minutes? At conferences, most hands go up initially but only a few remain after all three questions. Fowler argues that running CI tools on feature branches is 'CI theater' that misses the point—true CI is about keeping everyone's code synchronized, not about tooling.
key_points: True CI requires daily commits to a shared mainline, automated builds on every commit, and fixing broken builds within ten minutes or reverting
Most teams who believe they practice CI actually fail this basic three-question test
Running CI servers on feature branches is 'CI theater' that provides none of the actual benefits
CI was originally defined by Kent Beck as a human workflow practice, not a tool category
We should resist semantic diffusion by continually re-explaining the proper meaning of CI rather than abandoning the term
money_quotes: Continuous Integration—as it was originally described and named by Kent Beck as part of Extreme Programming—has nothing to do with tools.
The whole idea is that nobody is working on a code base that deviates significantly from anyone else's.
Running such a process otherwise, such as on every Feature Branch, is CI theater that debases the name.
If you can't fix and get a green commit build within ten minutes, then you should revert to the last green build.
We shouldn't give in to semantic diffusion, instead we need to keep working at re-explaining the proper meaning of Continuous Integration.
Continuous Integration means the team knows what the current state of the code truly is, we avoid big risky merges, and people can refactor as much as they need to.
posts.key_insight: True Continuous Integration is a discipline about keeping all developers synchronized on a shared mainline, not a category of tooling—and most teams who think they practice it actually don't.

posts.summary: Synthetic monitoring runs automated tests against live production systems on a regular schedule to detect business requirement failures in real-time. The technique addresses the challenge of testing microservices environments where pre-production cannot replicate exact production version combinations. By shifting focus from Mean-Time-Between-Failures to Mean-Time-To-Recovery, teams can extend testability into production. The article illustrates this with a car marketplace example running Selenium tests every 5 minutes against production, using parameters to exclude test traffic from analytics. Broad stack tests from the top of the test pyramid are well-suited for this approach.
