Fowler emphasizes that the key benefits mirror those of backend microservices: incremental upgrades, simpler codebases, independent deployment, and team autonomy. However, the pattern comes with tradeoffs including potential dependency duplication, environment consistency challenges, and increased operational complexity that organizations must be mature enough to handle.
key_points: Micro frontends solve the problem of scaling frontend development across multiple teams by decomposing monolithic frontends into independently deployable pieces
Build-time integration creates problematic coupling through lockstep releases; runtime integration (via JavaScript, iframes, or Web Components) is preferred
Cross-application communication should be minimized, with URL routing serving as a well-defined contract between micro frontends
Shared component libraries should be 'harvested' from proven patterns rather than built speculatively as a Foundation Platform
The architecture introduces tradeoffs: duplicate dependencies affecting payload size, development/production environment differences, and increased operational complexity
money_quotes: An architectural style where independently deliverable frontend applications are composed into a greater whole
In short, micro frontends are all about slicing up big and scary things into smaller, more manageable pieces, and then being explicit about the dependencies between them

We're not trying to exempt ourselves from thinking about our code and putting effort into its quality. Instead, we're trying to set ourselves up to fall into the pit of success by making bad decisions hard, and good ones easy

Just as with sharing a database across microservices, as soon as we share our data structures and domain models, we create massive amounts of coupling, and it becomes extremely difficult to make changes
Experience tells us that it's difficult, if not impossible, to guess what the components' APIs should be before you have real-world usage of them
The signature of this global function is the key contract between the container application and the micro frontends
When you choose micro frontends, by definition you are opting to create many small things rather than one large thing
posts.key_insight: Micro frontends enable frontend scalability through independent deployability and team autonomy, but require organizational maturity to manage the inherent tradeoffs of distributed architectures.

posts.summary: This post is an internal review of martinfowler.com's traffic analytics for 2014, examining which content performs best over time. Fowler introduces the concept of 'evergreen' articles—those that maintain consistent readership long after publication—and analyzes 86 URLs meeting this criteria. The microservices article he co-authored with James Lewis stands out as having catalyzed industry-wide interest in the term. Notably, infodecks (a content format he invested significant effort in) failed to produce any evergreen content, while long-form articles showed better staying power than short posts. The analysis reveals that word count shows no correlation with popularity, countering assumptions about modern attention spans.
key_points: Evergreen content (articles maintaining 1000+ views/month over time) is more valuable than articles that spike briefly at launch
The microservices article with James Lewis became the #1 article and demonstrably catalyzed industry interest in the term
Infodecks, despite significant investment, produced zero evergreen content—though sample size was small
Long-form articles have a higher proportion of evergreen success than short posts or bliki entries
Word count shows no correlation with long-term popularity, contradicting assumptions about short attention spans
Contributing authors and amplifying other voices is important, with 9 contributed articles making the evergreen list
money_quotes: It's easy to get too enamored of tracking numbers, but I find it useful to keep an eye on them, primarily to gauge what kind of material gets the most attention.
I suspect it is the articles that are both suitably aged and popular which are the ones that are most valuable. The hip term for these articles is 'evergreen'.

One, rather sobering, thing stood out to me from this analysis. Over the last couple of years I put a fair bit of effort into creating a new form of content: infodecks. I'd hoped they would provide an engaging medium that would help explain topics, yet not one of the evergreen 86 are infodecks.
I'm not claiming that all credit/blame for the microservices hype should be put at our feet.

That would lie in its longer term impact—primarily in how it puts down a reasonable definition of the term, which we hope will help reduce the semantic diffusion that gripped SOA so heavily from the start.

Editing articles like this is often considerable work, sometimes it feels like more work than writing them myself, but I think it's important for me to use the platform I have to help amplify other worthwhile voices.
posts.key_insight: True content value lies not in launch-day traffic spikes but in becoming evergreen—consistently useful reference material that maintains readership over years.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler presents a comprehensive traffic analysis of martinfowler.com for 2018, examining overall site growth, the concept of 'evergreen' articles that maintain sustained readership, and the relative success of articles written by guest authors versus himself. The site shows steady growth to over 8 million page views annually, with clear weekday/weekend traffic patterns. However, he notes a troubling plateau in new popular articles since 2016, coinciding with his focus on the refactoring book. The analysis reveals that guest-authored articles actually outperform his own on average, validating his editorial collaboration strategy. Many of the site's most popular pages are over a decade old, demonstrating the long-term value of quality technical content.
key_points: Site traffic grew steadily to 8.4 million page views in 2018, with weekday traffic roughly double weekend traffic
The number of 'evergreen' articles (sustained >1000 monthly views) plateaued around 2016 when Fowler shifted focus to book writing
Guest-authored articles outperform Fowler's own articles on average (median 586 vs 167 monthly views), validating the collaborative publishing model
38 of 103 evergreen pages were published over a decade ago, demonstrating the long-term value of foundational technical content
Article traffic follows a steep exponential decay after publication, making sustained readership the true measure of success
money_quotes: My goal is to write (and publish) articles that are useful to my readership over an extended period of time.
I feel I have a success when an article gets a sustained amount of traffic.
Notice that sharp exponential drop-off is visible even with a square root scale on the y axis!
I can conclude from this that working with other people to publish articles on martinfowler.com is worth the effort compared to focusing my energies solely on my own writing.
One of the important things to remember about this site is that many of the popular articles are older.
posts.key_insight: The true measure of technical writing success is not initial traffic but sustained readership over years, and collaborative publishing with other authors can be more impactful than solo writing.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces his passion for strategic hobby board games, distinguishing them from mass-market games like Monopoly. He maintains an active blog on BoardGameGeek called 'Martin's Seventh Decade' where he writes detailed reviews, session reports, and analytical pieces about modern board games. The page serves as an index to his extensive BoardGameGeek writing, covering topics from game reviews to theoretical discussions about game design concepts. His interests center on economic and route-building games like Brass, Age of Steam, and Power Grid, with particular attention to game mechanisms and design philosophy.
key_points: Strategic hobby games represent a fundamentally different category from mass-market board games, with thousands of new titles released annually
Fowler writes extensively on BoardGameGeek covering game reviews, monthly play reports, and analytical pieces on game design concepts
Key design concepts he explores include 'tightness' in games, victory point tyranny, player interaction types, and the distinction between setting and theme
His favorite games tend toward economic route-building games with strong historical themes, particularly the Brass family and Age of Steam
He attends dedicated gaming conventions and maintains detailed records of his plays and evolving game preferences
money_quotes: It's been many decades since I've played Monopoly, and I have no interest in ever playing it again.
Many people prefer games that are 'tight', but some games have such tightness in resources that a player can become 'wedged': unable to do any meaningful action.
Victory points are almost omnipresent in board gaming, yet often they encourage games to become unfocused.
I wonder if designers should strive to create victory objectives without them.
I like to follow Dan Thurot in making a distinction between setting (artwork and vocabulary of a game) and theme (the decisions and mindset).
Brass is a hugely successful family of games, and if forced to pick a favorite game, it's the one I'd pick.
Whenever I play a worker placement game, I submit it to the Caylus Test. Most games fail.
posts.key_insight: Strategic hobby board games represent a rich, serious intellectual pursuit distinct from mass-market games, worthy of detailed analysis and critical evaluation of their design mechanisms.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that lock-in is not a binary evil to be avoided at all costs, but a nuanced trade-off that architects must carefully evaluate. He identifies multiple dimensions of lock-in (vendor, product, version, architecture, platform, skills, legal, and mental) and demonstrates that open source is not a magic cure since it only addresses vendor lock-in. Using simple two-by-two matrices, he shows that lock-in can be acceptable when it provides unique utility, and that the cost of avoiding lock-in (complexity, effort, underutilization, new lock-ins) often exceeds the expected cost of the lock-in itself. The key insight is finding the optimal investment point where total cost is minimized, rather than striving for zero switching cost.
key_points: Lock-in has at least eight distinct dimensions: vendor, product, version, architecture, platform, skills, legal, and mental - open source only addresses vendor lock-in while leaving others intact
Acceptable lock-in exists when the unique utility gained justifies the switching cost - the goal is not to eliminate lock-in but to make conscious trade-offs
The total cost of avoiding lock-in includes effort, expense, underutilization of vendor features, added complexity, and often introduces new lock-ins
Minimizing switching cost is often not economical - if there's only a 5% chance of switching and the cost is $50k, investing more than $2,500 to reduce it is over-insurance
Mental lock-in is the most dangerous form because it invisibly constrains your thinking and causes you to reject viable alternatives based on outdated assumptions
money_quotes: Lock-in is far from an all-or-nothing affair, so understanding the different flavors can help you make more conscious architecture decisions.
Open source software isn't a magic cure for lock-in.
The more uncertain the environment, the more structured models can help you make better decisions.
A unique product feature doesn't always translate into unique utility for you.
Minimizing switching costs may not be the most economical choice.
Complexity can be the biggest price you pay for reducing lock-in.
The most subtle, but also the most dangerous type of lock-in is the one that affects your thinking.
Elevator Architects prefer to spend their time thinking over waiting for meetings.
posts.key_insight: Lock-in decisions should be evaluated using simple models that weigh switching cost against unique utility, and the cost of reducing lock-in against the probability-weighted cost of the lock-in itself - the goal is optimizing total cost, not eliminating lock-in.

posts.summary: This article extends Continuous Delivery principles to Machine Learning systems, addressing the unique challenges of managing code, data, and models as three axes of change. The authors define CD4ML as a software engineering approach where cross-functional teams produce ML applications in small, safe increments that can be reproduced and reliably released at any time. Using a sales forecasting application as an example, they walk through technical components including data versioning, reproducible model training with DVC, model serving patterns, testing strategies, experiment tracking with MLflow, deployment orchestration with GoCD, and production monitoring. The article emphasizes that while ML model outputs may be non-deterministic, the release process itself should be reliable and automated, creating feedback loops for continuous improvement.
key_points: ML systems have three axes of change—code, data, and models—each requiring different tools and versioning approaches, making traditional CD insufficient
Organizational silos between Data Engineers, Data Scientists, and ML Engineers create friction; cross-functional teams are essential for successful CD4ML
Model serving has three patterns: embedded models (simplest), model-as-service (independent deployment), and model-as-data (streaming/real-time scenarios)
Testing ML systems requires multiple strategies: data validation, contract tests for model interfaces, threshold tests for model quality, and bias/fairness checks
Production monitoring must capture model inputs, outputs, interpretability metrics, and user actions to close the feedback loop and detect training-serving skew
money_quotes: In real-world Machine Learning systems, only a small fraction is comprised of actual ML code. There is a vast array of surrounding infrastructure and processes to support their evolution.

A common symptom is having models that only work in a lab environment and never leave the proof-of-concept phase. Or if they make it to production, in a manual ad-hoc way, they become stale and hard to update.
The harder it is for Data Scientists to find the data they need, the longer it will take for them to build useful models.
While the model outputs can be non-deterministic and hard to reproduce, the process of releasing ML software into production is reliable and reproducible, leveraging automation as much as possible.
This makes the decision about when to release it a business decision rather than a technical one.

Closing this feedback loop is one of the main advantages of CD4ML, as it allows us to adapt our models based on learnings taken from real production data, creating a process of continuous improvement.
It is important to not just capture the input/output of a model, but also the ultimate decision taken by the consuming application to either use or override the model's output.
posts.key_insight: Continuous Delivery for Machine Learning requires treating code, data, and models as three distinct but interconnected axes of change, each needing specialized versioning, testing, and deployment strategies while maintaining the core CD principle that software should always be in a releasable state.

posts.summary: Fowler examines the origins and meaning of 'waterfall' in software development, tracing it to Winston Royce's 1970 paper while noting the term itself never appeared there. He defines waterfall's essential characteristic as decomposing effort into phases based on activity (analysis, design, coding, testing) rather than iteratively building features. Fowler argues waterfall is problematic because testing and integration—the hardest phases to predict—come last, forcing a predictive planning style that doesn't accommodate the inevitable requirement changes. He clarifies that while agile requires iteration, iterative development alone isn't sufficient for agility—adaptive planning and focus on business value are essential.
key_points: Waterfall's defining characteristic is breaking work into sequential phases by activity type, while iterative approaches complete all activities for one feature at a time
Late testing and integration phases in waterfall create unpredictable risks and make it difficult to assess true project progress
Waterfall assumes requirements are stable, but software projects invariably need significant changes within months as stakeholders learn more
Iterative development is necessary but not sufficient for agile—adaptive planning that embraces changing requirements is the key differentiator
Success measured by 'on-time and on-budget' indicates predictive thinking, while agile success is measured by business value delivered
money_quotes: You should use iterative development only in projects that you want to succeed
In the agile world, success is all about business value - regardless of what was written in a plan months ago

Anyone who says 'we were successful because we were on-time and on-budget' is thinking in terms of predictive planning, even if they are following an iterative process, and thus is not thinking with an agile mindset
It's too easy to declare all but the late phases as 'done', with much work missing, and thus it's hard to tell if the project is going well
Delivering a subset of features does more than anything to help clarify what needs to be done next
The notion that a phase should be finished before the next one is started is a convenient fiction
Waterfall means 'do one activity at a time for all the features' while iterative means 'do all activities for one feature at a time'
posts.key_insight: Waterfall's fatal flaw is not sequential phases per se, but the predictive planning mindset it enforces—assuming stable requirements in a domain where delivering working software is the primary mechanism for discovering what's actually needed.

posts.summary: Exploratory testing is a testing approach that emphasizes learning, discovery, and rapid iteration rather than following pre-written test scripts. While Fowler strongly advocates for automated testing, he argues that scripted testing—whether manual or automated—can only verify known conditions and cannot discover unknown behaviors. Exploratory testing complements automated tests by probing the boundaries of what's tested, finding failures that can be added to scripts and uncovering unexpected behaviors. The practice requires skilled, curious testers who can generate hypotheses and new tests during time-boxed sessions. Fowler considers it a red flag when teams rely solely on automated testing without any exploratory testing.
key_points: Scripted testing (manual or automated) can only catch bugs that testers already know to look for—it cannot discover unknown conditions
Exploratory testing is disciplined despite being fluid: time-boxed sessions with charters provide focus while allowing freedom to pursue discoveries
Teams can involve everyone in exploratory testing through pairing or mobbing, not just dedicated testers
Regular exploratory testing throughout development is recommended, with production bugs signaling potential gaps in test coverage
Even excellent automated testing is insufficient alone because it's inherently scripted
money_quotes: Scripted testing can only verify what is in the script, catching only conditions that are known about.
Such tests can be a fine net that catches any bugs that try to get through it, but how do we know that the net covers all it ought to?
Exploratory testing seeks to test the boundaries of the net, finding new behaviors that aren't in any of the scripts.
Exploratory testing requires skilled and curious testers, who are comfortable with learning about the software and coming up with new test designs during a session.
I would consider it a red flag if a team isn't doing exploratory testing at all - even if their automated testing was excellent.
Even the best automated testing is inherently scripted testing - and that alone is not good enough.
posts.key_insight: Automated testing, despite its power, shares a fundamental limitation with all scripted testing: it can only catch bugs you already know to look for, making exploratory testing an essential complement.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler recommends Heavy Cardboard, a media channel dedicated to complex board games run by Edward Uhler. The channel offers in-depth podcast reviews and live-streamed gameplay videos on YouTube, with Edward's rule teachings being particularly valuable for learning new games. Fowler became a patron and enjoys the patron-only Slack community. When Edward moved near Fowler's home in Massachusetts, Fowler began participating in the game group and appearing in videos and podcast episodes, including teaching Brass Birmingham and reviewing several games.
key_points: Heavy Cardboard provides deep reviews that help determine if complex games are worth trying
Edward Uhler's rule teachings before each stream are an effective way to learn games
The patron Slack channel is described as one of the most pleasant online communities Fowler has been part of
Fowler has become an active participant, appearing in videos and podcast reviews
money_quotes: I first ran into it when it was purely a podcast, enjoying it because it reviewed the kind of games I was interested in, and gave enough depth to the reviews that I could get a good picture of whether the game would interest me.
I also find Edward's teaching of the rules, which he does before every stream, an effective way to learn how to play a game.
It's a useful discussion forum and one of the most pleasant online groups I've been involved in.
I did do the teach for Brass Birmingham (a rather long teach as I added a lot of history and local color).
posts.key_insight: Quality media communities around niche hobbies can provide both practical value through in-depth content and social value through positive community spaces.

posts.summary: This comprehensive guide argues that pair programming—two developers working together at one machine—is a crucial practice for creating high-quality, maintainable software. The authors trace the practice from the ENIAC programmers through Kent Beck's formalization in Extreme Programming, then provide detailed guidance on styles (driver-navigator, ping pong, strong-style), logistics (time management, rotations, remote setup), and the broader concept of 'pair development' that extends beyond just coding. They acknowledge significant challenges including exhaustion, power dynamics, and organizational resistance, but argue these difficulties are worthwhile because working through them builds the very skills—communication, feedback, vulnerability, empathy—that make teams effective.
key_points: Pair programming encompasses multiple styles (driver-navigator, ping pong, strong-style) that should be adapted to the situation, not applied dogmatically
The benefits extend beyond code quality to include knowledge sharing, collective ownership, reduced work-in-progress, and faster onboarding
Power dynamics—both formal hierarchies and informal ones like junior-senior or gender dynamics—significantly impact pairing effectiveness and require conscious acknowledgment
Pairing should be the 'sensible default' rather than a rigid rule, with teams discussing exceptions for boring tasks or when junior developers need solo practice
The friction pairing creates is actually valuable because overcoming it builds the communication, feedback, and vulnerability skills that make teams resilient
money_quotes: For an idea to go from your head into the computer it MUST go through someone else's hands.
Pair programming does not happen in a space without hierarchies.
To pair requires vulnerability. It means sharing all that you know and all that you don't know.
Vulnerability is the birthplace of Innovation, Creativity and Change.
The advantage of pair programming is its gripping immediacy: it is impossible to ignore the reviewer when he or she is sitting right next to you.
Diverse Teams Feel Less Comfortable - and That's Why They Perform Better.
We prefer information that is more easily processed, and judge it to be more true, or more beautiful.
Pair programming is often avoided because it can create friction, but we would ask you to give it a chance.
posts.key_insight: Pair programming's challenges—exhaustion, power dynamics, vulnerability—are not reasons to avoid it but opportunities to build the communication and collaboration skills that distinguish highly effective teams.

posts.summary: Product-mode organizations with autonomous teams face unique challenges when initiatives require cross-team coordination (programs). Using a fictional fintech example, the article illustrates how preserving team autonomy at all costs led to unmanaged dependencies, poor communication, and missed delivery targets. The authors argue that programs require deliberate changes to operating models, including upfront kickoff workshops, adapted leadership styles, proactive dependency management through techniques like walking skeletons, and strong communication strategies. While this may temporarily challenge the autonomous culture, having a dedicated 'solution champion' role focused on strategic coordination—distinct from tactical delivery—is essential for program success.
key_points: Programs requiring cross-team coordination challenge product-mode organizations because teams optimize locally rather than for holistic customer value flow
Upfront investment in kickoff workshops that align stakeholders, define ways of working, and surface risks/dependencies is critical risk mitigation
Leaders may need to shift from empowering/delegating styles to more directive clarifying/defining approaches during program delivery
Walking skeletons can decouple team backlogs early, enabling autonomous delivery afterward and continuous integration throughout
A solution champion role focused on strategic coordination (not tactical delivery) should have explicit responsibilities distinct from product team roles
money_quotes: Programs — where delivering customer value demands orchestration across multiple teams — are a real challenge for product-mode organizations.
The delivery teams optimized locally rather than holistically for the flow of value to the customer.
Investing time upfront in a project kickoff like this is proper risk mitigation for a multi-month missed delivery date.

The intention isn't for the walking-skeleton to be used by a customer, but for the work that couples the product teams to each other to be mostly resolved before the teams proceed with delivery of their own backlogs.
Program leaders, acting like solution-champions, may find that they need to adapt their leadership style to the demands of the initiative.
No matter the mechanisms you choose, make sure you put in place a feedback loop with the program constituents, to learn and adapt whenever necessary.
posts.key_insight: Product-mode organizations must deliberately adapt their operating model and leadership approach when programs require cross-team coordination, accepting temporary constraints on team autonomy to deliver holistic customer value.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that software teams should be evaluated by outcomes (like increased sales or reduced support calls) rather than output (features delivered or code written). He contends that unused features represent not just wasted effort but actively harm the codebase by adding bloat. While acknowledging that outcomes are harder to apportion to specific teams, he maintains that measuring output is equally problematic and more importantly leads to optimizing for the wrong thing. The key insight is that focusing on outcomes changes what people think about and prioritize, regardless of measurement precision.
key_points: Output measures (lines of code, story points, features) don't matter if functionality doesn't help users improve their activity
Unused features are worse than wasted effort - they bloat the codebase and make future development harder
Measuring software output is famously difficult and easily gamed; outcome measurement is not inherently harder
An outcome should be a change in user/customer behavior that drives organizational benefit, distinct from broader 'impacts'
Even when apportioning outcomes to teams is difficult, focusing on outcomes changes what people think about and optimize for
money_quotes: Lots of unused features are wasted effort, indeed worse than that they bloat the code base making it harder to add new features in the future.
Lines of code are a useless measure even if they weren't so easily gamed.
The common phrase says 'you get what you measure', in this case it's more like 'you get what you try to measure'.
If you focus appraisal of success on output, then everyone is thinking about how to increase the output.
An outcome should be a change in behavior of a user, employee, or customer that drives a good thing for the organization.
Such a software development team needs to care about the usefulness of the new functionality, they improve as they deliver less features, but of greater utility.
posts.key_insight: Focusing teams on outcomes rather than output fundamentally changes what people think about and optimize for, which matters more than precise measurement.

posts.summary: Product-service partnerships between software vendors and service providers are ubiquitous arrangements where vendors build products while service providers handle installation, configuration, and customization. Product vendors avoid building their own services divisions due to lower margins, delivery risk, and revenue recognition complications. These partnerships involve dedicated organizational structures, certification requirements, and sometimes commissions, though they raise conflict-of-interest concerns when service providers give advisory recommendations. The rise of cloud computing has made such partnerships unavoidable even for custom software developers, creating new financial incentive complexities around usage-based pricing models.
key_points: Product vendors avoid building services divisions because services have lower margins, carry delivery risk, and create revenue recognition complications that delay software sale income
Large service providers maintain partnerships with multiple competing vendors, creating organizational divisions dedicated to each major product vendor
Certification programs, while often poor quality, serve as partnership metrics and revenue sources rather than true competence indicators
Size asymmetry in partnerships shifts the dynamic: larger partners seek exclusivity from smaller ones to gain competitive advantages or advocacy
Cloud computing has made vendor partnerships unavoidable for custom software development, with pay-for-usage models creating new incentive misalignments
money_quotes: That way, when things wrong, it's the service provider that carries the responsibility.
Nobody believes the go-to-market plan until the first deal is made.
As with so much in business, how smoothly the partnership works is primarily a function of the personal relationships of the leaders involved in the partner organizations.
This can lead to a surface competence, that in turn leads to a less effective delivery.

A wise service provider or product vendor realizes that to ensure long-term success for their work, they need to focus on improving the customer's performance in their work - not merely shipping product or selling people's time.
Any energy that either of the partners might invest in understanding customer outcome is reduced as the partners worry about managing the partnership.
posts.key_insight: Product-service partnerships optimize for output metrics and risk management rather than customer outcomes, and the rise of cloud computing makes navigating these arrangements an unavoidable concern for all software organizations.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reflects on Thoughtworks' experience responding to the early Covid-19 outbreak, sharing practical lessons from their China offices which were forced into remote-first operation. The company found that most teams adapted well, with 80% reporting equal or improved productivity when working from home. Fowler emphasizes that successful remote work requires proper infrastructure—good equipment, high-speed internet, collaboration tools, and security access. He argues that Thoughtworks' agile culture of embracing uncertainty and decentralized decision-making helped them cope effectively. The piece concludes with hope that the crisis will encourage more organizations to seriously adopt remote work and digital tools.
key_points: 80% of China-based teams reported equal or improved productivity when forced into remote-first work, though self-reported numbers should be treated with caution
Successful remote work requires practical infrastructure: decent desk and chair, high-speed internet, collaboration tools, and proper security access
Video calls became essential—audio-only calls now feel like 'going back to the Stone Age'
Country-by-country response teams worked better than global mandates, with experienced teams mentoring others
An agile mindset that embraces uncertainty in software development also helps organizations cope with real-world crises
money_quotes: I've got so used to doing video calls now that when another organization puts me on an audio-only call, it feels like we're going back to the Stone Age (or worse, the 1990's).

Software development is an activity that's mired in uncertainty, and our philosophy - to embrace that uncertainty and learn to cope with it (the agile mindset) - helps us when events like this appear in the wider world.
I always believed that coming into work when you're sick is unwise - you slow down your own recovery as well as infect others.
Cultivating trust and pushing decision making to those most in touch with the circumstances allows ThoughtWorkers at all levels to make better decisions.
Those that have lost productivity have mostly done so because they aren't set up for remote work.
posts.key_insight: Organizations built around agile principles—embracing uncertainty, decentralized decision-making, and trust—are better equipped to handle crises that force rapid operational changes like remote work.

posts.summary: The authors argue that software architects consistently overlook business value when making technical decisions, creating a major blind spot in architectural assessments. Through multiple case studies, they demonstrate how understanding revenue flows, failure costs, and business priorities should drive decisions about fault tolerance, microservice decomposition, and cross-functional requirements. They advocate for value stream mapping that extends into IT systems, tiered service levels based on business impact, and monitoring that captures business metrics alongside technical ones. The piece concludes that technical leaders must develop business acumen through ongoing interaction with business stakeholders, not just technical training.
key_points: Architecture teams typically don't know what business value their systems generate or how components contribute to overall business performance
Cross-functional requirements like resilience and flexibility should vary by component based on business value at risk, not follow blanket standards
Value stream mapping should extend through IT systems to understand how each component supports customer journeys and revenue generation
Feature-parity replacements during cloud migrations are often wasteful because much existing functionality delivers little value
Technical career development should include regular interaction with business leaders to understand value generation, not just technical training
money_quotes: The elephant that rarely comes up, however, is how different systems contribute to business value, and how this value interacts with these other architectural attributes.
When we do these assessments we usually ask to talk to the relevant people from the finance department. That's usually greeted by 'why would you want to talk to them?'
The trade off between consistency and availability, enshrined in the CAP theorem, is a business decision, not a technical one.
These are called 'soft' skills because they are harder than 'hard' skills.
A feature-parity replacement is harder than people usually think, and taking the time to avoid copying little-used functions pays for itself easily.
The barriers in communication between business and IT has been one of the sadly enduring themes of our long careers in software development.
Looking at the traffic revealed that so few citizens used such older browsers that rather than supporting the old browser, it would be cheaper to give them a new computer and a bunch of flowers.
posts.key_insight: Architectural decisions about resilience, flexibility, and system decomposition cannot be made soundly without understanding the business value each component generates and the cost of its failure.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler provides an update on how Thoughtworks is responding to the Covid-19 pandemic, outlining their governing priorities: employee health, client support, slowing community spread, and empowering regional teams. He describes the reopening of Chinese offices with safety measures like masks, temperature checks, and reduced capacity. The piece details how the company is adapting to remote work, including moving major events online and helping clients transition. Fowler reflects on the economic uncertainty ahead and how organizational resilience depends on decentralized decision-making and trust built during good times. He closes with cautious optimism that the world is finally taking the virus seriously enough to prevent worse outcomes.
key_points: Thoughtworks prioritizes flattening the curve not just for employee safety but as civic responsibility, recognizing that young healthy workers can still transmit to vulnerable populations
Remote work under crisis conditions risks giving a poor impression compared to well-implemented remote setups, potentially setting back acceptance of distributed teams
Organizations must balance maintaining controls against crisis flexibility - too rigid limits response effectiveness, too loose creates risk when absorption capacity is weakest
Trust and communication structures built during good times are investments that pay off during crises
Team partitioning (splitting into non-contacting sub-groups) provides resilience against infection spreading through an entire team
money_quotes: Even if the total numbers aren't reduced by a slower spread, it will reduce the strain on the health care infrastructure, giving those unlucky enough to suffer serious consequences a better chance of coping.
We think that tailoring local response to local conditions is more important than a globally consistent policy.

Involuntary remote working like this may show some people that remote working is more effective than they think. But there's also a risk - in these circumstances remote working is likely to be set up badly, and thus give a poor impression compared to how things can go when run well.

If an organization holds too firmly to its regular controls, it limits the effectiveness of its response to the novel situation. But if it lets them all go it exposes itself to large risks at exactly the time that its capacity to absorb the impacts is weak.
The work we do to develop trust and rich communication structures during good times is an investment we profit from in difficult times like this.
It's important not to overreact too much - this is no Spanish Flu or Black Death. But if we don't overreact a little, we'll see many more deaths and suffering than we should.
posts.key_insight: Organizational resilience in crisis comes from decentralized decision-making empowered by trust and communication networks built during stable times.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares practical guidelines for effective video calls based on Thoughtworks' transition from telephone to video meetings around 2011-2012, prompted by the Covid-19 pandemic forcing more people into remote work. The post emphasizes that audio quality matters more than video quality, and that seeing participants' faces and reactions is the key advantage of video over audio-only calls. He advocates for gallery view over speaker view, proper camera positioning, muting when not speaking, and using chat as a parallel communication channel. The advice balances professionalism with humanity, welcoming brief appearances by children and pets while maintaining meeting effectiveness.
key_points: Audio quality is more important than video quality - small audio glitches create serious barriers while video problems are usually manageable
Gallery mode is superior to speaker mode because the value of video is seeing everyone's reactions, not just the current speaker
Conference room video defeats the purpose of video calls by making faces too small to see properly - individuals should connect separately
Send presentation decks directly rather than using screen sharing so participants can control their own viewing while keeping faces visible
Standard meeting best practices apply: have clear purpose, agenda, introductions, breaks, and always question whether the meeting is needed at all
money_quotes: You can usually cope with problems in video, but even small audio glitches can be a serious barrier.
Unless there's a good reason, I'd consider it rude to not show video.
Gallery mode is much better here because the value of a video channel is to be able to see the faces of not just who's talking, but also the reactions of everyone else.
Our meatware visual systems are designed to react to movement, as that might be tasty food or a less-appealing predator.
Everyone knows we're at home, and providing it doesn't disrupt the call we shouldn't try to pretend we're in some clinical office.
If two people talk at once, then you can't hear either of them.
I've long lost count of the number of meetings that I think would be better served by a carefully-written memo.
posts.key_insight: The primary value of video calls over audio is seeing the reactions of all participants, not just the speaker, which requires gallery view and individual connections rather than conference room setups.

posts.summary: Pair Programming involves two developers working together at a single workstation, discussing each step as they write code. Fowler argues that the practice only seems wasteful if you believe typing is the hard part of programming. In reality, programming requires constant understanding-building and decision-making, where two minds work faster than one. While the intensive social contact may not suit everyone, many who expected to dislike it have come to appreciate it. The practice is a skill that can be done poorly, best learned from experienced practitioners.
key_points: The apparent 'waste' of two people on one task assumes typing is the bottleneck, when understanding and decision-making are the real challenges
Two programmers together often make faster progress and avoid analytical dead-ends that waste hours
Many people, including introverts, who expected to dislike pair programming came to love it after proper experience
Pair programming is a skill that can easily be done so badly it becomes counterproductive
money_quotes: But it's only a waste if the hardest part of programming is typing.
In reality programming is a constant sequence of building up your understanding of how the code is working and deciding how best to change it.
I certainly find I gain insight much faster with a second brain close at hand, and I'm far less likely to go down analytical rat-holes that can easily waste hours.
I've met many people, often including introverts, who thought they would dislike pair programming, but came to love it once they had experienced it properly.
Pair programming is a skill, and it's certainly easy to do it so badly that it becomes a Boiled Carrot.
posts.key_insight: Pair programming's value lies in accelerating understanding and decision-making, not doubling typing speed.

posts.summary: Clare Sudbery walks through a real-world refactoring of a personal accounting application where the ReconciliationIntro class had grown too large to reason about. The article demonstrates how to break a bloated class into smaller, focused classes (FileLoader, BudgetingMonthService, Communicator, PathSetter, DebugModeSwitcher) using tiny, incremental steps that keep tests passing at all times. The key insight is that refactoring neglect creates a vicious cycle where code becomes increasingly difficult to modify, and the solution is methodical extraction following Kent Beck's advice to 'make the change easy, then make the easy change.'
key_points: Large classes create a feedback loop: code that's hard to reason about discourages refactoring, making it even harder to modify over time
Moving in tiny steps is the most important refactoring principle - keep code compiling and tests passing after every small change
Before extracting a new class, analyze method relationships with diagrams to identify dependencies and plan the extraction order
When moving methods, start with the lowest leaves in the call hierarchy (methods with no internal dependencies) and work upward
Refactoring doesn't require writing new tests - existing tests verify functionality still works, and you simply move them to new test classes
money_quotes: In these circumstances it's horribly easy to think things like, 'Well I know this code is a mess, but I don't have time to get my head round it and fix it now...'
It's horribly easy to embark on a relatively simple refactor, only to find it has repercussions beyond your original intention.
'Fits In My Head' is one of the patterns in Dan Terhorst-North's Software, Faster book. He talks about the importance of being able to comprehend any chunk of code by 'fitting it in your head.'
As Kent Beck says, 'Make the change easy, then make the easy change.'
People can get so used to the TDD concept of writing tests before writing code that they assume you need to write new tests whenever you work on code. This is often not the case when refactoring.
By extracting this code into a separate class and giving it its own clear context, I'm already making this code a bit better.
Often what I'm doing is setting things up so that the next change will be as small and simple as possible. I'm making small changes to facilitate more small changes.
posts.key_insight: Refactoring large classes requires moving in tiny incremental steps that keep tests passing, starting by analyzing method relationships and extracting from the bottom of the call hierarchy upward.

posts.summary: Domain-Driven Design is a software development approach that builds rich domain models embedding business processes and rules directly into code. Originated by Eric Evans in his 2003 book, DDD provides a vocabulary for discussing domain modeling, including concepts like Ubiquitous Language, Bounded Contexts, and the Evans Classification of objects. While domain modeling existed before, Evans's contribution was creating a systematic framework and emphasizing that models should live in software and evolve over time, not just exist on paper. The approach is particularly valuable for complex domains and aligns naturally with Extreme Programming practices.
key_points: DDD's core innovation was providing vocabulary and conceptual framework for domain modeling, not inventing domain modeling itself
Ubiquitous Language embeds domain terminology directly into software systems, bridging business and technical understanding
Strategic Design addresses how to organize large domains into networks of Bounded Contexts - a problem previously unsolved
DDD emphasizes models should exist in code and evolve during product lifecycle, not be upfront paper exercises
The conceptual nature of DDD makes it applicable beyond object-oriented programming to any development approach
money_quotes: Eric Evans's 2003 book is an essential read for serious software developers
To develop software for a complex domain, we need to build Ubiquitous Language that embeds domain terminology into the software systems that we build

While many folks talked about developing such models, they were often only done on paper, and usually expected to be done up-front. DDD stresses doing them in software, and evolving them during the life of the software product
Eric is a strong proponent of Extreme Programming and sees Domain-Driven Design as a natural component of an extreme programming approach

A particularly important part of DDD is the notion of Strategic Design - how to organize large domains into a network of Bounded Contexts. Until that point, I'd not seen anyone tackle this issue in any compelling way
The core notions of Domain-Driven Design are conceptual, and thus apply well with any programming approach
posts.key_insight: Eric Evans's lasting contribution to software development was not inventing domain modeling, but creating a shared vocabulary and conceptual framework that moved domain models from paper artifacts into living, evolving code.

posts.summary: Dark launching is a deployment technique where new back-end functionality is called from production user flows without exposing results to users, allowing teams to assess performance and load impacts before public release. The approach integrates with Keystone Interface patterns to build features invisibly in production, while Feature Flags enable quick rollback if problems emerge. Dark launching also enables parallel running of reimplemented features to verify correctness before switching. The technique works best for features that enhance existing interactions rather than user-initiated actions, where Canary Releases are more appropriate.
key_points: Dark launching calls new back-end code in production without showing results to users, measuring real performance impact
Combines with Keystone Interface (build without UI) and Feature Flags (quick on/off) for safe production testing
Enables parallel running of old and new implementations to verify correctness while only returning one result
Best suited for features that enhance existing flows, not user-initiated actions where Canary Releases fit better
The term has suffered semantic diffusion and is now often confused with canary releases and other partial release strategies
money_quotes: Dark launching a feature means taking a new or changed back-end behavior and calling it from existing users without the users being able to tell it's being called.
This way the recommendation does all the work it would do when it's released, but nobody can see that it's doing it.
If we use a Feature Flag we can switch the recommendation on and off easily in production, so if we do see an worrying impact on performance we switch it off before anyone really notices.
Dark launching can also enable parallel running of a re-implemented feature.
Dark launching works best when it's a process that enhances existing user interactions and isn't something users choose to do.
posts.key_insight: Dark launching provides a risk-mitigation strategy for assessing real production impact of new features by executing them invisibly alongside existing user flows before public release.

posts.summary: The Humble Object pattern addresses the challenge of testing program elements that are inherently difficult or impossible to test automatically. By moving as much logic as possible out of hard-to-test elements into more testable parts of the codebase, developers reduce the risk of bugs hiding in untestable code. User interfaces are a prime example where this pattern applies, as UI controls often lack testing hooks or require complex setup. Patterns like Presentation Model (MVVM) and Passive View implement this principle by keeping UI controls behavior-minimal.
key_points: Some program elements are inherently untestable, making any logic within them prone to bugs and difficult to evolve
The solution is to move logic out of hard-to-test elements into more testable parts of the codebase
User interfaces are a common example where the Humble Object pattern applies effectively
Patterns like MVVM and Passive View implement this principle by minimizing UI control behavior
money_quotes: Some program elements are inherently difficult, or even impossible to test.
Any logic in these elements is thus prone to bugs and difficult to evolve.
By making untestable objects humble, we reduce the chances that they harbor evil bugs.
We can often test effectively by ensuring these controls have the absolute minimum of behavior.
posts.key_insight: Untestable code becomes safer when you minimize its logic, pushing complexity into testable components where bugs can be caught.

posts.summary: The Keystone Interface technique addresses the tension between frequent integration/deployment and not exposing incomplete features to users. Teams build and integrate all back-end code through multiple production releases while withholding only the user interface element that reveals the feature. This approach requires architecture that supports subcutaneous testing, allowing thorough validation without the UI. When the UI can't be reduced to a simple keystone, feature flags can be used, but should be limited to the UI layer to reduce complexity. The technique works best within an overall approach of building products through thin vertical slices.
key_points: Build and integrate back-end code across multiple releases while hiding features by withholding only the UI element that exposes them
Testing latent code requires architecture supporting subcutaneous tests - tests that don't go through the UI but exercise the same functionality
Moving tests to lower levels of the test pyramid speeds up deployment pipelines and enables continuous delivery
When the UI can't be a simple keystone, use feature flags but limit them to the UI layer to avoid scattering toggle points through back-end code
The approach risks back-end code not fitting the UI properly; mitigate by building products through thin vertical slices
money_quotes: Software development teams find life can be much easier if they integrate their work as often as they can.
The feature can be integrated and tested, but the UI is held back until the end until, like a keystone, it's added to complete the feature, revealing it to the users.
The user is unaware of all this latent code.
The latent code does need to be tested to the same degree of confidence that it would be if it were active.
Tests running through the UI are always more trouble to setup, even with the best tools to automate the process.
This avoids scattering lots of toggle points through the back end code, reduces the complexity of applying the toggle, and makes it easier to remove when the time comes.
A keystone approach works best within an overall approach that encourages building a product through thin vertical slices that lead to releasing small but fully working features rapidly.
posts.key_insight: By treating the user interface as the final keystone that reveals a feature, teams can continuously integrate and test complex back-end functionality without exposing incomplete work to users.

posts.summary: Feature branching is a source code pattern where developers work on isolated branches until a feature is complete before merging. While popular in open-source development, this isolation prevents early problem detection and discourages refactoring, which can deteriorate codebase health. The impact depends heavily on feature completion time—teams finishing features in days avoid most problems, while those taking weeks or months face significant integration difficulties. Fowler positions this as a trade-off between merge risk deferral and the benefits of continuous integration.
key_points: Feature branches defer all merge risk until feature completion, which sounds beneficial but prevents early problem detection
The isolation actively discourages refactoring, leading to codebase health deterioration over time
Integration frequency matters more than the branching strategy itself—short-lived branches (days) avoid most problems
The pattern is well-suited to open-source development but has significant trade-offs for team-based development
money_quotes: This isolation does prevent early detection of problems.
More seriously, it also discourages refactoring - and a lack of refactoring often leads to serious deterioration in the health of a codebase.
Two people, working on different feature branches, do not integrate their work until the second one merges their work into the common codebase.
A team that typically completes features in a day or two are able to integrate frequently enough to avoid the problems of delayed integration.
Teams that take weeks, or months, to complete a feature will run into more of these difficulties.
posts.key_insight: Feature branches trade early problem detection and refactoring opportunities for deferred merge risk, making their impact directly proportional to how long features take to complete.

posts.summary: This comprehensive article examines source code branching patterns, arguing that while branching is easy, merging is the hard part that teams must plan for carefully. Fowler presents two main categories of patterns: integration patterns (how developers combine work) and production path patterns (how code reaches deployment). He contrasts Feature Branching with Continuous Integration, presenting research evidence that high-frequency integration correlates with higher software delivery performance. The article advocates for understanding trade-offs in context rather than following any single approach dogmatically, while noting that teams using Continuous Integration typically achieve better outcomes through smaller merges and faster conflict detection.
key_points: Branching is easy but merging is harder - the cost of branching is paid at merge time, and this cost grows exponentially with time between integrations
High-frequency integration (Continuous Integration) reduces merge complexity and risk compared to Feature Branching, with scientific evidence from the State of DevOps Report supporting this
A healthy mainline with self-testing code is foundational - it enables developers to start work from a stable base and supports direct releases to production
Pre-integration reviews add friction that discourages frequent integration; pair programming and refinement reviews offer alternatives with faster feedback
Modularity and refactoring support each other with high-frequency integration - feature branching often discourages the refactoring needed to maintain good architecture
money_quotes: Branching is easy, merging is harder.

Feature Branching is a poor man's modular architecture, instead of building systems with the ability to easy swap in and out features at runtime/deploytime they couple themselves to the source control providing this mechanism through manual merging.
Integration is both a pull and a push - only once Scarlett has pushed is her work integrated with the rest of the project.
If it hurts - do it more often.
A source control system is a communication tool.
Pull requests add overhead to cope with low-trust situations. Imposing pull requests on devs in your own team is like making your family go through an airport security checkpoint to enter your home.
The difference between a beneficial drug and a poison is dosage.
posts.key_insight: The frequency of integration is the most critical decision in branching strategy - higher frequency reduces merge risk, enables refactoring, and correlates with better software delivery performance.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reviews the Kinesis Advantage2 ergonomic keyboard after three and a half years of daily use. He explains that while the keyboard is marketed for RSI prevention, he bought it purely for typing comfort rather than to address any current injury. The standout features include thumb clusters replacing the spacebar, foot pedals for modifier keys, and extreme customizability. Despite a steep learning curve in the first week, he found the transition worthwhile and can still switch between the Kinesis and regular keyboards without difficulty. He considers the $300+ investment well worth it for something used all day.
key_points: The thumb clusters replace the spacebar with multiple useful keys (return, backspace, space, delete), making better use of the strongest digits
Foot pedals for modifier keys like CTRL and Command reduce hand tangling in awkward key combinations
The concave key layout and unusual positioning required about a week of frustrating adjustment before becoming natural
Switching back to regular keyboards remains easy despite extended Kinesis use
The keyboard's extensive customizability is largely redundant for Emacs users who already have infinite customization options
money_quotes: $300 is rather a lot to pay for a keyboard, but as it's something I'm using all day when I'm at home, I'm happy to pay for something comfortable.
On a regular keyboard the only thing your thumbs do is insert a space. While that's a common occurrence, it seems a shame to have two of your strongest digits only do that one thing.
I'm sitting at my desk all day, why shouldn't I use my feet?
I haven't used any of this, since my editor is Emacs, and thus I already have as much customizability as anyone could ever need.
The first week was very frustrating - I certainly wasn't used to getting confused between typing a space and a carriage return. But after that week, things were mostly ok, and I steadily got better.
posts.key_insight: When you use a tool all day every day, investing significantly more money for genuine comfort improvements is a sound economic decision.

posts.summary: Fowler reviews 'Coup 53,' a documentary about the 1953 CIA-backed coup that overthrew Iran's democratically elected Prime Minister Mohammad Mosaddegh. The film combines historical narrative with a meta-documentary about finding a lost interview with British intelligence officer Norman Darbyshire, who claimed Britain played the leading role in the coup. While Fowler notes the documentary's reluctance to question Darbyshire's potentially ego-driven account, he finds the core historical story compelling and important. The review emphasizes the coup's lasting consequences: Iran lost a democratic leader and gained decades of autocracy, while America gained lasting hostility and established a pattern of supporting covert regime change.
key_points: The 1953 coup against Mosaddegh, who had nationalized Iran's oil industry, set the tone for US covert action and power projection for decades
The documentary features a meta-narrative about finding a lost interview with British spy Norman Darbyshire, who claimed Britain planned and ran the coup rather than the CIA
The filmmakers recreated the lost interview using Ralph Fiennes reading from transcripts, but failed to critically examine whether Darbyshire's account was accurate or self-aggrandizing
The coup's consequences were severe: Iran lost democratic governance, America gained 26 years of friendly tyranny followed by 40+ years of hostile tyranny
The film raises counterfactual questions about how different the Middle East might be had the US supported rather than overthrown Iranian democracy
money_quotes: What is often forgotten is the roots of that conflict go back to American actions, when in 1953 they drove a coup d'état to overthrow Prime Minister Mohammad Mosaddegh.
Most accounts of the coup have it ran by the CIA, but Darbyshire revealed that it was fact the British who planned and ran the coup.
Although his account is plausible, it's equally plausible his ego is speaking, puffing up his own role in the events.
Iran saw the loss of a leader who advocated an open democratic society, and since then has been ruled by autocrats, a huge loss for the Iranian people.
America gained a friendly brutal tyrant for 26 years, followed by a very unfriendly brutal tyranny for over forty years.
It sent a signal to the Middle-East and the world that the United States was keen on covert action to support friendly tyrants.
They raise the prospect of an alternative history where the United States dealt with Mosaddegh as a proper ruler, perhaps encouraging a worthwhile democracy.
posts.key_insight: The 1953 Iranian coup, though not widely known in the West, established a pattern of American covert intervention that shaped Middle Eastern politics for generations and continues to fuel hostility today.

posts.summary: The post argues that comparing groups of data using only averages (means) is dangerously misleading because it hides crucial information about the underlying distribution. Using a sales bonus decision as an example, Fowler demonstrates how one executive's seemingly superior 7.9% average actually stems from a single 80% outlier while all other accounts shrank. The article presents several visualization alternatives—strip charts, histograms, density plots, box plots, and violin plots—that reveal the true shape of data distributions. Fowler advocates for always examining distributions before drawing conclusions and suggests median is often more appropriate than mean for central tendency.
key_points: Averages are extremely vulnerable to outliers—one Bill Gates makes a hundred homeless people average billionaires, one hot account can mask overall poor performance
Two groups with identical averages can have completely different distributions (consistent vs bimodal performance)
Strip charts show every data point and work well for smaller datasets; adding a mean marker combines distribution visibility with summary statistics
Box plots excel at comparing many groups by showing quartiles, medians, and outliers in a compact form that summarizes thousands of data points
Tooling bias perpetuates the problem—SQL has AVG built-in but typically lacks easy median functions, encouraging mean overuse
money_quotes: Remember the average net worth of a hundred homeless people is $1B once Bill Gates enters the room.
My disdain is for those who *only* use means, or use them without examining the overall distribution.
Whenever you see an 'average', you should always consider which is better: median or mean?
If some day I become supreme leader, I will decree that no platform can have a mean function unless they also supply a median.
There's no point eagerly collecting the data you need to be a data-driven enterprise unless you know how to examine that data properly.
Every kind of chart illuminates different features of a dataset, and it's wise to use several to get a sense of what the data may be telling us.
posts.key_insight: Always visualize the underlying distribution before comparing groups by averages, because summary statistics can mask critically important patterns like outliers, bimodal distributions, and inconsistent performance.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler makes a direct political appeal urging Americans to vote against Donald Trump in the 2020 election. He argues that Trump's pattern of lies, blocking of oversight, and authoritarian tendencies pose a fundamental threat to democracy. Fowler emphasizes that victory must be by a substantial margin to be undeniable and to signal to the Republican Party that supporting demagogues is electorally catastrophic. He points to the COVID-19 death toll as concrete evidence of the cost of incompetent leadership, comparing U.S. deaths unfavorably to Germany's. The post concludes with a call to vote against both Trump and his enablers as a repudiation of authoritarianism.
key_points: Trump's shameless lying marks him as a dangerous demagogue threatening democratic systems
The margin of defeat must be large enough to be undeniable and to send a message to the Republican Party
COVID-19 deaths demonstrate the real-world cost of political incompetence—170,000 excess American deaths compared to German outcomes
Every citizen must have a concrete voting plan, especially given potential obstacles to voting
Voting against Trump's enablers is equally important, even in safe seats where margin changes send messages
money_quotes: His shameless lying was the sign of a dangerous demagogue who would damage our democratic system.
The margin of victory must be beyond even his capacity for lying.
The Republican Party has got itself hooked onto the crack-cocaine of Trumpism.
The cost of incompetence in the U.S. is 170,000 lives.
For non-activists like myself, it's easy to look on political events as some sporting event, one that doesn't have much effect on our everyday lives.
Authoritarianism is a disease that's haunted humanity since history started to be written.
This country was founded to oppose this disease and, despite its many faults, that vision is still one to cherish.
posts.key_insight: Democratic institutions require active defense through voting, and the 2020 election represents a critical moment to repudiate authoritarianism with an undeniable margin.

posts.summary: Fowler describes writing a Google Apps Script for a non-programmer friend to correlate data from Slack and Patreon. He argues that despite programmers' disdain for spreadsheets, Google Sheets provides an excellent deployment platform for simple scripts because users already know the interface, sharing is built-in, and no installation is required. The main technical challenges were OAuth authentication (which he mostly sidestepped) and poor documentation. He advocates for treating spreadsheet scripts with the same design discipline as regular code, separating data sheets from logic and avoiding cell-level manipulation in favor of JavaScript data structures.
key_points: Google Sheets is an underappreciated deployment platform for simple scripts aimed at non-programmers who are uncomfortable with command lines
Authentication with OAuth remains unnecessarily complex even for simple use cases—Google could simplify this dramatically
Spreadsheet scripts benefit from the same design principles as regular code: separating raw data, application logic, and output into distinct sheets with one-way data flow
Performance gotcha: appendRow is extremely slow; use Range.setValues with a defined range for acceptable speed
API designers should always support batch lookups, not just single-ID queries
money_quotes: Spreadsheets are often coerced into tasks that they are ill sorted for, often acting as cobbled together databases, tables whose relationships are a web of tangled VLOOKUPs.
But these spreadsheets are widely used for a good reason. The tabular metaphor is a simple yet effective way to handle structured data.

There is a real opportunity here for Google to simplify the whole authentication flow. I should be able to just call a fetch method on a remote service, and have the infrastructure sort out the authentication flows without me having to investigate and program them myself.
That's about the best thing I can say about it.
Should I get run over by a bus, he can easily share the sheet with someone else, who can easily edit and run the sheet.
It's not the comfortable home that I'm accustomed to, but it's fine for an overnight stay.
In particular, for any task that would be a simple shell script, but your users aren't comfortable with console windows and text files.
posts.key_insight: Google Sheets scripts deserve consideration as a legitimate deployment platform for simple automation tasks, especially when your users aren't programmers—the familiar interface and built-in sharing outweigh the crude development environment.

posts.summary: In response to social media platforms blocking an unverified political story during the 2020 election, Fowler proposes a middle ground between full blocking and unrestricted sharing: temporary dampening. He argues that sensational stories spread faster than investigations can verify them, creating asymmetric harm. Rather than asking 'is this true,' platforms should ask 'can we tell yet if this is a damaging lie?' This approach acknowledges uncertainty rather than demanding binary decisions, and weighs short-term virality against long-term truth.
key_points: Social media platforms face a different editorial challenge than newspapers—they must decide how much to reduce human curation while still preventing harm
The speed asymmetry between lies spreading and truth emerging justifies temporary intervention
Platforms should evaluate story provenance and how other media organizations are reacting before amplifying
Context matters: stories breaking near elections or those that could incite violence warrant extra caution
People's discomfort with probabilistic reasoning leads to false demands for certainty when uncertainty is the honest position
money_quotes: A lie can travel halfway around the world before the truth can get its boots on.
Instead of asking 'is this true', they ask 'can we tell yet if this is a damaging lie?'
There's a desire for certainty which is really a failure to face up to the reality that there is so much we don't know.
If the picture is sufficiently uncertain, then there is justification for blocking it provisionally.
It's perfectly reasonable to wait for more information before giving it the powerful amplification that a popular social network delivers.
A story breaking just before an election, that is likely to change votes, is one to be careful of because any refutation could easily come too late.
posts.key_insight: When facing unverified but potentially damaging stories, social media platforms should embrace uncertainty by temporarily dampening spread rather than making premature binary decisions about blocking or allowing.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler recounts the death of his wife Cindy's cat, Goldman Sachs (named for her impressive girth), who was killed by coyotes in their garden. Rather than a straightforward account, Fowler weaves an elaborate fantasy narrative where the cat's demise was orchestrated revenge by a widowed cardinal whose mate Goldie had killed. The piece blends genuine memoir about pet ownership, suburban wildlife, and loss with whimsical storytelling that anthropomorphizes the local animals into conspirators. It's a meditation on nature's violence—how predators become prey—wrapped in dark humor and delivered with Fowler's characteristic wit.
key_points: The cat Goldman Sachs was named for her enormous size (nineteen pounds) and successfully slimmed down under portion control
Goldie killed Mrs Cardinal, leaving Mr Cardinal searching futilely for his lost mate
The narrative imagines an elaborate revenge plot where the cardinal recruited coyotes and chipmunks to eliminate the cat
The piece explores the violence inherent in nature, noting how 'our feline predator was quickly turned into prey'
Despite the dark subject matter, the tone remains playful and self-aware about its own anthropomorphization
money_quotes: A state of nature is a state of violence, and our feline predator was quickly turned into prey.
Cindy, however, came complete with cats, and for several years I knew I could never rise above #2 in her affections.
Her old name was something boring, and being a cat she never deigned to recognize it.

She soon learned that outdoors was a great source of toys, which she could bring into the house and play with, although their batteries soon ran out and she would leave them for the staff to clean up.
It's wrong to anthropomorphize wild animals, but we couldn't help being happy at the sight of a loving couple.
The chipmunks were, of course, delighted at the prospect of Goldie becoming a dish rather than the diner.
He wasn't too happy with his role as a canine yelp, as he prefers to steer clear of nature's violence on the ground.
posts.key_insight: Nature's cycle of predator and prey operates indifferently to human attachments, and sometimes the only way to process loss is through storytelling that gives meaning to meaningless violence.

posts.summary: Computational notebooks are environments that combine prose documentation with executable code, making them ideal for data science exploration and analysis. Fowler explains how they allow researchers to keep notes, code, and results together in a single document, facilitating iterative exploration. He traces their history from Mathematica in the 1980s through modern tools like Jupyter and R Markdown. While notebooks excel at exploration and report generation, Fowler cautions against using them in production systems, as their structure encourages interactivity at the expense of modularity.
key_points: Computational notebooks combine prose, executable code, and results in a single document, ideal for data exploration
Text-based formats like R Markdown enable version control and easier diffing compared to binary formats
Notebooks serve dual purposes: exploration (recording what was tried and observed) and report generation (reproducible documents)
The coupling of code and results is a form of illustrative programming that appeals to lay programmers
Notebooks should not be used in production systems—their structure works against the modularity needed for broader codebases
money_quotes: When I'm exploring some data, it's useful to keep my notes close together with the code that performs the exploration.
The document acts as a record of what's been tried and all the observations the researcher makes as they try things.
By keeping the code and results together the writer can see exactly what they did and what results that generated.
This coupling of code and results is a form of IllustrativeProgramming, making the environment appealing to lay programmers.

The notebook structure - with its casual mix of IO, calculation, and UI - is there to encourage interactivity, but works against the modularity needed for code that is used as part of a broader code base.
It's best to think of notebooks as a way of exploring logic, once you've found a path, that logic should be replicated into a library designed for production use.
posts.key_insight: Computational notebooks are powerful tools for exploration and documentation, but their strength in encouraging interactivity becomes a liability when mistakenly used as production system components.

posts.summary: Fowler argues against deploying data science notebooks directly into production codebases. Notebooks excel at interactive exploration and demos but conflate concerns that should be separated in production systems—storage, visualization, and business logic. The real problem isn't technical but organizational: data scientists and software developers often don't understand each other's needs. The solution is embedding data scientists within delivery teams where they can learn software development practices while developers learn data science basics. Rather than productionizing notebooks, teams should extract the relevant domain logic into structured, testable codebases while building experimentation capabilities into the pipeline itself.
key_points: Notebooks combine storage, visualization, and business logic in ways that work for exploration but violate separation of concerns needed for production systems
Putting notebooks in production includes experimental code, creates security risks through shell access, and undermines reproducibility and auditability
