The underlying problem is poor communication between data scientists and software developers, not technical limitations
Data scientists should be embedded in delivery teams and learn software development fundamentals—this actually makes them more productive
Experimentation should be built into the production pipeline through model registries and multi-environment execution rather than through notebook integration
money_quotes: The essence of the problem is that data scientists and software developers do not always communicate very well or understand what the other needs to do.
This is to say that data scientists should strive to learn software development and work fully embedded in the delivery team responsible for delivery of production software.
A notebook is also a fully powered shell, which is dangerous to include inside a production system.
They'll find that using many of the techniques of software development actually makes them more productive as data scientists.
The smaller the gap between the environment of the experiment and the actual implementation, the more we can be confident that the change really creates value.

Why would I use a database, a Java application and Javascript frontend just to do some simple operations to calculate the payroll for the dozen employees that I employ at my startup? That's what spreadsheets are great at. But that doesn't mean a spreadsheet should be used to handle payroll for a major international bank.
posts.key_insight: The push to productionize notebooks is a symptom of organizational dysfunction between data science and engineering teams—the solution is cross-functional collaboration and proper software practices, not better notebook tooling.

posts.summary: Data mesh is a paradigm for managing analytical data at scale through four core principles: domain-oriented decentralized ownership, treating data as a product, self-serve data infrastructure, and federated computational governance. The approach addresses the failures of centralized data lakes and warehouses by distributing data ownership to domain teams closest to the data, while maintaining interoperability through global standards. Rather than extracting data into a central repository, each domain becomes responsible for serving its own analytical data products with quality guarantees. The architecture introduces the 'data product' as its quantum—a self-contained unit combining code, data, and infrastructure that domains can independently deploy and maintain.
key_points: Data mesh decentralizes analytical data ownership to domain teams rather than centralizing it in data lakes or warehouses, following the organizational seams of business domains
The 'data product' serves as the architectural quantum—the smallest independently deployable unit containing code, data, metadata, and infrastructure with high functional cohesion
Self-serve data platforms must provide multiple planes of abstraction: infrastructure provisioning, developer experience, and mesh-level supervision capabilities
Federated governance replaces centralized control, with domain representatives collectively defining global interoperability standards that are computationally enforced by the platform
The paradigm inverts traditional responsibility models by shifting data quality accountability upstream to source domains rather than downstream to central data teams
money_quotes: Data mesh follows the seams of organizational units as the axis of decomposition.
The accountability of data quality shifts upstream as close to the source of the data as possible.

Striking a balance between what shall be standardized globally, implemented and enforced by the platform for all domains and their data products, and what shall be left to the domains to decide, is an art.
Placing a system in a straitjacket of constancy can cause fragility to evolve.
A domain dataset only becomes a data product after it locally, within the domain, goes through the process of quality assurance.
Data mesh completely decentralizes this concern.
Measure success based on the network effect—the connections representing the consumption of data on the mesh.
posts.key_insight: Scaling analytical data requires distributing ownership to domain teams who serve data as products, connected through a self-serve platform that computationally enforces federated governance standards.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his six favorite musical discoveries of 2020, reflecting on his longtime habit of buying four albums per month and his recent shift to purchasing through Bandcamp. He highlights artists spanning jazz, world, and roots music, including GoGo Penguin, You Are Wolf, and Somi. Fowler emphasizes Bandcamp's appeal for both supporting artists directly and sampling music before purchase. The post serves as a personal music recommendation list while acknowledging the sources that led him to these discoveries.
key_points: Fowler maintains a decades-old habit of buying four albums monthly and rotating through his accumulated collection
He switched from emusic to Bandcamp because it passes more money to artists and allows pre-purchase listening
His musical tastes center on jazz influenced by minimalist classical and EDM, plus world and roots music
The six highlighted albums span Manchester jazz trio GoGo Penguin, avant-garde English folk from You Are Wolf, and international jazz collaborations
He credits Dave Sumner's Bandcamp Daily columns and the OK Jazz podcast as his best discovery sources
money_quotes: Writing about music is like dancing about architecture.
I'm still stuck in the mold of buying albums.
From what I can tell, the site passes on a bigger share of my dollars to the artist than buying through someone like Amazon.
My tastes are not mainstream, and I doubt I'd find any of these albums on my car radio.
You Are Wolf reimagines this so that it sounds ahead of its time in the twenty-first.
The resulting album is uplifting and joyful, and I hope their relationship mirrors it.
posts.key_insight: Bandcamp has become the ideal platform for music discovery because it combines ethical artist compensation with the ability to sample before committing to a purchase.

posts.summary: Written in response to the January 6th Capitol insurrection, Fowler argues that the event was a direct consequence of sustained political lies, particularly Trump's baseless claims about election fraud. He draws historical parallels to the Beer Hall Putsch and early Ku Klux Klan to warn that farcical-seeming events can lead to serious consequences if not confronted. While acknowledging Trump as a symptom rather than root cause, Fowler emphasizes that the broader 'ulceration of lies' in American society poses an ongoing threat to democracy. He calls for consequences for those who spread disinformation, including holding social media platforms to publisher-level responsibility, while acknowledging the difficulty of balancing free speech protections.
key_points: Historical parallels (Beer Hall Putsch, KKK) show that initially farcical events can become serious threats when not confronted early
Trump is a symptom, not the root cause—the deeper problem is widespread belief in demonstrably false narratives
Politicians who amplify lies bear greater responsibility than those who act on them, as they generate the false beliefs
Social media platforms should be held to publisher-level responsibility for content, not treated as judgment-free pipes
Free speech must be protected, but speakers should face consequences for harmful lies—finding this balance is genuinely difficult
money_quotes: A glance at history shows that often farcical events can turn into serious adversity
While we must have free speech we must not be free of the consequences of that speech
This ulceration of lies is why, even if Trump decides to spend the rest of his life playing golf, the problems of the last few years won't go away
The proper course for him and his fellow renegades was laid out by Senator Romney: 'the best way we can show respect for the voters who are upset is by telling them the truth'
Algorithms that optimize for engagement don't optimize for enlightenment
The excuse that tech-media companies are merely judgment-free pipes has always been flimsy, but it grows threadbare with each new event
Public figures who demonize the media wholesale are those with something to hide
posts.key_insight: Lies spread by elites and amplified by social media algorithms pose an existential threat to democracy, and society must find ways to impose consequences on those who spread them while protecting legitimate speech.

posts.summary: This article argues that engineering organizations undergoing digital transformations often fail because they neglect developer effectiveness, introducing too many new processes and tools that create friction. Fowler contrasts highly effective environments where developers make incremental progress with low effectiveness environments characterized by blockers, bureaucracy, and 'death by 1,000 cuts.' The key to improvement lies in optimizing feedback loops—both large cycles like deploying to production and micro-feedback loops like running unit tests. Organizations must measure these loops, empower developers to improve them, and eventually scale solutions through platform thinking. Case studies from Spotify and Etsy demonstrate how leading companies have embedded developer effectiveness into their culture with measurable results.
key_points: Digital transformations fail when organizations introduce too many new processes and tools, adding complexity rather than improving productivity
Developer effectiveness should be measured by feedback loop speed, not by lines of code or spotting underperformers
Micro-feedback loops (things developers do 10-200 times daily) compound dramatically—a 2-minute compile time can cost 100 minutes daily plus context-switching overhead
Effective organizations create internal platforms that treat developer tools as products with their own product managers and success metrics
Governance in effective organizations shifts from centralized approval processes to guardrails, nudges, and automated architecture fitness functions
money_quotes: This is akin to death by a 1,000 cuts. Slowly, productivity is destroyed by small inefficiencies, which have compounding effects.
Being productive motivates developers. Without the friction, they have time to think creatively and apply themselves.
If organizations do not do this, then in my experience the best engineers will leave.
Developers will run the feedback loops more often if they are shorter.
Those two minutes can add up quickly, and could top 100 minutes a day. These small pauses are opportunities to lose context and focus.
It starts with a recognition by leadership that technology — and removing friction from development teams — is vital to the business.
Developer experience means technical capabilities should be built with the same approaches used for end-user product development.
posts.key_insight: Developer effectiveness is determined by the speed and simplicity of feedback loops at every level, and organizations must treat developer experience with the same product thinking they apply to customer-facing products.

posts.summary: Pull requests are a mechanism designed to implement feature branching and pre-integration reviews, popularized by GitHub for open-source contribution workflows. While they provide convenient tooling for code review and managing contributions from untrusted sources, they come with significant costs including reduced integration frequency, complicated merges, and integration friction. Fowler argues that their value depends heavily on team social structure, and many teams use them by default when they would be better served by alternatives like pair programming or post-integration review. The key insight is that pull requests are not the only way to do code reviews, and for many teams, pre-integration review isn't the best choice.
key_points: Pull requests implement feature branching and pre-integration review patterns, which are sometimes valuable and sometimes painful depending on context
Feature branching limits integration frequency, leads to complicated merges, and deters refactoring
Teams can use pull requests within a CI environment if they keep them small enough and responsive enough to integrate at least daily
Code review is valuable but pull requests are not the only mechanism - pair programming provides continuous review without integration friction
The claim that pull requests are necessary for code reviews is false - pre-integration review is just one approach and often not the best
money_quotes: Feature Branching is a good way of packaging together a logical contribution so that it can be assessed, accepted, or deferred as a single unit.
But Feature Branching comes at a cost, which is that it usually limits the frequency of integration, leading to complicated merges and deterring refactoring.
Pre-Integration Reviews provide a clear place to do code review at the cost of a significant increase in integration friction.
I suspect that since pull requests are so popular, a lot of teams are using them by default when they would do better without them.
Total time waiting in 2020 for 7000 PRs was 130,000 hours.
I often hear people say that pull requests are necessary because without them you can't do code reviews - that's rubbish.
Pre-integration code review is just one way to do code reviews, and for many teams it isn't the best choice.
posts.key_insight: Pull requests are valuable for managing untrusted contributions but their popularity has led many teams to adopt them by default when alternative review mechanisms like pair programming would better support continuous integration.

posts.summary: Fowler challenges the narrow view that code review only happens through pull requests, arguing that the most powerful form of code review is perpetual refinement by the entire team. He contends that software's defining characteristic is its malleability, making continuous improvement both possible and necessary. The approach triggers review each time code is read rather than only when it's added, naturally concentrating attention on frequently-used areas. While acknowledging exceptions like security concerns that need targeted review, Fowler argues that teams with self-testing code, continuous integration, and refactoring skills can rely on ongoing refinement as their primary code review strategy.
key_points: Pull request reviews are just one mechanism; refinement done by the whole team during regular development is a more powerful form of code review
Problems that code reviews seek to fix often only become problems when code is read later, so there's logic in addressing them at that point rather than pre-integration
The refinement approach naturally concentrates review attention on code that's read most often, which is usually where it matters most
Security and other high-impact rare concerns require targeted reviews rather than relying solely on organic refinement
This practice requires self-testing code, continuous integration, refactoring skills, and collective code ownership to work effectively
money_quotes: Such a narrow view of code reviews doesn't just ignore a host of explicit mechanisms for review, it more importantly neglects probably the most powerful code review technique - that of perpetual refinement done by the entire team.
The key property of software is that it is soft, and can be as easily modified after it's released as it was when initially composed in the programmer's editor.
As soon as I have an understanding about the code that wasn't immediately apparent from reading it, I have the responsibility to take that understanding out of my head and put it into the code.
This process of refinement is exactly the same as what happens in a code review, but it's triggered each time the code is looked at rather than when the code is added to the codebase.
If the code never needs to be looked at again, we don't need to spend effort on making it comprehensible.
If I have a pristine mainline, and ensure that every commit merged into that mainline is pristine - can I be sure that the codebase is still pristine after six months? I'd argue that I can't.
posts.key_insight: Code review should be reconceived as an ongoing team practice of refinement triggered by reading code, not just a gate at integration time.

posts.summary: Bitemporal history addresses the complexity of tracking property changes over time when retroactive updates occur. The concept distinguishes between 'actual' history (when something truly changed) and 'record' history (when we learned about it). The author uses a payroll example where an employee's salary raise is communicated retroactively, creating discrepancies between what was recorded at the time of payroll and what was actually true. While bitemporal modeling adds significant complexity, it becomes necessary when actions are based on past states that later change retroactively. The pattern can be implemented through bitemporal data structures or event sourcing, with the latter offering more flexibility for different query needs.
key_points: Time in data systems has two dimensions: actual history (when something truly occurred) and record history (when we learned about it)
Record history is append-only - we don't change what we thought we knew, we layer new knowledge on top
Bitemporal history should be avoided when possible due to complexity, but becomes necessary when retroactive changes affect actions already taken
The concept of 'perspective' generalizes record time to include different agents (departments, authorities) who learn facts at different times
Implementation can use either bitemporal data structures with date ranges or event sourcing where each event carries both actual and record timestamps
money_quotes: There is a difference between the actual history, and our record of the history.
Bitemporal history is a way of coming to terms that communication is neither perfect nor instantaneous.
By layering an append-only record history over the actual history, we allow the actual history to be modified while creating a reliable history of its modifications.
If we can avoid using bitemporal history, then that's usually preferable as it does complicate a system quite significantly.
One of the hardest parts of this is educating users on how bitemporal history works. Most people don't think of a historical record as something that changes.
A lot of effort goes into sorting out problems caused by different agents learning the same facts at different times.
Pay attention to the fact that if events need to support bitemporal history, they need to be bitemporal themselves.
posts.key_insight: Bitemporal history separates what actually happened from when we learned about it, enabling systems to handle retroactive changes while maintaining an auditable record of how knowledge evolved over time.

posts.summary: This post explains how to create muted-spaghetti line charts in R using ggplot2, a visualization technique that shows one highlighted data series against a background of related comparison series. Fowler demonstrates the technique using COVID-19 death rates, showing how Massachusetts data becomes more meaningful when contextualized against all other US states. The key insight is that raw numbers need context to be interpretable. The post includes practical R code for both single charts and faceted grids, with a clever trick of renaming the grouping column to make facets work correctly with background spaghetti lines.
key_points: Raw numbers without context are meaningless - a $10M sales figure tells you nothing without comparison
Muted-spaghetti charts layer a highlighted line over grayed-out comparison lines to provide visual context
The technique requires plotting the background lines before the foreground to ensure the primary data pops
For faceted grids, renaming the grouping column prevents ggplot from faceting the background spaghetti lines
This visualization pattern has no generally accepted name, though 'spaghetti line chart' describes multiple overlaid lines
money_quotes: If someone tells me their sales last month was $10M - what do I make of it? With just the bare number, I don't know what to think.
To make sense of the number I need context, perhaps over time, perhaps compared to compatible companies.
Using a data visualization can help me put a number into a context that allows me to make sense of it.
As far as I can tell, there's no generally accepted term for this kind of plot.
Note that I plot the background before the foreground line to ensure the foreground line pops clearly on top.
By renaming the grouping column, ggplot only facets the primary line and plots the spaghetti on each facet.
posts.key_insight: Numbers only become meaningful when visualized with appropriate context, and muted-spaghetti charts provide an elegant way to show how a single data series compares against many related series.

posts.summary: Internal developer productivity platforms promise to reduce cognitive load and accelerate time to market, but there's a significant execution gap between platform hype and successful implementation. The author argues that building effective platforms requires foundational competencies in business value articulation, product thinking, operational excellence, software engineering, and healthy team structures. Rather than mandating platform adoption, organizations should start small with voluntary adoption, treating platform services as products that must earn developer trust through quality and customer focus. Success depends on maintaining a modest scope while executing with high quality, planning for eventual deprecation, and keeping cognitive load manageable for platform teams.
key_points: Building an internal platform is fundamentally an economic decision that must continuously justify its business value against commercial alternatives and maintenance burden
Platform services must be treated as products requiring customer empathy, marketing, roadmap communication, and earned adoption rather than managerial mandates
Operational excellence including SRE practices, incident management, and on-call support are prerequisites for the trust product teams must place in platform dependencies
Start with the smallest viable platform service and always plan for deprecation—architectural seams for replacement should be built in from the start
Voluntary adoption keeps platforms loosely coupled and maintains product thinking discipline, while mandatory migration erodes customer focus
money_quotes: A digital platform is a foundation of self-service APIs, tools, services, knowledge and support which are arranged as a compelling internal product.
The aim is not to reinvent commercially available functionality (the world does not need another homegrown Kubernetes) but to bridge the gap between what you can buy and what is really needed.
A product that no one uses is not a successful product, no matter its technical merits.

Resist the temptation to rely on managerial mandates as an adoption strategy. You may have captive users, but compelling them to use products supposedly for their own good does not foster a productive relationship.
The secret is not to compromise on the quality of your execution, but to be modest in the scope of your ambition - at first.
Digital platforms are force multipliers, so there is a fine line between developing a competitive advantage and introducing a significant productivity blocker.
Deprecation is a fundamental part of the platform product lifecycle, and failure to consider it may undermine the business benefits you hoped to gain by offering it in the first place.
posts.key_insight: The gap between platform enthusiasm and successful execution can only be bridged by starting small with voluntary adoption, treating platform services as products that must earn developer trust, and never compromising on execution quality even while limiting initial scope.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler addresses the debate between test pyramid vs honeycomb/trophy approaches to testing portfolios. He argues that the debate is largely muddled because terms like 'unit test' and 'integration test' have no agreed-upon definitions. He traces the history of these terms from waterfall-era isolation testing through Kent Beck's XP usage to the modern sociable/solitary distinction. Fowler suggests that honeycomb advocates criticizing 'unit tests' are specifically targeting solitary (heavily mocked) tests, making the debate somewhat moot since pyramid proponents include both styles. He concludes that the entire discussion is a distraction from what really matters: writing expressive, reliable tests with clear boundaries.
key_points: The terms 'unit test' and 'integration test' have always been murky and inconsistently defined across the industry
Kent Beck originally used 'unit test' to mean any test written by programmers rather than a separate testing team
The sociable vs solitary distinction (tests with real collaborators vs mocks) is more useful than unit vs integration
The pyramid vs honeycomb debate appears moot once you realize honeycomb advocates' 'integration tests' are essentially sociable unit tests
Debating test distribution percentages is a distraction from the real goal of writing expressive, reliable tests
money_quotes: The second biggest issue I have with this discussion is that it's rendered opaque by the fact that it's not clear what people see as the difference between unit and integration tests.

I remember considerable discussion over this use of 'unit test'. One test expert vigorously lambasted Kent for this usage. We asked him how he would define unit test, and his reply was something like 'in the first morning of my training course I cover 24 different definitions of unit test'.
From the early days of XP-inspired unit testing there were those who disliked the term 'unit test' and proposed using names like 'microtest' or 'programmer test' instead.
This makes the pyramid versus honeycomb discussion moot, since any descriptions I've heard of the test pyramid consider unit tests to be sociable and/or solitary.

The take-away here is when anyone starts talking about various testing categories, dig deeper on what they mean by their words, as they probably don't use them the same way as the last person you read did.

People love debating what percentage of which type of tests to write, but it's a distraction. Nearly zero teams write expressive tests that establish clear boundaries, run quickly & reliably, and only fail for useful reasons. Focus on that instead.
posts.key_insight: The pyramid vs honeycomb testing debate is largely a semantic argument caused by inconsistent terminology, and teams should focus on writing high-quality tests rather than debating their distribution.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler announces his retirement from public speaking, revealing that despite being a successful conference keynote speaker with hundreds of thousands of YouTube views, he experiences severe anxiety and panic attacks before every talk. He describes physical symptoms including racing heart, chest tightness, and overwhelming urges to flee. While he's managed these feelings professionally for years, the COVID pandemic's break from speaking made him realize he doesn't want to return to that stress. He plans to redirect his energy toward writing and helping colleagues communicate their ideas through articles on his website.
key_points: Despite natural comfort with public speaking as a young person, Fowler developed severe pre-talk anxiety that includes panic attack symptoms
The anxiety doesn't just occur before talks but can strike days earlier, affecting sleep and work, and even manifests while writing about the topic
Once on stage, adrenaline typically takes over within minutes, though online talks lack this compensating effect
Fowler feels fortunate to have reached a career point where he can decline speaking without significant consequences
His future focus will be on writing and editorial work, helping colleagues publish their insights on martinfowler.com
money_quotes: I loathe giving talks.
I far prefer a trip to the dentist to have fillings done.
The prospect of a future of frequent speaking was the only thing that's seriously made me consider leaving Thoughtworks.
Before I go on stage I get intense feels of dread, an overwhelming desire to flee - to lock myself in a toilet or lose myself in some strange city.

After all my father worked in a factory that ruined his lungs, countless people take serious health risks in their daily work - compared to that I don't feel I can complain too much about these bursts of anxiety.
Certainly my experience has given me an understanding of why so many people in the performing arts struggle with drug addictions.
I've reached the point in my life where I'm lucky enough to be able to avoid things that make me miserable, and want to take advantage of my good fortune.
posts.key_insight: Professional success and public visibility don't immunize you from anxiety, and reaching a point where you can decline work that makes you miserable is a privilege worth exercising.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains the Gateway pattern, which creates a single point of interaction between your code and external systems by translating foreign APIs into terms native to your application. The gateway wraps external services, libraries, or databases, converting their awkward interfaces into convenient ones while containing translation logic in one place. He distinguishes the gateway from similar GoF patterns (Facade, Adapter, Mediator) and connects it to Domain-Driven Design's Anticorruption Layer concept. The pattern provides significant testing benefits by allowing test doubles to be inserted at the connection layer, eliminating slow remote calls during testing.
key_points: A gateway translates foreign APIs into the vocabulary and types your application uses, containing the translation logic in a single place rather than letting awkwardness spread through the codebase

The pattern differs from Facade (written by service provider for general use) and Adapter (assumes both interfaces already exist) - a gateway is written by the client, defining its interface as it wraps the foreign element
Adding a connection object to the gateway structure enables test doubles and encapsulates remote call mechanics like REST API manipulations
Teams must exercise judgment on whether to adopt external vocabulary or translate it - sometimes wrapping pervasive platform facilities isn't worth the effort
The gateway aligns with DDD's Bounded Context concept and implements what DDD calls an Anticorruption Layer
money_quotes: A gateway acts as a single point to confront this foreigner. Any code within our system interacts with the interface of the gateway, which is designed to work in the terms that our system uses.
Rather than let the awkwardness spread through my code, I contain to a single place in the gateway.
While Facade simplifies a more complex API, it's usually done by the writer of the service for general use. A gateway is written by the client for its particular use.
With a gateway I'm defining the gateway's interface as I wrap the foreign element.
Although this benefit is handy, its hardly ever a reason to use a gateway, since just encapsulating the foreign API is justification enough.
I have come across situations where a team has translated a widely-understood foreign vocabulary into a particular one for their code base because 'they didn't like the names'.

By doing this, the treatment planning application doesn't have to deal with the language of the equipment booking service. It can pretend that the equipment booking service works seamlessly in the world of treatment planning.
posts.key_insight: A gateway contains foreign API awkwardness to a single place, allowing your application code to work entirely in its own vocabulary while gaining significant testing and maintainability benefits.

posts.summary: Ship/Show/Ask is a branching strategy that reconciles Pull Requests with Continuous Integration by categorizing changes into three approaches: Ship (merge directly to mainline), Show (open a PR but merge immediately without waiting for approval), and Ask (open a PR and wait for feedback). The strategy addresses how Pull Requests have eroded the 'ready to ship' mentality of CI, causing delayed integration, stale PRs, and rubber-stamp approvals. The key innovation is the 'Show' option, which captures CI's speed while preserving PR tooling benefits for asynchronous feedback. Teams naturally fall somewhere on the Ship-to-Ask continuum, and the framework encourages consciously choosing the right approach for each change based on novelty, risk, and team trust levels.
key_points: The 'Show' approach merges PRs immediately while still creating space for asynchronous feedback and conversation, combining CI speed with PR tooling benefits
Mandatory approval processes often mask underlying trust issues that should be addressed through training, team discussions, and ensemble programming rather than review gates
Pull Requests are not a replacement for other forms of conversation - teams should discuss approaches before starting work to avoid the sunk-cost bias of reviewing already-built solutions
The right balance depends on team maturity: established patterns and high trust favor Shipping, while new teams or novel work favor Showing and Asking
Branches should be short-lived and frequently rebased, with Feature Toggles and other CD techniques keeping mainline releasable
money_quotes: We've lost some of the 'Ready to Ship' mentality we had when we did Continuous Integration.
We also get tired of the number of Pull Requests we have to review, so we don't talk about the code anymore. We stop paying attention and we just click 'Approve' or say 'Looks good to me'.

By the time a Pull Request is opened, time has been invested in a solution that may be sub-optimal. Reviewers are influenced by the selected solution and find it harder to suggest alternative approaches.
'All changes must be approved' or 'Every pull request needs 2 reviewers' are common policies, but they show a lack of trust in the development team.
An approval step is only a band-aid – it won't fix your underlying trust issues.
Every time a developer 'Shows' rather than 'Asks' is an opportunity for them to build trust with their team.
Ship/Show/Ask reminds us that each team's approach sits on a continuum somewhere between 'Always Ship' and 'Always Ask'.
posts.key_insight: Mandatory code review approval is often a trust band-aid rather than a quality mechanism, and teams can ship faster while maintaining feedback culture by merging PRs immediately and treating reviews as post-hoc conversations.

posts.summary: The article examines how organizations can implement compliance processes within DevOps cultures while maintaining agility. It traces an evolution from manual compliance checklists through pipeline-embedded fitness functions to composition-based approaches using pre-certified container images. The most sophisticated pattern—Point-of-Change Compliance—separates measurement from validation, uses cryptographically signed evidence, and enforces policies at deployment time. Each progressive approach shrinks the unique compliance work per team while reducing bottlenecks and second-order effects like shadow IT. The key architectural insight is that separating concerns in compliance architecture positions organizations to evolve their processes as they scale.
key_points: Manual compliance processes often become 'security theater' that delays delivery without materially improving security, with some taking 6-9 months to complete
Centralizing pipeline ownership to enforce compliance creates massive friction and consistently ranks among the top sources of developer slowdown
The concept of 'Compliance Quanta'—progressively decomposing compliance into smaller reusable units—enables efficiency gains at scale
Point-of-Change Compliance uses cryptographically signed evidence and Policy-as-Code to decouple measurement from validation, enabling independent policy evolution
Composition-based compliance assumes compliance is distributive over composition, which may only hold for zero-customization container images with limited practical utility
money_quotes: The unfortunate reality is that our experience shows manual compliance processes to be a form of 'security theater' resulting in delayed delivery of value without any material increase in the safety and security of the system.

Quite often, the pressure of delivery overwhelms the Compliance process, and development teams actively abuse, subvert, or avoid the Compliance process altogether, resulting in 'shadow IT', compromised design and architecture decisions, or even falsifying Compliance responses.
Our experience shows that due to the high frequency of change within a team's pipelines, central ownership of pipelines is one of the largest sources of friction within the development process.
We are aware of cases where such central 'ownership' decisions are made with the express desire to slow down releases.
The acceleration factor of returning pipeline ownership to development teams is consistently in the top three value-returning investments the engineering organization can make.
Any similarities between systems represents wasted work within the Compliance effort, especially at scale.
posts.key_insight: Effective compliance at scale requires progressively decomposing both architectural and compliance units into smaller, reusable quanta—ultimately separating measurement from validation to return pipeline ownership to teams while maintaining audit integrity.

posts.summary: Teams should limit technology choices to three per category: a sensible default, an experimental trial, and a legacy option being retired. This constraint forces intentional decisions about adopting new technologies—before adding something new, teams must either complete retiring the legacy option or abandon the failed experiment. At organizational scale, this requires more coordination time but can be facilitated through tools like internal technology radars that communicate preferred technologies across verticals.
key_points: Limit each technology category to three options: default (current standard), trial (experiment), and retire (legacy being phased out)
Adding new technology requires first completing migration off legacy or abandoning failed experiments
This approach relates to the concept of 'innovation tokens'—limiting how much novelty a team takes on
Team-level decisions are easier due to trust and communication; org-level alignment takes longer
Publishing an internal technology radar helps communicate technology preferences across an organization
money_quotes: Within each normal-sized team, limit the choice of alternatives for any class of technology to three.
If we want to add another messaging tech then we have two choices. Either migrate all of our apps off the legacy tech, or properly rid ourselves of the failed experiment.
At a team level these kinds of limits are relatively easy to maintain and discuss and act upon, because we have common priorities and ways of working and high trust, high bandwidth communication.
posts.key_insight: Constraining technology choices to default-trial-retire forces teams to complete their existing technology transitions before accumulating more complexity.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues against one-size-fits-all technology governance, proposing instead that architectural rigor should scale with organizational distance. Using MYOB's structure as an example, he introduces the concept of 'strong' and 'weak' forces of alignment between teams, where teams within the same domain can tolerate tighter coupling due to easy communication, while cross-organization integrations require strict decoupling and versioning. The model recognizes that coupling isn't inherently evil—its cost depends on how easily affected parties can coordinate changes. This framework helps organizations achieve 'aligned autonomy' by matching governance strictness to the actual blast radius of decisions.
key_points: Technology governance should vary based on organizational distance and coordination difficulty, not be uniformly strict or permissive
Teams within a domain have 'strong forces' of alignment, enabling tighter coupling with lower costs because changes can be negotiated quickly
Cross-organization integrations have 'weak forces' requiring strict schemas, versioning, and backwards compatibility
Shared code and infrastructure should be limited at org-wide levels to highly stable, well-encapsulated services
The goal is 'aligned autonomy'—empowering teams to make decisions while maintaining appropriate coordination at each scope level
money_quotes: Neither of those approaches are ideal.
Coupling between systems at a domain level is not always the evil monster that must be vanquished at all costs, in fact coupling at this scope can be a useful thing.
The social distance between the people in one domain and another is getting stretched. This makes negotiation and reaching alignment more strained and slower.
It is quite hard to make changes atomically across the landscape - mostly because the prioritisation of work for each vertical is deliberately independent.
Even 'forbidden' approaches like database-level integration will have less impact within the systems in a single domain.
Being aware of these forces of alignment within our organisation allows us to understand what is going to be easy and what is going to be hard, and make better technology choices as a result.
posts.key_insight: The appropriate level of architectural coupling and governance rigor should be calibrated to the organizational distance between teams—tight coupling is acceptable where coordination is easy, while strict decoupling is necessary where it's hard.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that commercial integration tools (ESBs, ETL, API platforms) are essentially programming languages, not solutions you can purchase off-the-shelf. Organizations mistakenly treat integration as a tactical concern of wiring systems together, when it should be a strategic focus on building clean interfaces over digital capabilities. Low-code integration DSLs suffer from programming-over-time limitations—poor diffing, testing friction, scaling challenges, and operational observability gaps. While these tools can help with workflow simplification and B2B integration long tails, they should never own the interface evolution. Integration strategy is the key architectural driver of organizational acceleration and competitive advantage.
key_points: Integration tools are programming languages bundled with runtimes—when you buy them, you're agreeing to build the actual integration yourself in a commercial DSL
The real goal of integration is creating clean interfaces over capabilities, not wiring systems together—abstract the capability, not the system
Low-code platforms have severe programming-over-time limitations: poor diffability, modularity constraints, testing friction, limited ecosystem evolution, and operational observability gaps
Use general purpose languages to manage interface evolution; use integration DSLs only for bounded implementation concerns like workflow simplification or B2B adapter patterns
Integration strategy lives in the 'second derivative'—it's the key driver of organizational acceleration, not a tactical nuisance to overcome
money_quotes: You can buy programming languages. You can't buy integration.
When you buy an integration product, you are agreeing to build the integration itself in a commercial programming language.
You don't need an SAP API, because your API users don't care about SAP, but you might need an order management API. Abstract the capability, not the system.
The key to simplifying the interface is to accept a more complex implementation.
The lines between applications become the critical cost driver over time.
The role of the architect is to ensure that you don't let a vendor's product strategy become your architectural strategy.
Integration tools 'think' in terms of implementation.
You can't buy integration, but that's OK; it's worth the investment to build it yourself. After all, it may be the most strategic software in your portfolio.
posts.key_insight: Integration is not a tactical problem to be solved with purchased tooling but a strategic capability that requires building clean interfaces over digital capabilities using general-purpose programming languages.

posts.summary: This article presents an alternative to traditional top-down software architecture by introducing a decentralized, conversation-based approach centered on the 'Advice Process.' Rather than having architects make all decisions, anyone can make architectural decisions provided they consult those affected and those with relevant expertise. The approach is supported by four elements: Architectural Decision Records (ADRs), Architecture Advisory Forums, team-sourced principles, and organization-specific technology radars. The author argues this method produces better, faster, more accountable decisions while developing architectural thinking across teams rather than concentrating it in a few individuals.
key_points: The Advice Process allows anyone to make architectural decisions, but requires consulting those affected and those with expertise - seeking input without requiring consensus
Architecture Advisory Forums provide a regular time and place for conversations where advice-seeking happens publicly, enabling organizational learning
Architectural Decision Records serve as both a thinking tool and documentation, capturing context, options, consequences, and all advice received
Team-sourced architectural principles provide direction without control, serving as evaluation criteria that teams feel ownership over
The biggest failure mode is architects failing to trust people and continuing 'shadow architecture' behind the scenes
money_quotes: I stopped taking architectural decisions. Completely.
It is the developer's assumptions which get shipped to production.

It is very much about ensuring that conversations that are needed to be happening are happening - not always initiating them, nor always helping to focus or navigate them, but ensuring they do happen.
We are not looking for consensus here, but we are looking for a broad range of inputs and voices.
The sole benefit of this failure mode is that it becomes evident very rapidly as all the benefits I've listed above fail to materialize.

Once you empower people, giving them an environment in which to succeed, and recognise their successes, they will rapidly, and as a collective, start thinking about things which haven't even crossed your mind.
The real benefit of this kind of approach: access to the collective intelligence of the many, over reliance on the much more restricted intelligence of the few.
If everyone is empowered to make a decision, how do we ensure that the sum of all the individual decisions combine to form a coherent whole?
posts.key_insight: Effective architecture at scale comes not from centralized decision-making but from creating conversational structures that enable distributed decision-making with appropriate advice-seeking, accountability, and shared learning.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares six of his favorite musical discoveries from 2021, ranging from jazz-techno fusion to Indian-influenced brass bands and Latin-African crossover. The selections reflect his eclectic, non-mainstream tastes with an emphasis on cross-cultural musical fusion and interesting rhythmic explorations. He highlights artists like Johanna Burnheart (jazz violin meets techno), Christian Scott aTunde Adjuah (modern jazz trumpet), and Red Baraat (Indian-influenced brass). The post serves as an annual tradition of sharing new-to-him music discoveries with readers.
key_points: Fowler's tastes gravitate toward cross-genre fusion, particularly where jazz meets electronic music or where different cultural traditions intersect
The selections span diverse influences: Berlin club scene, Mediterranean folk, Indian brass bands, and Latin-African collaboration
Christian Scott aTunde Adjuah's trumpet work evokes comparisons to Miles Davis's experimental 70s period
The post acknowledges key music discovery sources like Bandcamp Daily and OK Jazz podcasts
money_quotes: She describes her music as existing 'at the point where modal jazz and techno meet'
As soon as the percussion gets roaring soon after the start of X. Adjuah [I Own the Night], I knew I'd run into a special artist
This produces a sound that feels like it could be one the places Miles Davis might have ended up on the path from his work in the early 70's
Anyone who enjoys interesting rhythms will naturally turn their ears to India
There's long been a fruitful cross-pollination between Latin American and African music
posts.key_insight: The most rewarding musical discoveries often emerge at the intersection of genres and cultures, where artists blend traditions in unexpected ways.

posts.summary: The article provides a comprehensive guide for building infrastructure platforms that enable product teams to focus on building solutions rather than managing hosting infrastructure. It argues that organizations should start with a clear problem statement and measurable goal before investing in platform teams. The authors emphasize treating the platform as a product with real users (developers), conducting proper discovery, onboarding users early for feedback, and avoiding over-complication. Success depends on creating a great user experience that teams want to adopt voluntarily rather than being mandated to use.
key_points: Define a clear problem statement and measurable goal before building a platform - without this, executives may scrap the initiative when it fails to demonstrate value
Treat developers as customers by conducting proper discovery research, including Event Storming sessions to understand their pain points and needs
Onboard users as early as possible using 'Shortest Path to Value' thinking rather than waiting for a fully viable product
Communicate technical vision early using C4 Diagrams and Architectural Decision Records to prevent duplicate efforts and build stakeholder confidence
Keep the platform simple - every component adds maintenance burden and potential failure modes, and Conway's Law can introduce unnecessary complexity
money_quotes: By building infrastructure platforms you can save time for product teams, reduce your cloud spend and increase the security and rigour of your infrastructure.
If you can't put together a problem statement maybe you don't need an infrastructure platform.
Many organisations build platform infrastructure, and then can't get any teams to use them.
When you're developing an infrastructure platform that other teams are dependent upon; your customers' dev environments are your production environments.
Things don't always go so well if you have to mandate the usage of your infra products, so you're going to have to actually make an effort to make people want to use your product.
All software is broken. Not to put too much of a downer on things, but every line of code that you write has a very high chance of becoming quickly obsolete.
Every moving part you develop is an investment in post-live support and another potential failure mode.
posts.key_insight: Infrastructure platforms should be treated as products with developers as customers, requiring clear goals, user research, early feedback, and simplicity to succeed rather than being mandated top-down.

posts.summary: The lean inception is a one-week workshop designed to help teams quickly align on building a Minimum Viable Product before starting agile development. Paulo Caroli developed this approach at Thoughtworks after needing to compress traditional multi-week inceptions due to personal circumstances. The workshop focuses specifically on understanding the MVP through structured daily activities including product vision, personas, features, and user journeys. Unlike traditional inceptions, teams don't write or estimate user stories—instead they focus on validating business assumptions about what users actually need. The approach works well for both large projects needing lean orientation and startups evolving ideas into software products.
key_points: Traditional Thoughtworks inceptions took 2-4 weeks; the lean inception compresses this to one week by removing non-product activities
The central property of an MVP is that it's built to learn whether continuing to build the product is worthwhile
The workshop creates alignment between business and technical people on product direction without detailed user story estimation
Lean inception complements but doesn't replace ideation sessions, customer research, architectural review, or competitive analysis
The structured week includes specific activities: product vision, personas, features, technical review, user journeys, and MVP canvas
money_quotes: Naive agile has no up-front work, but in practice we realize we have to do some.
The central property of an MVP is that it is something we build in order to learn whether it is worthwhile to continue building a product.
The team doesn't write and estimate user stories anymore.
We choose features based on testing our assumptions of what is valuable to our users.
This workshop is specifically about understanding an MVP, it doesn't substitute for ideation sessions, customer research, architectural review, or competitive analysis.
posts.key_insight: Effective project starts require enough upfront alignment to understand what MVP to build and why, but not so much that teams get bogged down in detailed planning before learning from real users.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler explains his personal approach to using Twitter effectively, emphasizing curation over engagement. He values Twitter's brevity constraint and uses it primarily to discover interesting articles rather than for conversations, having learned in the 90s that online discussions rarely prove worthwhile. He carefully curates who he follows, uses lists to organize topic-specific feeds, insists on chronological ordering, and avoids replies and arguments. He acknowledges Twitter's harassment problems while noting his privileged position in not being targeted, and expresses skepticism about Musk's understanding of content moderation challenges.
key_points: Twitter's character limit is a feature, not a bug - it forces brevity and makes skimming efficient
Online conversations have been toxic since the 90s newsgroups; Twitter just continues the pattern
Careful curation of who you follow is essential - some prolific accounts are best experienced through retweets
Lists are underutilized but powerful for topic-based reading at appropriate times
Having your own internet domain provides independence from platform instability
money_quotes: One of the big lessons I learned about the internet in the 90's was that conversations online were rarely worthwhile.
90's newsgroups had all the toxic behavior we see on online platforms now, for much the same reasons.

It's easy for me to underweight the importance of a problem I don't have to deal with, but I know good people who avoid online spaces entirely because of a fear of harassment, often a fear born of bitter experience.
Some people tweet a lot of good stuff, but too much - those I don't follow reckoning that anything particularly good will be retweeted by those that I do follow.
I try to avoid getting into arguments, and am mostly successful at that.
As ever, I'm happy that I have my own internet domain.
posts.key_insight: Twitter becomes useful when you treat it as a curated discovery feed rather than a conversation platform, deliberately avoiding the engagement traps that make social media toxic.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler documents his transition from Twitter to Mastodon following Elon Musk's acquisition, providing practical guidance on federation, verification, cross-posting, and community norms. He explains the federated architecture where users choose servers (instances) and can easily migrate between them, contrasting this with Twitter's centralization. The article chronicles his learning process over several months, covering topics from basic account setup to list management and content warnings. He concludes with engagement data showing surprisingly similar interaction rates between his 344,000 Twitter followers and 18,000 Mastodon followers, suggesting the Fediverse has become equally important for his tech-savvy audience.
key_points: Mastodon's federation model distributes verification responsibility to individual instances - organizations can verify their members simply by running their own server
The ability to easily migrate between Mastodon instances exemplifies the agile principle that making decisions reversible reduces complexity
Content warnings (CWs) are a writer's tool for helping readers, not a universal mandate - their appropriate use depends on context and community norms
Volunteer moderators face enormous challenges managing disputes and content risks, justifying selective membership policies
Despite having 20x more Twitter followers, Fowler's engagement metrics (boosts, comments) are nearly identical on both platforms, suggesting the Fediverse is viable for tech audiences
money_quotes: As an advocate of agile software design, I recognize this as an example of the principle that making decisions easy to change reduces complexity.
The phrase I use to anchor my approach in matters like this is 'I can't choose whether someone is offended by my actions. I can choose whether I care.'
Any active writer should own their own domain and post all significant writing on that domain.
Unlike Twitter, which needs to scale to a vast amount of users, a Mastodon instance can be small enough for the organization running it to verify its members.
The Fediverse equivalent of gmail will appear and it will be a proprietary single entity, in many ways not that different from the entity that is Twitter now.
Part of my role as an author is to figure out what my readers need, which isn't the same thing as what they want.
Based on these engagement figures, I'd say that the Fediverse is now just as important to me as Twitter.
posts.key_insight: Decentralized, federated systems like Mastodon embody good software design principles by making decisions reversible and distributing responsibility to appropriate organizational boundaries.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that Conway's Law—the observation that software architecture mirrors the communication structure of its development organization—is one of the few principles in software architecture that practitioners universally accept. He outlines three responses: ignoring the law (doomed to fail), accepting it by ensuring architecture doesn't clash with team communication patterns, or employing the Inverse Conway Maneuver to deliberately reshape team structure to encourage desired architecture. The post emphasizes that modular decomposition of systems and organizational structure must evolve together throughout an enterprise's life. Fowler notes that while the Inverse Conway Maneuver is useful, it's not a quick fix for rigid existing systems, and changes require small steps with vigilant feedback.
key_points: Conway's Law states that system design inevitably mirrors the communication structure of the organization that builds it
Small teams (a dozen or two people) naturally create monoliths due to deep informal communication; the law becomes critical when scaling
The Inverse Conway Maneuver deliberately restructures development teams to encourage desired software architecture
Activity-oriented team organization (front-end, back-end, database layers) creates friction because features require cross-layer collaboration
Domain-Driven Design's Bounded Contexts help define organizational structures that align with value flow
money_quotes: If there is one thing they all agree on, it's the importance and power of Conway's Law. Important enough to affect every system I've come across, and powerful enough that you're doomed to defeat if you try to fight it.
Any organization that designs a system (defined broadly) will produce a design whose structure is a copy of the organization's communication structure.
Conway understood that software coupling is enabled and encouraged by human communication.
I made my first architectural decision. There are going to be six major subsystems. I have no idea what they are going to be, but there are going to be six of them.
Putting teams on separate floors of the same building is enough to significantly reduce communication.
This, indeed, is why I describe microservices as primarily a tool to structure a development organization.
The modular decomposition of a system and the decomposition of the development organization must be done together.
posts.key_insight: Software architecture and organizational structure are inextricably linked; you cannot successfully change one without deliberately addressing the other.

posts.summary: Fowler argues that organizations—whether companies, governments, or NGOs—should run their own Mastodon servers rather than depend on centralized platforms like Twitter or Facebook. The Fediverse model allows organizations to control their social media presence using their own domain, establishing trust and authenticity without platform intermediaries. Organizations can set their own rules of engagement, invite employees or partners, and never lose followers since accounts are portable. Thoughtworks has successfully used their Mastodon instance for employee engagement and talent attraction. The piece positions federated social media as a strategic investment against the uncertainties of centralized platforms.
key_points: Running your own Mastodon server gives organizational control over social presence, rules of conduct, and brand identity through domain ownership
The Fediverse works like email—you can communicate across servers while maintaining clear organizational identity
Account portability means communities built on Mastodon are never locked into one platform
Organizations can offer social media accounts to employees as a benefit that amplifies organizational voice while providing individual credibility
Other federated alternatives exist (Peertube for video, Pixelfed for photos) that interconnect with Mastodon
money_quotes: We spend so much effort building communities on these large social networks, and by that we make ourselves very dependent on their operating model.
By using your own domain name, your brand, you are creating a recognizable social presence in the Fediverse, without the need to associate it with facebook.com, twitter.com or instagram.com.
And once you run your own corner of a social network, you can decide who you invite there, what are your rules of engagement and code of conduct.
You are not stuck in one place, in one network, with one account. You can regroup or move your account to a different place.
People wanting to understand your company culture would have an authentic place to look at.
The Fediverse is currently one of the most promising alternatives to the centralized social media monopolies like Facebook or Twitter.
posts.key_insight: Organizations should invest in federated social media infrastructure now to own their communication channels and escape dependency on unpredictable centralized platforms.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his annual list of six musical discoveries from 2022, spanning genres from techno-jazz big band to Mediterranean trance, Afrobeat, English folk, accordion jazz, and manipulated free jazz improvisation. The post is a personal diversion from his usual software topics, highlighting lesser-known artists and albums with links to Bandcamp samples. He also gives an honorable mention to Queen's 1986 Wembley concert recording, reflecting on their unmatched live energy. The piece concludes by crediting his music discovery sources and linking to his other annual music posts.
key_points: Fowler highlights genre-crossing artists like Jazzrausch Bigband (techno meets big band) and Vincent Peirani (accordion in jazz) who challenge instrumental and stylistic boundaries
The selections emphasize global musical cross-pollination: Mediterranean trance, London-based Afrobeat, and New York free jazz with electronica editing
Queen's 1986 Wembley concert gets a special mention as a reminder that studio recordings understate the band's live power
Bandcamp and jazz podcasts serve as Fowler's primary discovery channels for non-mainstream music
money_quotes: Jazzrausch Bigband is compelling evidence that, however unlikely it sounds, Duke Ellington had a baby with Kraftwerk.
The Mediterranean is a fascinating mixing pot: Graeco-Roman classicism, catholic Europe, the Maghreb, Ottoman, and Arabian cultures united by trade over the course of centuries.
With folks like these, Afrobeat has a bright future.
Vincent Peirani lays out proof that it has far wider abilities, capable of playing as central a role in Jazz groups as the piano.

This album was recorded by having a free jazz improvisational session, later edited and manipulated in the studio. The result is music from a dream that combines improvisational melodies with an electronica-enhanced rhythmic texture.
Queen in the studio was like Dread Pirate Roberts fighting left-handed.
Few bands can match their energy, none are fronted by the greatest showman of them all.
posts.key_insight: Musical innovation thrives at the intersection of genres and traditions, and the most compelling artists are those who challenge the boundaries of their instruments and styles.

posts.summary: The article describes Thoughtworks' 'Data Mesh Accelerate Workshop,' a structured multi-day workshop designed to help organizations begin their Data Mesh transformation. It identifies two common pitfalls in Data Mesh adoption: starting with technology without business alignment, and spending too long in big design upfront analysis. The workshop provides a middle path with collaborative activities that build shared understanding, define a nirvana vision, establish metrics and OKRs, explore use cases, and discover data products. The piece is essentially a practitioner's guide with step-by-step facilitation instructions for each workshop activity.
key_points: Data Mesh transformations commonly fail by either focusing too much on technology without business alignment or falling into big design upfront analysis paralysis

The workshop bridges business and technical stakeholders through structured activities: defining a nirvana statement, assessing 4 Key Metrics, setting OKRs, exploring use cases, and discovering data products
Product thinking is central to Data Mesh — data products are the architectural quantum, and use cases must connect back to business objectives to avoid 'build it and they will come' pitfalls
The workshop emphasizes evolutionary, incremental approaches over comprehensive upfront planning, with short feedback loops
money_quotes: To start from the technical side without being very aligned with the business strategic goals is like packing for a holiday without knowing where you are going.
Diagrams and plans are great, but the value comes from making them real and delivering value to the users and the business.
Build it and they will come is a pitfall that many transformation efforts fall into.
Tell me how you measure me and I will tell you how I behave.
Before talking about options, actions, initiatives, or any execution task towards achieving something big, it is essential to align on that high level goal.
posts.key_insight: Successful Data Mesh transformations require starting with business-technical alignment through collaborative workshops rather than diving into technology or exhaustive upfront analysis.

posts.summary: Aino Vonge Corry identifies common antipatterns that undermine retrospectives, drawing from her experience as a facilitator. She argues that retrospectives have become widespread due to agile adoption, but many teams go through the motions without learning proper facilitation. Through a Danish team's story, she illustrates three antipatterns: Wheel of Fortune (jumping to solutions without understanding root causes), In the Soup (wasting time discussing things the team cannot change), and Loudmouth (allowing one person to dominate discussion). Each antipattern comes with a refactored solution drawn from established facilitation techniques. The core message is that retrospectives fail not because the concept is flawed, but because teams skip the hard work of generating genuine insights.
key_points: Skipping the 'generate insights' stage of retrospectives leads teams to treat symptoms rather than root causes, making the exercise feel wasteful
Teams should categorize issues into things they can control, influence, or must accept ('in the soup') to avoid frustration over unchangeable circumstances
Dominant voices in retrospectives can be managed through structural changes like small-group discussions and writing exercises rather than confrontation
The popularity of agile has spread retrospectives widely but without the facilitation knowledge needed to make them effective
The most important facilitation skill is listening and de-escalating conflict, not memorizing activities
money_quotes: When people claim that retrospectives are a waste of time, I often agree with them, when I hear how they do it.
If the post-it notes only show solutions to symptoms, not the actual problems, you can only fix the surface.
When teams ask for fewer meetings, it is often improved meeting hygiene that can solve the real problem.
When a team 'solves' symptoms instead of problems, the problems will still be there, and they will show up again.
Your time is better spent accepting and finding a way to adapt to the situation. Or changing your situation by removing yourself from the soup.

The most important skill a facilitator can have is not to know a lot of activities by heart, but to listen, to use their intellect to de-escalate conflict and to continue to reflect and learn what works for them.
posts.key_insight: Retrospectives fail when teams skip generating genuine insights about root causes and instead jump directly from surface-level symptoms to action items.

posts.summary: This article argues that React applications should be structured using established UI patterns like layered architecture rather than cramming all logic into components. The author walks through the progressive evolution of a Payment feature, demonstrating how to extract concerns into custom hooks, domain model classes, pure presentational components, and network access layers. Using the Strategy pattern, the article shows how to eliminate shotgun surgery when adding country-specific payment logic. The core thesis is that frontend applications deserve the same separation of concerns that backend and desktop GUI applications have long employed, and React should be treated as just the view layer.
key_points: React is a view library, not a framework — frontend apps should separate view code from business logic, data access, and state management just like any other software
The evolutionary path from single-component apps to layered architecture follows natural steps: extract components, extract hooks for state, extract domain models, then separate data access
Domain model classes (like PaymentMethod) encapsulate business logic and data transformation, preventing logic leakage into views and hooks
The Strategy pattern eliminates shotgun surgery when country-specific variations would otherwise scatter conditional branches across multiple files
An Anti-Corruption Layer between network fetching and domain models limits the blast radius of API changes to a single file
money_quotes: There are front-end applications written in JavaScript or TypeScript that happen to use React as their views. However, I think it's not fair to call them React applications, just as we wouldn't call a Java EE application JSP application.

Frontend applications should not be treated too differently from regular software applications. In the frontend world, you still use separation of concerns in general to arrange the code structure. And all the proven useful design patterns still apply.
Trying to squeeze everything into React components or hooks is generally not a good idea. The reason is mixing concepts in one place generally leads to more confusion.
The reader must constantly reset their logic flow and jump back and forth from different levels of details.
These test statements in a view can be considered a logic leak, and gradually they can be scattered in different places and make modification harder.
You can think of it this way: the React view is only one of the consumers of your non-view code.

Building React application, or a frontend application with React as its view, should not be treated as a new type of software. Most of the patterns and principles for building the traditional user interface still apply.
posts.key_insight: React applications should treat React as just the view layer and apply established separation-of-concerns patterns — domain models, layered architecture, and strategy pattern — to keep business logic independent, testable, and reusable outside any UI framework.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler celebrates Brass Birmingham reaching the #1 spot on BoardGameGeek's rankings, detailing his personal history with the hobby and BGG's ranking system. He explains what makes Brass Birmingham special: its Industrial Revolution theme, its symbiotic player interaction model where helping others also helps yourself, and its beautiful production quality. Fowler distinguishes this heavy strategy game from casual fare and traces its lineage from Brass Lancashire (2007). He recommends playing it twice within a week to internalize the rules and appreciate the depth.
key_points: Brass Birmingham displaced Gloomhaven as BGG's #1 ranked game, returning the top spot to a one-off strategy game rather than a campaign game
The game features a rare symbiotic interaction model where players benefit from using each other's resources, rather than purely destructive or resource-competition mechanics
The game is set during the Industrial Revolution in England, which Fowler calls 'arguably the most important period of recorded human history'
Brass Birmingham (2018) is a successor to Brass Lancashire (2007), both designed by Martin Wallace and published by the small company Roxley
money_quotes: Brass offers a rarer form of interaction, where players are involved in a form of symbiotic growth.
The German revolution in boardgames in the 1990s made a point of avoiding this kind of interaction, preferring one based on competing for resources.
Clara could build her own iron works to supply her cotton mill, but if Clara and Alice specialize, they both build higher-scoring industries.

It's set at the birth of the Industrial Revolution in England, arguably the most important period of recorded human history, where humanity went from a world powered by muscle into a world powered by steam engines.
I've certainly found them a useful way to identify games that might interest me, aware that while many highly ranked games aren't to my taste, those that do fit are almost always worth trying out.
posts.key_insight: Brass Birmingham's defining quality is its symbiotic player interaction model, where using other players' resources creates mutual benefit, elevating it beyond traditional competitive or resource-blocking mechanics.

posts.summary: Continuous flow is an agile scheduling approach where teams prioritize user stories in a list and pull new work as they complete items, using WIP limits to stay focused on finishing rather than accumulating nearly-done work. It eliminates the overhead of iteration planning, estimation, and capacity calculation. However, the approach works best for experienced teams handling unpredictable work streams, while less experienced teams benefit from the feedback loops that timeboxed iterations provide. Teams using continuous flow should maintain enough slack to handle surges—a continuously busy team is a warning sign.
key_points: WIP limits prevent accumulation of 'nearly done' work and keep teams focused on completing stories
Continuous flow suits unpredictable work streams like bug fixing, but teams need slack capacity to handle surges
The approach eliminates iteration ceremony but loses the feedback loops that help spot problems like technical debt
Continuous flow works well for skilled teams but less experienced teams benefit from iteration structure
The method handles unpredictable arrival of work better than unpredictable nature of work
money_quotes: Once they reach the WIP limit, they can't start any more stories until they complete one already in progress.
WIP limits are important because they keep the team focused on finishing stories, otherwise it's too easy to build up a pile of 'nearly done' work.
If a continuous flow team is always busy, that's usually a red flag.
The regular cadence of iterations provide a feedback loop that helps a team spot problems such as cruft building up in the code base.
Continuous flow is effective for skilled teams that want to reduce the ceremony in their work, but less experienced teams are better off with iterations.
Continuous flow is good for the unpredictable arrival of work, but less so when the nature of the work is unpredictable or poorly understood.
posts.key_insight: Continuous flow trades iteration overhead for autonomy, but that trade-off only pays off for experienced teams who don't need the guardrails that regular cadences provide.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that deliberately leaving unallocated time (slack) in iteration planning, rather than maximizing story allocation, actually improves team productivity. He recommends planning at the team's lowest consistent velocity to increase predictability. Slack time can be productively used for tackling technical debt, improving tooling, deepening customer understanding, or responding to urgent cross-team requests. While slack might seem inefficient, teams without it cannot address impediments that accumulate and slow them down over time. The principle applies equally to timeboxed iterations and continuous flow approaches.
key_points: Plan at your lowest consistent velocity (e.g., 15 stories if range is 15-22) to increase predictability, which is often worth more than maximizing throughput
Slack time is best spent on removing impediments—build inefficiencies, code cruft, tooling gaps—that compound into larger productivity drains
High utilization increases latency; slack enables faster response to urgent requests and cross-team collaboration
Teams that are always busy have 'not enough slack'—a smell indicating they cannot maintain their working environment
Slack is a seasoning, not the main dish—too much gives up visibility, but running without it is like skimping on oil changes
money_quotes: For planning and coordination, higher confidence is often worth more than trying to maximize throughput.
Most teams are slowed by factors in their working environment.
The most common productivity problem teams face is due to a congested schedule that allows these impediments to fester.
