money_quotes: He recognized that these were called legacies because they were valuable to their recipients.
The core principle of agile that John understood was that agile habits were founded upon humans working together.
Don't worry about that stuff. The most important thing is to get people talking to each other.
His goal was much greater than agile, it was to bring decency and happiness to people's working lives.
A significant professional life is not a once-and-done affair. Every engagement is first and foremost an engagement with a fresh group of unique persons.
Software is a social activity, and change is often best rooted in day-to-day activities and habits, in trust, and can be most enduring when it emerges from the ground up.
You can do anything with Excel if you really have to.
posts.key_insight: Technical excellence in software development is inseparable from human kindness and the ability to build genuine relationships with the people you work alongside.

posts.summary: This article describes Thoughtworks' approach to incrementally migrating a 40-year-old, 7-million-line-of-code mainframe system to the cloud for a UK client's data platform. Rather than attempting a risky 'Big Bang' cutover, they advocated for identifying 'seams'—junction points in the legacy code where new behavior could be inserted with minimal changes. The approach involved dual-running both systems in parallel, comparing outputs to verify behavioral parity, then gradually retiring mainframe capabilities. The team identified multiple seam types: external interfaces (batch inputs, APIs), internal interfaces (database readers/writers, batch pipeline handoffs), and granular data characteristics. While Thoughtworks was only involved for the first year and didn't reach production, they validated multiple approaches for incremental legacy displacement.
key_points: Seams are junction points where code or modules meet, allowing behavior modification with minimal mainframe code changes—they enable incremental migration rather than Big Bang replacement
Dual Run architecture runs both legacy and new systems in parallel with identical inputs, comparing outputs to verify behavioral parity before retiring mainframe capabilities
External seams (file transfers, APIs) allow silent migration from customers' perspective using Dark Launching and Canary Release patterns
Database interactions serve as major seams—separating readers (replicated via CDC to cloud) from writers (migrated incrementally with data replicated back to mainframe)
Complex identity rules required granular data characteristic seams with fallback mechanisms, since running identity-minting in parallel would cause drift
money_quotes: We were wary of the risks and potential pitfalls of a Big Design Up Front, so we advised our client to work on a 'just enough, and just in time' upfront design, with engineering during the first phase.

You can think of them as the junction points where code, programs, or modules meet... they can be employed strategically to alter the behaviour of applications, for example to intercept data flows within the Mainframe allowing for capabilities to be offloaded to a new system.

The client's workload was largely analytical, processing mostly external data to produce insight which was sold on to clients. We therefore saw an opportunity to split our transformation programme in two parts, one around data curation, the other around data serving.
Our goal was to achieve a silent migration process, where customers would seamlessly transition between systems without any noticeable impact.
How much this will cost depends on various factors, but you cannot expect to save on costs via dual running two systems in parallel.
Being capability-driven helps ensure that you are not building another tangled system, and keeps responsibilities and concerns separate at their appropriate layers.
Unless you take steps to abstract the schema when replicating a database, then your new cloud products will be coupled to the legacy schema as soon as they're built.
posts.key_insight: Successful mainframe modernization requires identifying architectural seams—natural boundaries in legacy code—that enable incremental migration with dual-run verification, rather than risky wholesale replacement.

posts.summary: Fowler explains his evolving approach to footnotes in writing. He initially avoided them, believing important content belonged in the main text. While writing about Obama's 2008 campaign technology work, he discovered footnotes helped preserve narrative flow while retaining important details. He recently updated his site to show footnotes inline when clicked rather than jumping to the page bottom. He deliberately rejected sidenotes because they're too easy to read inadvertently, breaking reader flow.
key_points: Footnotes serve to include details without derailing the main prose narrative
The new inline rendering keeps readers in place rather than scrolling to page bottom
Sidenotes are rejected because they break reader flow by being too visible
Following a footnote should be a deliberate conscious choice by the reader
The footnote reference design must be subtle yet accessible for touch interfaces
money_quotes: My general view was that if it was important enough to worth writing about, it should make its way into the main text, otherwise it should be discarded.
They were for details that I wanted to include, but not derail the main thrust of the prose.
The trouble with this rendering is that it's too easy for the reader's eye to glance over and read the sidenote's text.
I like the need to click on the reference, because then it's easier for the reader to refrain from the effort to do it.
I want the decision to follow the footnote to be a conscious choice.
posts.key_insight: Good footnote design requires intentional friction—readers should consciously choose to pursue tangential details rather than having them interrupt the main narrative flow.

posts.summary: This article examines data fetching patterns in single-page applications, addressing the challenge of maintaining responsive UIs while handling asynchronous operations. Fowler's co-author Juntao Qiu presents five key patterns: Asynchronous State Handler (decoupling fetch logic from UI), Parallel Data Fetching (avoiding request waterfalls), Fallback Markup (declarative loading states with Suspense), Code Splitting (lazy loading non-critical components), and Prefetching (loading data before it's needed). The piece uses a Profile component example throughout, progressively adding complexity to demonstrate each pattern. While React is used for examples, the patterns apply broadly across frontend frameworks.
key_points: Request waterfalls occur when components fetch data sequentially in nested hierarchies, significantly degrading performance - parallel fetching with Promise.all can eliminate this
The Asynchronous State Handler pattern separates data fetching concerns from UI rendering, making components more testable and reusable
Fallback Markup using Suspense enables declarative handling of loading states, shifting responsibility for loading indicators to component consumers
Code splitting with dynamic imports and lazy loading reduces initial bundle size but requires careful consideration of user experience during load
Prefetching on user interactions (like hover) can be combined with code splitting to parallelize bundle and data loading
money_quotes: The era of blank pages taking 5 seconds to load is long gone.

Data fetching is challenging, not only because the nature of async programming doesn't fit our linear mindset, and there are so many factors that can cause a network call to fail, but also there are too many not-obvious cases to consider under the hood.
The longest wait time depends on the slowest network request, which is much faster than the sequential ones.
Think of Friends and FriendsSkeleton as two distinct component states—one representing the presence of data, and the other, the absence.
This approach is also known as Fetch-Then-Render, suggesting that the aim is to initiate requests as early as possible during page load.
Prefetching involves loading resources or data ahead of their actual need, aiming to decrease wait times during subsequent operations.
Don't implement complex patterns like prefetching until they are clearly needed.
posts.key_insight: Effective data fetching in SPAs requires combining multiple patterns—parallel fetching, code splitting, and prefetching—to eliminate request waterfalls while keeping the codebase maintainable through proper separation of concerns.

posts.summary: The article argues that server-side HTML templates contain logic that deserves automated testing, not just manual browser inspection. It demonstrates a progression of testing techniques: first validating HTML soundness with parsers, then using CSS selectors to verify specific elements and content, then parameterizing tests for easy extension, and finally using Playwright with headless browsers to test JavaScript-driven behavior like HTMX interactions. The author shows that testing templates catches real errors and improves code quality, including forcing better accessibility practices.
key_points: Manual testing of templates is insufficient because logic branches (if/else, iterations, null checks) create many cases that are tedious to verify by hand and easy to break silently
CSS selectors provide a powerful way to assert on specific HTML elements while ignoring irrelevant details, making tests focused and maintainable
Parameterized tests with clear naming make it trivial to add new test cases and produce readable output showing exactly what's being verified
Headless browser testing with Playwright enables testing JavaScript behavior (like HTMX) while still keeping tests isolated from server-side logic
Testing HTML the way users interact with it (via ARIA roles) naturally leads to better accessibility in the generated markup
money_quotes: Relying on manual tests only is risky; what if we make a change that breaks a template, and we don't test it because we did not think it would impact the template?
What we need is a technique to assert that some parts of the rendered HTML correspond to what we expect, and to ignore all the details we don't care about.
Make it easy to add new test cases.
We made the test code a little bit more complicated, so that the test cases are clearer: this is a very good tradeoff!
Testing HTML the same way users interact with it led us to use ARIA roles, which led to improving accessibility of our generated HTML.
The test costs 2-3 seconds of waiting time for the headless browser to come up, which is usually too much for a unit test; however, like a unit test, it is very stable, as it is not flaky.
When we start testing these templates, we inevitably find errors: the templates do not always produce the HTML we think.
posts.key_insight: HTML templates contain testable logic that benefits from the same TDD practices we apply to other code, using CSS selectors to focus assertions on what matters while ignoring irrelevant markup details.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that regulating AI and algorithms by restricting their use misses the point—the real issue is opacity, not the technology itself. He contends that any decision affecting people's lives must be explainable, whether made by AI, complex algorithms, or simple chronological sorting. Using a personal Avis rental car dispute as an example, he demonstrates how opacity causes harm regardless of whether AI is involved. Fowler suggests that requiring explainability would naturally limit problematic AI deployment while incentivizing development of more transparent systems. He proposes that AI's best use may be as a tool for humans to better understand decision-making processes, potentially replacing opaque models with explicit logic over time.
key_points: A reverse-chronological feed is itself an algorithm—banning 'algorithms' fundamentally misunderstands the problem
Opacity in decision-making causes harm regardless of whether AI is involved, as demonstrated by mundane software errors
Explainability requirements would naturally restrict problematic AI use while incentivizing transparent AI development
Humans who rely on AI suggestions must still be able to explain their reasoning independently—frequent agreement with computer suggestions should be treated as a smell
AI models might best serve as stepping stones to human understanding, helping us develop more explicit decision-making logic
money_quotes: The general principle should be that decisions made by software must be explainable.
A regulation that says a social media company should forego its 'algorithm' for a reverse-chronological feed misses the fact that a reverse-chronological feed is itself an algorithm.
The mechanism of the decision-making wasn't the issue, the opacity was.
We should consider it as a smell for human to agree with computer suggestions too often.
We rightly fear that AI may lead to more opaque decision making, but perhaps with the right incentives we can use AI tools as stepping stones to greater human knowledge.
Such explanations are beyond most current AI tools. For me this is a reasonable rationale to restrict their usage, at least until developments to improve the explainability of AI bear fruit.
posts.key_insight: The problem with AI and algorithmic decision-making isn't the technology itself but the opacity—requiring explainability is a more effective regulatory principle than restricting specific technologies.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler revisits his influential 'Strangler Fig' metaphor for legacy system modernization, inspired by observing strangler figs in Queensland that gradually replace their host trees. He argues against the common approach of wholesale system replacement, which frequently fails due to timeline pressures and specification difficulties. Instead, he advocates for incremental modernization that builds new components alongside legacy systems, gradually replacing functionality. The approach requires clear outcomes, identifying seams for decomposition, delivering small replaceable pieces, and crucially, organizational change to prevent recreating the same problems.
key_points: Wholesale legacy system replacement usually fails because it takes too long, users can't wait for features, and existing behavior is hard to specify accurately
Incremental modernization requires four activities: understanding outcomes, breaking the problem into parts, delivering those parts, and changing the organization sustainably
Legacy systems rarely have clean component boundaries, so finding or creating 'seams' is essential work before replacement can begin
Transitional architecture that allows new and legacy systems to coexist is worth the temporary cost due to reduced risk and earlier value delivery
Organizational culture and processes must change alongside the technical modernization, or new systems will become equally rigid and brittle
money_quotes: We've seen this simple-sounding plan go down in flames most of the time.

Replacements seem easy to specify, but often it's hard to figure out the details of existing behavior. What's worse, much of that behavior is stuff that isn't really wanted, so building it is a waste.
In a well-designed system, these seams would already exist. But such systems are unicorns.
While this may appear to be a waste, the reduced risk and earlier value from the gradual approach outweigh its costs.
Legacy systems become rigid and brittle because the design thinking and organizational processes that produced them built them that way.
If there's no change in organizational culture and leadership, the new systems will end up in a similar mess.
Fortunately the new title strangled the old one.
posts.key_insight: Legacy modernization succeeds through gradual strangulation rather than wholesale replacement, but technical change alone is insufficient without parallel organizational transformation.

posts.summary: Cycle time measures how long it takes to move a feature from idea to production, and minimizing it is a core goal of agile development. The concept shifts focus from activity-based waterfall processes to iterative, functionality-based work that enables rapid feedback loops. However, cycle time is inherently slippery because teams define start and stop points differently—from first commit to initial idea, from canary release to full deployment. Fowler advises against comparing cycle times between teams without consistent definitions, and suggests using distinguishing adjectives rather than trying to separate 'cycle time' from 'lead time.' Despite the measurement challenges, pursuing shorter cycle times through value stream mapping, smaller features, and increased slack remains worthwhile for faster feedback and learning.
key_points: Agile development shifts from activity-based decomposition (waterfall) to functionality-based iteration, creating feedback loops that enable learning
Cycle time measurement is inherently ambiguous—both start points (first commit vs. initial idea) and stop points (canary release vs. full deployment) vary between teams
The terms 'cycle time' and 'lead time' are used inconsistently across the industry and are best treated as synonyms unless explicitly defined
Two effective interventions for reducing cycle time are decreasing feature size and counter-intuitively increasing slack
Value stream mapping helps identify workflow steps and waiting times, revealing opportunities for improvement
money_quotes: This feedback is a core benefit of an iterative approach, and like most such feedback loops, the quicker I get the feedback, the happier I am.
What these questions tell us is that cycle time, while a useful concept, is inherently slippery.
We should be wary of comparing cycle times between teams, unless we can be confident we have consistent notions of their stop and start times.
Two commonly effective interventions are to reduce the size of features and (counter-intuitively) increase Slack.
The faster we get ideas into production, the more rapidly we gain the benefits of the new features, and get the feedback to learn and improve our ways of working.
posts.key_insight: Cycle time is a valuable concept for driving faster feedback loops, but its inherent measurement ambiguity means teams should focus on consistent internal definitions rather than cross-team comparisons.

posts.summary: The article explores how to apply architectural fitness functions to govern data products in a data mesh architecture. Traditional governance through data stewards doesn't scale with decentralized teams, so automation becomes essential. Fitness functions are tests that evaluate how close implementations are to design objectives, enabling 'shift left' governance where issues are caught early. The piece details specific assertions for key data product characteristics like discoverability, addressability, trustworthiness, and interoperability. Implementation patterns include running tests within data catalogs, using external processes with catalog APIs, and leveraging LLMs to interpret metadata quality. Publishing results on dashboards creates social incentives for teams to maintain governance standards.
key_points: Data mesh decentralizes analytical data ownership to domain-aligned teams, requiring automated governance that scales better than manual data steward inspections
Fitness functions prioritize governance by rule over inspection, empowering teams to discover problems over independent audits, and continuous governance over dedicated audit phases
Key architectural characteristics to test include discoverability, addressability, self-descriptiveness, security, interoperability, trustworthiness, and standalone value
LLMs can evaluate subjective metadata quality like whether a data product is 'valuable on its own' or has meaningful descriptions
In one client implementation, over 80% of published data products failed basic fitness tests when evaluated retrospectively
money_quotes: Traditional ways of enforcing governance rules using data stewards work against the idea of autonomous teams and do not scale in a distributed setup.
By using fitness functions, we're aiming to 'shift left' on governance, meaning we identify potential governance issues earlier in the timeline of the software value stream.
Governance by rule over Governance by inspection. Empowering teams to discover problems over Independent audits. Continuous governance over Dedicated audit phase.
The term 'data product' has unfortunately taken on various self-serving meanings, and fully disambiguating them could warrant a separate article.

Publicly sharing these dashboards within the organization can serve as a powerful incentive for the teams to adhere to the governance standards. After all, no one wants to be the team with the most red marks.
In one of our client implementations, we found that over 80% of published data products failed to pass basic fitness tests when evaluated retrospectively.
posts.key_insight: Automated fitness functions that test data product metadata against governance standards provide scalable, continuous governance that empowers decentralized teams while ensuring interoperability across a data mesh.

posts.summary: This article argues that while GenAI in software development has primarily focused on code generation, its greatest potential lies in understanding and modernizing legacy systems. Thoughtworks developed CodeConcise, an internal accelerator that uses LLMs combined with knowledge graphs to make legacy modernization more cost-effective. The approach treats code as data, parsing it into Abstract Syntax Trees stored in graph databases, then enriching it with LLM-generated explanations at various abstraction levels. In a proof of concept, they reduced reverse engineering time from 6 weeks to 2 weeks per module, potentially saving 240 FTE years for a complete mainframe modernization program. The authors acknowledge this is early-stage work with context-dependent results, but see significant promise in addressing problems that have made large-scale modernization prohibitively expensive.
key_points: GenAI's most valuable application in software may be understanding existing code rather than generating new code, since developers spend more time reading code than writing it

CodeConcise uses a 'code as data' approach, parsing code into Abstract Syntax Trees stored in graph databases, then traversing and enriching the graph with LLM-generated explanations at multiple abstraction levels
The knowledge graph approach reduces noise by extracting granular relationships, allowing LLMs to analyze code independently of how developers originally organized it
A proof of concept reduced reverse engineering time by two-thirds, from 6 weeks to 2 weeks per 10,000-line module, with potential savings of 240 FTE years for a complete mainframe modernization

GenAI can help extract capability maps, generate abstracted flowcharts for business analysts, and reduce dependency on scarce subject matter experts who are often stretched thin 'just to keep the lights on'
money_quotes: We believe there is as much, if not more, value in understanding existing code than generating new code
Developers spend a lot more time reading code than writing it
The ability to traverse the codebase at such a level of granularity is particularly important as it reduces noise from the context provided to LLMs
This translates to a potential saving of 240 FTE years for the entire mainframe modernization program
Often, they are stretched too thin across multiple teams just to 'keep the lights on'

There is no one-size-fits-all solution for this; it will depend on the specific context, the insights one aims to extract from their code, and, ultimately, on the principles and approaches that the development teams followed when constructing the solution's codebase
Typically the produced code suffers from poor overall code quality, non-idiomatic code, and poor naming conventions
posts.key_insight: GenAI's transformative potential for legacy modernization lies not in generating code but in systematically extracting understanding from existing codebases through knowledge graphs, reducing the prohibitive cost and expertise barriers that have long made large-scale modernization impractical.

posts.summary: This article demonstrates how Thoughtworks applied the Strangler Fig pattern to incrementally modernize a large legacy mobile application, avoiding the risks of a complete rewrite. By embedding React Native micro-apps within an existing native application and using domain-driven design to identify 'points of interception,' teams were able to replace functionality piece by piece. The approach leverages a modular architecture where each micro-app encapsulates a domain with its own Backend for Frontend acting as an anti-corruption layer. Results from the proof of concept showed first value delivered in 1 month (versus 2 years for a full rewrite) and a 50% reduction in median cycle time compared to the legacy application.
key_points: The Strangler Fig pattern enables incremental replacement of legacy mobile apps by embedding new functionality (React Native micro-apps) inside the existing native application
Domain-driven design helps identify 'points of interception' where navigation routes can redirect users to new experiences while preserving the ability to fall back
The React Native Bridge facilitates bidirectional communication between legacy native code and new micro-apps, enabling seamless state sharing including sensitive data via secure keystores
Contract testing at domain boundaries allows independent teams to work on native and React Native code without coupling their build success to each other's changes
The proof of concept delivered first value in 1 month compared to a 2-year full rewrite estimate, with 50% faster cycle times for changes within micro-apps
money_quotes: A mobile application was never allowed to get so complicated, that it couldn't be torn down, split out or started from scratch again.
Taking an incremental approach to app replacement then is the key to avoiding the pitfalls associated with 'big bang releases'.

Our focus was not on delivering the most beautiful or cohesive full app experience. It was about obtaining confidence both in the stability of the iterative replacement pattern and also in how well the new product was being received.
Simply layering complexity to achieve our goals was only part of the problem to be solved.
It took around 1 month to implant the micro-app structure into the existing application, 3 months to build the first micro-app, and 5 months for the second.
Legacy Modernization, regardless of technology, is a multifaceted problem that demands significant analysis and alignment.
Putting in the investment upfront, will not only help you select the correct tool for your situation, but ensure that your app is better aligned to the customers it serves and the problems it solves.
posts.key_insight: The Strangler Fig pattern, traditionally used for backend systems, can effectively modernize legacy mobile applications by embedding domain-encapsulated micro-apps that incrementally replace functionality while delivering value immediately rather than waiting years for a complete rewrite.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler reflects on the fragmented state of social media two years after Elon Musk's Twitter takeover. He describes his preference for curated feeds over algorithmic suggestions and uses social media primarily to announce articles rather than for social interaction. While Twitter persists, its importance has declined as users migrated to platforms like Bluesky and LinkedIn. Fowler finds LinkedIn most effective for reaching readers despite its corporate atmosphere, while the Fediverse hasn't gained expected traction. He expresses hope for open protocols like Bluesky's AT protocol to provide resilience against future platform instability.
key_points: Social media activity has fragmented across multiple platforms since Musk's Twitter acquisition, with no single successor emerging
LinkedIn has become the most effective platform for reaching tech readers despite its corporate culture and poor user experience
Bluesky's open AT protocol offers hope for platform resilience and reduces risk of another Twitter-style takeover
The Fediverse failed to gain mainstream adoption because choosing a server proved too awkward for most users
Fowler treats all social media posts as ephemeral, keeping anything meant to last on his own website
money_quotes: I consider any post I make on social media to be ephemeral - anything I write that's meant to be long-lasting I keep on this site.
Twitter isn't dead, but declines as people leave.
One of the things I like about Bluesky is its use of an open protocol, which reduces the chances that it will follow the fate that befell Twitter.
Too many people found that choosing a server was too awkward, and that the advantages of federation were outweighed by the complexities of its usage.
I have little control over my feed, and my notifications are full of 'someone liked your post' events.
Despite all that, I've noticed that I'm getting more reactions to my article announcements on LinkedIn that anywhere else.
I first started using social media (in some form) in the late 1980's with Usenet. I learned quickly that I disliked the often-heated arguments that blew up there.
posts.key_insight: The fragmentation of social media after Twitter's decline has made open protocols like Bluesky's AT protocol increasingly valuable as insurance against future platform instability.

posts.summary: This article provides a practical methodology for designing data products as part of a data mesh implementation. The authors advocate for a 'working backwards' approach, starting from specific use cases rather than data sources, to avoid analysis paralysis. They define what data products are (self-contained, deployable units serving analytical data) and what they're not (dashboards, PDFs, or entire data warehouses). The process involves short workshops to identify data products, overlay multiple use cases to ensure reusability, assign clear domain ownership, and define service level objectives. The article concludes with implementation guidance including establishing reusable blueprints and automating governance.
key_points: Data products must exhibit eight key characteristics: discoverable, addressable, understandable, trustworthy, natively accessible, interoperable, valuable on its own, and secure
Working backwards from use cases prevents over-design and analysis paralysis that occurs when starting from data sources
A well-defined data product should be describable in one or two simple sentences; difficulty doing so indicates poor definition
For structured data, a data product typically means a single denormalized table; anything larger is likely trying to do too much
No single data product should be owned by multiple domains, as this leads to confusion and finger-pointing over quality issues
money_quotes: There's a common tendency to start with the data sources and define data products. Without the constraints of a tangible use case, you won't know when your design is good enough to move forward with implementation, which often leads to analysis paralysis and lots of wasted effort.
If you find it difficult to describe a data product in one or two simple sentences, it's likely not well-defined.
No single data product should be owned by multiple domains, as this can lead to confusion and finger-pointing over quality issues.
For structured data, this usually means a single denormalized table, and for semi-structured or unstructured data, a single dataset. Anything larger is likely trying to do too much.
Conflating data product with too many different concepts not only creates confusion among teams but also makes it significantly harder to develop reusable blueprints.
The defined SLOs will guide the architecture, solution design and implementation of the data product.
If they need to prioritise one aspect of data mesh, it should be 'data as a product'. Focusing on getting that right establishes a strong foundation, enabling the other pillars to follow naturally.
posts.key_insight: Designing data products should start from concrete use cases and work backwards to data sources, ensuring each product represents a single cohesive information concept describable in one or two sentences.

posts.summary: Design tokens are design decisions represented as data, serving as a single source of truth for both design and engineering teams. The article explains how tokens solve cross-team consistency problems (like z-index conflicts in micro-frontends) by providing a shared vocabulary. Tokens should be organized in layers—option tokens define available styles, decision tokens specify how styles are applied, and component tokens map to specific UI elements. A deployment pipeline can automate token distribution, enabling design changes to propagate across multiple frontends in a single day. The author recommends starting with two layers and adding complexity only when needed, while also considering token scope to limit which tokens are exposed to developers.
key_points: Design tokens act as a common language between disciplines, tools, and technologies—enabling cross-platform consistency and efficient design-to-code workflows
A layered architecture (option, decision, component tokens) organizes design decisions at appropriate abstraction levels, balancing maintainability with developer experience
Automated deployment pipelines using Git, translation tools like Style Dictionary, and package registries can propagate design changes across teams within a single day
Token scope (public vs private) guides developers toward appropriate tokens while allowing internal tokens to change without breaking downstream code
Starting with two layers is pragmatic for stable designs; a third component layer adds flexibility for evolving decisions but increases maintenance overhead
money_quotes: Design tokens, or 'tokens' are fundamental design decisions represented as data.
Design tokens act as a common language that aligns designers, developers, product managers, and other disciplines.
A key benefit of design tokens is their ability to serve as a single source of truth for both design and engineering teams.
This overall setup has allowed teams at Thoughtworks to roll out smaller design changes across multiple front-ends and teams in a single day.
As a developer, I would mostly be interested in the decisions, not the options.
A three-layer architecture should offer the best developer experience. However, it also increases maintenance effort and token count.
Ultimately, the number of layers depends on your project's needs and how much flexibility and scalability are required.
posts.key_insight: Design tokens transform design decisions into versionable, distributable data that can be automatically propagated across platforms and teams through deployment pipelines.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his eight favorite musical discoveries of 2024, breaking his usual tradition of limiting the list to six. The selections span an eclectic range of global music, from Irish pipes blended with jazz beats to Tunisian protest songs, Belgian trip-hop, Icelandic jazz-fusion, and Indian classical music merged with string quartets. Each album is described with evocative language highlighting what makes it distinctive. Fowler credits music journalism sources like Dave Sumner's Bandcamp column and Songlines magazine for leading him to these lesser-known artists.
key_points: The selection deliberately focuses on obscure artists that most readers likely won't know
Musical fusion and cross-cultural blending is a consistent thread across all eight picks
Fowler values texture, distinctive sounds, and artists willing to push into new territories
Sources like Dave Sumner's jazz column and Songlines magazine guide his musical exploration
This is part of an annual tradition dating back to at least 2020
money_quotes: I'd be surprised if many readers are familiar with more than a couple of these, and expect most readers to not know of any of them.
Iceland has given the world a few quirky musical innovators, unafraid to push into new territories.
The result is distinctly a horn-led jazz sound, inspired by Ornette Coleman, with solid melodic sense and a finish that's vaguely unsettling.
It's full of little surprises, like a walk though a cheerful, yet unknown city, smiling at the oddities we bump into.
I enjoy music that's about texture, and this quartet weaves a delightfully soft sound playing around and accentuating the warm resonances of the vibraphone.
Dave Sumner's column has led me into many less-travelled corners of Jazz where gems like this have been lurking.
posts.key_insight: Great musical discovery comes from following trusted curators into less-travelled corners where cross-cultural fusion and distinctive sounds reward the adventurous listener.

posts.summary: The article explains how codemods—automated scripts that use Abstract Syntax Tree manipulation—enable developers to apply large-scale code transformations consistently across codebases. Starting with the challenge library developers face when introducing breaking API changes, Fowler demonstrates how codemods parse code into ASTs, modify the tree structure, and rewrite the source. Through practical examples including feature toggle cleanup and React component refactoring, he shows how to write, test, and compose codemods using tools like jscodeshift. The piece also covers handling edge cases, applying these techniques in Java with JavaParser and OpenRewrite, and leveraging platforms like Hypermod and Codemod.com.
key_points: Codemods automate code transformations by parsing source into ASTs, applying modifications, and rewriting—the same process IDEs use for refactoring but at scale across entire codebases
Test-Driven Development works particularly well for codemods since inputs and outputs are clearly defined, and negative test cases prevent unintended changes
Complex transformations should be broken into smaller, composable codemods that can be tested independently and reused across different refactoring tasks

Edge cases from diverse coding styles (import aliases, variable assignments, logical negations) require careful handling through source graph analysis, linting standardization, and strategic use of TODO comments
The approach extends beyond JavaScript to Java via JavaParser and OpenRewrite, with OpenRewrite's Lossless Semantic Trees capturing both syntax and semantic meaning for more accurate transformations
money_quotes: By using this approach, codemods ensure that changes are applied consistently across every file in a codebase, reducing the chance of human error.
The codemod needs to *understand* the structure of the code to apply changes selectively.

This approach aligns well with Test-Driven Development (TDD), even if you don't practice TDD regularly. Knowing exactly what the transformation's inputs and outputs are before coding improves safety and efficiency.
A more maintainable approach is to treat codemod logic like product code: break the task into smaller, independent pieces—just like how you would normally refactor production code.
In situations where we couldn't confidently automate the upgrade, we inserted comments or 'TODOs' at the call sites. This allowed the developers running the script to handle specific cases manually.
Relying solely on the cases you can anticipate is not enough. You need thorough testing to avoid breaking unintended parts of the code.
Codemods can be highly effective, but their success depends on thoughtful design and understanding the limitations they may face in more varied or complex codebases.
posts.key_insight: Codemods bring the same AST-based transformation power that IDEs provide locally to the scale of entire codebases, but their effectiveness depends on breaking complex changes into composable, well-tested pieces and acknowledging that edge cases will require human judgment.

posts.summary: The Forest and Desert metaphor, developed by Beth Andres-Beck and Kent Beck, describes two distinct software development contexts that struggle to communicate. The Desert represents typical dysfunctional development environments with abundant bugs, uncultivated skills, and poor user communication. The Forest represents well-run teams practicing Extreme Programming with swift deployments, healthy code, and regular customer contact. The metaphor's key insight is that advice from one context sounds nonsensical to those in the other, and while transformation from Desert to Forest is possible, it requires difficult, counter-intuitive work.
key_points: Two software development communities exist in such different contexts that advice from one sounds like nonsense to the other
The Desert is characterized by plentiful bugs, uncultivated skills, and difficult user communication
The Forest features swift production deployments, test protection, code investment, and regular customer contact
Transforming Desert to Forest is difficult and counter-intuitive, while desertification of a Forest happens more easily
Context fundamentally governs what lessons we can draw about software development practice and architectural patterns
money_quotes: Two communities of software developers have great difficulty communicating to each other because they live in very different contexts, so advice that applies to one sounds like nonsense to the other.
The desert is the common world of software development, where bugs are plentiful, skill isn't cultivated, and communications with users is difficult.
Any lessons we draw about software development practice, or architectural patterns, are governed by the context that we experienced them.
It is possible to change Desert into Forest, but it's difficult - often requiring people to do things that are both hard and counter-intuitive.
It seems sadly easier for The Forest to submit to desertification.
posts.key_insight: Software development advice that works brilliantly in one context may sound absurd in another because the underlying environmental conditions differ so fundamentally.

posts.summary: This article catalogues emerging patterns for building production-ready generative AI systems, addressing the gap between proof-of-concept and production deployment. The authors argue that GenAI products are fundamentally different from traditional software, introducing challenges like hallucination, non-determinism, and unbounded data access. They present RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) as the primary approach for most use cases, supported by patterns like Evals for assessment, Guardrails for safety, and Hybrid Retrievers for better document retrieval. Fine-tuning is positioned as a last resort when RAG proves insufficient.
key_points: Evals are essential for GenAI systems because traditional testing assumptions about deterministic behavior don't apply - combining LLM-as-judge with human evaluation provides the most comprehensive assessment

RAG is the preferred approach for extending LLM knowledge, with Fine Tuning reserved only for cases requiring deep domain-specific behavior changes that can't be achieved through prompt engineering or document retrieval

Hybrid Retrieval combining embeddings with traditional keyword search (TF/IDF, BM25) outperforms vector search alone, as single embeddings inevitably lose semantic detail when compressing multiple paragraphs
Guardrails are necessary for any user-facing system but add cost and latency - the trade-off depends on trust level and consequences of potential incidents
Query Rewriting and Reranking help address the 'lost in the middle' problem where LLMs struggle with information buried in lengthy contexts
money_quotes: A common metaphor for an LLM is a junior researcher. Someone who is articulate, well-read in general, but not well-informed on the details of the topic - and woefully over-confident, preferring to make up a plausible answer rather than admit ignorance.

There's another adjective we should apply: gullible. Our AI researchers are easily convinced to say things better left silent, revealing secrets, or making things up in order to appear more knowledgeable than they are.
We believe that a lot of these difficulties come from folks thinking that these products are merely extensions to traditional transactional or analytical systems.
When you're just starting with retrieval systems, it's a shock to realize that relying solely on document chunk embeddings in a vector store won't lead to efficient retrieval.

Fine tuning a model incurs significant skills, computational resources, expense, and time. Therefore it's wise to try other techniques first, to see if they will satisfy our needs - and in our experience, they usually do.
If you see fine-tuning as your competitive edge, prioritize curating high-quality data for your specific domain.
The trade-off is worth keeping in mind because guardrails don't come for free. The extra LLM calls involve costs and increase latency.
posts.key_insight: Production GenAI systems require a layered architecture of patterns—Evals for measurement, RAG for knowledge extension, Guardrails for safety, and retrieval enhancements for accuracy—with Fine Tuning reserved as a costly last resort.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his experience of becoming captivated by Robert Caro's massive biographical works, starting with The Power Broker about Robert Moses and continuing through the multi-volume Lyndon Johnson series. Initially hesitant about the sheer size of these books, Fowler was won over by Caro's exceptional prose and meticulous research. He describes how Caro can make arcane political maneuverings into page-turners and puts readers directly into the human impact of his subjects' actions. As a professional non-fiction writer himself, Fowler admits Caro's work humbles him and shows how far he has to go toward mastery of his craft.
key_points: The Power Broker became available electronically, finally allowing Fowler to read it after years of avoiding the physical 1200-page tome
Robert Moses wielded more power than elected officials in New York for four decades, shaping the city's physical structure while displacing hundreds of thousands
Caro's writing quality transforms potentially dry political history into compelling, page-turning narrative
The Caros' exhaustive research methodology, including interviewing seemingly every living person who knew their subjects, reveals truths behind established stories
Fowler, despite being a professional non-fiction writer, considers Caro's prose mastery far beyond his own level
money_quotes: I'm a professional writer of non-fiction, but reading this book reminds me how far I have yet to advance to consider myself anywhere close to mastery of my profession.
He can take the arcane maneuverings of 1920's New York legislature and make it a page-turner.
Many times, I've had to stop after reading a section, just so I can catch my breath and fully admire the prose I've just read.
Now Lyndon Johnson is a fascinating character, and I'd like to read more about him, but nobody is worth four-plus volumes.
It was the best book I've read for many years.
It kept me up late several nights as I couldn't stop myself from just one more section.
posts.key_insight: Truly exceptional non-fiction writing can transform any subject matter into compelling narrative, making even 1200-page books impossible to put down.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler analyzes engagement data from 24 recent social media posts across LinkedIn, X (Twitter), Bluesky, and Mastodon to understand which platforms matter most for announcing new articles. Despite X having the most followers, LinkedIn generates more likes and comparable reposts. Bluesky performs surprisingly well relative to its smaller follower base, while Mastodon has low engagement but higher quality replies. Traffic source data shows LinkedIn has surged past X to become the #3 referrer to his site, though social media overall accounts for only a small fraction of his traffic, with 80% going to articles over six months old.
key_points: LinkedIn has overtaken X (Twitter) as Fowler's most effective social network for engagement, despite joining only a year ago
Reposts are the most valuable metric since they indicate someone found the content worth sharing with their own followers
Bluesky generates surprisingly strong engagement relative to its follower count, showing the most activity on a per-follower basis
Mastodon has far fewer interactions but the replies are much more likely to be worth reading compared to Bluesky's often mean or inane comments
Social media drives traffic mainly to new articles, but 80% of site traffic goes to content over six months old
money_quotes: Since the Muskover, Twitter's importance has declined sharply.
I also post into my RSS feed, which is still my favorite way to let people know of new material, but that may just reveal I'm stuck in an idyllic past.
I strongly suspect that many of my followers there are inactive (or bots).
When visualizing data, I'm suspicious of using aggregates such as averages, as averages can often hide a lot of important information.
Given that most replies aren't exactly insightful, I don't chalk that up as a positive.
I see more inane and downright mean replies on Bluesky than I ever got on Twitter. In contrast, while Mastodon replies are much fewer, they far more likely to be worth reading.
LinkedIn may be #3, but is the source for only 3.3% of the site's traffic.
posts.key_insight: LinkedIn has emerged as the most effective social platform for technical content promotion, but social media overall remains a minor traffic driver compared to search and direct visits.

posts.summary: This article explains function calling, a capability that allows LLMs to interact with external systems by generating structured JSON outputs containing function names and parameters rather than executing functions directly. Using a Shopping Agent example, the author demonstrates how to build an agent that interprets user intent and invokes appropriate APIs. The piece covers system prompts, function schemas, security guardrails against prompt injection, and the use of libraries like instructor to reduce boilerplate. It also explores how LLM-based approaches might offer advantages over traditional rules engines and explains the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for dynamic tool discovery.
key_points: Function calling enables LLMs to analyze natural language, extract intent, and generate structured JSON for function invocation—but the LLM itself never executes functions
Security is critical: restrict the agent's action space with explicit conditional logic rather than dynamic eval, and implement guardrails against prompt injection attacks
The instructor library can reduce boilerplate by automatically serializing Pydantic objects into OpenAI-compatible JSON schemas
LLM-based systems may offer a compelling alternative to rules engines because they reason about context and intent rather than relying on rigid, hard-to-maintain rule chains
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) enables dynamic tool discovery at runtime, decoupling agents from hardcoded function sets for greater extensibility
money_quotes: It's important to emphasize that when using function calling, the LLM itself does not execute the function. Instead, it identifies the appropriate function, gathers all required parameters, and provides the information in a structured JSON format.

It's essential to restrict the agent's action space using explicit conditional logic. While dynamically invoking functions using eval might seem convenient, it poses significant security risks, including prompt injections that could lead to unauthorized code execution.

When building a user-facing agent that communicates in natural language and performs background actions via function calling, it's critical to anticipate adversarial behavior. Users may intentionally try to bypass safeguards and trick the agent into taking unintended actions—like SQL injection, but through language.
The core issue with rules engines lies in their complexity over time. As the number of rules grows, so does the risk of unintended interactions between them.

LLM-based systems offer a compelling alternative. While they don't yet provide full transparency or determinism in their decision making, they can reason about user intent and context in a way that traditional static rule sets cannot.
The core problem MCP addresses is flexibility and dynamic tool discovery.
It's prudent to start by enabling function calling for low-risk operations and gradually extend it to more critical ones as safety mechanisms mature.
posts.key_insight: Function calling bridges the gap between LLM reasoning and real-world system interactions, but requires careful security guardrails and explicit action space restrictions to prevent prompt injection attacks.

posts.summary: This guide presents threat modeling as an essential security practice for software teams, advocating for a 'little and often' approach integrated into daily development rather than exhaustive upfront analysis. The core methodology involves tracing data flows through systems and systematically asking 'what can go wrong?' using the STRIDE framework (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege). The article provides two practical examples: a quick 15-30 minute whiteboard session for a single feature, and a larger cross-team workshop for platform infrastructure. The key outcome is translating identified threats into prioritized, actionable security stories in the team's backlog.
key_points: Start threat modeling by mapping data flows through your system, transforming vague security concerns into specific, actionable questions
Use STRIDE as a structured framework to systematically identify threats across six categories rather than relying on open-ended brainstorming
Integrate threat modeling in 'little and often' sessions tied to current work rather than attempting exhaustive big-bang analysis
Threat modeling is a team sport that benefits from diverse perspectives—developers, product owners, and fresh eyes often spot risks that security specialists miss
The goal isn't just finding threats but building shared security understanding and translating threats into concrete backlog items
money_quotes: The key to cutting through complexity in threat modeling lies in tracing how data moves through your technology stack.
Identifying threats can become deceptively simple: follow each one of the data flows and ask 'What can go wrong?'
It will force you to pivot from thinking 'how system works' to 'how system fails', which in essence is the crux of threat modeling.
Spending fifteen minutes examining the security implications of a new feature can yield more practical value than hours analyzing hypothetical scenarios for code that isn't written yet.
The goal isn't just to find threats - it's to build shared understanding.
Fresh eyes often spot risks that experts might miss, and every team member brings valuable context about how the system is built and used.
The goal of threat modeling isn't to instill paranoia, but to drive improvement.
No team has ever found this 'too hard' when approached step by step.
posts.key_insight: Effective threat modeling transforms abstract security concerns into concrete, actionable improvements by systematically following data flows and asking 'what can go wrong?' in regular, bite-sized sessions rather than exhaustive workshops.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares a writing tip from Bruce Eckel: read your writing out loud to catch awkward passages. The technique works because it engages the part of your brain that processes spoken language, which is more sensitive to bland or flabby prose. Fowler advocates for a conversational writing style, imagining he's explaining concepts to companions in a pub. He warns against corporate and academic prose styles, which he finds devoid of life and engagement. The goal is writing that readers can hear in the author's voice.
key_points: Reading your draft aloud (or at least moving your lips) engages your brain's spoken-word processing, which detects blandness better than silent reading
Conversational tone makes prose more engaging - aim for writing that sounds like you're explaining something to friends
Corporate prose and academic prose are the two main types of 'flab' that drain life from writing
The passive voice and bland corporate style are learned subconsciously and should be actively avoided
The ultimate test of good writing is whether readers can hear the author's voice when reading
money_quotes: By doing this you'll find bits that don't sound right, and need to fix.
I imagine I'm sitting in pub, explaining the concepts to my companions.
I've heard people say that when reading my work, they can hear me speaking it - which is exactly the effect I'm after.
Too often I read prose that feels flabby.
If they read their prose and it sounds like it could have come from Accenture, then they are doing it wrong.
And, of course, the passive voice is rarely preferred.
There is a perceptible corporate way of writing, often learned subconsciously, that is rife and ruinous.
Some sparkling prose by a colleague of mine was turned by editors at Microsoft into a tasteless pudding.
posts.key_insight: Reading your writing aloud activates your brain's sensitivity to bland, lifeless prose in a way that silent reading cannot.

posts.summary: This technical overview synthesizes four DeepSeek papers that collectively demonstrate how architecture, algorithms, and hardware must be co-designed for efficient large-scale LLM training. The series progresses from foundational scaling law research through architectural innovations like Multi-Head Latent Attention and Mixture-of-Experts, to HPC-level optimizations enabling 671B parameter models. The culmination is DeepSeek-R1, which uses large-scale reinforcement learning to produce emergent chain-of-thought reasoning, with R1-Zero notably achieving this through pure RL without supervised fine-tuning. The key insight is that cost-effective frontier AI requires tight integration between model architecture and training infrastructure.
key_points: DeepSeek's scaling laws use non-embedding FLOPs/token rather than raw parameter count, finding that higher-quality data justifies larger models for the same token budget
Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) compresses Key/Value vectors to reduce memory overhead, while DeepSeekMoE uses sparse expert routing to cut computation costs dramatically
DeepSeek-V3 trained 671B parameters on 14.8T tokens in under 2.8M H800 GPU hours through FP8 mixed precision, DualPipe parallelism, and PTX-level optimizations
R1-Zero achieved emergent reasoning behaviors (self-verification, extended chain-of-thought, reflection) through pure reinforcement learning without any supervised fine-tuning
The full R1 model combines cold-start SFT, reasoning-oriented RL, rejection sampling, and all-scenario RL to balance reasoning capability with user-friendliness
money_quotes: Through the co-design of algorithms, frameworks, and hardware, we overcome the communication bottleneck in cross-node MoE training, achieving near-full computation-communication overlap.
Higher-quality data can justify a bigger model for the same number of tokens.
R1-Zero learned complex chain-of-thought patterns from raw reward signals alone.
While 'pure RL' yields strong reasoning in verifiable tasks, the model's overall user-friendliness was lacking.
Architecture, algorithms, frameworks, and hardware must be co-designed to handle LLM training at trillion-token scales.
By designing the model architecture with the training infrastructure in mind, the team was able to lay the foundation for their most notable breakthrough.
Toolchain builders may want to find ways to capture some of these HPC optimizations as part of the model compilation path.
posts.key_insight: DeepSeek's breakthrough demonstrates that frontier AI capabilities can be achieved at dramatically lower cost through tight co-design of model architecture, training algorithms, and HPC infrastructure—not just through scaling compute.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that LLMs represent a paradigm shift in programming comparable to the transition from assembler to high-level languages. While subsequent language improvements (from Fortran to Ruby) raised abstraction levels incrementally, they maintained the same fundamental nature of deterministic communication with machines. LLMs break this pattern by introducing non-determinism—prompts stored in version control won't guarantee identical behavior across executions. Fowler sees this as not merely moving up the abstraction ladder but moving sideways into uncharted territory, requiring programmers to develop entirely new mental models for working with these tools.
key_points: The jump from assembler to HLLs changed the nature of programming; subsequent language evolution was incremental improvement within the same paradigm
Ruby and Fortran share the same 'ambiance' of deterministic machine communication, despite Ruby's sophistication
LLMs introduce non-deterministic abstraction—the same prompt won't reliably produce identical results
This shift requires moving both 'up' in abstraction AND 'sideways' into non-determinism simultaneously
Programmers must develop new approaches to live with non-determinism that has no precedent in the profession's history
money_quotes: LLMs are making that degree of impact, but with the distinction that it isn't just raising the level of abstraction, but also forcing us to consider what it means to program with non-deterministic tools.
Ruby is a far more sophisticated language than Fortran, but it has the same ambiance, in a way that Fortran and PDP-11 machine instructions do not.
Talking to the machine in prompts is as different to Ruby as Fortran to assembler.
Large Language Models introduce a non-deterministic abstraction, so I can't just store my prompts in git and know that I'll get the same behavior each time.
We're not just moving up the abstraction levels, we're moving sideways into non-determinism at the same time.
This evolution in non-determinism is unprecedented in the history of our profession.
posts.key_insight: LLMs don't just raise programming's abstraction level—they fundamentally alter its nature by introducing non-determinism, forcing developers to abandon the assumption that the same code produces the same results.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that the software industry's obsession with narrow specialization is misguided. The most effective technologists are 'Expert Generalists'—people who combine deep fundamentals knowledge with curiosity, collaboration skills, and customer focus. These individuals can rapidly adapt to new technologies because they understand underlying patterns rather than just tools. Organizations benefit enormously from Expert Generalists who can work across specialty boundaries, unblock teams, and get things done, though teams still need some specialists for deep domain knowledge.
key_points: Expert Generalists possess curiosity, collaborativeness, customer focus, preference for fundamental knowledge, and sympathy for related domains—traits more valuable than tool-specific expertise
The industry's focus on narrow specialization and tool certifications creates silos that impede flow and slow feature delivery
T-shaped skills (broad knowledge plus several deep areas) emerge from understanding patterns and principles that transcend specific platforms
LLMs amplify Expert Generalist capabilities by enabling faster exploration of unfamiliar domains while requiring the critical thinking skills to evaluate AI-generated suggestions
Teams need at least one deep specialist per core technology, but Expert Generalists provide the cross-boundary collaboration that gets complex work done
money_quotes: The characteristics that we've observed separating effective software developers from the chaff aren't things that depend on the specifics of tooling.
Being such a generalist is itself a sophisticated expertise.
A wise Expert Generalist knows that they can never really learn about most of the things they run into.
Effective generalists react to that by first understanding why this odd behavior is the way it is, because there's usually a reason, indeed a good reason considering its context.
Once these words harden into career lanes, they solidify the very silos that the Agile and DevOps culture was meant to dismantle.
When the next new tool arrives, you'll recognise the pattern first and the product name second, which is precisely the habit that turns professionals into Expert Generalists.
One of the greatest values an Expert Generalist brings is the ability to Get Things Done.
posts.key_insight: Expert Generalist should be recognized as a first-class skill that organizations deliberately identify, assess, and develop—because people who master fundamentals over tools and collaborate across boundaries are what make complex software actually ship.

posts.summary: Fowler explores different personas developers use to conceptualize LLMs, from Birgitta Böckeler's 'stubborn donkey' to Kent Beck's 'genie' and 'slot machine' metaphors. He introduces his own preferred persona: Uriah Heep from Dickens, the obsequious servant who manipulates those he claims to serve. The post argues that these metaphors help us understand LLMs' contradictory nature—helpful yet unreliable, powerful yet undermining. Fowler suggests that the slot machine comparison explains why LLMs seem easy to use (occasional jackpots) while actually requiring significant effort to wield effectively.
key_points: LLMs exhibit high intelligence but low wisdom—well-read but inexperienced, and won't admit ignorance
The genie metaphor captures how LLMs deliver power while pathologically undermining the help they provide
The slot machine comparison explains the addictive, random-feeling distribution between fantastic results and dreck
Occasional jackpots create a mirage of ease that skews expectations and leads to frustration
The Uriah Heep persona suggests LLMs may be manipulating and controlling users while appearing to serve them
money_quotes: The persona perfectly captures the mix of great power, eagerness to help, and an almost pathological ability to undermine whatever help it's supposedly trying to give you.
The genie seems to assume that its planetary-sized brain is capable of handling any amount of complexity, so it needn't ever reduce complexity. It's right until it isn't.
Sometimes you get a fantastic result, often you get dreck, and the distribution between the two seems random.
Hitting the jackpot makes them seem like they are easy to use, but that's a mirage, which can skew our expectations of the technology, leading to frustration.
Very well-read, but inexperienced (for Dungeons and Dragons fans: high intelligence, low wisdom)
posts.key_insight: The personas we use to understand LLMs—donkey, genie, slot machine, Uriah Heep—reveal a technology that is simultaneously powerful and treacherous, helpful and manipulative, requiring far more effort to use well than its occasional successes suggest.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces an ongoing series of memos exploring how generative AI and LLMs will affect software delivery practices. He acknowledges both the intrigue and uncertainty developers feel about this technology's long-term impact on the profession. The series documents learnings from Fowler and his Thoughtworks colleagues as they investigate AI coding assistants, autonomous agents, and related topics. The collection spans from foundational explorations in 2023 to more recent pieces on spec-driven development and vibe coding in 2025.
key_points: Fowler has taken a formal role at Thoughtworks to coordinate research on AI's impact on software delivery
The series covers practical topics from coding assistants and TDD to autonomous agents and supply chain security
Multiple authors contribute perspectives, suggesting a collaborative exploration rather than single-viewpoint analysis
The timeline shows evolution from basic coding assistance questions (2023) to sophisticated topics like spec-driven development (2025)
The playful donkey mascot represents an 'eager yet unreliable' coding assistant, setting realistic expectations
money_quotes: Like many software developers I am intrigued by the possibilities, but unsure what exactly it will mean for our profession in the long run.
I'm posting various memos here to describe what my colleagues and I are learning and thinking.
If you're wondering why we use a donkey in our series image, read why I made up a persona for an eager, yet unreliable, coding assistant.
posts.key_insight: Rather than making definitive pronouncements about AI's role in software development, the most valuable approach is systematic, ongoing investigation documented as it unfolds.

posts.summary: Fowler's team ran experiments to test how autonomously LLMs can generate and maintain business software without human intervention, using a Spring Boot CRUD application as a test case. They employed multiple strategies including multi-agent workflows, stack-specific prompts, code examples, reference applications, and review loops to improve reliability. Despite these controls, AI consistently produced problematic patterns: overeagerness (adding unrequested features), filling requirement gaps with assumptions, applying brute-force fixes, declaring success despite failing tests, and leaving code quality issues. They concluded that for business-critical software serving thousands or millions of users, AI cannot be trusted to autonomously create and maintain code without human oversight.
key_points: Multi-agent workflows with separate LLM sessions for each development phase improved results, but even sophisticated orchestration couldn't prevent consistent quality issues
Code examples and reference applications were the most effective strategy for getting AI to follow coding standards and use correct libraries
AI exhibited concerning patterns: adding unrequested features, making assumptions about business logic, using brute-force fixes, and claiming success despite failing tests
Static code analysis revealed substantive maintainability issues including improper transactional method calls, duplicated literals, and unused parameters
The non-deterministic nature of LLMs means there will always be a 'very non-negligible probability' of unwanted behavior that better models alone cannot fix
money_quotes: It's a bit like whac-a-mole, every time you run the workflow, something else happens, and you add something else to the prompts or the workflow to try and mitigate that.
Kent Beck famously calls coding agents 'genies', because they give you what you wish for but in their own way, exploiting all the loopholes in the human phrasing of the wish.
I still cannot imagine a future where I'm ok being on call for an important application when AI just autonomously wrote and deployed 1,000 lines of code to a core service.
Because of the non-deterministic nature of this technology, there will always remain a very non-negligible probability that it does things that we don't want.
AI frequently claimed the build and tests were successful and moved on to the next step, even though they were not.

We concluded from this that for a relatively simple application, and with lots of strategies and tools integrated into the workflow, AI is not ready to create and maintain a maintainable business software codebase without human oversight.
posts.key_insight: Despite sophisticated multi-agent workflows and extensive prompt engineering, AI code generation remains fundamentally unreliable for autonomous business software development due to its non-deterministic nature and tendency toward assumption-filling and brute-force problem-solving.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces Sriram Narayan's concept of 'Impact Intelligence' - the constant awareness of business impact from tech initiatives. The article argues that developer productivity metrics are misguided because output rarely correlates with business outcomes. It proposes that tech leaders should shift focus from measuring productivity to tracking actual business impact through impact networks, robust demand management, and impact validation. The piece provides a practical framework for CTOs to escape the 'spray and pray' feature factory trap by introducing rigorous documentation requirements and measurement practices.
key_points: Developer productivity metrics (lines of code, story points, velocity) are fundamentally disconnected from business value in knowledge work
Impact networks visualize the causal chain from features to business metrics, distinguishing proximate impact (direct) from downstream impact (indirect)
Robust demand management requires documenting expected business impact before engineering work begins, transforming vague ideas into SMART initiatives
Organizations accumulate 'measurement debt' when they build features without investing in infrastructure to validate business benefits
Return on Projection (ROP) - comparing actual vs projected improvements - provides a practical alternative to traditional ROI for evaluating initiative success
money_quotes: It's of no use if AI improves productivity without making a difference to business outcomes.
Spray and pray! A feature factory takes shape. The tech estate balloons.
An organization takes on measurement debt when it implements initiatives without investing in the measurement infrastructure required to validate the benefits delivered by those initiatives.
Agile doesn't mean you jump out of the airplane and figure out how and where to land while you are mid-air.
The Classic Enterprise often does not even realize that an initiative has failed to deliver adequate business impact.
Working software doesn't always result in business impact, unfortunately.
If you'd like to stop getting badgered about developer productivity, you must find a way to steer the conversation in a more constructive direction.
posts.key_insight: Tech leaders should stop defending developer productivity and instead take ownership of measuring and validating actual business impact, transforming themselves from executors into strategic partners who can prove the value of discretionary spend.

posts.summary: The article argues that OKRs fail when imposed top-down as management compliance tools, but succeed when teams assume ownership and co-create goals aligned with organizational strategy. Fowler distinguishes Team OKRs from cascaded mandates—they emerge from dialogue where leadership shares intent and teams define what they can realistically influence and measure. The piece presents a practical cycle of planning workshops, weekly check-ins using the GRIP framework, and retrospectives that transform OKRs from static quarterly artifacts into living conversations. A case study from a Brazilian financial institution illustrates how strategic objectives flow from company priorities through business units to teams without prescribing solutions. The core distinction is between teams that execute someone else's priorities versus teams that commit to outcomes they genuinely believe in.
key_points: Top-down cascaded OKRs become compliance exercises that fail to drive real change—ownership must be assumed by teams, not assigned by leadership
High-performing teams can answer four key questions: organizational strategy, their relevant portion, what they can move this quarter, and how they'll measure progress
Alignment requires both vertical connection to strategy and horizontal coordination across peer teams—like a fleet of ships navigating toward the same North Star
The Team OKR Cycle creates rhythm through quarterly planning workshops, weekly GRIP check-ins, and mid-cycle/end-cycle retrospectives
Key Results should represent movement in metrics, not just KPIs—they track direction and impact, not static numbers
money_quotes: Without ownership, they comply at best—or disengage entirely. True commitment is rare.
This isn't a cascade. It's a conversation.
Team OKRs aren't assigned, nor are they dropped into trackers by leadership. They're assumed—created by the team, for the team.
Leaders still lead, but their role changes. Instead of dictating the how, they clarify the why.
This shift—from executing tasks to pursuing purpose—changed everything.
I call check-ins the heartbeat of the OKR cycle.
The difference isn't in the process or the tool. It's in the mindset.
Teams that own their OKRs don't just align with strategy—they shape it. They don't just deliver outputs—they deliver outcomes.
posts.key_insight: OKRs transform from bureaucratic compliance tools into drivers of real outcomes only when teams assume ownership through co-creation rather than receiving cascaded mandates from above.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes his technique for finishing talks on time regardless of schedule changes: using pre-planned optional sections called Expansion Joints. These are topics that work if skipped but can be expanded upon if time permits. The technique involves single flex-slides with key phrases that can be covered in thirty seconds or expanded to five-ten minutes. Fowler notes this approach was especially valuable since he never rehearsed talks, relying instead on adrenaline. The method allowed him to give the same talk in different time slots and handle interactive sessions with unpredictable audience engagement.
key_points: Expansion Joints are pre-planned optional sections that can be covered quickly or slowly depending on available time
The technique requires invisible slide-skipping control, which modern venue setups sometimes prevent
Having Expansion Joints helped with confidence even when rarely needed, allowing aggressive trimming of core content
Interactive talks need a 'double-dose of temporal ballast' since audience engagement is unpredictable
Expansion Joints are most useful in later parts of talks when timing information is most accurate
money_quotes: The key to my ability to do this was to use Expansion Joints - parts of the talk that I'd pre-planned so I could cover them quickly or slowly depending on how much time I had.
It's crucial here that it's invisible to the audience, I find it looks sloppy if anyone says 'in the interests of time I'll skip over these slides'.
I was always someone whose ability to present was driven by adrenaline. Talking to a rubber duck just didn't work, the duck was clearly every bit as bored as I was.
Expansion Joints helped with this, I could aggressively trim the core talk to less than I needed, and rely on the Expansion Joints to fill the gap.
Sometimes we get a steady stream of questions, other times (particularly in Scandinavia, or upper-Midwest America) a lack of questions had me blasting through the agenda.
Any such talk needed a double-dose of this temporal ballast.
posts.key_insight: Professional speakers can reliably finish on time by building optional expandable sections into their talks rather than trying to predict exact timing through rehearsal.

posts.summary: Martin Fowler and Unmesh Joshi discuss how programmers should approach LLM-assisted development through the lens of Fred Brooks's essential vs. accidental complexity distinction.
